Crick's **Wobble Hypothesis** (1966) addresses the apparent paradox of how **61 sense codons** can be recognized by only about **30–40 tRNA species**.

**Postulate:** The base-pairing between the **3rd base of the mRNA codon** and the **1st base of the tRNA anticodon** is **less strict** than Watson-Crick pairing. This flexible "wobble" allows non-standard pairings (e.g., G–U pairing, or inosine pairing with A/U/C).

**Allowed Wobble Pairings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anticodon 5' Base} &\to \text{Codon 3' Bases Recognized} \\
\text{G} &\to \text{C, U} \\
\text{U} &\to \text{A, G} \\
\text{I (Inosine)} &\to \text{A, U, C} \\
\text{C} &\to \text{G (only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**How it Accounts for Degeneracy:**

- Most synonymous codons differ **only at the 3rd position** (e.g., GCU, GCC, GCA, GCG all code for Alanine).
- The wobble allows a single tRNA anticodon to recognize **all codons in a "four-fold degenerate" family**.
- For example, an anticodon with **I (inosine) at the wobble position** can pair with A, U, or C — covering three of the four codons in a family.
- This reduces the required tRNA count from 61 to ~40–45, making translation **economical**.

**Implication:** The wobble mechanism explains why the **first two bases of a codon** are more critical for amino acid identity, while the **third base** acts as a "fine-tuner". **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) With a suitable example, explain the concept of Open Reading Frames (ORFs) and reading frames. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reading Frame Definition:**
A reading frame is one of **three possible ways** to group a continuous nucleotide sequence into a series of non-overlapping codons. Since codons are triplets, the starting offset (position 1, 2, or 3) defines the frame.

**Open Reading Frame (ORF):**
An ORF is a reading frame that:
- Begins with a **start codon (ATG in DNA / AUG in mRNA)**.
- Contains a series of **sense codons**.
- Ends with a **stop codon (TAA, TAG, TGA in DNA)**.
- Has a minimum length threshold (typically ≥ 100 codons / 300 bp in prokaryotes, or ≥ 50–100 in eukaryotes depending on the algorithm).

**Example:**
Consider the DNA sequence:
$$
5'\text{-TACATGCAAACCGATTAGGTTAATA-}3'
$$

Let us examine the three forward reading frames:

**Frame 1 (start at position 1):**
$$
\text{TAC-ATG-CAA-ACC-GAT-TAG-GTT-AAT-A}
$$
- Contains a **TAC** (Tyr), **ATG** (Met, START), ..., **TAG** (STOP)
- Valid ORF: **ATG-CAA-ACC-GAT** = Met-Gln-Thr-Asp (4 amino acids before stop)

**Frame 2 (start at position 2):**
$$
\text{ACA-TGC-AAA-CCG-ATT-AGG-TTA-ATA}
$$
- No ATG start codon and no in-frame stop — no ORF.

**Frame 3 (start at position 3):**
$$
\text{CAT-GCA-AAC-CGA-TTA-GGT-TAA-TA}
$$
- No ATG start — no ORF.

**Computational Implication:**
For any DNA sequence, **6 reading frames** must be analyzed (3 forward + 3 reverse complement). Tools like **ORFfinder (NCBI)**, **Glimmer**, and **Prodigal** automate this search to identify protein-coding genes. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Discuss the universality of the genetic code. List exceptions found in mitochondrial genomes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Universality:**
The standard genetic code (NCBI Table 1) is **near-universal** — the same codon assignments are used by virtually all organisms, from *E. coli* to humans. This universality is one of the strongest pieces of evidence for a **common evolutionary origin** of all life on Earth.

**Evidence for Universality:**
- Recombinant DNA technology: Genes can be transferred from one species to another and still produce the correct protein (e.g., human insulin produced in *E. coli*).
- Evolutionary conservation of codon-amino acid mappings across all three domains of life.

**Exceptions in Mitochondrial Genomes:**

Mitochondria use a **slightly modified genetic code**, inherited from their bacterial ancestor and shaped by reduced genome size and unique tRNA populations.

| Organism | Codon | Standard Code | Mitochondrial Code |
|----------|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Vertebrates | **UGA** | Stop | Tryptophan (Trp) |
| Vertebrates | **AGA, AGG** | Arginine | Stop |
| Vertebrates | **AUA** | Isoleucine | Methionine |
| Yeast | **CUN** | Leucine | Threonine |
| Invertebrates | **AGA, AGG** | Arginine | Serine |
| Echinoderms | **AAA** | Lysine | Asparagine |

**Computational Importance:**
Bioinformatics tools (e.g., **BLAST, ExPASy Translate, EMBOSS transeq**) allow the user to **select the appropriate genetic code** (NCBI provides 33+ translation tables) before translating a sequence. Using the wrong table produces **incorrect amino acid sequences** — a frequent and costly bug in annotation pipelines.

**Conclusion:**
The genetic code is **almost universal but not absolutely so**. The exceptions are evolutionarily significant and computationally critical for accurate genome annotation. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "degenerate" with "ambiguous"** — Degenerate means *one amino acid → many codons*; Ambiguous would mean *one codon → many amino acids* (the genetic code is NOT ambiguous).
> 2. **Forgetting the start codon** — Many students list only stop codons. **AUG (Methionine) is the start codon**, and this dual role is a favorite KTU question.
> 3. **Assuming 100% universality** — Mitochondrial codes differ! Examiners love to test this.
> 4. **Confusing "anticodon" with "codon"** — Codons are on mRNA (5'→3'); anticodons are on tRNA (3'→5' read, but conventionally written 5'→3').
> 5. **Skipping the Wobble explanation** — When asked about degeneracy, always mention wobble to earn full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Genetic Codes:**

- ✅ The genetic code is a **triplet, non-overlapping, comma-less, directional** code read 5'→3'.
- ✅ $4^3 = 64$ codons encode **20 amino acids + 3 stop signals**.
- ✅ **Degenerate** (redundant) but **unambiguous** (each codon → only one amino acid).
- ✅ **Start codon = AUG** (Methionine in eukaryotes, fMet in prokaryotes/bacteria).
- ✅ **Stop codons = UAA, UAG, UGA** (no tRNA, recognized by release factors).
- ✅ **Universal** with exceptions in mitochondrial and some microbial genomes.
- ✅ **Crick's Wobble Hypothesis** explains degeneracy: 3rd base pairing is flexible, allowing one tRNA to recognize multiple codons.
- ✅ **6 reading frames** (3 forward + 3 reverse complement) are analyzed to find ORFs.
- ✅ **ORF = Start codon + sense codons + stop codon** (with minimum length).
- ✅ **Bioinformatics applications:** Gene prediction (Glimmer, Prodigal), translation tools (ExPASy, EMBOSS), codon usage analysis, comparative genomics.
- ✅ NCBI provides **33+ genetic code translation tables** — always select the correct one for your organism/system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Datatypes - Gene Expression Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Datatypes - Gene Expression Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Datatypes – Gene Expression Data

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEITT861 (Bioinformatics)**, **Gene Expression Data** refers to the quantitative, high-dimensional molecular data generated by measuring the transcriptional activity of thousands of genes simultaneously across one or more biological samples (cells, tissues, conditions, or time points). Each datapoint captures the abundance of mRNA transcripts produced from a specific gene, providing a snapshot of the cell's functional state at the molecular level.

The two principal datatypes that dominate this field are:

1. **Continuous Intensity Data** – generated by **DNA Microarrays** (e.g., Affymetrix GeneChips), yielding fluorescence intensities typically in the range of **100 to 65,000 arbitrary units (a.u.)**.
2. **Discrete Count Data** – generated by **RNA Sequencing (RNA-Seq)** platforms (e.g., Illumina NovaSeq), yielding integer read counts ranging from **0 to several hundred thousand** per gene per sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The classification of gene expression datatypes is foundational. The University examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **microarray-based continuous data** and **RNA-Seq-based count data**, because the choice of downstream normalization and statistical method is directly driven by the datatype.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "City Traffic Report" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a city with **20,000 neighborhoods (genes)**. You want to know which neighborhoods are "busy" (highly expressed) and which are "quiet" (silenced).
>
> - A **Microarray** is like shining a **single floodlight on every neighborhood at once** and measuring how much light bounces back. The reading is a *continuous, relative* measurement — a dim reflection might mean a quiet neighborhood, *or* a dirty window blocking the light.
> - **RNA-Seq** is like **counting every single car that leaves every neighborhood** during one hour. You get an *exact, integer count* — far more precise, but the counts depend on the size of the neighborhood (longer genes produce more cars just by being longer).
>
> The **datatype** you work with dictates the tools, the math, and the answers you can get.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical/Biological Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total human protein-coding genes | $\approx$ **20,000 – 25,000** |
| Typical microarray probe sets per chip | $\approx$ **500,000** |
| Typical RNA-Seq reads per sample (modern) | **20 – 100 million** paired-end reads |
| Reference transcriptome size (human) | $\approx$ **3.2 × 10⁹** bases |
| Minimum expression threshold (TPM) | $\geq$ **1 TPM** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Datatypes

### 2.1.1 Microarray Gene Expression Data
- **Probe Design:** Short oligonucleotide probes (25-mers for Affymetrix, 60–70-mers for Agilent) are immobilized on a solid surface.
- **Hybridization:** Fluorescently labeled cDNA from the sample binds (or not) to complementary probes.
- **Signal Output:** A laser excites the fluorophore; a photomultiplier records a **continuous intensity value**.
- **Raw Format:** `.CEL` files (Affymetrix) containing a matrix of intensity values per probe per array.
- **Data Matrix Shape:** $M \times N$, where $M$ = number of probes/genes ($\approx 20,000$ for human) and $N$ = number of samples.

### 2.1.2 RNA-Seq Gene Expression Data
- **Library Preparation:** mRNA is fragmented, reverse-transcribed to cDNA, and sequencing adapters are ligated.
- **Sequencing:** High-throughput sequencer generates **short reads** (50–150 bp).
- **Alignment:** Reads are mapped to a reference genome or transcriptome.
- **Quantification:** For each gene, the number of reads mapping within its boundaries is counted.
- **Raw Format:** `BAM/SAM` (alignment) or simple **count matrices** (gene × sample) of integer values.
- **Data Matrix Shape:** $M \times N$, where $M$ = number of genes and $N$ = number of samples; entries $c_{ij} \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The data matrix is the fundamental object of every downstream analysis — clustering, differential expression, and pathway analysis all operate on this matrix. The *type* of number inside the matrix (continuous intensity vs. integer count) is what forces different normalization strategies.

## 2.2 The Three Normalization Universes

The **"Why"** behind normalization: Genes, samples, and sequencing runs differ in length, depth, and efficiency. Normalization removes technical noise so that biological signal can be compared.

### 2.2.1 RPKM (Reads Per Kilobase per Million mapped reads)
Used for **single-end** RNA-Seq data.

### 2.2.2 FPKM (Fragments Per Kilobase per Million mapped fragments)
Used for **paired-end** RNA-Seq data (a "fragment" can generate two reads).

### 2.2.3 TPM (Transcripts Per Million)
A length-normalized metric that is **comparable across samples** by design.

### 2.2.4 Microarray RMA Normalization
**Robust Multi-array Average (RMA):** A three-step pipeline — *background correction* → *quantile normalization* → *log2 summarization*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Domain / Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $RPKM_{i} = \dfrac{r_{i} \cdot 10^{9}}{L_{i} \cdot R_{total}}$ | $r_i$ = reads mapping to gene $i$; $L_i$ = gene length in **bases**; $R_{total}$ = total mapped reads | $L_i \geq 1$ bp; $R_{total} > 0$ |
| 2 | $FPKM_{i} = \dfrac{f_{i} \cdot 10^{9}}{L_{i} \cdot F_{total}}$ | $f_i$ = fragments mapping to gene $i$; $L_i$ = length in **bases**; $F_{total}$ = total fragments | Paired-end RNA-Seq only |
| 3 | $TPM_{i} = \dfrac{r_{i} / L_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}(r_{j} / L_{j})} \cdot 10^{6}$ | $N$ = total number of genes | $\sum TPM_{i} = 10^{6}$ |
| 4 | $\log_2(\text{fold change})_{AB} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{\bar{X}_{A}}{\bar{X}_{B}}\right)$ | $\bar{X}_A, \bar{X}_B$ = mean normalized expression in groups A and B | Threshold: $\vert \log_2 FC \vert \geq 1$ |
| 5 | $RMA_{ij} = \log_2\left(S_{ij}^{bg\text{-corrected, q-norm}}\right)$ | $S_{ij}$ = probe-level signal for probe $j$ in array $i$ | Affymetrix microarray only |
| 6 | $TMM_{factor,k} = \dfrac{(1 - p_{k}) \cdot G'_{k}}{p_{k} \cdot G'_{k,\text{reference}}}$ | $p_k$ = proportion of reads in sample $k$; $G'$ = geometric mean of gene counts | Used in **edgeR** for count data |
| 7 | $\text{Dispersion}_{i} = \dfrac{\mu_{i}^{2}}{V_{i}} + \phi \cdot \mu_{i}$ | $\mu_i$ = mean count; $V_i$ = variance; $\phi$ = biological CV | Negative Binomial model for counts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Identity (frequently tested):** $TPM_{i} = \dfrac{FPKM_{i}}{\sum_{j} FPKM_{j}} \cdot 10^{6}$. In other words, **FPKM and TPM carry the same biological information**; they only differ by a per-sample rescaling.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Science

Gene expression datatypes are the input to:
- **Differential Expression Analysis** (DESeq2, edgeR, limma) — identifies disease biomarkers.
- **Clustering & Classification** (k-means, hierarchical clustering, SVM) — discovers cancer subtypes.
- **Pathway & Network Analysis** (KEGG, GO enrichment) — interprets biological mechanisms.
- **Machine Learning Pipelines** — used in precision medicine, drug response prediction, and clinical decision support.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RPKM from First Principles

We want a number that reflects **how much** of a transcript is present, independent of (a) how long the gene is, and (b) how deep we sequenced.

**Step 1 — Remove the effect of gene length (per-base normalization).**

A longer gene will naturally capture more reads simply because it has more bases for reads to map to. We divide by gene length (converted to kilobases, kb) to get a *per-base* read density:

$$d_{i} = \dfrac{r_{i}}{L_{i}/1000} = \dfrac{r_{i} \cdot 1000}{L_{i}}$$

where $L_{i}$ is in bases.

**Step 2 — Remove the effect of sequencing depth (per-million normalization).**

If we sequenced twice as deeply, every gene would have roughly twice as many reads. We normalize by the total number of mapped reads (converted to millions):

$$n_{i} = \dfrac{d_{i}}{R_{total}/10^{6}} = \dfrac{d_{i} \cdot 10^{6}}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 3 — Combine both normalizations into a single formula.**

Substituting the expression for $d_{i}$ from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
RPKM_{i} &= \dfrac{r_{i} \cdot 1000}{L_{i}} \cdot \dfrac{10^{6}}{R_{total}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1000} \\
&= \dfrac{r_{i} \cdot 10^{9}}{L_{i} \cdot R_{total}}
\end{aligned}$$

> The factor $10^{9}$ is simply $\dfrac{10^{6} \cdot 10^{3}}{10^{0}}$ — it makes the units consistent when $L_{i}$ is in bases and $R_{total}$ is in raw read count.

## 3.2 Derivation: TPM from RPKM

TPM is a rescaling of RPKM such that the TPM values across all genes sum to **exactly one million** for every sample. This makes TPM **column-comparable** across samples.

**Step 1 — Compute the per-base rate for gene $i$:**

$$a_{i} = \dfrac{r_{i}}{L_{i}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of per-base rates across all $N$ genes in the sample:**

$$S = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{j} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \dfrac{r_{j}}{L_{j}}$$

**Step 3 — Scale each $a_{i}$ so that the total equals $10^{6}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TPM_{i} &= \dfrac{a_{i}}{S} \cdot 10^{6} \\
&= \dfrac{r_{i} / L_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}(r_{j} / L_{j})} \cdot 10^{6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (sum-to-million identity):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} TPM_{i} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{a_{i}}{S} \cdot 10^{6} \\
&= \dfrac{10^{6}}{S} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_{i} \\
&= \dfrac{10^{6}}{S} \cdot S \\
&= 10^{6} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** In an RNA-Seq experiment, gene **BRCA1** has the following measurements:

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Reads mapping to BRCA1, $r_{BRCA1}$ | **1,800** |
| Gene length of BRCA1, $L_{BRCA1}$ | **7,200 bases** |
| Total mapped reads in the sample, $R_{total}$ | **20,000,000** |
| Sum of $r_j / L_j$ over all genes, $S$ | **800 (units: reads/base)** |

**Compute RPKM, then TPM, and verify the sum-to-million rule for the whole sample.**

### Step A — Compute RPKM for BRCA1

$$\begin{aligned}
RPKM_{BRCA1} &= \dfrac{r_{BRCA1} \cdot 10^{9}}{L_{BRCA1} \cdot R_{total}} \\
&= \dfrac{1800 \cdot 10^{9}}{7200 \cdot 20{,}000{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{12}}{1.44 \times 10^{11}} \\
&= 12.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** BRCA1 is expressed at **12.5 RPKM**, a moderately abundant transcript in this sample.

### Step B — Compute TPM for BRCA1

The per-base rate for BRCA1 is:

$$a_{BRCA1} = \dfrac{1800}{7200} = 0.25 \text{ reads/base}$$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
TPM_{BRCA1} &= \dfrac{0.25}{800} \cdot 10^{6} \\
&= 0.0003125 \cdot 10^{6} \\
&= 312.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** BRCA1 contributes **312.5 transcripts per million** in this sample.

### Step C — Numerical Sanity Check (Sum-to-Million)

By the identity derived in Step 4 of §3.2, summing TPM across all $N$ genes:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} TPM_{i} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{800} \cdot 800 = 1{,}000{,}000 = 10^{6} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Examiners love asking students to compute **RPKM → TPM** conversion. The one-line identity to remember is:
> $$TPM_{i} = \dfrac{RPKM_{i}}{\sum_{j} RPKM_{j}} \cdot 10^{6}$$
> This works because RPKM is already proportional to $r_i / L_i$.

## 3.4 The Negative Binomial Model for Count Data (Analytical Matrix)

For RNA-Seq count data, the gene-level read count $K_{ij}$ (gene $i$ in sample $j$) is modeled as:

$$K_{ij} \sim \text{NB}(\mu_{ij}, \phi_{i})$$

with probability mass function:

$$P(K = k) = \binom{k + \phi^{-1} - 1}{k} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \mu\phi}\right)^{\phi^{-1}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu\phi}{1 + \mu\phi}\right)^{k}$$

where $\mu_{ij}$ is the expected count and $\phi_{i}$ is the gene-specific dispersion. This model is the **engine** behind DESeq2 and edgeR — the tools most often used in KTU-level analysis questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Gene Expression Data Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end flow** from raw biological sample to analysis-ready expression matrix. This is the kind of workflow the KTU examiner expects you to be able to draw and label.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Biological Sample RNA]) --> A1[RNA Extraction and QC]
    A1 --> A2{Platform Choice}
    A2 -->|Microarray| B1[Label cDNA with Fluorophore]
    A2 -->|RNA-Seq| B2[Fragment and Build cDNA Library]
    B1 --> C1[Hybridize to Probe Array]
    B2 --> C2[Sequence on High Throughput Platform]
    C1 --> D1[Scan Laser Capture Intensity]
    C2 --> D2[Generate FASTQ Reads]
    D1 --> E1[Raw CEL Intensity Matrix]
    D2 --> E2[Aligned BAM and Count Matrix]
    E1 --> F1[RMA Normalization]
    E2 --> F2{Tool Choice}
    F2 -->|DESeq2| G1[Negative Binomial Model]
    F2 -->|edgeR| G2[TMM Normalization then NB]
    F2 -->|limma-voom| G3[Voom Transform then Linear Model]
    F1 --> F3[log2 Intensity Matrix]
    G1 --> H1[Normalized Count Matrix]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    F3 --> H2[Normalized Continuous Matrix]
    H1 --> I1([Differential Expression and Clustering])
    H2 --> I1
```

## 4.2 Datatype Decision Tree (Microarray vs. RNA-Seq)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1([Gene Expression Data]) --> Q2{Is the data\ninteger counts?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[RNA-Seq Count Data]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Microarray Continuous Data]
    R1 --> S1[Use TPM / FPKM\nand NB Models]
    R2 --> S2[Use RMA / log2\nand Linear Models]
    S1 --> T1([Differential Expression])
    S2 --> T1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture: The Expression Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Expression Matrix M of size G by N] --> M1[Rows G1 to GG\nare Genes]
    M0 --> M2[Columns S1 to SN\nare Samples]
    M1 --> M3[Entry cij =\nExpression of gene i in sample j]
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4{Data Type}
    M4 -->|Microarray| M5[Continuous Log2 Intensity\nvalue typically 2 to 16]
    M4 -->|RNA-Seq| M6[Integer Count\nvalue typically 0 to 100000]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**Differentiate between Microarray and RNA-Seq gene expression data with respect to datatype, output, and normalization methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Microarray | RNA-Seq |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Datatype** | Continuous fluorescence intensity (a.u.) | Discrete integer read counts |
| **Output** | `.CEL` intensity matrix | FASTQ → BAM → count matrix |
| **Normalization** | RMA, quantile, log2 | TPM, FPKM, DESeq2 size factors, TMM |
| **Dynamic Range** | Limited (~10² – 10⁴) | High (10⁰ – 10⁵) |
| **Background Noise** | High (cross-hybridization) | Low |

Microarray data suffers from **background noise and saturation**, while RNA-Seq provides a **broader dynamic range** and **single-base resolution**, but at higher computational cost.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**What is TPM? Why is it preferred over RPKM/FPKM for cross-sample comparison?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TPM (Transcripts Per Million)** is a length-normalized gene expression metric defined as:

$$TPM_{i} = \dfrac{r_{i} / L_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}(r_{j} / L_{j})} \cdot 10^{6}$$

**Why preferred:**
1. **Column-comparable:** The TPM values across all genes in a sample **sum to exactly 1,000,000**, making the sum-invariant and thus directly comparable across samples without further scaling.
2. **Length-normalized within the formula itself**, removing the need for an extra rescaling step.
3. RPKM/FPKM values across samples **do not sum to the same constant**, leading to subtle biases when comparing expression levels.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks** (7 + 7).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a gene expression data matrix. With a neat block diagram, describe the steps involved in converting raw microarray `.CEL` data into a log2 expression matrix using the RMA pipeline.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gene **TP53** has 4,500 reads mapped to it, its length is **4,500 bases**, and the total mapped reads in the sample is **15,000,000**. Calculate:
- (i) RPKM of TP53
- (ii) TPM of TP53, given that $\sum_{j=1}^{N}(r_{j}/L_{j}) = 950$ across the entire sample.

**Verify** whether the TPM values across all genes sum to one million.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A gene expression data matrix is a **two-dimensional numerical matrix** $M$ of dimensions $G \times N$, where:
- $G$ = number of genes (rows, typically $\approx 20,000$ for human)
- $N$ = number of samples (columns, typically 3 – 100)
- Each entry $c_{ij}$ = the measured expression of gene $i$ in sample $j$

**RMA Pipeline (3 main steps, 5 marks for the diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**

1. **Background Correction:** Subtracts non-specific hybridization noise from each probe intensity using a model based on the distribution of *Mis-Match (MM)* and *Perfect-Match (PM)* probes.
2. **Quantile Normalization:** Forces the distribution of probe intensities across all arrays to be **identical**, removing sample-level technical variation.
3. **Log2 Summarization:** The corrected, normalized probe intensities for each gene are **median-polished** and then **log2-transformed**, yielding the final expression matrix.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute RPKM (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
RPKM_{TP53} &= \dfrac{r_{TP53} \cdot 10^{9}}{L_{TP53} \cdot R_{total}} \\
&= \dfrac{4500 \cdot 10^{9}}{4500 \cdot 15{,}000{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{12}}{6.75 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= 66.67
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute TPM (3 marks):**

The per-base rate is:

$$a_{TP53} = \dfrac{4500}{4500} = 1.0 \text{ reads/base}$$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
TPM_{TP53} &= \dfrac{1.0}{950} \cdot 10^{6} \\
&= 1052.63
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (1 mark):**

By the sum-to-million identity proved in §3.2:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} TPM_{i} = 10^{6} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **RPKM, FPKM, and TPM** as RNA-Seq normalization metrics. State the exact formula and one key limitation of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **DESeq2 workflow** for differential expression analysis of RNA-Seq count data. Mention the role of the **Negative Binomial distribution** and **size factors**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Metric | Formula | Key Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RPKM** | $\dfrac{r_i \cdot 10^{9}}{L_i \cdot R_{total}}$ | Single-end only; not comparable across samples without rescaling |
| **FPKM** | $\dfrac{f_i \cdot 10^{9}}{L_i \cdot F_{total}}$ | Paired-end; total FPKM varies between samples, causing bias |
| **TPM** | $\dfrac{r_i / L_i}{\sum_j (r_j / L_j)} \cdot 10^{6}$ | Requires a complete count matrix; not directly comparable to RPKM values from a different sample |

**RPKM** was the first popular metric but treats each sample independently and **does not sum to a constant**, so a TPM-like rescaling is often required afterward. **FPKM** is the paired-end analog of RPKM. **TPM** is internally rescaled and is the **preferred metric for cross-sample comparisons** because $\sum TPM = 10^{6}$ always holds.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Block Diagram in Words):**

The **DESeq2 workflow** consists of the following blocks:

1. **Input:** Raw integer count matrix ($G \times N$) and sample metadata (conditions, batches).
2. **Size Factor Estimation:** Computes $s_j$ for each sample $j$ using the **median-of-ratios** method, normalizing for sequencing depth and library composition:

$$s_{j} = \text{median}_{i \mid g_{i}^{R} > 0} \left( \dfrac{k_{ij}}{g_{i}^{R}} \right)$$

where $g_{i}^{R}$ is the geometric mean of counts for gene $i$ across all samples.

3. **Dispersion Estimation:** For each gene, fits the **Negative Binomial** dispersion parameter $\alpha_i$ by:
   - First, estimating gene-wise dispersion $\alpha_i^{gw}$ via maximum likelihood.
   - Then, fitting a **trended dispersion curve** $\alpha_i^{tr}(\mu_i)$ across all genes.
   - Finally, **shrinking** the gene-wise estimates toward the trend using an empirical Bayes prior (yielding the final $\alpha_i^{final}$).
4. **Model Fitting:** Estimates the mean $\mu_{ij} = s_{j} \cdot q_{ij}$ where $q_{ij}$ depends on the experimental design.
5. **Wald Test / Likelihood Ratio Test:** Tests for differential expression between conditions.
6. **Output:** A results table with log2 fold change, **p-value**, and **adjusted p-value (Benjamini–Hochberg FDR)**.

**Role of the Negative Binomial Distribution (1 mark):**
RNA-Seq counts are **over-dispersed** — their variance exceeds the mean (unlike Poisson). The Negative Binomial model $NB(\mu, \alpha)$ adds a dispersion parameter $\alpha$ to handle this biological variability, making DESeq2 statistically robust for count data.

**Role of Size Factors (1 mark):**
Size factors correct for **library size and composition biases** (e.g., when a few highly expressed genes dominate total counts), ensuring that samples with the same biological expression profile have similar normalized scales.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Gene Expression Data Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in RPKM** — Students forget to convert $L_i$ from bases to kilobases. If you use bases, the constant becomes $10^{9}$, not $10^{6}$. *Both are accepted if used consistently, but mismatching them costs full marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting the Sum-to-Million identity in TPM** — Examiners expect you to **state and verify** $\sum TPM_i = 10^{6}$ wherever TPM is computed.
> 3. **Confusing FPKM with RPKM** — FPKM is for **paired-end**; RPKM is for **single-end**. Mixing them up costs 2 marks easily.
> 4. **Ignoring the datatype** — Applying RMA normalization to RNA-Seq counts is a **fatal conceptual error**. Always state the datatype first.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — In a 7-mark sub-question, a diagram is worth **2 to 3 marks** on its own. Even a rough sketch earns partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gene Expression Data** is the quantitative measurement of mRNA abundance for thousands of genes across samples.
- The two principal datatypes are **Microarray (continuous)** and **RNA-Seq (discrete counts)**.
- The **expression matrix** is $G \times N$ with genes as rows and samples as columns.
- **Microarray** uses the **RMA pipeline** (background correction → quantile normalization → log2 summarization).
- **RNA-Seq** uses **count-based normalization**: TPM, FPKM, RPKM, or model-based methods (DESeq2, edgeR).
- **RPKM** = $\dfrac{r_i \cdot 10^{9}}{L_i \cdot R_{total}}$ (single-end, length in bases).
- **FPKM** = $\dfrac{f_i \cdot 10^{9}}{L_i \cdot F_{total}}$ (paired-end).
- **TPM** = $\dfrac{r_i / L_i}{\sum_j(r_j / L_j)} \cdot 10^{6}$, and the **sum-to-million identity** always holds.
- **TPM is preferred for cross-sample comparison**; RPKM/FPKM require an extra rescaling step.
- **DESeq2** uses the **Negative Binomial model** with **size factors** (median-of-ratios) and **empirical Bayes dispersion shrinkage**.
- **edgeR** uses **TMM normalization** + Negative Binomial + exact/F-tests.
- **limma-voom** applies a voom transformation to counts, then uses linear models (suitable for both).
- Always state the **datatype first**, choose the **right normalization**, and **verify the sum-to-million identity** when using TPM.
- A KTU diagram of the **expression matrix + RMA pipeline** is the most frequently asked 7-mark sub-question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microarrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microarrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Microarrays - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Microarray (Biochip / Gene Chip):** A high-throughput, parallel hybridization-based analytical platform that enables the simultaneous monitoring of the expression levels, genotypic variation, or molecular interaction profiles of thousands to millions of DNA, RNA, cDNA, oligonucleotide, protein, or tissue samples within a single experimental run.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Bioinformatics (PEITT861), microarrays are classified as a **hybridization-based functional genomics technology** that operates on the fundamental principle of **complementary base pairing (Watson-Crick base pairing)**, governed by the second law of thermodynamics and the nearest-neighbor thermodynamic model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to understand the underlying chemistry, types, fabrication methods, experimental workflow, and data interpretation pipeline of microarrays as a foundational tool of modern bioinformatics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy - The "Library of Phone Directories" Intuition

Imagine a massive public library that contains **10,000 different phone directories** — one for each of 10,000 cities around the world. Now, suppose a detective wants to find out, in a single day, which cities currently have someone named "Rohit" registered. Instead of sending a person to each city, the detective makes a single **"name probe"** carrying the string "ROHIT" and drops it on all 10,000 directories at once.

- The directories where the probe **sticks (binds)** light up because "Rohit" is present.
- The directories where the probe **doesn't stick** remain dark.
- The **brightness** of each light indicates **how many "Rohit" entries** are in that city.

**Translating the analogy to microarrays:**
- **10,000 phone directories** = the microarray slide/chip with thousands of immobilized **DNA probes**.
- **"ROHIT" probe** = the **fluorescently labeled sample (cDNA/target)**.
- **Sticky match (hybridization)** = complementary base pairing (A-T, G-C).
- **Light intensity (fluorescence)** = the **gene expression level**.
- **No light** = gene is **not expressed** (or absent in the sample).

> [!TIP]
> This analogy works perfectly for **DNA microarrays**. For **protein microarrays**, the phone directories become antibodies, and the probe becomes a protein sample looking for binding partners.

## 1.3 Key Physical and Chemical Constants

The following constants and standards are crucial for understanding microarray thermodynamics:

| Constant / Standard | Symbol | Value / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **$6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$** |
| Base Pairing Energy (GC) | $\Delta G_{GC}$ | **More negative (stronger)** than AT |
| Base Pairing Energy (AT) | $\Delta G_{AT}$ | **Less negative (weaker)** than GC |
| Standard Hybridization Temperature | $T_m$ | **Typically 65°C** for cDNA arrays |
| Laser Excitation Wavelength (Cy3) | $\lambda_{Cy3}$ | **532 nm (green)** |
| Laser Excitation Wavelength (Cy5) | $\lambda_{Cy5}$ | **635 nm (red)** |
| Typical Probe Length | $L$ | **25 to 70 mer** (oligonucleotide) |
| Typical Feature Size | $A$ | **5 to 200 $\mu$m** diameter spot |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the knowledge of **Cy3 (green) vs Cy5 (red)** fluorophore assignments and their corresponding emission wavelengths. Memorize these for two-color competitive hybridization experiments.

## 1.4 Classification of Microarrays

Microarrays are broadly classified into the following categories, each designed to answer a different biological question:

> [!NOTE]
> **1. DNA Microarrays (cDNA Arrays):** Use long (500-5000 bp) PCR-amplified cDNA fragments spotted onto glass slides via robotic printing.
>
> **2. Oligonucleotide Microarrays:** Use short (15-70 mer) synthetic oligonucleotides synthesized *in situ* using photolithography (Affymetrix) or ink-jet technology.
>
> **3. SNP Microarrays:** Specifically designed to detect Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms using Allele-Specific Oligonucleotide (ASO) hybridization.
>
> **4. Protein Microarrays (Protein Chips):** Used to study protein-protein interactions, antibody-antigen binding, and enzyme activity at high throughput.
>
> **5. Tissue Microarrays (TMA):** Paraffin-embedded tissue sections arrayed on a single slide for histopathological profiling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle - The Thermodynamic Foundation

A microarray experiment is fundamentally a **massive parallel application of the hybridization reaction**. The stability of the duplex formed between the immobilized probe and the fluorescent target is governed by **thermodynamic equilibrium**, not by irreversible binding. The key concepts are:

### 2.1.1 Melting Temperature ($T_m$)

The temperature at which **50% of the duplexes have denatured** (separated) into single strands is called the melting temperature. It is the single most important parameter for designing a successful hybridization experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $T_m$ critical?** If the hybridization temperature is much higher than $T_m$, no duplexes form (no signal). If much lower, even mismatched sequences bind (high background noise). KTU exams often ask for the empirical formula for $T_m$.

**Wallace Rule (for short oligonucleotides, $L < 20$):**

$$T_m = 2^\circ C \times (A + T) + 4^\circ C \times (G + C)$$

**Nearest-Neighbor Model (more accurate, for $L \ge 20$):**

$$T_m = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln \left( \frac{C_T}{x} \right)} - 273.15$$

where:
- $\Delta H$ = Enthalpy of base stacking (kcal/mol), sum of nearest-neighbor contributions
- $\Delta S$ = Entropy change upon duplex formation (cal/mol·K)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **$1.987 \text{ cal mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$**
- $C_T$ = Total strand concentration (molar)
- $x$ = Fraction of strands in duplex (typically 0.5 for melting)

### 2.1.2 The Hybridization Equation

The fraction of target bound to the probe at equilibrium follows **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** in its simplest form:

$$\theta = \frac{[T]}{[T] + K_d}$$

where $\theta$ is the fractional occupancy of probes, $[T]$ is the target concentration, and $K_d$ is the equilibrium dissociation constant.

### 2.1.3 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

Microarray data quality is fundamentally governed by:

$$SNR = \frac{\mu_{signal} - \mu_{background}}{\sigma_{background}}$$

> [!TIP]
> A reliable microarray spot must have an **SNR $\ge 3$** to be considered above background noise. This is a frequently tested KTU value.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Wallace $T_m$ | $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$ | ${}^\circ C$, for $L < 20$ mer |
| Nearest-Neighbor $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln(C_T/x)} - 273.15$ | Kelvin, accurate form |
| GC Content | $\%GC = \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | Percentage, dimensionless |
| Duplex Stability Index | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | kcal/mol, more negative = more stable |
| Langmuir Binding | $\theta = \dfrac{[T]}{[T] + K_d}$ | Fractional, 0 to 1 |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = \dfrac{\mu_{sig} - \mu_{bg}}{\sigma_{bg}}$ | Threshold $\ge 3$ |
| Fold Change (2-color) | $FC = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{R_{treated}}{R_{control}} \right)$ | $\vert FC \vert \ge 1$ = differential |
| Molar Quantity Conversion | $n = \dfrac{m \times N_A}{MW}$ | moles from mass |
| Probe Density | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{probes}}{A_{slide}}$ | probes per $\text{cm}^2$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> The **Fold Change** formula uses $\log_2$ scale. A fold change of 2 means a **4-fold** change on linear scale ($2^2 = 4$). This is a classic KTU pitfall where students confuse the units.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

Microarray technology is a cornerstone of **functional genomics, clinical diagnostics, and pharmacogenomics**. Its real-world engineering utilities include:

1. **Disease Diagnostics:** Gene expression profiling to distinguish cancer subtypes (e.g., BRCA1/BRCA2 in breast cancer).
2. **Drug Discovery:** Pharmacogenomic profiling to identify patient responders vs. non-responders.
3. **Pathogen Detection:** Rapid identification of viral and bacterial strains (e.g., influenza subtyping, HIV genotyping).
4. **Toxicology (Toxico-genomics):** Environmental toxin exposure profiling.
5. **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Stress tolerance gene identification in crops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Context:** Understanding how raw microarray data is transformed into biologically meaningful conclusions via statistical normalization (e.g., LOWESS normalization) and clustering (hierarchical, K-means) is essential for both Module 1 and subsequent modules on sequence analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Nearest-Neighbor Melting Temperature Formula

We will now derive the nearest-neighbor model for $T_m$ in exhaustive steps, as this is a high-yield KTU question.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: The Thermodynamic Starting Point

At equilibrium between single strands ($S$) and duplexes ($D$):

$$S_1 + S_2 \rightleftharpoons D$$

The equilibrium constant is:

$$K_{eq} = \frac{[D]}{[S_1][S_2]}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Relate $K_{eq}$ to Thermodynamic Parameters

From the **van't Hoff equation**:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$

Rearranging:

$$K_{eq} = e^{-\Delta G^\circ / RT}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Express $\Delta G^\circ$ via $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$

$$K_{eq} = e^{-(\Delta H - T\Delta S)/RT} = e^{-\Delta H/RT} \cdot e^{\Delta S/R}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Apply the Melting Condition

At the melting temperature $T_m$, the strands are 50% denatured, so $x = 0.5$. The total strand concentration is $C_T$, so the duplex concentration equals $C_T/2$ (assuming equal strand concentrations).

Substituting into the equilibrium expression:

$$K_{eq} = \frac{C_T/2}{(C_T/2)^2} = \frac{2}{C_T}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Equate and Solve for $T_m$

Setting $K_{eq} = 2/C_T$ in the thermodynamic expression:

$$\frac{2}{C_T} = e^{-\Delta H/RT_m} \cdot e^{\Delta S/R}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{2}{C_T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{RT_m} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

Rearranging for $1/T_m$:

$$\frac{\Delta H}{RT_m} = \frac{\Delta S}{R} - \ln\left(\frac{2}{C_T}\right)$$

$$T_m = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S - R \ln(C_T/2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When written with $x$ in place of the factor 2 (for general fractional melting), we recover the standard nearest-neighbor formula presented in Section 2.1.1.

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Generalization for Fraction $x$

When a fraction $x$ of the strands are duplexed:

$$K_{eq} = \frac{xC_T}{(1-x)^2 C_T^2} = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2 C_T}$$

For $x = 0.5$, this simplifies to $2/C_T$, confirming Step 4.

The general formula is therefore:

$$T_m = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln\left(\frac{C_T}{x}\right)} - 273.15 \quad \text{(converted to Celsius)}$$

> [!TIP]
> Note the **$- 273.15$** at the end to convert from Kelvin to Celsius. Many KTU students forget this and lose 2 marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Calculating $T_m$ for a 20-mer Probe

**Given:**
- Probe sequence: 5'-GATCGATCGATCGATCGATC-3'
- $\Delta H$ (nearest-neighbor sum) = $-152$ kcal/mol
- $\Delta S$ = $-420$ cal/mol·K = $-0.420$ kcal/mol·K
- $C_T$ = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ M
- $x$ = 0.5 (melting condition)

**Step 1: Count bases**

$$A = 5, \quad T = 5, \quad G = 5, \quad C = 5$$
$$\%GC = \frac{5+5}{20} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Step 2: Apply Wallace Rule for quick estimate**

$$T_m^{Wallace} = 2(5+5) + 4(5+5) = 20 + 40 = 60^\circ C$$

**Step 3: Apply Nearest-Neighbor Model**

$$T_m = \frac{-152}{-0.420 + 1.987 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln\left(\frac{10^{-6}}{0.5}\right)} - 273.15$$

Computing the denominator term:

$$\ln\left(\frac{10^{-6}}{0.5}\right) = \ln(2 \times 10^{-6}) = \ln 2 + \ln 10^{-6} = 0.693 - 13.816 = -13.123$$

$$1.987 \times 10^{-3} \times (-13.123) = -0.02608$$

$$-0.420 + (-0.02608) = -0.44608$$

$$T_m^{Kelvin} = \frac{-152}{-0.44608} = 340.75 \text{ K}$$

$$T_m^{Celsius} = 340.75 - 273.15 = 67.6^\circ C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The nearest-neighbor model gives $T_m = 67.6^\circ C$, which is **7.6°C higher** than the Wallace rule estimate. This shows the Wallace rule underestimates $T_m$ for higher-GC-content probes and underscores why the nearest-neighbor model is the gold standard for precise hybridization temperature prediction.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Microarray Platforms

| Parameter | cDNA Microarray | Affymetrix Oligonucleotide | Agilent Oligonucleotide | Protein Microarray |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Probe Length** | 500-5000 bp | 25 mer | 60 mer | Antibodies (variable) |
| **Immobilization** | Robotic spotting | Photolithography | Ink-jet synthesis | Robotic spotting |
| **Sample Type** | cDNA | cRNA / fragmented cDNA | cRNA | Protein lysate |
| **Fluorophore** | Cy3, Cy5 (2-color) | Streptavidin-PE (1-color) | Cy3, Cy5 (2-color) | Cy3/Cy5 or HRP |
| **Cost per Array** | Low | High | Medium | High |
| **Specificity** | Moderate | Very High (PM-MM pairs) | High | Variable |
| **KTU Exam Focus** | Workflow steps | PM-MM mismatch concept | Ink-jet vs. photolithography | Antibody validation |

> [!NOTE]
> The **PM-MM probe pair** concept is a hallmark of Affymetrix GeneChips. For each perfect match (PM) probe, a mismatch (MM) probe is synthesized with a single base change at the 13th position. The difference PM-MM corrects for non-specific binding, dramatically improving specificity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microarray Experimental Workflow

The following block diagram illustrates the end-to-end pipeline of a typical two-color DNA microarray experiment, covering every step from biological sample acquisition to biologically meaningful interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sample A: Treated Cells"] --> B["mRNA Extraction"]
    C["Sample C: Control Cells"] --> D["mRNA Extraction"]
    B --> E["Reverse Transcription to cDNA"]
    D --> F["Reverse Transcription to cDNA"]
    E --> G["Label with Cy5 Red Dye 635nm"]
    F --> H["Label with Cy3 Green Dye 532nm"]
    G --> I["Pool and Mix Hybridization Mix"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Apply to Microarray Slide with 10000 Probes"]
    J --> K["Incubate at 65C for 16 to 20 Hours"]
    K --> L["Stringency Washes to Remove Nonspecific Binding"]
    L --> M["Dual Laser Scanning at 532nm and 635nm"]
    M --> N["Image Acquisition with CCD Detector"]
    N --> O["Grid Alignment and Spot Segmentation"]
    O --> P["Background Subtraction and Normalization"]
    P --> Q["LOWESS Normalization to Remove Dye Bias"]
    Q --> R["Calculate Log2 Ratio Treated versus Control"]
    R --> S["Statistical Filtering: Fold Change 2 and p value 0.05"]
    S --> T["Differentially Expressed Gene List"]
    T --> U["Hierarchical Clustering and Heatmap Visualization"]
    U --> V["Gene Ontology and Pathway Enrichment Analysis"]
    V --> W["Biological Interpretation and Publication"]
```

## 4.2 Affymetrix GeneChip Architecture (PM-MM Probe Pair)

The following architecture diagram explains how Affymetrix's high-specificity probe design works at the molecular level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TargetSequence["Target mRNA Fragment"]
        TS["5 prime ... A U G C U A G C U A G C ... 3 prime"]
    end

    TargetSequence --> HybridPM
    TargetSequence --> HybridMM

    subgraph ProbePair["Probe Pair on GeneChip"]
        direction TB
        ProbePM["Perfect Match PM Probe: 25 mer sequence 100 percent complementary to target"]
        ProbeMM["Mismatch MM Probe: 25 mer with single substitution at position 13 to detect nonspecific binding"]
    end

    HybridPM["Hybridizes strongly to PM probe with high fluorescence"] --- ProbePM
    HybridMM["Hybridizes weakly to MM probe with low fluorescence"] --- ProbeMM

    HybridPM --> SignalCalc["Signal equals PM minus MM intensity, removes background noise"]
    HybridMM --> SignalCalc
    SignalCalc --> FinalOutput["Clean Expression Score for One Gene on One Probe Set of 11 to 20 Pairs"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Two-Color Hybridization Event

The following diagram illustrates the molecular events during the hybridization reaction at a single microarray feature (spot).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Immobilized["Immobilized on Glass Slide"]
        Probe["Single Stranded DNA Probe 60 mer, 5 prime end attached to aminosilane coating"]
    end

    subgraph Solution["Free in Hybridization Solution"]
        Target1["Perfect Match Target: 100 percent complementary, Cy5 labeled Red"]
        Target2["Mismatch Target: 1 base mismatch, Cy5 labeled Red"]
        Target3["Noncomplementary RNA: no binding, washed away"]
    end

    Probe -.->|"Strong binding: Delta G highly negative"| Bind1["Stable Duplex Forms: High Fluorescence Signal"]
    Probe -.->|"Weak binding: Delta G near zero"| Bind2["Unstable Duplex: Dissociates During Wash"]
    Probe -.->|"No binding"| WashAway["Washed Away: No Signal"]

    Target1 --> Bind1
    Target2 --> Bind2
    Target3 --> WashAway
```

## 4.4 Microarray Data Processing Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Image: TIFF 16 bit from Scanner"] --> B["Spot Quantification: Grid Alignment using GAL File"]
    B --> C["Foreground Intensity F minus Background Intensity B per Spot"]
    C --> D["Log2 Transformation to Stabilize Variance"]
    D --> E["Within-Slide Normalization: LOWESS or Quantile"]
    E --> F["Between-Slide Normalization: Scale or Quantile"]
    F --> G["Statistical Testing: t-test or SAM or Limma"]
    G --> H{"Passes Filter: p less than 0.05 AND Log2FC greater than 1"}
    H -->|"Yes"| I["Differentially Expressed Gene DEG"]
    H -->|"No"| J["Excluded as Noise"]
    I --> K["Clustering: Hierarchical or K-means or SOM"]
    K --> L["Visualization: Heatmap with Red for Upregulated and Green for Downregulated"]
    L --> M["Functional Enrichment: GO and KEGG and Reactome"]
    M --> N["Final Biological Conclusion"]
```
