## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — DDBJ Questions:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse **DDBJ** with **PDB** (Protein Data Bank) or **UniProt**. Always clarify: DDBJ stores **nucleotide sequences only** (not protein structures or protein sequences).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students often write "**DNA Database of Japan**" — the correct expansion is **"DNA Data Bank of Japan"** (note the word "Bank", not "Base").
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forget to mention the **daily data exchange** cycle (24 hours). This is a frequently tested point.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confuse **DDBJ accession number format** with GenBank's. Both follow the INSDC standard, but DDBJ-specific prefixes (e.g., `AB`, `LC`, `BD`) are sometimes not memorized.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture and operational workflow of DDBJ. Describe the submission-to-release process, including the tools used and the data exchange protocol with INSDC partners.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the flat-file format of a DDBJ entry. With a suitable example, explain the major sections: LOCUS, DEFINITION, ACCESSION, FEATURES, and ORIGIN.

### Model Solution (Question A):

#### Part (a) — Architecture & Workflow [7 marks]

**1. Governing Body & Location [1 mark]**

DDBJ is maintained by the **National Institute of Genetics (NIG)** at **Mishima, Japan**, under the **Research Organization of Information and Systems (ROIS)** and funded by **MEXT (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology, Japan)**.

**2. Submission Portal [1 mark]**

Researchers submit nucleotide sequence data through the **Nucleotide Sequence Submission System (NSSS)**, also accessible via the legacy **SAGEN** tool. Submissions include:
- The raw nucleotide sequence.
- Source organism information.
- Feature annotations (gene, CDS, exon, etc.).
- Publication reference.

**3. Validation & Curation Pipeline [2 marks]**

Submitted data undergoes:
- **VecScreen** — detects cloning vector contamination.
- **Taxonomy validation** — verifies organism name against the NCBI Taxonomy.
- **Length checks** — sequences >350 kb require special handling.
- **Character validation** — only A, T, G, C, N (or U for RNA) allowed.
- **Annotation review** by trained curators at NIG.

**4. Accession Number Assignment [1 mark]**

A **permanent, stable accession number** is issued (e.g., `AB123456`, `LC001234`). Format: 1-2 alphabetic prefix + 5-6 numeric digits. **Accession numbers never change** — they are the canonical citation identifiers.

**5. Hold Period & Public Release [1 mark]**

Authors may request a **hold period** until publication, after which the entry is released to the public.

**6. INSDC Daily Exchange [1 mark]**

Within **24 hours** of public release, the new and updated entries are **exported to GenBank (NCBI) and ENA (EBI)** via the standardized **INSDC exchange protocol**, ensuring all three databases contain **identical data**. This harmonization enables universal access regardless of the user's preferred portal.

---

#### Part (b) — Flat-File Format [7 marks]

A DDBJ entry is stored as a structured **ASCII flat file** with the following sections (each is a 1-mark field, plus a 2-mark example):

**1. LOCUS [1 mark]**

A single-line header containing the locus name, sequence length, molecule type (DNA, RNA, mRNA), topology (linear/circular), and division code.

**2. DEFINITION [1 mark]**

A short, human-readable description of the entry (e.g., "Homo sapiens BRCA1 gene, partial cds").

**3. ACCESSION [1 mark]**

The permanent, stable identifier assigned at submission time.

**4. FEATURES Table [2 marks]**

A structured table of biological annotations. Each feature is defined by a key (e.g., `source`, `gene`, `CDS`) and a set of qualifiers (e.g., `/organism="..."`, `/gene="..."`, `/codon_start=1`, `/protein_id="..."`, `/translation="..."`).

**5. ORIGIN [1 mark]**

Contains the raw nucleotide sequence, formatted as 60 bases per line with line numbers. The entry terminates with `//`.

**Illustrative Mini-Example:**

```
LOCUS       AB123456              1245 bp    mRNA    linear   PRI 26-MAY-2020
DEFINITION  Homo sapiens BRCA1 mRNA, complete cds.
ACCESSION   AB123456
VERSION     AB123456.1
KEYWORDS    breast cancer; tumor suppressor.
SOURCE      Homo sapiens (human)
  ORGANISM  Homo sapiens
            Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata; Craniata; Vertebrata; Euteleostomi;
            Mammalia; Eutheria; Euarchontoglires; Primates; Haplorrhini;
            Catarrhini; Hominidae; Homo.
FEATURES             Location/Qualifiers
     source          1..1245
                     /organism="Homo sapiens"
                     /mol_type="mRNA"
     gene            1..1245
                     /gene="BRCA1"
     CDS             1..1245
                     /gene="BRCA1"
                     /protein_id="BAA12345.1"
                     /translation="MDSNE..."
BASE COUNT      324 a  278 c  298 g  345 t
ORIGIN
        1 atgctgcatg ctagctagct agctagcatc gtatcgatcg atcgatcgat cgtagctagc
       61 tagctacgta gctagcatcg atcgatcgat cgtagctacg tagctagcta gctagctacg
//
```

**Mark Distribution (Part b):**
- 1 mark — LOCUS explanation
- 1 mark — DEFINITION + ACCESSION
- 2 marks — FEATURES table with example
- 1 mark — ORIGIN + `//` terminator
- 1 mark — Bonus: correct identification of `BASE COUNT` field
- 1 mark — Neatness and accurate reproduction of the flat-file structure

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the specialized sub-databases hosted by DDBJ. Differentiate between **DRA**, **DOR**, **BioProject**, and **BioSample**. What is the role of DRA in next-generation sequencing (NGS) workflows?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the INSDC tripartite collaboration. Describe how data flows from submission in any one of the three partner databases to worldwide public access. Discuss the importance of this collaboration for the global bioinformatics community.

### Model Solution (Question B):

#### Part (a) — DDBJ Sub-Databases [7 marks]

**1. Traditional DDBJ Main Database [1 mark]**

Stores annotated nucleotide sequences, including **EST (Expressed Sequence Tags)**, **GSS (Genome Survey Sequences)**, **HTC (High-Throughput cDNA)**, **WGS (Whole Genome Shotgun)**, and **TSA (Transcriptome Shotgun Assembly)** entries.

**2. DRA (DDBJ Sequence Read Archive) [2 marks]**

- **Full form:** DDBJ Sequence Read Archive.
- **Purpose:** Archives **raw next-generation sequencing (NGS) reads** from platforms such as Illumina, Ion Torrent, PacBio, and Oxford Nanopore.
- **Equivalent partners:** **SRA** at NCBI (USA) and **ERA** at EBI (Europe). Daily exchange ensures all three contain identical data.
- **Role in NGS workflows:** A typical NGS project produces millions of short reads. These are too large and too redundant to store as traditional DDBJ entries. DRA stores them in a compact **SFF/binary format** with accompanying quality scores (Phred) and metadata.

**3. DOR (DDBJ Omics Archive) [2 marks]**

- **Full form:** DDBJ Omics Archive.
- **Purpose:** Hosts **high-throughput functional omics data** including **microarray** expression profiles, **proteomics** mass-spec results, and **metabolomics** measurements.
- **Significance:** Complements DRA by archiving not just sequence reads but also the **downstream functional measurements** of genes, proteins, and metabolites.

**4. BioProject and BioSample [2 marks]**

- **BioProject:** A **project-level umbrella** that groups multiple submissions under one research initiative (e.g., "Human Microbiome Project"). Common to all INSDC partners.
- **BioSample:** A **sample-level** record describing the biological source material (e.g., tissue type, isolation method, geographic origin). Common to all INSDC partners.
- **Significance:** Together, they enable **standardized metadata reporting** across DDBJ, GenBank, and ENA, supporting reproducibility and meta-analyses.

**Differentiating Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | DRA | DOR | BioProject | BioSample |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Type | NGS raw reads | Functional omics | Project metadata | Sample metadata |
| Level | Experiment | Experiment | Project | Sample |
| Scope | Sequence reads | Function | Initiative | Specimen |
| INSDC Counterpart | SRA / ERA | GEO / PRIDE | BioProject (NCBI/EBI) | BioSample (NCBI/EBI) |

---

#### Part (b) — INSDC Tripartite Collaboration [7 marks]

**1. Founding and Purpose [1 mark]**

INSDC was formalized to provide a **single, global, public-domain nucleotide sequence data resource** by harmonizing the operations of three continental databases: **DDBJ (Japan)**, **GenBank (USA)**, and **ENA (Europe)**. It is governed by a **Memorandum of Understanding** signed between NIG, NCBI, and EBI.

**2. Data Flow Pipeline [3 marks]**

The flow can be summarized in five stages:

- **Stage 1 — Regional Submission:** A researcher in India submits a new rice genome contig to DDBJ. A researcher in Germany submits a fly gene to EBI. A researcher in the USA submits a viral sequence to GenBank.
- **Stage 2 — Local Curation:** Each partner's curators validate, annotate, and assign accession numbers using their own tools (NSSS for DDBJ, tbl2asn for NCBI, Webin for EBI).
- **Stage 3 — Daily Exchange:** At a fixed time every 24 hours, each partner exports its new and updated entries to the other two partners using the **INSDC exchange protocol** (a standardized XML/flat-file format).
- **Stage 4 — Import and Re-indexing:** Each partner imports the foreign records, re-indexes them with its own portal, and makes them searchable through its own interface.
- **Stage 5 — Global Public Access:** A scientist in Brazil can search the same data via DDBJ Search, NCBI Entrez, or EBI ENA Search — whichever portal they prefer — and retrieve identical records.

**3. Importance for the Global Bioinformatics Community [3 marks]**

- **Universal Access:** Researchers need not know which partner hosts the data; the system is location-agnostic.
- **Redundancy & Resilience:** Three geographically distributed mirrors provide **fault tolerance** against regional outages, natural disasters, or political disruptions.
- **Data Standardization:** INSDC mandates a common **Feature Table Definition**, controlled vocabularies, and taxonomy — making cross-database queries seamless.
- **Cost Sharing:** No single country bears the entire financial burden of global data curation.
- **Scientific Reproducibility:** Citation of a stable accession number (e.g., `LC123456.1`) allows any researcher to retrieve the exact data record used in a published study, regardless of the portal used.
- **Acceleration of Discovery:** Drug-target identification, evolutionary studies, and disease-gene discovery all depend on this harmonized, comprehensive resource.

**Mark Distribution (Part b):**
- 1 mark — INSDC founding
- 3 marks — Five-stage data-flow pipeline
- 3 marks — Importance points (one each for access, redundancy, standardization, reproducibility, etc.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DDBJ (Module 1, PEITT861):**

- ✅ **Full form:** DNA **Data Bank** of Japan (NOT "Database")
- ✅ **Established:** 1984
- ✅ **Host institute:** **National Institute of Genetics (NIG)**, Mishima, Japan
- ✅ **Function:** Public nucleotide sequence database (DNA/RNA, not protein)
- ✅ **INSDC partners:** DDBJ + GenBank (NCBI) + ENA (EBI)
- ✅ **Data exchange cycle:** **24 hours** (daily synchronization)
- ✅ **Accession format:** 1-2 letter prefix + 5-6 digits (e.g., `AB123456`, `LC001234`); **permanent and stable**
- ✅ **Submission tool:** **NSSS (Nucleotide Sequence Submission System)** / legacy SAGEN
- ✅ **Validation tools:** VecScreen, taxonomy check, length check
- ✅ **Search tools:** DDBJ Search, ARSA, getentry, BLAST
- ✅ **Sub-databases:** DRA (NGS reads), DOR (omics), BioProject, BioSample
- ✅ **Traditional sub-categories:** EST, GSS, HTC, WGS, TSA
- ✅ **Flat-file sections:** LOCUS → DEFINITION → ACCESSION → VERSION → KEYWORDS → SOURCE → REFERENCE → FEATURES → BASE COUNT → ORIGIN → `//`
- ✅ **DRA equivalent abroad:** SRA (NCBI, USA), ERA (EBI, Europe)
- ✅ **DOR equivalent abroad:** GEO (NCBI, USA), PRIDE / ArrayExpress (EBI, Europe)
- ✅ **Access policy:** Open, free, unrestricted (public domain)
- ✅ **Key growth metric (numerical):** ~22% annual growth; doubling time ~3 years
- ✅ **Key formulas:** $S(t) = S_{0} e^{rt}$, $C_{GC} = (n_G + n_C)/L \times 100\%$, $B_{storage} = L \times 2 \times N$, $T_{identity} = 1 - H_{edit}/L$
- ✅ **Difference from GenBank:** Operated from Japan; preferred submission hub for Asia-Pacific researchers; same data content (mirrored)
- ✅ **Difference from PDB:** PDB is for **3D protein/nucleic-acid structures**; DDBJ is for **1D nucleotide sequences**

**One-Line KTU Mantra:**

> *"DDBJ = Japan's nucleotide sequence database, mirrored daily with GenBank and ENA under INSDC, providing free global access to DNA/RNA data."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMBL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** EMBL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **EMBL** in the bioinformatics context refers to two tightly coupled entities: 1. **European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL)** — An intergovernmental research organization founded in **1974** in **Heidelberg, Germany**, dedicated to molecular biology research and the development of cutting-edge bioinformatics infrastructure. 2. **EMBL Nucleotide Sequence Database (EMBL-Bank / ENA)** — A comprehensive, publicly accessible, peer-reviewed **nucleotide sequence database** maintained by the **European Bioinformatics Institute (EBI)**, an outstation of EMBL located in **Hinxton, United Kingdom**. It is now operationally known as the **European Nucleotide Archive (ENA)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In your examinations, "EMBL" almost always refers to the **EMBL Nucleotide Sequence Database** (also called **EMBL-Bank** or **ENA**), NOT the research laboratory. The database is one of the three pillars of the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🧠 Plain English Analogy — "The World's Genetic Library"** > > Imagine humanity has collected every sentence ever spoken in every language — but the "sentences" here are strands of DNA/RNA, and the "languages" are the four nucleotide alphabets: **A (Adenine), T (Thymine), G (Guanine), C (Cytosine)**. EMBL is the **librarian's master catalogue** for this vast genetic library. > > Every entry in EMBL is like a **library card** containing: > - A unique **barcode** (Accession Number) so the book is never lost > - The **title** (Sequence Description) > - The **author** (Submitter/Scientist) > - The **publication date** (Submission/Publication Date) > - The **contents** (Feature Table — where genes, exons, promoters are annotated) > - The **actual text of the book** (the raw nucleotide sequence) > > The **EMBL flat file format** is the standardized "card layout" used universally so that any scientist, anywhere, can read the catalogue in the same way. --- ### 1.3 EMBL at a Glance — Key Metrics | Parameter | Value / Description | |---|---| | **Founded (Lab)** | **1974**, Heidelberg, Germany | | **Database Name** | **EMBL-Bank** (now **ENA — European Nucleotide Archive**) | | **Maintained By** | **European Bioinformatics Institute (EBI)**, Hinxton, UK | | **Number of Member States** | **> 27** (as of 2024) | | **Partner Databases** | **GenBank (NCBI, USA)** & **DDBJ (Japan)** | | **Standard Format** | **EMBL Flat File Format (.embl)** | | **Accession Format** | **1–3 letters + 5–6 digits** (e.g., `X56734`, `AB123456`) | | **Data Update Frequency** | **Daily synchronization** with INSDC partners | | **Primary Web Access** | `https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The **EMBL Flat File Format** is the most frequently asked KTU topic from this module. Memorize the structure of every line type (ID, AC, DT, DE, KW, OS, OC, FT, SQ). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Major Nucleotide Databases (INSDC Triad) > [!NOTE] > **INSDC (International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration)** is a tripartite partnership ensuring global, free access to nucleotide sequence data. All three databases exchange data **every 24 hours**. ``` ┌──────────────────────┐ │ INSDC │ │ (Global Federation) │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ │ GenBank│ │ EMBL │ │ DDBJ │ │ (NCBI) │◄──►│ (EBI) │◄────►│ (Japan)│ │ USA │ │ UK │ │ │ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ ``` > **Logical Rule of INSDC:** *A sequence submitted to ANY one of the three databases is automatically shared with the other two within 24 hours.* This eliminates data redundancy and ensures global consistency. --- ### 2.2 EMBL Flat File Format — Complete Structural Breakdown An **EMBL flat file** is a plain-text, line-oriented file where **each line begins with a two-letter code** identifying its field. The file is divided into **three logical sections**. #### 2.2.1 Section A — Header (Identification & Bibliographic Information) | Line Code | Field Name | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **ID** | Identification | Entry name, molecule type, length, data class | `ID X56734; SV 1; linear; mRNA; STD; HUM; 1450 BP.` | | **AC** | Accession Number | **Unique, stable identifier** (never changes) | `AC X56734;` | | **DT** | Date | Creation / last update / release dates | `DT 15-JAN-1991 (Rel. 28, Created)` | | **DE** | Description | Human-readable description of the sequence | `DE Human insulin mRNA, complete cds.` | | **KW** | Keywords | Searchable biological keywords | `KW insulin; diabetes mellitus.` | | **OS** | Organism Species | Source organism (scientific name) | `OS Homo sapiens (human)` | | **OC** | Organism Classification | Taxonomic lineage | `OC Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata; ...` | | **OG** | Organelle | If mitochondrial/chloroplast etc. | `OG Mitochondrion.` | | **RN / RC / RP / RX / RG / RA / RT / RL** | References | Publication, authors, title, journal | `RL Nature 321:522-525(1986).` | | **CC** | Comments | Free-text comments from submitters | `CC This sequence was submitted ...` | | **DR** | Database Cross-Reference | Links to other databases (e.g., UniProt) | `DR UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot; P01308; INS_HUMAN.` | | **FH** | Feature Header | Marks beginning of feature table | `FH Key Location/Qualifiers` | #### 2.2.2 Section B — Feature Table (Biological Annotation) | Line Code | Field Name | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **FT** | Feature | Describes biological regions of the sequence | `FT CDS 34..456`
`FT /gene="INS"`
`FT /product="insulin"` | **Common Feature Keys:** - `source` — origin of the sequence - `CDS` — coding sequence (translates to protein) - `gene` — gene name - `exon` / `intron` — splicing features - `promoter` / `enhancer` — regulatory elements - `mRNA` / `rRNA` / `tRNA` — RNA types - `repeat_region`, `misc_feature`, `variation` #### 2.2.3 Section C — Sequence | Line Code | Field Name | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **SQ** | Sequence Header | Begins the sequence block, shows total length and base count | `SQ Sequence 1450 BP; 386 A; 412 C; 348 G; 304 T; 0 other;` | | *(60 chars/line)* | Sequence | The raw nucleotide string in blocks of **10 bases**, numbered in groups of 60 | ` 1 ATGCGTTTGT TGCCTGCTCT TCTTTGGGGC CTGTGCCGTC ... 60` | | **`//`** | Terminator | Mandatory end-of-entry marker | `//` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):** > - **Accession Number (AC)** is the *permanent* identifier — even if the entry is updated, the AC never changes. > - The **ID line** name may change between releases, but the **AC is forever stable**. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Notation | Definition / Rule | Unit / Example | |---|---|---|---| | Accession Number Format | `AC` | `[1–3 letters][5–6 digits]\.[version]` | `X56734.1` | | Flat File Line Prefix | `Lₙ` | Two-character uppercase field code | `ID`, `AC`, `FT` | | Sequence Block Width | `W` | 60 nucleotides per line, in 10-base groups | 60 nt | | Total Sequence Length | `L` | Sum of all A + C + G + T + U + other | bp or nt | | Feature Range Notation | `[a..b]` | Inclusive base range; `[a..b]` or `complement(a..b)` | `34..456` | | INSDC Update Cycle | `T_sync` | 24 hours daily | 1 day | | Reading Frame | `f` | Frame = 1, 2, or 3 (or `-1`, `-2`, `-3` for reverse) | dimensionless | | Codon-to-Amino-Acid | `n_codons` | $n_{codons} = \lfloor (L_{CDS} - \text{stop}) / 3 \rfloor$ | unitless integer | | Sequence Composition | `n_A, n_C, n_G, n_T` | Sum equals total length L | nt | | GC Content | `GC%` | $GC\% = \frac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100$ | percent (%) | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Bioinformatics & Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Why EMBL matters in practice:** > > 1. **Genomic Medicine:** Clinical labs query EMBL to identify pathogenic mutations in patient samples. > 2. **Drug Discovery:** Researchers screen EMBL for homologous protein-coding sequences to identify drug targets. > 3. **Forensic Science:** DNA fingerprints from crime scenes are matched against EMBL entries. > 4. **Evolutionary Biology:** Phylogenetic analyses rely on EMBL-stored homologs from different species. > 5. **Synthetic Biology & Genetic Engineering:** Gene synthesis companies validate orders against EMBL annotations. > 6. **Agriculture:** Identifying stress-resistance genes in crops via EMBL cross-references. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Parsing a Sample EMBL Entry Below is a **fully annotated** real-world-style EMBL flat file. Each line is dissected. ``` ID HUMINS01A; SV 1; linear; mRNA; STD; HUM; 1450 BP. XX AC X56734; XX DT 15-JAN-1991 (Rel. 28, Created) DT 10-MAR-2005 (Rel. 83, Last updated, Version 2) XX DE Human insulin preproprotein mRNA, complete cds. XX KW insulin; diabetes; preproprotein. XX OS Homo sapiens (human) OC Eukaryota; Metazoa; Chordata; Craniata; Vertebrata; Mammalia; OC Eutheria; Primates; Catarrhini; Hominidae; Homo. XX RN [1] RP 1-1450 RX PUBMED; 3513311 RA Bell G.I., Swain W.F., Pictet R., Cordell B., Goodman H.M.; RT "Nucleotide sequence of a human gene for insulin"; RL Nature 321:522-525(1986). XX DR UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot; P01308; INS_HUMAN. XX FH Key Location/Qualifiers FH FT source 1..1450 FT /organism="Homo sapiens" FT /mol_type="mRNA" FT gene 1..1450 FT /gene="INS" FT CDS 34..456 FT /gene="INS" FT /product="insulin preproprotein" FT /protein_id="CAA28129.1" FT /translation="MALWMRLLPLLALLALWGPDPAAAFVNQHLCGSHLVEALYLVCG..." FT misc_feature 100..200 FT /note="5' UTR regulatory region" XX SQ Sequence 1450 BP; 386 A; 412 C; 348 G; 304 T; 0 other; 1 ATGCGTTTGT TGCCTGCTCT TCTTTGGGGC CTGTGCCGTC GGGACCCAAC 50 51 GCAGCCTTTG TGAACCAACA CCTGTGCGGG TCACACCTGG TGGAGGCTCT 100 101 CTACTTAGTG GGGACCAGCA GGCAGCTGCA GGTGGGCAGC GTGGAGCTGG 150 ... [remaining lines up to base 1450] 1451 // ``` --- ### 3.2 Analytical Walk-through (Step-by-Step) > **Step 1 — Identify the entry type (`ID` line):** > `HUMINS01A` = entry name; `SV 1` = sequence version 1; `linear` = topology; `mRNA` = molecule type; `STD` = standard (non-CON/gss/env sample); `HUM` = data class abbreviation; `1450 BP` = total length. > → **Conclusion:** This is a **complete mRNA** entry of **1450 base pairs**. > **Step 2 — Verify the unique identifier (`AC` line):** > `X56734` is the stable accession. This is the **citation key** used in publications. > → **Conclusion:** Always cite by `AC`, never by `ID`. > **Step 3 — Track the chronology (`DT` lines):** > Created Jan 1991, updated Mar 2005. The `SV` (sequence version) incremented from 1 to 2. > → **Conclusion:** Version updates are tracked; the accession persists. > **Step 4 — Decode the biology (`DE`, `OS`, `OC`):** > - **Description:** Insulin preproprotein mRNA. > - **Source:** *Homo sapiens* (human). > - **Taxonomy:** Full 12-rank lineage down to *Homo*. > → **Conclusion:** This is a **human insulin gene transcript**. > **Step 5 — Map the annotations (`FT` block):** > - `source 1..1450` — the entire sequence is from human mRNA. > - `gene 1..1450` — the *INS* gene spans the whole entry. > - `CDS 34..456` — the **protein-coding region** runs from base 34 to base 456. > - `/protein_id="CAA28129.1"` — links to a separate protein database entry. > - `/translation="MALWMRLLPL..."` — the translated protein string is provided directly. > → **Conclusion:** A 423-nt CDS encodes a 141-aa insulin preproprotein. > **Step 6 — Verify sequence composition (`SQ` line):** > $n_A = 386$, $n_C = 412$, $n_G = 348$, $n_T = 304$, $n_{other} = 0$. > **Step 7 — Apply the GC% formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} GC\% &= \frac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{348 + 412}{1450} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{760}{1450} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 0.5241 \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 52.41\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** The insulin mRNA is **GC-balanced** (close to the 50% expected for mammalian coding regions). > **Step 8 — Verify CDS length and translation consistency:** > $L_{CDS} = 456 - 34 + 1 = 423$ nucleotides (inclusive range). $$ \begin{aligned} n_{codons} &= \frac{L_{CDS} - \text{stop codon}}{3} \\[6pt] &= \frac{423 - 3}{3} \\[6pt] &= \frac{420}{3} = 140 \text{ amino acids (excluding stop)} \\[6pt] \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** The CDS encodes a **141-aa preproprotein** (140 aa + 1 stop codon position) — consistent with the annotation. --- ### 3.3 EMBL Submission Workflow — Step-by-Step > [!NOTE] > **How does a sequence get INTO EMBL? (Common viva question)** | Step | Action | Tool / Form | |---|---|---| | **1** | Prepare the sequence (validated, vector-screened) | Stand-alone tools (e.g., `VecScreen`) | | **2** | Annotate the features (gene, CDS, exon) | Author's annotation pipeline | | **3** | Choose submission tool | **Webin** (ENA's submission portal) | | **4** | Submit metadata + sequence | Web-based or programmatic (curl/API) | | **5** | Receive temporary **accession number** (private) | Auto-assigned | | **6** | Optional: hold until publication (`hold until published` flag) | Set release date | | **7** | Public release | Visible in EMBL/ENA, mirrored in GenBank & DDBJ | | **8** | Daily synchronization to INSDC partners | Automated pipeline | --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — EMBL vs. GenBank vs. DDBJ | Feature | **EMBL (ENA)** | **GenBank (NCBI)** | **DDBJ** | |---|---|---|---| | **Country** | United Kingdom (EBI) | United States (NCBI) | Japan | | **Year Founded** | 1980 (database) | 1982 | 1986 | | **Maintained By** | EBI (EMBL outstation) | NCBI (NLM, NIH) | National Institute of Genetics | | **Web Access** | ebi.ac.uk/ena | ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genbank | ddbj.nig.ac.jp | | **Format** | EMBL flat file | GenBank flat file | DDBJ flat file | | **Accession** | 1–3 letters + 5–6 digits | 1–2 letters + 6 digits, or 2 letters + 6 digits | Same as GenBank | | **INSDC Member** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Identifier Type** | Primary + secondary | Primary + secondary | Primary + secondary | | **Cross-Sync Cycle** | 24 h | 24 h | 24 h | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — EMBL Flat File Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EMBL Flat File Entry] --> B[Header Section] A --> C[Feature Table Section] A --> D[Sequence Section] B --> B1[ID Line: Entry name, type, length] B --> B2[AC Line: Stable Accession Number] B --> B3[DT Lines: Date stamps] B --> B4[DE Line: Description] B --> B5[KW Line: Keywords] B --> B6[OS OC Lines: Taxonomy] B --> B7[Reference Block: RN RA RL] B --> B8[DR Line: Cross-references] B --> B9[CC Line: Comments] B --> B10[FH Line: Feature Header] C --> C1[FT source: 1..1450] C --> C2[FT gene: INS] C --> C3[FT CDS: 34..456] C --> C4[FT misc_feature: regulatory] D --> D1[SQ Line: Composition Summary] D --> D2[Sequence Blocks: 10 nt per group, 60 nt per line] D --> D3[End Marker: //] A --> E[Submission to INSDC Network] E --> F[Synchronization to GenBank] E --> G[Synchronization to DDBJ] ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — EMBL Submission & Retrieval Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR Researcher[Scientist Researcher] -->|Raw Sequence| Validate[Vector & Quality Validation] Validate -->|Cleaned FASTA| Annotate[Feature Annotation] Annotate -->|Annotated Entry| Webin[Webin Submission Portal ENA EBI] Webin -->|Temporary ID| Hold{Publish Now?} Hold -->|Yes| Release[Public Release ENA Browser] Hold -->|No| Pending[Held Until Publication] Pending --> Release Release --> Inhouse[ENA Database at EBI UK] Inhouse -->|24h Sync| GenBank[GenBank USA NCBI] Inhouse -->|24h Sync| DDBJ[DDBJ Japan] Inhouse -->|Indexed for Search| QuerySrv[Text Search SRS ENA Browser] GenBank -->|Entrez Crosslinks| QuerySrv DDBJ -->|getentry| QuerySrv QuerySrv --> UserData[User Query Result] UserData --> Researcher ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — EMBL's Position in the Bioinformatics Ecosystem ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InhouseLab["In-house Research Labs"] WetLab[Wet Lab Sequencing] end subgraph PublicDBs["Public Nucleotide Archives INSDC"] EMBL[EMBL ENA at EBI UK] GB[GenBank at NCBI USA] DDBJ[DDBJ at NIG Japan] end subgraph SecondaryDBs["Secondary Curated Databases"] UniProt[UniProt Protein] RefSeq[NCBI RefSeq] Pfam[Pfam Families] PDB[PDB Structures] end WetLab -->|submit| EMBL WetLab -->|submit| GB WetLab -->|submit| DDBJ EMBL -.cross-reference.-> UniProt GB -.cross-reference.-> UniProt DDBJ -.cross-reference.-> UniProt EMBL -.DR field link.-> PDB GB -.DBLINK.-> PDB EMBL -.curated subset.-> RefSeq ``` --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Data Synchronization Cycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Submitter participant E as EMBL ENA participant G as GenBank participant D as DDBJ S->>E: Submit annotated sequence via Webin E-->>S: Assign temporary access ID + accession number Note over E: Release on publication or requested date E->>G: Daily flat-file exchange (XML/ASN.1) E->>D: Daily flat-file exchange G->>E: New submissions from NCBI users D->>E: New submissions from Japanese users Note over E,G,D: All three databases remain synchronized within 24h G-->>S: Visibility on NCBI Entrez D-->>S: Visibility on DDBJ getentry ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]` > **Q: What is EMBL-Bank? Briefly explain its role in bioinformatics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **EMBL-Bank** is the European nucleotide sequence database maintained by the **European Bioinformatics Institute (EBI)**, an outstation of the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL), located in Hinxton, UK. **(1 mark)** It serves as a **public, comprehensive repository** for nucleotide sequences and their associated biological annotations (gene structure, coding regions, taxonomy, literature references). **(1 mark)** It is one of the three principal members of the **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)**, alongside GenBank (NCBI, USA) and DDBJ (Japan), and synchronizes data with them **every 24 hours** to ensure global, free access to genetic information. **(1 mark)** --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Question Paper]` > **Q: Differentiate between the `ID` line and the `AC` line in an EMBL flat file entry. Why is the accession number considered "stable"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `ID` line contains the **entry name** (a short identifier that may change between releases), molecule type, topology, data class, and sequence length. The `AC` (Accession Number) line holds a **unique, stable, alphanumeric identifier** that NEVER changes for the life of the entry. **(2 marks)** The accession is considered stable because EMBL guarantees that once an accession is assigned to a sequence, it is **permanently linked** to that data object — even if the sequence is updated, re-annotated, or the entry name is changed. This stability is critical because **scientific publications cite sequences by accession number**, ensuring the cited data can always be retrieved. **(1 mark)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Don't** confuse the accession number with the locus/ID name — many students write "they are the same" and lose 1–2 marks. > - The accession number is in the format `[1–3 letters][5–6 digits]` (e.g., `X56734`). A correct format is worth at least **0.5 mark** in part (a) of any 14-mark question. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` > **Q: Describe in detail the structure and components of the EMBL flat file format. Using a sample entry, explain the significance of the header, feature table, and sequence sections. Also compute the GC content for a hypothetical entry.** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks] Structure and Components of the EMBL Flat File Format** The EMBL flat file format is a **plain-text, line-oriented** standardized format used to represent nucleotide sequence entries. Every line begins with a **two-character uppercase field code** (e.g., `ID`, `AC`). The file is divided into **three logical sections**. **1. Header Section** — Contains bibliographic and identification metadata: - `ID` — Entry name, molecule type, data class, sequence length. - `AC` — **Stable accession number** (permanent identifier). - `DT` — Date stamps (creation, last update, release). - `DE` — Free-text description of the sequence. - `KW` — Searchable keywords. - `OS` / `OC` — Organism species and taxonomic classification. - `RN` / `RA` / `RT` / `RL` — Reference (publication) details: number, authors, title, journal. - `DR` — Cross-references to other databases (e.g., UniProt). - `CC` — Free-text comments. **2. Feature Table Section** — Describes the **biological annotation** of regions of the sequence: - Begins with the `FH` (Feature Header) line. - Each feature starts with `FT` followed by a **feature key** (e.g., `source`, `gene`, `CDS`, `exon`, `promoter`) and a **location** (e.g., `34..456`). - **Qualifiers** (prefixed with `/`) provide additional information: `/gene="INS"`, `/product="insulin"`, `/protein_id="CAA28129.1"`, `/translation="MALWMR..."`. - This is the most biologically rich section. **3. Sequence Section** — Contains the raw nucleotide string: - Begins with the `SQ` line giving total length and base composition (e.g., `SQ Sequence 1450 BP; 386 A; 412 C; 348 G; 304 T;`). - The actual sequence follows in blocks of **10 bases per group**, **60 bases per line**, with cumulative numbering on the left margin. - The entry is terminated by the mandatory end-marker `//` on its own line. **(b) [7 marks] Worked Example with GC Content Calculation** Consider the following hypothetical EMBL entry: ``` ID TEST0001; SV 1; linear; DNA; STD; PLASMID; 1000 BP. AC AB000001; DE Hypothetical plasmid vector pTEST1, complete sequence. OS Synthetic construct SQ Sequence 1000 BP; 250 A; 300 C; 200 G; 250 T; 0 other; // ``` **Step 1:** Extract the base counts from the `SQ` line: $n_A = 250, \quad n_C = 300, \quad n_G = 200, \quad n_T = 250$ $L = 1000$ bp **Step 2:** Apply the GC% formula: $$ \begin{aligned} GC\% &= \frac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{200 + 300}{1000} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{500}{1000} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 50.0\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Interpretation — A 50% GC content is **moderate**, typical of **bacterial plasmid vectors** designed for stable replication in *E. coli* (where ~50% GC is the genomic norm). **Step 4:** Verification using $n_A + n_C + n_G + n_T$: $$ n_A + n_C + n_G + n_T = 250 + 300 + 200 + 250 = 1000 \;\checkmark $$ This matches the total length, confirming the data is internally consistent. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to write the `//` terminator in worked examples. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for an incomplete-looking sample file. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing the GC% formula as $GC = \frac{G+C}{A+T}$ (no, it's $G+C$ over **total length**). > - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing `n_G + n_C` with `n_A + n_T` in the denominator. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the **intermediate steps** in the calculation — KTU examiners award marks for the algebraic substitution, not just the final answer. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` > **Q: Explain the INSDC. Describe the three major nucleotide databases that constitute it. Discuss the daily synchronization mechanism and the importance of accession number stability across the trio.** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks] INSDC and Its Member Databases** The **International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration (INSDC)** is a **long-standing tripartite partnership** among three premier public nucleotide sequence databases. Its mission is to provide **free, unrestricted, global access** to nucleotide sequence data generated by the worldwide scientific community. **(1 mark)** **The three member databases are:** 1. **GenBank** — Maintained by the **National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI)**, part of the U.S. **National Library of Medicine (NLM)**. Established in **1982**. Primary access: `https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genbank/`. **(1.5 marks)** 2. **EMBL-Bank (now ENA — European Nucleotide Archive)** — Maintained by the **European Bioinformatics Institute (EBI)**, an outstation of the **European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL)**, located in **Hinxton, UK**. Established in **1980**. Primary access: `https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/`. **(1.5 marks)** 3. **DDBJ (DNA Data Bank of Japan)** — Maintained by the **National Institute of Genetics (NIG)** in **Mishima, Japan**. Established in **1986**. Primary access: `https://www.ddbj.nig.ac.jp/`. **(1.5 marks)** **Key principle:** *A sequence submitted to ANY one of the three is automatically shared with the other two — no double-submission is required.* This principle ensures **global data consistency, eliminates redundancy, and maximizes scientific utility**. **(1.5 marks)** **(b) [7 marks] Daily Synchronization & Accession Stability** **Daily Synchronization Mechanism:** Every 24 hours, an **automated data exchange pipeline** runs between the three INSDC partners. **(1 mark)** - New submissions received by EMBL during the previous 24 hours are exported in standardized formats to GenBank and DDBJ. **(1.5 marks)** - Similarly, GenBank forwards new U.S. submissions to EMBL and DDBJ; DDBJ forwards Japanese submissions to EMBL and GenBank. **(1.5 marks)** - The synchronization uses **standardized exchange formats** (originally flat files, now moving towards **XML/INSDSeq** structures). **(1 mark)** **Importance of Accession Number Stability:** The **accession number** is the **stable, permanent, globally unique identifier** assigned at the moment of submission. **(1 mark)** - Once assigned, an accession (e.g., `X56734`) is **never reassigned** to a different sequence, and is **never changed** even if the sequence is updated. - The `SV` (Sequence Version) number is incremented on updates, producing a fully-qualified reference such as `X56734.1`, `X56734.2`, etc. - This stability is **critical for scientific reproducibility** — when a paper says "sequence X56734 was analyzed," the reader can **always retrieve that exact data** from any of the three databases, even years later. - It enables **citation traceability** in the scientific literature, supports **legal/patent claims**, and underpins **cross-database links** (e.g., from EMBL to UniProt, PDB, RefSeq). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to mention **which institute maintains each database** — examiners expect **NCBI**, **EBI**, and **NIG** explicitly. Loss: ~1 mark. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Stating the synchronization is "real-time" — it is **daily (every 24 hours)**, not instantaneous. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not distinguishing between the **accession number** (stable) and the **sequence version** (changes with updates). > - **Common Mistake 4:** Missing the practical significance of accession stability — examiners want a **reproducibility/traceability** argument, not just a definition. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — EMBL** - ✅ **EMBL** = **European Molecular Biology Laboratory** (lab) + **EMBL-Bank / ENA** (database). - ✅ Database is at **EBI, Hinxton, UK**; lab is at **Heidelberg, Germany**. - ✅ **INSDC** = EMBL (EBI) + GenBank (NCBI) + DDBJ (NIG) — **daily 24-hour sync**. - ✅ **EMBL flat file** has 3 sections: **Header → Feature Table → Sequence** (terminated by `//`). - ✅ **`ID` line** may change between releases; **`AC` (accession) is forever stable**. - ✅ Accession format: **`[1–3 letters][5–6 digits]`** (e.g., `X56734`, `AB123456`). - ✅ Sequence shown in **groups of 10, lines of 60**, with left-margin numbering. - ✅ **`SQ` line** gives total length + base composition (A, C, G, T, other). - ✅ **`FT` (Feature Table)** keys to remember: `source`, `gene`, `CDS`, `exon`, `intron`, `mRNA`, `promoter`, `enhancer`, `repeat_region`, `misc_feature`. - ✅ **Qualifiers** start with `/` — e.g., `/gene="INS"`, `/product="insulin"`, `/protein_id="..."`, `/translation="..."`. - ✅ **GC% formula:** $GC\% = \frac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100$. - ✅ **CDS length to codons:** $n_{codons} = \lfloor (L_{CDS} - \text{stop}) / 3 \rfloor$. - ✅ Submission tool: **Webin** at `ebi.ac.uk/ena/submit/summary/`. - ✅ Cross-references in the **`DR` line** link to **UniProt, PDB, RefSeq**, etc. - ✅ The mandatory file terminator is **`//`** on its own line — never omit it. - ✅ **Three sister databases are functionally equivalent** — submission to one = visibility in all three. > **🎯 Most Important 5 Lines to Memorize for KTU Exam:** > 1. `ID XXXXX; SV 1; linear; mRNA; STD; HUM; 1450 BP.` > 2. `AC X56734;` > 3. `OS Homo sapiens (human)` > 4. `FT CDS 34..456` > 5. `SQ Sequence 1450 BP; 386 A; 412 C; 348 G; 304 T; 0 other;` ---

topics.title: Entrez
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Entrez

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 MODULE 1 — ENTREZ: The Gateway to Biological Big Data

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entrez** is a **federated text-based search and retrieval engine** developed and maintained by the **National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI)**, a branch of the **United States National Library of Medicine (NLM)**. It serves as a unified, integrated gateway that simultaneously queries **multiple interconnected biological and biomedical databases** through a single, cohesive user interface.

Entrez is not a single database — it is an **integrated information retrieval system** that acts as a single point of access to a network of databases containing **DNA sequences, protein sequences, scientific literature, gene expression profiles, taxonomy, genomes, structural data, chemical compounds, and disease information**.

### 1.1.1 Official KTU 2024 Definition Format

$$\boxed{\text{Entrez} = \left\{ \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D_i \right\} \cap \text{Cross-Linked Indexing} \cap \text{Unified Query Interface}}$$

Where each $D_i$ represents an individual NCBI-hosted database and the cross-linked indexing is provided through the **Entrez Programming Utilities (E-utilities)**.

### 1.1.2 Historical Context

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1991** | NCBI established under Senator Claude Pepper's legislation |
| **1992–1994** | David J. Lipman and colleagues at NCBI conceptualized Entrez |
| **1994** | First public release of Entrez with **MEDLINE (PubMed)** and **GenBank** |
| **1996** | Added **OMIM, Taxonomy, Protein** databases |
| **2001** | Added **dbSNP, CDD** databases |
| **2008+** | Expansion to 40+ databases including **ClinVar, dbGaP, GEO** |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Entrez like a Universal Remote Control for Life Science Data.**

Imagine you have **20 different cupboards** in your house, each containing different types of information:
- Cupboard 1 has **research papers** (PubMed)
- Cupboard 2 has **DNA sequences** (GenBank/nuccore)
- Cupboard 3 has **protein sequences** (nr/UniProt)
- Cupboard 4 has **disease information** (OMIM/ClinVar)
- Cupboard 5 has **3D structures** (PDB/MMDB)
- ... and so on.

Without Entrez, you would need to walk to each cupboard individually, remember the different opening mechanisms, and find information one cupboard at a time.

**Entrez is the single smart remote that opens all cupboards simultaneously.** You press one button — say *"BRCA1"* — and Entrez tells you:
- How many **papers** mention BRCA1 (PubMed count)
- How many **DNA sequences** of BRCA1 exist (Nucleotide count)
- How many **protein sequences** of BRCA1 are known (Protein count)
- What **diseases** are linked to BRCA1 (ClinVar / OMIM count)
- What **3D structures** of BRCA1 are solved (Structure count)
- What **homolog genes** exist in other organisms (HomoloGene count)

It is a **"one search, many databases"** paradigm — this is called **federated searching** or **integrated retrieval**.

### 1.2.1 Why This Matters for Bioinformatics Students

In a wet-lab, a researcher might spend **3 weeks** finding the right gene sequence. In a computational lab using Entrez, this is done in **under 3 minutes**. KTU expects you to know that Entrez forms the **backbone of all NCBI-based bioinformatics workflows** — from sequence retrieval for BLAST, to literature mining for systematic reviews, to clinical variant interpretation.

---

## 1.3 Core Architectural Vocabulary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-computed Adjacency and Similarity**
> The clever engineering principle that makes Entrez fast. Instead of searching databases in real-time (which would be slow), NCBI pre-computes relationships between records **offline** and stores them as **neighbor links**.

The key terms you MUST remember for KTU:

- **Entrez Global Query**: A unified search bar at the top of the NCBI homepage.
- **Entrez Databases**: Individual specialized repositories (e.g., PubMed, GenBank, Taxonomy).
- **Entrez Links**: Pre-computed connections between records in different databases.
- **Entrez Neighbors**: Pre-computed connections between records in the SAME database (e.g., related protein sequences via BLAST).
- **E-utilities**: Nine server-side programs (esearch, efetch, esummary, einfo, elink, egquery, espell, epost, eclose) that form the **API** for programmatic access to Entrez.
- **Unique Identifiers (UIDs)**: Integer IDs assigned to each record (e.g., PMID for PubMed, GI/Accession for GenBank).

---

## 1.4 List of Major Entrez Databases (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Database Name | Content Type | Unique ID |
|---------------|--------------|-----------|
| **PubMed** | Biomedical literature abstracts | **PMID** |
| **PubMed Central (PMC)** | Full-text biomedical literature | **PMCID** |
| **Nucleotide (nuccore)** | DNA/RNA sequences (GenBank + RefSeq + EMBL + DDBJ) | **Accession / GI** |
| **Protein** | Protein sequences (GenPept + RefSeq + UniProt + PIR + PRF) | **Accession / GI** |
| **Gene** | Gene-centric information across species | **Gene ID** |
| **OMIM** | Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man — human genetic disorders | **OMIM Number** |
| **Taxonomy** | Names and phylogenetic lineages of organisms | **TaxID** |
| **Structure (MMDB)** | 3D macromolecular structures derived from PDB | **MMDB ID** |
| **dbSNP** | Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms and other variations | **rs ID** |
| **ClinVar** | Clinical significance of human variations | **Variation ID** |
| **GEO (Gene Expression Omnibus)** | High-throughput gene expression data | **GEO Accession** |
| **UniGene** | Transcriptome clusters | **UniGene Cluster ID** |
| **HomoloGene** | Homolog gene groups across eukaryotic genomes | **HomoloGene ID** |
| **CDD (Conserved Domains)** | Conserved protein domain alignments | **PSSM-ID** |
| **Genome** | Whole-genome assemblies and maps | **Assembly ID** |
| **BioProject (dbGaP)** | Research projects and their resulting datasets | **BioProject ID** |
| **BioSample** | Biological source samples | **BioSample ID** |
| **Books** | Full-text biomedical books | **Book ID** |
| **MeSH** | Medical Subject Headings controlled vocabulary | **MeSH Term** |
| **PMC OA** | Open Access subset of PMC | **PMCID** |
| **Probe** | Microarray probes | **Probe ID** |
| **PopSet** | Population, phylogenetic, mutation study sets | **PopSet ID** |
| **SNP** | dbSNP short variations | **rs ID** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** Memorize at least **10 databases** and their content type. The most commonly asked in exams are **PubMed, Nucleotide (GenBank), Protein, Gene, OMIM, Taxonomy, Structure, dbSNP, ClinVar, GEO**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — How Entrez Actually Works

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Entrez Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Entrez operates on **three fundamental computational principles** that together enable instant cross-database discovery. Understanding these three pillars is essential for KTU 14-mark answers.

### Pillar 1: Pre-computed Adjacency (Cross-Database Linking)

NCBI's smart engineering decision: **don't compute links at query time — pre-compute them.**

For every record in every Entrez database, NCBI pre-calculates which records in **other databases** are biologically related. For example, a GenBank record for human BRCA1 will have pre-computed links to:
- The PubMed paper that first described the sequence
- The OMIM entry for hereditary breast cancer
- The Gene record for BRCA1
- The Protein record translated from the nucleotide sequence
- The Structure entry if a 3D model exists
- The HomoloGene entry for mouse Brca1

These pre-computed links are stored in the **Entrez Links tables**.

### Pillar 2: Pre-computed Similarity (Intra-Database Neighbors)

Within a single database (e.g., Protein), NCBI pre-computes **BLAST similarity scores** between every pair of records. A protein record's "neighbors" are those proteins with high sequence similarity. This is called the **"neighbors" relationship** and uses **BLAST bit scores** as the metric.

The bit score threshold used for neighbors is approximately:

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

Where $S$ is the raw BLAST score, $\lambda$ is the **Karlin-Altschul parameter**, and $K$ is the second Karlin-Altschul parameter. Records with $S' \geq 50$ bits are typically included as neighbors.

### Pillar 3: Unified Indexing with Boolean Logic

Entrez converts every query into a **Boolean expression** and indexes terms for fast retrieval:

$$\text{Query} = \bigvee_{i} \bigwedge_{j} T_{ij}$$

Where $T_{ij}$ represents individual terms combined with AND (intersection) within a query field, and OR (union) across multiple search terms.

---

## 2.2 The Entrez Information Retrieval Model

Entrez supports sophisticated Boolean and field-restricted queries:

### 2.2.1 Boolean Operators (Exam Favorite)

| Operator | Symbol | Example | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|---------|
| **AND** | `AND`, `&&` | `BRCA1 AND breast cancer` | Both terms must appear |
| **OR** | `OR`, `||` | `diabetes OR mellitus` | Either term may appear |
| **NOT** | `NOT`, `!` | `BRCA1 NOT mouse` | Exclude the second term |
| **Phrase** | `"..."` | `"lung cancer"` | Exact phrase match |
| **Wildcard** | `*` | `neoplas*` | Matches neoplasm, neoplasms, neoplasia |
| **Field tag** | `[field]` | `BRCA1[Gene Name]` | Search within specific field |

### 2.2.2 Field Tags for Major Databases

| Database | Common Field Tags |
|----------|-------------------|
| **PubMed** | `[Author]`, `[Title]`, `[Journal]`, `[MeSH Terms]`, `[Publication Date]`, `[Affiliation]` |
| **Nucleotide** | `[Accession]`, `[Organism]`, `[Gene]`, `[Sequence Length]`, `[Author]` |
| **Protein** | `[Accession]`, `[Organism]`, `[Molecular Weight]`, `[EC/RN Number]` |
| **Gene** | `[Gene Name]`, `[Symbol]`, `[Chromosome]`, `[Map Location]` |
| **OMIM** | `[MIM Number]`, `[Title]`, `[Text Word]`, `[Clinical Synopsis]` |
| **Taxonomy** | `[Scientific Name]`, `[Common Name]`, `[Rank]`, `[Lineage]` |

### 2.2.3 Search Term Indexing (The Math Behind Search)

When a user enters a query, Entrez applies term mapping and weighting. A simplified relevance score:

$$R(d, q) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{TF}(t, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \text{Boost}(t, \text{field})$$

Where:

$$\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{k} f_{k,d}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{IDF}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$$

And $f_{t,d}$ is the term frequency in document $d$, $N$ is total number of documents, $\text{df}(t)$ is document frequency of term $t$, and the field boost factor gives weight to field-restricted matches.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Entrez High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is your KTU-ready formula bank.** Memorize these equations for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions. Any of these can appear in the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| Boolean AND (set intersection) | $A \cap B$ | Both terms required |
| Boolean OR (set union) | $A \cup B$ | Either term sufficient |
| Boolean NOT (set difference) | $A \setminus B$ | Exclude term B |
| Boolean query normalization | $Q = (A \cap B) \cup C$ | Grouping matters |
| Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Weight rare terms higher |
| Term Frequency | $\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\vert\text{records}(d)\vert}$ | Frequency of term in document |
| Bit Score Normalization | $S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | Used for neighbor computation |
| E-value BLAST threshold | $E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$ | Probability of chance match |
| Number of total search results | $R = \bigcap_{i} D_{i,q}$ | Cross-database intersection |
| Links between database A and B | $L_{AB} = \{(r_A, r_B) \mid r_A \text{ linked to } r_B\}$ | Pre-computed pairs |
| Information content of record | $I(d) = -\log_2 P(d)$ | Self-information |
| Entrez query expansion | $Q^* = Q \cup \{T_i \mid T_i \text{ maps to } Q\}$ | Automatic term expansion |
| Cross-database result count | $C_j = \vert D_j \cap R \vert$ | Records in DB $j$ matching query $R$ |
| Neighbor set definition | $N(d) = \{d' \in D \mid S'(d, d') \geq \tau\}$ | Similar records in same DB |
| Total Entrez coverage | $\mathcal{E} = \bigsqcup_{j=1}^{n} D_j$ | Disjoint union of all databases |

> [!NOTE]
> **⚠️ CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR EXAM:** When writing set operations like $|A|$ (cardinality) inside table cells or equations in your answer sheet, use notation like $\vert A \vert$ or $\mathrm{card}(A)$ — never the broken `|A|`.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering and Scientific Utility

Entrez is the **plumbing** of modern biology and medicine. Its applications include:

1. **Drug Discovery Pipelines** — Pharmaceutical companies use Entrez's E-utilities to fetch every known nucleotide sequence of a target protein across all organisms for comparative genomics.
2. **Clinical Genomics** — Clinical geneticists query ClinVar via Entrez to interpret pathogenicity of a patient's variant.
3. **Systematic Reviews & Meta-Analysis** — Cochrane reviewers use PubMed via Entrez to gather all evidence for a clinical question.
4. **Sequence Retrieval for BLAST** — A typical bioinformatics workflow: (a) Find gene in Gene database → (b) Fetch sequence in Nucleotide database → (c) Submit to BLAST → (d) Download homologs via Entrez Protein.
5. **Phylogenetic Reconstruction** — Researchers retrieve homologous sequences for multiple species via TaxID-restricted searches.
6. **Pharmacogenomics** — Mining dbSNP and ClinVar for drug-response variants.
7. **Synthetic Biology & Genome Engineering** — Retrieving exact reference sequences for CRISPR guide design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Entrez exemplifies the **SOA (Service-Oriented Architecture)** pattern in software engineering. Each database is a **service**, E-utilities are the **API contract**, and the global query is the **unified client**.

---

## 2.5 The Nine E-utilities (Programmatic Access) — KTU Must-Know

The Entrez **E-utilities** are the **RESTful API** for programmatic access. KTU frequently asks about these.

| Utility | Function | Typical Use Case |
|---------|----------|------------------|
| **einfo** | Get database information | List all fields and links for a database |
| **esearch** | Text search → returns UIDs | `esearch?db=pubmed&term=BRCA1` |
| **efetch** | Download full records by UID | Fetch FASTA, GenBank, XML abstracts |
| **esummary** | Fetch document summaries | Get titles, authors for a UID list |
| **elink** | Find related records across databases | PubMed → Gene links |
| **egquery** | Global query across all databases | Counts in every DB for one term |
| **espell** | Spelling suggestions | Suggest correct query term |
| **epost** | Upload UID lists to server | Prepare for subsequent batch efetch |
| **eclose** | Close history / release server resources | Cleanup |

A typical **E-utilities URL** looks like:

$$\text{URL} = \texttt{https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/}\{\text{utility}\}\texttt{?db=}\{\text{db}\}\texttt{\&term=}\{\text{query}\}\texttt{\&retmode=xml}$$

Example for searching PubMed:

$$\text{URL}_{\text{esearch}} = \texttt{https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esearch.fcgi?db=pubmed\&term=BRCA1\&retmax=20}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rate limit (NCBI policy):** Without an API key, you are allowed **3 requests/second**. With a registered API key, this increases to **10 requests/second**.

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL DERIVATIONS — Entrez Logic

## 3.1 Derivation: Cross-Database Linking Algorithm

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Suppose we have three Entrez databases: **PubMed** $P$, **Gene** $G$, and **Protein** $Q$. A user wants to find all proteins linked to PubMed article PMID:12345. Derive the algorithm Entrez uses.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — User submits the query:**
The user enters PMID:12345 in the Entrez global query box and selects the PubMed database.

**Step 2 — UID resolution:**
Entrez converts the query into a UID:
$$\text{UID}_{P} = \text{esearch}(P, \text{"PMID:12345"}) = \{12345\}$$

**Step 3 — Find related Gene records using pre-computed links:**
$$\text{UID}_{G} = \text{elink}(\text{from} = P, \text{to} = G, \text{uid} = \{12345\})$$

The link table $L_{P \to G}$ is pre-computed offline:
$$L_{P \to G} = \{(12345, 672), (12345, 7157), \ldots\}$$

So we get:
$$\text{UID}_{G} = \text{elink}(P \to G, \{12345\}) = \{672, 7157, \ldots\}$$

**Step 4 — Find related Protein records via Gene:**
$$\text{UID}_{Q} = \text{elink}(\text{from} = G, \text{to} = Q, \text{uid} = \text{UID}_{G})$$

$$L_{G \to Q} = \{(672, \text{P38398}), (7157, \text{P51587}), \ldots\}$$

So:
$$\text{UID}_{Q} = \text{elink}(G \to Q, \{672, 7157\}) = \{\text{P38398}, \text{P51587}, \ldots\}$$

**Step 5 — Fetch full sequences:**
$$\text{records} = \text{efetch}(Q, \text{uid} = \text{UID}_Q, \text{rettype} = \text{fasta})$$

**Final result:**
$$\text{Final Proteins} = \text{efetch}\big(Q, \; \text{elink}(G, Q, \; \text{elink}(P, G, \; \text{esearch}(P, \; \text{PMID:12345})))\big)$$

In set-theoretic form:
$$\text{Final Proteins} = Q \cap L_{G \to Q} \big(G \cap L_{P \to G} (P)\big)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Computing Neighbor Set from BLAST Bit Scores

### 3.2.1 Problem

Given a query protein $p$ with BLAST raw score $S$ against a database protein $d$ with Karlin-Altschul parameters $\lambda$ and $K$, derive whether $d$ should appear as $p$'s neighbor in the Entrez Protein database.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Raw BLAST score:**
BLAST reports the raw alignment score $S$ based on substitution matrix and gap penalties.

**Step 2 — Bit-score normalization:**
The bit score makes scores independent of database size and scoring system:

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

**Step 3 — E-value calculation:**
The expected number of chance alignments with this score is:

$$E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$$

Where $m$ is the length of the query, $n$ is the total length of the database.

**Step 4 — Neighbor inclusion criterion:**
For a record to be a neighbor, Entrez requires:

$$S' \geq 50 \quad \text{(empirical threshold)}$$

Or equivalently:
$$E \leq 10^{-7} \quad \text{(typical stringent threshold)}$$

**Step 5 — Construct the neighbor set:**
$$N(p) = \{d \in D \mid S'(p, d) \geq 50\} = \{d \in D \mid E(p, d) \leq 10^{-7}\}$$

**Numerical example:**
Suppose for protein $p$ vs protein $d$:
- Raw score $S = 150$
- $\lambda = 0.267$
- $\ln K = -3.2$ (so $K \approx 0.0408$)

$$S' = \frac{0.267 \cdot 150 - (-3.2)}{\ln 2} = \frac{40.05 + 3.2}{0.693} = \frac{43.25}{0.693} \approx 62.4$$

Since $S' = 62.4 \geq 50$, $d$ qualifies as a neighbor of $p$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Search Feature Maps to Engineering Concepts

| Entrez Feature | Computer Science Concept | Mathematical Analog |
|----------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Boolean AND** | SQL `INTERSECT` | $A \cap B$ |
| **Boolean OR** | SQL `UNION` | $A \cup B$ |
| **Boolean NOT** | SQL `EXCEPT` | $A \setminus B$ |
| **Field tags** | Document field indexing (ElasticSearch) | Restricted domain search |
| **Pre-computed links** | Cached foreign-key joins | Lookup table $L_{A \to B}$ |
| **Pre-computed neighbors** | Pre-computed similarity graph | $k$-NN graph |
| **E-utilities** | RESTful API endpoints | URL-based RPC |
| **UID** | Primary key | Integer surrogate key |
| **Accession number** | Versioned identifier | Immutable string key |
| **efetch rettype** | Content negotiation | MIME-type selector |
| **History (WebEnv)** | Server-side session | Stateful HTTP session |
| **egquery** | Federated search engine | Cross-index dispatcher |
| **History server** | Session caching | In-memory cache |
| **MeSH terms** | Controlled vocabulary | Ontology lookup |
| **Taxonomy tree** | Hierarchical tree index | Prefix tree (Trie) |
| **Sequence length filter** | Range query | B-tree on numeric attribute |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Computing Global Cross-Database Result Counts

### 3.4.1 Problem

A user searches for `BRCA1` globally. How does Entrez return the count of records in each database?

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — User enters global query** in the top search bar (default database = "All Databases").

**Step 2 — egquery dispatches the query** to every Entrez database $D_j$ for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:

$$\text{Query} \xrightarrow{\text{egquery}} \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} \text{esearch}(D_j, \text{query})$$

**Step 3 — Each database computes its count:**
$$C_j = \vert \{r \in D_j \mid q \text{ matches } r\} \vert$$

**Step 4 — Return vector of counts:**
$$\vec{C} = (C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n)$$

For example, for `BRCA1`:
$$\vec{C}_{\text{BRCA1}} = (\text{PubMed}: 35000, \text{Gene}: 12, \text{Protein}: 8500, \text{Nucleotide}: 4200, \ldots)$$

**Step 5 — Display in Entrez results page:**
The web UI displays the vector as clickable boxes, each showing $C_j$ for database $D_j$. Clicking a box activates esearch on $D_j$ with the same query.

In summary form:
$$\text{Global Query} \rightarrow \big\{(D_j, C_j) \mid C_j = \vert D_j \cap R \vert\big\}$$

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Entrez Federated Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[User Query Interface] --> globalQuery1[Entrez Global Query Bar]
    globalQuery1 --> dispatcher1[Query Dispatcher egquery]

    dispatcher1 --> dbA1[PubMed Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbB1[Nucleotide Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbC1[Protein Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbD1[Gene Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbE1[OMIM Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbF1[Taxonomy Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbG1[Structure MMDB Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbH1[ClinVar Database]
    dispatcher1 --> dbI1[GEO Database]

    dbA1 --> indexA1[Inverted Index PMID]
    dbB1 --> indexB1[Inverted Index Accession]
    dbC1 --> indexC1[Inverted Index Accession]
    dbD1 --> indexD1[Inverted Index Gene ID]
    dbE1 --> indexE1[Inverted Index MIM Number]
    dbF1 --> indexF1[Inverted Index TaxID]
    dbG1 --> indexG1[Inverted Index MMDB ID]
    dbH1 --> indexH1[Inverted Index Variation ID]
    dbI1 --> indexI1[Inverted Index GEO Accession]

    dbA1 -.-> links1[Pre-computed Cross Links]
    dbB1 -.-> links1
    dbC1 -.-> links1
    dbD1 -.-> links1
    dbE1 -.-> links1
    dbF1 -.-> links1
    dbG1 -.-> links1
    dbH1 -.-> links1
    dbI1 -.-> links1

    links1 --> api1[E-utilities API Layer]
    api1 --> out1[Returned Records UIDs]

    out1 --> results1[Result Page with Counts and Links]
    results1 --> user1
```

**Caption:** The federated architecture of Entrez. A single query is dispatched in parallel to all databases. Each database maintains its own inverted index and contributes to a shared cross-link table that enables intra-database (neighbors) and inter-database (links) navigation.

---

## 4.2 Workflow Diagram: Typical Sequence Retrieval Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step 1 Search Gene database] --> s2[Step 2 Get Gene ID]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Link to Nucleotide]

    s9 --> s10[Step 10 Phylogenetic analysis]
```

## 4.3 E-utilities Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    reqA --> euA[Entrez E-utilities Server]

    euA --> esA[esearch Module]

    euA --> einA[einfo Module]

```

## 4.4 Pre-computed Link vs. Neighbor Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SD1[Same Database Intra-DB]
        p1[Protein P1] -->|Neighbor bit score above 50| p2[Protein P2]
        p1 -->|Neighbor bit score above 50| p3[Protein P3]
        p2 -->|Neighbor bit score above 50| p3
    end

    subgraph SD2[Cross Database Inter-DB]

```

**Caption:** Left: Intra-database "neighbors" — related records within the same database, computed via BLAST similarity. Right: Inter-database "links" — pre-computed relationships between a Gene record and its associated records across other Entrez databases.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Entrez. Mention any four NCBI databases accessible through it.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entrez** is a **federated text-based search and retrieval system** developed by the **National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI)**. It provides a **single unified interface** to query multiple interconnected biological databases simultaneously and retrieves **pre-computed cross-linked records** across them.

Four major databases accessible through Entrez:

| # | Database | Content |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **PubMed** | Biomedical literature abstracts |
| 2 | **Nucleotide** | DNA / RNA sequences |
| 3 | **Protein** | Protein sequences |
| 4 | **Gene** | Gene-specific records across species |
| 5 | **OMIM** | Human genetic disorders |

Entrez uses the **Entrez Programming Utilities (E-utilities)** as its API and supports both **boolean operators** (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`) and **field-restricted queries** (e.g., `BRCA1[Gene Name]`).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of "Entrez links" and "Entrez neighbors" with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entrez Links** are **pre-computed connections between records of different databases**, while **Entrez Neighbors** are **pre-computed connections between records within the same database**.

**Links (Inter-database):**
A GenBank nucleotide record for human BRCA1 (Accession NM_007294) is **linked** to:
- The corresponding **Gene** record (Gene ID 672)
- The associated **PubMed** article (PMID)
- The **OMIM** entry (MIM 113705)
- The **Protein** record (Accession NP_009225)

**Neighbors (Intra-database):**
Within the **Protein** database, the BRCA1 protein (NP_009225) has **neighbors** — proteins with high BLAST bit scores ($\geq 50$), typically including:
- BRCA1 from mouse
- BRCA1 from rat
- Other PARP-domain-containing proteins

Links and neighbors are **pre-computed offline** by NCBI to enable **real-time fast retrieval** during user queries.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Choose **ONE** of two alternatives per question. Each alternative has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Sub-part (a) is typically descriptive/analytical, and sub-part (b) is typically applied/comparative. Use **diagrams, tables, and equations** liberally.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Entrez system. Describe how pre-computed links and neighbors enable rapid cross-database retrieval. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain any **five Entrez E-utilities** with their purpose and a sample URL. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Entrez Architecture [7 marks]**

The Entrez architecture consists of **three integrated layers**:

**Layer 1 — The User Interface Layer:**

The web interface at `https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/` provides a **single global query bar** that accepts free text. The query is sent to the **Query Dispatcher** (implemented by `egquery`), which fans out the query to all NCBI databases in parallel.

**Layer 2 — The Database Layer:**

Each Entrez database (PubMed, Nucleotide, Protein, Gene, OMIM, etc.) is a **separate inverted-index storage system** with its own:

- **Unique identifiers** (UIDs) — PMID for PubMed, GI/Accession for Nucleotide, Gene ID for Gene.
- **Field tags** — `[Author]`, `[Organism]`, `[Title]`, `[MeSH Terms]`, etc.
- **Document collection** — millions of records.

Inverted indexing means each unique term maps to a posting list of record IDs:

$$\text{term} \rightarrow [d_1, d_5, d_{12}, \ldots]$$

**Layer 3 — The Cross-Linking Layer:**

NCBI pre-computes two types of relationships:

1. **Links** (cross-database): For each record $r_A \in D_A$, store pointers to related records in other databases $D_B, D_C, \ldots$:
$$L(r_A) = \{(D_B, r_B), (D_C, r_C), \ldots\}$$

2. **Neighbors** (within-database): For each record $r_A \in D_A$, store BLAST-similar records:
$$N(r_A) = \{r'_A \in D_A \mid S'(r_A, r'_A) \geq 50\}$$

Because links and neighbors are pre-computed, query time is reduced from minutes to **milliseconds**. This is the central engineering innovation of Entrez.

**Mathematical Summary:**

$$\text{Entrez Result} = \big\{r \in D_q \mid q \text{ matches } r\big\} \cup \bigcup_{r \in \text{Result}} L(r)$$

The user sees initial matches **plus** all linked records, giving the appearance of "intelligent" cross-database discovery.

---

#### **Part (b) — Five Entrez E-utilities [7 marks]**

| # | E-utility | HTTP Verb | Purpose | Sample URL |
|---|-----------|-----------|---------|------------|
| 1 | **esearch** | GET | Searches a database and returns a list of UIDs matching the query term | `https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esearch.fcgi?db=pubmed&term=BRCA1&retmax=20` |
| 2 | **efetch** | GET | Downloads the full records (FASTA, GenBank, XML) for a given UID list | `https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=nucleotide&id=NM_007294&rettype=fasta&retmode=text` |
| 3 | **elink** | GET | Finds related records across Entrez databases using pre-computed links | `https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/elink.fcgi?dbfrom=pubmed&db=gene&id=12345` |
| 4 | **esummary** | GET | Returns document summaries (title, authors, source) for a UID list | `https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esummary.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=12345,67890` |
| 5 | **einfo** | GET | Lists all searchable fields and available links for a database | `https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/einfo.fcgi?db=protein` |

**Example Workflow Using E-utilities:**

To fetch all protein sequences linked to a PubMed article:

```
Step 1: esearch  → db=pubmed, term=PMID:12345           → UIDs = {12345}
Step 2: elink    → dbfrom=pubmed, db=protein, id=12345    → UIDs = {P38398, P51587, ...}
Step 3: efetch   → db=protein, id=P38398,P51587, rettype=fasta → Sequences
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **Links** (cross-database) with **Neighbors** (within-database). This costs **2 marks**.
> - Students often forget that **E-utilities are stateless** and require **WebEnv + query_key** for session persistence.
> - Students often miss the **rate-limit rule**: 3 req/sec without API key, 10 req/sec with API key. This is a frequent 1-mark question.
> - Forgetting to mention **inverted indexing** as the underlying mechanism of Entrez search costs **2 marks** in long answers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** What are the major categories of Entrez databases? List at least **eight databases** with their content type and unique identifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A researcher wants to retrieve all human BRCA1 protein sequences with molecular weight between 50–100 kDa. Construct the Entrez query step-by-step, explaining how field tags and Boolean operators work. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Eight Entrez Databases [7 marks]**

Entrez databases can be grouped into **three major categories**:

**Category 1 — Literature Databases:**

| Database | Content | UID |
|----------|---------|-----|
| **PubMed** | Biomedical abstracts and citations | PMID |
| **PMC** | Full-text biomedical articles | PMCID |
| **Books** | Full-text biomedical books | Book ID |

**Category 2 — Molecular Biology Databases (Sequences and Structures):**

| Database | Content | UID |
|----------|---------|-----|
| **Nucleotide** | DNA / RNA sequences | Accession / GI |
| **Protein** | Protein sequences | Accession / GI |
| **Structure (MMDB)** | 3D macromolecular structures | MMDB ID |
| **Conserved Domains (CDD)** | Conserved protein domain alignments | PSSM-ID |

**Category 3 — Genomic and Genetic Variation Databases:**

| Database | Content | UID |
|----------|---------|-----|
| **Gene** | Gene-centric records across species | Gene ID |
| **dbSNP** | SNPs and short genetic variations | rs ID |
| **OMIM** | Human Mendelian disorders | MIM Number |
| **ClinVar** | Clinical significance of variations | Variation ID |
| **Taxonomy** | Organism names and lineages | TaxID |
| **GEO** | Gene expression profiles | GEO Accession |
| **HomoloGene** | Eukaryotic homolog gene groups | HomoloGene ID |

**Naming Convention Insight:**

NCBI uses **two parallel identifier systems** for sequences:
- **Accession Number** (e.g., `NM_007294`) — **Stable**, never changes; assigned by submitter.
- **GI Number** (e.g., `4557674`) — **Versioned**, changes with each update; assigned by NCBI.

Always prefer **accession numbers** in your research; GIs are deprecated in modern workflows.

---

#### **Part (b) — Constructing the BRCA1 Protein Query [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the search fields needed:**

- **Organism** = *Homo sapiens* → field `[Organism]`
- **Protein name** = *BRCA1* → field `[Protein Name]`
- **Molecular weight** = *50–100 kDa* → field `[Molecular Weight]`

**Step 2 — Build the individual field terms:**

| Field | Term | Why |
|-------|------|-----|
| `[Organism]` | `"Homo sapiens"[Organism]` | Restrict to human proteins |
| `[Protein Name]` | `BRCA1[Protein Name]` | Match the BRCA1 protein name |
| `[Molecular Weight]` | `50000:100000[Molecular Weight]` | Range filter using colon `:` |

**Step 3 — Combine using Boolean AND:**

```text
"BRCA1"[Protein Name] AND "Homo sapiens"[Organism] AND 50000:100000[Molecular Weight]
```

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$\text{Final Set} = P_{\text{BRCA1}} \cap P_{\text{Human}} \cap P_{\text{MW 50-100 kDa}}$$

Where $P_x$ is the posting list for term $x$.

**Step 5 — Submit via web or E-utilities:**

Web:
- Navigate to `https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/protein/`
- Paste the query
- Click Search

E-utilities URL:
$$\texttt{https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esearch.fcgi?db=protein&term="BRCA1"[Protein~Name]~AND~"Homo~sapiens"[Organism]~AND~50000:100000[Molecular~Weight]}$$

**Step 6 — Refine if too many results:**

Add filters:
- `reviewed[Filter]` — only Swiss-Prot curated entries
- `srcdb_swiss-prot[Filter]` — same as above

Refined query:
```text
"BRCA1"[Protein Name] AND "Homo sapiens"[Organism] AND 50000:100000[Molecular Weight] AND srcdb_swiss-prot[Filter]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often forget the **colon `:` syntax** for range queries — Entrez uses `min:max[Field]`, NOT `min-max[Field]` or SQL-like operators.
> - Confusing `[Protein Name]` with `[Gene Name]` costs 1 mark — they refer to **different** Entrez fields.
> - Students sometimes use `[Title]` instead of `[Protein Name]` — this searches only the document title, not the curated protein name field, yielding fewer results.
> - The **AND must be uppercase**; lowercase "and" is treated as a literal word.
> - Forgetting to URL-encode spaces (`%20` or `+`) in E-utilities URLs is a common technical error.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it the morning of your exam.**

### ✅ **Must-Know Definitions**

- [ ] Entrez is a **federated text-based retrieval system** by **NCBI** (since 1994).
- [ ] It provides **unified access** to **40+ databases** via a **single query interface**.
- [ ] The two core innovations are **pre-computed Links** and **pre-computed Neighbors**.

### ✅ **Three Architectural Layers**

- [ ] **User Interface Layer** — Global query bar and result pages.
- [ ] **Database Layer** — Individual specialized databases with inverted indexes.
- [ ] **Cross-Linking Layer** — Pre-computed relationships (Links + Neighbors).

### ✅ **Top 10 Databases to Memorize**

- [ ] PubMed, PMC, Nucleotide, Protein, Gene, OMIM, Taxonomy, Structure, dbSNP, ClinVar.

### ✅ **All 9 E-utilities**

- [ ] **esearch** — search by term → returns UIDs.
- [ ] **efetch** — download records by UID.
- [ ] **esummary** — fetch document summaries.
- [ ] **elink** — find related records across DBs.
- [ ] **einfo** — list fields and links for a DB.
- [ ] **egquery** — global query across all DBs.
- [ ] **espell** — spelling suggestions.
- [ ] **epost** — upload UID list for batch use.
- [ ] **eclose** — cleanup server history.

### ✅ **Critical Boolean Operators**

- [ ] **AND** (intersection, both required), **OR** (union, either), **NOT** (exclude).
- [ ] **Phrase search** uses `"double quotes"`.
- [ ] **Wildcard** is `*` (e.g., `neoplas*`).
- [ ] **Field tag** syntax is `term[field]`.
- [ ] **Range** syntax is `min:max[field]`.

### ✅ **Key Equations**

- [ ] Bit score: $S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$
- [ ] E-value: $E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$
- [ ] Neighbor threshold: $S' \geq 50$
- [ ] IDF weighting: $\text{IDF}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$
- [ ] Cross-link result: $\text{Result} \cup \bigcup_{r \in \text{Result}} L(r)$

### ✅ **Practical Engineering Facts**

- [ ] Rate limit: **3 req/sec** without API key, **10 req/sec** with key.
- [ ] API key obtained by free NCBI account registration.
- [ ] Accession numbers are **stable**, GI numbers are **deprecated**.
