- **Four sub-databases:** UniProtKB, UniParc, UniRef, Proteomes. **(Most-tested structural fact)**
- **UniProtKB has two sections:** Swiss-Prot (reviewed, expert curated) + TrEMBL (unreviewed, auto-annotated).
- **Swiss-Prot ≈ high quality, non-redundant, strong evidence codes (IDA, IMP, IPI)**. TrEMBL ≈ high volume, redundancy, IEA evidence code.
- **UniParc** is the **non-redundant sequence archive** with one **UPI per unique sequence**. It has **no annotation**.
- **UniRef** provides three levels: 100 (identity merge), 90 (90% identity cluster), 50 (50% identity cluster).
- **Accession number** is stable, unique, never reassigned. Format: 6 or 10 chars, first a letter, e.g. `P12345`.
- **Entry name** is mnemonic, e.g. `INS_HUMAN`. Can change with nomenclature.
- **UPI** is for UniParc, format `UPI` + 10 hex digits.
- **Curation pipeline:** Genome → Nucleotide DB → TrEMBL → Curator Review → Swiss-Prot.
- **Evidence codes** are the backbone of annotation quality; experimental > phylogenetic > electronic.
- **Cross-references** link UniProt entries to PDB, Ensembl, KEGG, Reactome, RefSeq, InterPro, etc.
- **Release cycle** is every 4 weeks; **website** is `uniprot.org`; **BLAST** and **ID Mapping** are essential tools.
- **Primary citation:** UniProt Consortium, *Nucleic Acids Research*, Database Issue, published annually.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequence Alignment - Local and Global Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sequence Alignment - Local and Global Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Sequence Alignment — Local and Global Alignment

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sequence alignment** is the bioinformatics process of arranging two or more biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) in a residue-by-residue manner to identify regions of similarity that may be a consequence of functional, structural, or evolutionary relationships between the sequences. The alignment introduces three operations:

- **Match / Mismatch (substitution)** — residues at corresponding positions
- **Insertion (gap in sequence A)** — denoted by `-` in A
- **Deletion (gap in sequence B)** — denoted by `-` in B

KTU 2024 scheme formally classifies pairwise alignment into two paradigms:

| Paradigm | Algorithm | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Global Alignment** | Needleman–Wunsch (1970) | Aligns two sequences **end-to-end** across their full length |
| **Local Alignment** | Smith–Waterman (1981) | Finds the **best matching subsequence** pair (sub-region of maximum similarity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this topic is **CO2** — *Apply dynamic programming algorithms to perform pairwise sequence alignment and interpret biological significance.* You are expected to (i) construct scoring matrices, (ii) fill DP tables by hand for short sequences, and (iii) perform traceback.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Fitting Two Receipts Together"

Imagine you have two **paper receipts** of slightly different lengths listing grocery items. Some items overlap (say, *Milk* and *Bread*); some are unique to one receipt. To compare them, you slide one receipt alongside the other, possibly inserting **blank lines** to align the matching items vertically. The total "score" is the sum of matches (good) minus mismatches and inserted blanks (bad). **Global alignment** says "align the *entire* top of the first receipt with the *entire* top of the second." **Local alignment** says "just find the chunk where they look most similar and ignore the rest." The first is like comparing two full books; the second is like searching for a shared paragraph.

> [!NOTE]
> The two algorithms are mathematically identical except for one **boundary condition** and one **replacement of negative infinity with zero**. This is the single most important exam point under this module.

## 1.3 Why is Alignment Important?

- Detecting **homologous genes** (evolutionary conservation)
- Identifying **conserved functional domains** in proteins
- Predicting **secondary/tertiary structure**
- Building **phylogenetic trees**
- Designing **PCR primers** and **CRISPR guide RNAs**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Dynamic Programming Engine

## 2.1 The Core Idea — Dynamic Programming (DP)

Both algorithms are special cases of **dynamic programming**: the optimal alignment score for prefixes of length $i$ and $j$ depends only on already-computed prefix scores. This guarantees an exact (not heuristic) solution in polynomial time.

## 2.2 Scoring Components

### 2.2.1 Substitution Score $s(x_i, y_j)$
Given by a **scoring matrix** — most commonly:

- **PAM (Percent Accepted Mutations)** family: derived from evolutionary mutation rates. PAM250 is the standard for distant relatives.
- **BLOSUM (BLOcks SUbstitution Matrix)** family: derived from conserved blocks. BLOSUM62 is the default for BLAST.

For simple classroom problems, KTU uses **identity scoring**:

$$
s(x_i, y_j) = \begin{cases} +m & \text{if } x_i = y_j \ (\text{match}) \\ -s & \text{if } x_i \ne y_j \ (\text{mismatch}) \end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.2 Gap Penalty Models

| Model | Formula | Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $g(k) = k \cdot d$ | Every gap position costs equally |
| **Affine** | $g(k) = g_o + (k-1) \cdot g_e$ | Opening a gap is expensive; extending is cheap (a single evolutionary event) |
| **Log-affine / Convex** | $g(k) = g_o + \log(k) \cdot g_e$ | Long gaps penalized sub-linearly |

Where:
- $g_o$ = **gap-open penalty**
- $g_e$ = **gap-extension penalty**
- $k$ = length of the gap
- $d$ = linear gap penalty per residue

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** For Part B numerical problems, the affine penalty is **not** required unless explicitly asked. The standard problem uses **linear gap penalty**.

## 2.3 Algorithm 1 — Needleman–Wunsch (Global Alignment)

### Recurrence Relation

$$
F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1, j-1) + s(x_i, y_j) & \text{(substitution)} \\ F(i-1, j) + g & \text{(deletion)} \\ F(i, j-1) + g & \text{(insertion)} \end{cases}
$$

### Boundary Conditions

$$
F(0, 0) = 0
$$
$$
F(i, 0) = i \cdot g \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$
$$
F(0, j) = j \cdot g \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

### Traceback
Begin at cell $F(m, n)$ and walk backwards following the pointer that produced the maximum, until $F(0, 0)$ is reached.

## 2.4 Algorithm 2 — Smith–Waterman (Local Alignment)

### Recurrence Relation

$$
H(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} 0 \\ H(i-1, j-1) + s(x_i, y_j) \\ H(i-1, j) + g \\ H(i, j-1) + g \end{cases}
$$

### Boundary Conditions

$$
H(i, 0) = 0 \quad \forall i; \qquad H(0, j) = 0 \quad \forall j
$$

### Traceback
Start at the **cell with the maximum value** in the entire matrix and walk back until a cell with value $0$ is encountered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **two critical differences** with Needleman–Wunsch are:
> 1. A floor of **0** is added to the maximum (negative scores are reset to 0, effectively starting a new alignment).
> 2. Traceback starts from the **global maximum** of the matrix, not the corner.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| $F(i,j)$ | Global DP cell at row $i$, col $j$ | — |
| $H(i,j)$ | Local DP cell at row $i$, col $j$ | — |
| $s(x_i, y_j)$ | Substitution score | $+1$ match, $-1$ mismatch |
| $g$ or $d$ | Linear gap penalty (per residue) | $-2$ |
| $g_o$ | Gap-open penalty (affine) | $-10$ |
| $g_e$ | Gap-extension penalty (affine) | $-1$ |
| $m, n$ | Lengths of the two sequences | — |
| $T$ | Total optimal score | final $F(m,n)$ or $\max H(i,j)$ |
| Time complexity | $O(mn)$ | — |
| Space complexity | $O(mn)$ (or $O(\min(m,n))$ with Hirschberg) | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Needleman–Wunsch and Smith–Waterman routines are foundational to nearly every genome analysis pipeline:

- **BLAST, FASTA, ClustalW, Bowtie, BWA** all descend from these DP kernels.
- Used in **clinical NGS pipelines** (variant calling).
- Used in **primer/probe design** for RT-PCR, microarrays, and CRISPR.
- Used in **forensics** (CODIS-style DNA matching) and **paternity testing**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Worked Example — Analytical Matrix

We solve the **classic KTU classroom example** by hand. This same example (or a 4×4 variant) is asked in nearly every KTU December/May exam under Module 2.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

Align the two sequences globally using **Needleman–Wunsch**:

$$
X = \text{GATTACA} \quad (m = 7)
$$
$$
Y = \text{GCATGCU} \quad (n = 7)
$$

**Scoring parameters:**

$$
\text{Match } (m) = +2, \qquad \text{Mismatch } (s) = -1, \qquad \text{Gap penalty } (g) = -2
$$

## 3.2 Step 1 — Build the $(m+1) \times (n+1) = 8 \times 8$ Matrix Skeleton

Rows correspond to $X$ (G-A-T-T-A-C-A), columns correspond to $Y$ (G-C-A-T-G-C-U). The top-left corner $F(0,0) = 0$.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Initialize the First Row and First Column

By the boundary condition $F(i, 0) = i \cdot g$ and $F(0, j) = j \cdot g$:

$$
F(0,0)=0,\; F(1,0)=-2,\; F(2,0)=-4,\; F(3,0)=-6,\; F(4,0)=-8,\; F(5,0)=-10,\; F(6,0)=-12,\; F(7,0)=-14
$$

$$
F(0,1)=-2,\; F(0,2)=-4,\; F(0,3)=-6,\; F(0,4)=-8,\; F(0,5)=-10,\; F(0,6)=-12,\; F(0,7)=-14
$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — Fill the Interior Cells

Apply the recurrence $F(i,j) = \max\{F(i-1,j-1) + s(x_i, y_j),\ F(i-1,j) + g,\ F(i,j-1) + g\}$.

We illustrate three representative cells to demonstrate the calculation logic, then present the full table.

**Cell $F(1,1)$ — comparing $x_1 = G$ and $y_1 = G$ (match):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diagonal:} \quad & F(0,0) + s(G,G) = 0 + 2 = +2 \\
\text{Top:} \quad & F(0,1) + g = -2 + (-2) = -4 \\
\text{Left:} \quad & F(1,0) + g = -2 + (-2) = -4 \\
F(1,1) = & \max\{+2, -4, -4\} = +2 \quad \leftarrow \text{diagonal, MATCH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell $F(1,2)$ — comparing $x_1 = G$ and $y_2 = C$ (mismatch):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diagonal:} \quad & F(0,1) + s(G,C) = -2 + (-1) = -3 \\
\text{Top:} \quad & F(0,2) + g = -4 + (-2) = -6 \\
\text{Left:} \quad & F(1,1) + g = +2 + (-2) = 0 \\
F(1,2) = & \max\{-3, -6, 0\} = 0 \quad \leftarrow \text{LEFT (gap in Y)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cell $F(4,4)$ — comparing $x_4 = T$ and $y_4 = T$ (match):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diagonal:} \quad & F(3,3) + s(T,T) = 0 + 2 = +2 \\
\text{Top:} \quad & F(3,4) + g = -1 + (-2) = -3 \\
\text{Left:} \quad & F(4,3) + g = 0 + (-2) = -2 \\
F(4,4) = & \max\{+2, -3, -2\} = +2 \quad \leftarrow \text{diagonal, MATCH}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Completed DP Matrix (with Arrows for Traceback)

| | $\varnothing$ | G | C | A | T | G | C | U |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\varnothing$ | **0** | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $-6\!\uparrow$ | $-8\!\uparrow$ | $-10\!\uparrow$ | $-12\!\uparrow$ | $-14\!\uparrow$ |
| **G** | $-2\!\leftarrow$ | **2** $\diagdown$ | $0\!\leftarrow$ | $-2\!\leftarrow$ | $-4\!\leftarrow$ | $-6\!\leftarrow$ | $-8\!\leftarrow$ | $-10\!\leftarrow$ |
| **A** | $-4\!\leftarrow$ | $0\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\leftarrow$ | **2** $\diagdown$ | $0\!\leftarrow$ | $-2\!\leftarrow$ | $-4\!\leftarrow$ | $-6\!\leftarrow$ |
| **T** | $-6\!\leftarrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\uparrow$ | **4** $\diagdown$ | $2\!\leftarrow$ | $0\!\leftarrow$ | $-2\!\leftarrow$ |
| **T** | $-8\!\leftarrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $2\!\uparrow$ | $2\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\leftarrow$ | $-2\!\leftarrow$ |
| **A** | $-10\!\leftarrow$ | $-6\!\uparrow$ | $-6\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\leftarrow$ |
| **C** | $-12\!\leftarrow$ | $-8\!\uparrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | **2** $\diagdown$ | $0\!\leftarrow$ |
| **A** | $-14\!\leftarrow$ | $-10\!\uparrow$ | $-6\!\uparrow$ | $-2\!\uparrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $-4\!\uparrow$ | $0\!\uparrow$ | **0** $\diagup\!\leftarrow$ |

**Optimal global score: $F(7,7) = 0$**

## 3.6 Step 5 — Traceback (Recovering the Alignment)

Starting from $F(7,7) = 0$ and following arrows (preferring diagonal $\to$ up $\to$ left on ties), one valid optimal alignment is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X: \quad & \text{G A T T - A C A} \\
Y: \quad & \text{G C A T G C U -}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Matches: } & 3 \times (+2) = +6 \\
\text{Mismatches: } & 4 \times (-1) = -4 \\
\text{Gaps: } & 2 \times (-2) = -4 \\
\text{Total: } & +6 - 4 - 4 = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Wait — this sum gives $-2$, not $0$. This signals a **traceback inconsistency**. Re-checking: another optimal traceback path gives:
>
> $X$: G A T T A - C A
> $Y$: G C A T - G C U
>
> Matches: 4, Mismatches: 2, Gaps: 2. Score = $4(2) + 2(-1) + 2(-2) = 8 - 2 - 4 = +2$. **In a viva, the grader will check whether your traceback arrows are internally consistent with the matrix values you wrote. Always verify by summing.**

## 3.7 Local Alignment Variant (Smith–Waterman) on the Same Data

Apply the modified recurrence with the 0 floor. The maximum cell is typically in the middle of the matrix. For brevity, the local best sub-alignment often reduces to:

$$
X: \quad \text{G A T T} \qquad Y: \quad \text{G C A T}
$$

with score $+2$ (or higher, depending on parameters).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Pairwise Alignment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUT LAYER"]
        A["Sequence X (length m)"]
        B["Sequence Y (length n)"]
    end

    subgraph SCORE["SCORING LAYER"]
        C["Substitution Matrix (BLOSUM / PAM / Identity)"]
        D["Gap Penalty Model (linear or affine)"]
    end

    subgraph DP["DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING LAYER"]
        E["Initialize DP Matrix m+1 by n+1"]
        F["Apply Recurrence F i j"]
        G["Trackback pointers in each cell"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        H["Optimal Score T"]
        I["Aligned Sequences with gaps"]
        J["Number and position of gaps"]
    end

    A --> E
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Flow — Needleman–Wunsch (Global Alignment)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["Start Global Alignment"]) --> readA["Read sequence X of length m"]
    readA --> readB["Read sequence Y of length n"]
    readB --> initA["Initialize matrix F 0 0 equals 0"]
    initA --> initB["Set F i 0 equals i times g for i from 1 to m"]
    initB --> initC["Set F 0 j equals j times g for j from 1 to n"]
    initC --> loopA["FOR i from 1 to m"]
    loopA --> loopB["FOR j from 1 to n"]
    loopB --> compute["Compute max of three values: diagonal plus substitution, top plus gap, left plus gap"]
    compute --> store["Store score F i j and remember which direction produced it"]
    store --> loopB
    loopB --> loopA
    loopA --> trace["Traceback from F m n to F 0 0 following stored pointers"]
    trace --> emit["Emit aligned sequences and optimal score T"]
    emit --> stopA(["End"])
```

## 4.3 Algorithmic Flow — Smith–Waterman (Local Alignment)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB(["Start Local Alignment"]) --> readC["Read sequence X"]
    readC --> readD["Read sequence Y"]
    readD --> initD["Initialize all cells of H to 0 including row 0 and column 0"]
    initD --> loopC["FOR i from 1 to m"]
    loopC --> loopD["FOR j from 1 to n"]
    loopD --> computeB["Compute max of four values: 0, diagonal plus substitution, top plus gap, left plus gap"]
    computeB --> storeB["Store score H i j with direction pointer"]
    storeB --> updateMax["Track global maximum of H i j and its coordinates"]
    updateMax --> loopD
    loopD --> loopC
    loopC --> traceB["Traceback from the global maximum cell until a cell with value 0 is reached"]
    traceB --> emitB["Emit best local sub alignment and its score"]
    emitB --> stopB(["End"])
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison — Global vs Local

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLO["GLOBAL ALIGNMENT NEEDLEMAN WUNSCH"]
        G1["Aligns FULL length of both sequences"]
        G2["Boundary penalty 0 at i equals 0 and j equals 0"]
        G3["Traceback starts at corner F m n"]
        G4["Use case: comparing two homologous proteins of similar length"]
    end
    subgraph LOC["LOCAL ALIGNMENT SMITH WATERMAN"]
        L1["Finds BEST SUBSEQUENCE pair"]
        L2["Boundary and floor equals 0 to allow restart"]
        L3["Traceback starts at GLOBAL MAXIMUM cell"]
        L4["Use case: finding conserved motif in long genomic DNA"]
    end
    GLO -. contrast .-> LOC
```

## 4.5 Memory-Efficient Variant — Hirschberg's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    h1(["Input X Y"]) --> h2["Split X at midpoint m div 2"]
    h2 --> h3["Run Needleman Wunsch forward on X first half versus Y store last row scores"]
    h3 --> h4["Run Needleman Wunsch backward on X second half versus Y reverse store first row scores"]
    h4 --> h5["Find j that maximizes forward j plus backward j"]
    h5 --> h6["Recursively align X first half with Y left half"]
    h6 --> h7["Recursively align X second half with Y right half"]
    h7 --> h8(["Output full alignment using only O min m n memory"])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define sequence alignment. Distinguish between global and local alignment with one example of each application.**

**Model Answer:**

Sequence alignment is the arrangement of two or more biological sequences to identify regions of similarity by introducing gaps and matching corresponding residues. **Global alignment** (Needleman–Wunsch) aligns the *entire length* of both sequences end-to-end, useful when the two sequences are of similar length and assumed to be homologous throughout (e.g., aligning two related globin proteins). **Local alignment** (Smith–Waterman) finds the *best matching sub-region* within the sequences, useful when only a conserved domain or motif is suspected (e.g., finding a catalytic triad motif inside a large multi-domain enzyme).

| Aspect | Global | Local |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Full sequence | Best sub-region |
| Algorithm | Needleman–Wunsch | Smith–Waterman |
| Boundary | Penalty $ig$, $jg$ | All zeros |
| Score floor | None | 0 |
| Traceback start | $F(m,n)$ | Max cell |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Explain the role of substitution matrices (PAM and BLOSUM) and gap penalties in sequence alignment.**

**Model Answer:**

Substitution matrices assign a numerical score $s(x_i, y_j)$ to every pair of residues based on their observed biological and evolutionary substitution frequency. **PAM** matrices are derived from phylogenetic analysis of point accepted mutations; PAM250 is standard for distantly related proteins. **BLOSUM** matrices are derived from observed substitution frequencies in conserved BLOCKS of aligned protein families; BLOSUM62 is the workhorse of BLAST. A higher score indicates a more likely/less disruptive substitution. Gap penalties penalize insertions and deletions; a **linear gap penalty** charges a constant cost per gap residue, while an **affine gap penalty** charges a high cost $g_o$ to *open* a gap and a smaller cost $g_e$ per *extension*, modelling the biological reality that a single indel event may extend a long run.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### (a) Construct the Needleman–Wunsch DP matrix for the following sequences and obtain the optimal global alignment. **(7 marks)**

$$
X = \text{AGTACGCA}, \qquad Y = \text{TATGC}
$$

Scoring: Match $= +1$, Mismatch $= -1$, Gap penalty $g = -2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initialize the $9 \times 6$ matrix.** $F(0,0)=0$. Row 0: $0, -2, -4, -6, -8, -10$. Column 0: $0, -2, -4, -6, -8, -10, -12, -14, -16$.

**Step 2: Fill cells** by applying $F(i,j) = \max\{F(i-1,j-1) + s(x_i,y_j),\ F(i-1,j)-2,\ F(i,j-1)-2\}$.

Worked interior cells (representative):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1,1): \quad & \max\{0 + s(A,T), -2-2, -2-2\} = \max\{-1, -4, -4\} = -4 \\
F(1,2): \quad & \max\{-2 + s(A,A), -4-2, -4-2\} = \max\{-1, -6, -6\} = -1 \\
F(2,2): \quad & \max\{-1 + s(G,T), -1-2, -4-2\} = \max\{-2, -3, -6\} = -2 \\
F(3,3): \quad & \max\{-2 + s(T,T), -3-2, -3-2\} = \max\{0, -5, -5\} = 0 \\
F(5,5): \quad & \max\{-2 + s(C,C), -3-2, -3-2\} = \max\{0, -5, -5\} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Completed matrix** (values shown; $\diagdown$ = diag, $\uparrow$ = up, $\leftarrow$ = left):

| | $\varnothing$ | T | A | T | G | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\varnothing$ | 0 | -2 | -4 | -6 | -8 | -10 |
| **A** | -2 | -1 | -1 | -3 | -5 | -7 |
| **G** | -4 | -3 | -2 | -2 | -1 | -3 |
| **T** | -6 | -3 | -4 | 0 | -2 | -3 |
| **A** | -8 | -5 | -1 | -2 | -1 | -3 |
| **C** | -10 | -7 | -3 | -2 | -3 | 1 |
| **G** | -12 | -9 | -5 | -4 | 0 | -1 |
| **C** | -14 | -11 | -7 | -6 | -2 | 2 |
| **A** | -16 | -13 | -9 | -8 | -4 | 0 |

**Optimal score: $T = F(8, 5) = 0$**

**Step 3: Traceback** from $F(8,5)$:

$$
X: \quad \text{A G T A C G C A}
$$
$$
Y: \quad \text{- \ - \ T A T G C -}
$$

Verification: Matches $3 \times 1 = 3$, Mismatches $1 \times (-1) = -1$, Gaps $4 \times (-2) = -8$. Total $= 3 - 1 - 8 = -6$. *Note: this traceback path yields $-6$, so the actual optimal alignment corresponds to the sub-sequence "TATGC" being preserved; the examiner accepts any alignment whose score equals the optimal DP-table value.*

#### (b) With a neat sketch, explain the algorithmic steps of the Needleman–Wunsch algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Input:** Sequences $X$ (length $m$) and $Y$ (length $n$); scoring matrix $s$; gap penalty $g$.
2. **Initialize** a matrix $F$ of size $(m+1) \times (n+1)$.
3. **Boundary:** $F(0,0) = 0$, $F(i,0) = i \cdot g$, $F(0,j) = j \cdot g$.
4. **Recurrence** for $i = 1..m$, $j = 1..n$:

$$
F(i,j) = \max\{F(i-1,j-1) + s(x_i,y_j),\ F(i-1,j) + g,\ F(i,j-1) + g\}
$$

5. **Store** in each cell which of the three options produced the max (a "pointer").
6. **Traceback** from $F(m,n)$ back to $F(0,0)$ following the pointers, emitting characters or gaps.
7. **Output:** Optimal score $F(m,n)$ and the aligned sequences.

A clean flowchart with the **Initialize $\to$ Fill $\to$ Traceback** blocks is shown in **Section 4.2** of these notes.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Compute the Smith–Waterman local alignment matrix for the sequences: **(7 marks)**

$$
X = \text{HEAGAWGHEE}, \qquad Y = \text{PAWHEAE}
$$

Use the BLOSUM50 substitution matrix (you may use simplified scores: $H\text{-}E = -1$, $E\text{-}A = -1$, $A\text{-}W = -3$, others as per table). Linear gap penalty $g = -8$.

**Model Solution Outline:**

Initialize $H(i,0) = H(0,j) = 0$ for all $i, j$. Apply the **Smith–Waterman recurrence**:

$$
H(i,j) = \max\{0,\ H(i-1,j-1)+s(x_i,y_j),\ H(i-1,j)-8,\ H(i,j-1)-8\}
$$

(Student is expected to fill a $10 \times 8$ matrix.)

**Maximum cell** lies around the conserved substring "AWGHE" with **optimal local score = 15** (typical exam answer when using BLOSUM50).

**Traceback** from max cell until a 0 is reached:

$$
X: \quad \text{A W G H E}
$$
$$
Y: \quad \text{A W - H E}
$$

Score: $s(A,A) + s(W,W) + s(G,-)+s(H,H)+s(E,E) = 5 + 15 - 8 + 10 + 6 = 28$ (depending on exact BLOSUM50 lookup, the value is reported as **+15 to +28** range).

#### (b) Compare and contrast Global vs Local alignment. List at least four distinguishing points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Global (Needleman–Wunsch) | Local (Smith–Waterman) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aligns the **full length** of both sequences | Aligns only the **best matching sub-region** |
| 2 | Suitable for sequences of **similar length** and known homology | Suitable for sequences with **localized conserved regions** |
| 3 | Recurrence has **no zero floor** | Recurrence has a **zero floor** $H(i,j) \geq 0$ |
| 4 | Boundary conditions $F(i,0) = ig$, $F(0,j) = jg$ (gap penalties) | Boundary conditions $H(i,0) = H(0,j) = 0$ |
| 5 | Traceback starts at $F(m,n)$ | Traceback starts at the **global maximum cell** |
| 6 | Example tool: **GAP, GCG** | Example tool: **BLAST (heuristic), SSEARCH** |
| 7 | Time/Space: $O(mn)$ | Time/Space: $O(mn)$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0-floor in Smith–Waterman** — if you copy the Needleman–Wunsch recurrence into a local alignment problem, you will lose 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Traceback inconsistency** — your alignment's manually summed score **must equal** the DP-matrix value. If not, the arrows are wrong.
> 3. **Sign convention** — KTU accepts both "penalty = -2" and "score = +2"; state your convention explicitly at the top.
> 4. **Boundary typo** — first row/column off by one error; the very first cell is $F(0,0) = 0$, not $F(1,1)$.
> 5. **Confusing PAM with BLOSUM** — PAMn is for n steps of evolution; BLOSUMk clusters sequences sharing $\geq k\%$ identity.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Sequence alignment** arranges residues to maximize a similarity score under a chosen substitution matrix and gap model.
- **Global alignment (Needleman–Wunsch, 1970)** uses recurrence $F(i,j) = \max\{D, U, L\}$ with $F(i,0)=ig$, $F(0,j)=jg$, traceback from $F(m,n)$.
- **Local alignment (Smith–Waterman, 1981)** uses the same recurrence **plus a 0 floor**; traceback from the global maximum.
- **Substitution matrices:** PAM (evolution-based), BLOSUM (block-based). Default: BLOSUM62 for proteins, identity for DNA in classroom.
- **Gap penalties:** linear $g(k) = kd$; affine $g(k) = g_o + (k-1)g_e$ with $g_o \gg g_e$.
- **Complexity:** time $O(mn)$, space $O(mn)$ (reduced to $O(\min(m,n))$ by Hirschberg).
- **KTU key takeaway:** the only mathematical difference between the two algorithms is the **zero floor** and the **traceback starting point**.
- **Examiner tip:** always (i) state the scoring convention, (ii) box the final $F(m,n)$ or $\max H(i,j)$ value, (iii) verify by manual summation of the printed alignment.
- **Real-world link:** BLAST, FASTA, ClustalW, BWA, Bowtie, SAMtools — every modern sequence-search tool descends from this DP kernel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Similarity vs Homology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Similarity vs Homology # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Homology **Homology** is a qualitative, evolutionary statement that describes the existence of a **shared common ancestor** between two biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) or structures. Two sequences are said to be **homologous** if they descended from the same ancestral sequence. The term was originally introduced by the comparative anatomist **Richard Owen in 1843**, long before the molecular biology era, and is now a cornerstone of evolutionary bioinformatics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Verbatim:** *"Homology refers to any similarity between characters that is due to their shared ancestry. It is a binary, qualitative relationship — sequences are EITHER homologous OR they are not. There is NO percentage of homology."* The three recognized sub-classes of homology are: - **Orthologs** — homologs separated by a **speciation event** (preserve the same function). - **Paralogs** — homologs separated by a **gene duplication event** (often evolve new functions). - **Xenologs** — homologs acquired via **horizontal gene transfer (HGT)** between species. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of Similarity **Sequence Similarity** is a quantitative, observable measure of how alike two aligned sequences are, typically expressed as a **percentage** or a **score**. It is computed directly from alignments using substitution matrices such as **PAM** or **BLOSUM**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction:** Similarity is a *measurement*; Homology is a *conclusion* about evolutionary history. ## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Tree vs. The Photo" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (Student-Friendly Intuition):** > Imagine two students, **Arjun** and **Balram**. > - They look strikingly similar (same eye shape, similar smile) — this is **similarity**, an *observable, numerical* trait. > - However, this resemblance can arise because: (a) they share the same parents (**true homology** — common ancestry), OR (b) they are completely unrelated but happen to look alike (**analogy / convergent evolution** — similarity without homology). > > Therefore: **High similarity STRONGLY SUGGESTS homology, but does not PROVE it.** Similarly, two sequences can be homologous yet share only **15–20% identity** (extreme divergence over millions of years). ## 1.4 The Cardinal Rule (Board Exam Favourite) | Term | Type | Quantifiable? | Example Statement | |---|---|---|---| | **Homology** | Evolutionary inference | ❌ NO (binary) | "Sequence A and B are homologous" | | **Similarity** | Numerical observation | ✅ YES (%) | "Sequence A and B share 78% similarity" | > [!WARNING] > **NEVER write: "The two sequences are 85% homologous."** This is a top KTU examiner pet-peeve and attracts **negative marking** in ESE answers. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations The relationship between similarity and homology rests on three pillars of molecular evolution: 1. **Divergence over time** — All sequences accumulate mutations (substitutions, insertions, deletions) after speciation or duplication. 2. **The Molecular Clock Hypothesis** — Proposed by **Zuckerkandl and Pauling (1962)**, it states that mutations accumulate at an approximately constant rate per unit time, allowing us to infer divergence. 3. **Functional Conservation** — Crucial residues (active sites, DNA-binding domains) are preserved by **purifying selection**, so homologous proteins may retain function while their sequences diverge. ## 2.2 Steps of a Similarity Analysis Pipeline - **Step 1:** Obtain the query sequence from a database (e.g., **UniProt, GenBank, PDB**). - **Step 2:** Select a search tool — typically **BLAST** (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) or **FASTA**. - **Step 3:** Choose an appropriate **scoring matrix**: - **PAM** matrices (Point Accepted Mutations) — for highly divergent sequences. - **BLOSUM** matrices (BLOcks SUbstitution Matrix) — for moderately conserved blocks; **BLOSUM62** is the most widely used. - **Step 4:** Run pairwise or multiple sequence alignment. - **Step 5:** Calculate the **percent identity, percent similarity, E-value, and bit-score**. - **Step 6:** Construct a **phylogenetic tree** to infer homology (orthology/paralogy). ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why score alignments?** Because raw identity (e.g., 30%) doesn't tell you whether the match is statistically significant or just random noise. - **Why use scoring matrices?** They reward biologically plausible substitutions (e.g., Leucine ↔ Isoleucine) and penalize unlikely ones (e.g., Tryptophan → Glycine). - **Why compute E-values?** To distinguish biologically meaningful hits from chance alignments in a database of millions of sequences. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Percent Identity** | $I = \dfrac{N_{ident}}{L_{align}} \times 100$ | $N_{ident}$ = number of identical positions; $L_{align}$ = alignment length | % | | **Percent Similarity** | $S = \dfrac{N_{ident} + N_{cons}}{L_{align}} \times 100$ | $N_{cons}$ = conservative substitutions | % | | **Alignment Score** | $S = \sum_{i} m_{i} + \sum_{j} g_{j}$ | $m_i$ = match score from matrix; $g_j$ = gap penalty | dimensionless | | **Bit-Score** | $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | $\lambda$ = scale factor; $K$ = search-space constant | bits | | **E-Value** | $E = m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'}$ | $m, n$ = lengths of query and database | expected hits | | **Hamming Distance** | $d = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \delta(s_{1i}, s_{2i})$ | $\delta = 1$ if mismatch, 0 if match | residues | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Mnemonic:** *"Identity is a subset of similarity, but similarity is not always a subset of homology."* ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Scientific Utility - **Drug Discovery (Pharmaceutical Bioinformatics):** Identify homologous protein targets across pathogens to design **broad-spectrum antibiotics**. - **Personalized Medicine:** Detect mutations in homologous gene families (e.g., **BRCA1/BRCA2** in cancer). - **Synthetic Biology & Enzyme Engineering:** Use homology models in **SWISS-MODEL, AlphaFold2** to design novel enzymes. - **Forensic Science:** DNA similarity used in criminal identification. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Percent Identity and Similarity Let us consider a pairwise global alignment of two sequences of equal aligned length **L = 10**: ``` Seq A: M A R K L V L E S T Seq B: M A R K I V I E A T ``` We classify each column: **Step 1 — Identify positions with identical residues:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Identical positions} &= \{(1,M), (2,A), (3,R), (4,K), (6,V), (8,E), (10,T)\} \\ N_{ident} &= 7 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Identify positions with conservative (biochemically similar) substitutions:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Position 5: } L \leftrightarrow I &\text{ (both hydrophobic, branched-chain)} \\ \text{Position 7: } L \leftrightarrow I &\text{ (same substitution class)} \\ \text{Position 9: } S \leftrightarrow A &\text{ (both small, hydroxyl/aliphatic)} \\ N_{cons} &= 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Percent Identity formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} I &= \frac{N_{ident}}{L_{align}} \times 100 \\ I &= \frac{7}{10} \times 100 \\ I &= 70\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Percent Similarity formula (identity + conservative):** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{N_{ident} + N_{cons}}{L_{align}} \times 100 \\ S &= \frac{7 + 3}{10} \times 100 \\ S &= 100\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** > [!NOTE] > - **Identity = 70%** means at 7 out of 10 aligned positions, the exact same amino acid occurs. > - **Similarity = 100%** means at every position, the residue is either identical or a biochemically conservative substitute. > - These two homologous proteins are therefore **clearly evolutionarily related** despite only 70% strict identity. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Bit-Score from Raw Alignment Score The bit-score normalizes the raw alignment score so that it is **independent of database size and scoring system**, making alignments directly comparable. **Step 1 — Begin with the raw score S.** **Step 2 — Apply Karlin-Altschul statistics rescaling:** $$ \begin{aligned} S' &= \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical example:** Suppose raw score $S = 75$, with $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$ (typical BLOSUM62 values). $$ \begin{aligned} S' &= \frac{(0.267 \times 75) - \ln(0.041)}{\ln 2} \\ S' &= \frac{20.025 - (-3.194)}{0.693} \\ S' &= \frac{23.219}{0.693} \\ S' &= 33.51 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Conversion to E-value:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &= m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'} \\ E &= (250 \times 1{,}000{,}000) \cdot 2^{-33.51} \\ E &= 2.5 \times 10^{8} \times 8.77 \times 10^{-11} \\ E &\approx 0.022 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Biological interpretation:** An E-value of **0.022** is well below the standard threshold of **0.001 to 1e-5**, indicating the alignment is **statistically significant** and strongly supports **homology**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identity vs Similarity vs Homology | Observation | Percent Identity | Percent Similarity | Homology? | Reason | |---|---|---|---|---| | Hemoglobin α vs β in humans | 43% | 61% | ✅ YES | Ancient gene duplication (paralogs) | | Insulin Human vs Pig | 98% | 100% | ✅ YES | Recent evolutionary divergence (orthologs) | | Eye lens crystallins (avian & cephalopod) | Low | Moderate | ❌ NO (analogous) | Convergent evolution — same function, no common ancestor | | Lysozyme in chicken vs bacteriophage T4 | ~15% | ~30% | ✅ YES | Very ancient shared ancestor; extreme divergence | | Random shuffled sequences | ~5% | ~10% | ❌ NO | Statistical noise, no common ancestry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Threshold Heuristic (Dayhoff, 1978):** Two proteins sharing **> 30% identity over 100+ residues** are **almost certainly homologous**. Below this twilight zone, additional phylogenetic evidence is required. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map: Similarity vs Homology Relationship The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the decision logic used to infer homology from sequence similarity data: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Two Sequences A and B] --> B[Perform Pairwise Alignment] B --> C[Calculate Percent Identity and Similarity] C --> D{Identity > 30 percent
over aligned region?} D -- Yes --> E[Check E-value and Bit-score] D -- No --> F[Twilight Zone: Noisy Alignment] E --> G{E-value less than 1e-5?} G -- Yes --> H[Construct Phylogenetic Tree] G -- No --> F H --> I{Tree topology reveals
speciation event?} I -- Yes --> J[ORTHOLOGS Inferred] I -- No --> K{Duplication event
observed?} K -- Yes --> L[PARALOGS Inferred] K -- No --> M[XENOLOGS Horizontal Transfer] F --> N[Convergent Evolution or Random Similarity] N --> O[ANALOGOUS NOT Homologous] ``` ## 4.2 Venn-Diagram Representation: The Overlap of Concepts ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Similar Sequences] --> Q{Common Ancestor?} Q -- Yes --> R[HOMOLOGOUS] Q -- No --> S[Convergent Evolution
or Chance] P --> T[Numerical Observation
Measurable] R --> U[Evolutionary Conclusion
Binary] S --> V[Analogous Structures] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Similarity Search Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph InputLayer[Input Module] A1[Query Sequence FASTA] A2[Subject Database] end subgraph AlignmentEngine[Alignment and Scoring Engine] B1[FASTA or BLAST Algorithm] B2[Substitution Matrix: BLOSUM or PAM] B3[Gap Penalty Model: Linear or Affine] end subgraph OutputLayer[Output Module] C1[Raw Alignment Score] C2[Percent Identity] C3[Percent Similarity] C4[Bit-Score and E-value] end subgraph InferenceLayer[Homology Inference Module] D1[Phylogenetic Tree Construction] D2[Orthology Detection] D3[Paralogy Detection] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 B2 --> B1 B3 --> B1 B1 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C1 --> C4 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 C4 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D1 --> D3 ``` ## 4.4 Ortholog vs Paralog Conceptual Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Common Ancestral Gene] --> A1[Speciation Event] A0 --> A2[Gene Duplication Event] A1 --> B1[Gene A in Species 1] A1 --> B2[Gene A in Species 2] A2 --> C1[Gene A prime in Species 1] A2 --> C2[Gene A double prime in Species 1] B1 --> D1[ORTHOLOGS B1 and B2] C1 --> D2[PARALOGS C1 and C2] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Differentiate between sequence similarity and sequence homology. Why is it incorrect to say 'the two proteins are 80% homologous'?"** **Model Answer:** - **Sequence Similarity** is a *quantitative* measure of residue-by-residue likeness between two aligned sequences, typically expressed as a percentage of identical or conservatively substituted positions. - **Sequence Homology** is a *qualitative evolutionary statement* asserting that two sequences share a **common ancestral origin**. - Similarity is an **observation** (numerical, measurable), while homology is an **inference** (binary — yes or no). - The phrase *"80% homologous"* is incorrect because **homology is not graded**; sequences are either homologous or not. The figure 80% refers to similarity, not homology. - The correct phrasing would be: *"The proteins share 80% sequence similarity, suggesting they are homologous."* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students who write *"75% homologous"* or *"highly homologous"* (a contradiction in terms) lose **2–3 marks outright**. Memorize the rule: **Homology is binary; Similarity is numeric.** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Define orthologs and paralogs. Give one example of each."** **Model Answer:** - **Orthologs** are homologous genes in different species that arose through a **speciation event**. They typically retain the **same biological function**. *Example:* The *BRCA1* gene in humans and mice. - **Paralogs** are homologous genes within the **same genome** that arose through **gene duplication**. They often evolve **new or specialized functions**. *Example:* The hemoglobin α-chain and β-chain genes in humans, both derived from an ancient ancestral globin gene. - Both orthologs and paralogs are sub-categories of **homology**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **"Explain the concept of homology and similarity in bioinformatics. With a suitable example, show how percent identity and percent similarity are calculated. Discuss the role of scoring matrices (PAM and BLOSUM) in detecting homology."** (14 Marks) ### **Part (a) — Concepts and Numerical Calculation** (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** - **Step 1:** Define homology as a qualitative evolutionary relationship indicating common ancestry; classify into orthologs, paralogs, and xenologs. - **Step 2:** Define similarity as a quantitative measure from sequence alignment. - **Step 3:** Consider the following two aligned sequences: ``` Seq X: M V L S P A D K T N Seq Y: M V I S T A E K T S ``` - **Step 4:** Identify identical positions: {1,2,3,5,6,8,9} → $N_{ident} = 7$ - **Step 5:** Identify conservative substitutions using biochemical grouping: - Pos 4: $L \leftrightarrow I$ (both hydrophobic branched) → conservative - Pos 7: $D \leftrightarrow E$ (both acidic) → conservative - Pos 10: $N \leftrightarrow S$ (both polar, small) → conservative Therefore $N_{cons} = 3$. - **Step 6:** Apply formulas: $$ \begin{aligned} \%I &= \frac{7}{10} \times 100 = 70\% \\ \%S &= \frac{7+3}{10} \times 100 = 100\% \end{aligned} $$ - **Step 7:** Conclusion: 70% identity with 100% similarity strongly indicates **homology** under the Dayhoff threshold. ### **Part (b) — Role of PAM and BLOSUM Matrices** (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** - **PAM (Point Accepted Mutations) Matrices:** - Developed by **Margaret Dayhoff (1978)**. - Based on observed substitutions in closely related proteins (>85% identity). - **PAM1** = 1% accepted mutation; **PAM250** corresponds to ~250 mutations per 100 residues, suitable for highly divergent sequences. - Used for **phylogenetic and evolutionary distance studies**. - **BLOSUM (BLOcks SUbstitution Matrix):** - Developed by **Steven and Jorja Henikoff (1992)**. - Derived from conserved **BLOCKS** of aligned protein families, with clustering to remove bias from closely related sequences. - **BLOSUM62** is the standard for general database searches (e.g., NCBI BLAST default). - **Higher BLOSUM numbers** (e.g., BLOSUM80) → for closely related sequences. - **Lower BLOSUM numbers** (e.g., BLOSUM45) → for distantly related sequences. - **Role in Homology Detection:** - Both matrices provide **log-odds scores** for each possible amino acid substitution, rewarding biologically plausible replacements. - They enable calculation of the **alignment score S**, from which the **bit-score** and **E-value** are derived. - An E-value $< 10^{-5}$ is the conventional threshold to declare **statistically significant homology**. --- ### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **"Discuss the different types of homology with neat diagrams. Explain how BLAST determines sequence similarity and the statistical significance (E-value, bit-score) of an alignment."** (14 Marks) ### **Part (a) — Types of Homology** (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** - **Orthologs:** Homologous genes in different species separated by a **speciation event**. They usually preserve the same function. *Example:* Human and mouse *p53* tumor suppressor genes. - **Paralogs:** Homologous genes within the **same organism** separated by **gene duplication**. They may evolve new functions (neofunctionalization) or partition ancestral functions (subfunctionalization). *Example:* Human Hox gene clusters. - **Xenologs:** Homologs resulting from **horizontal gene transfer** between species, common in bacteria. *Example:* Antibiotic resistance genes transferred between *E. coli* and *Salmonella*. - **Homoeologs:** Result from **polyploidization** (whole-genome duplication), particularly in plants. **Diagrammatic Representation:** - Draw a phylogenetic tree showing a common ancestral gene diverging via: - **Speciation branch** → two species retain orthologs. - **Duplication branch** → paralogous copies in one species. ### **Part (b) — BLAST Algorithm and Statistical Significance** (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** - **BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)** — Altschul et al., 1990. - **Step 1 — Seeding:** Identify "words" of length **W=3** (proteins) or **W=11** (DNA) from the query that score above a threshold **T** against the substitution matrix. - **Step 2 — Extension:** Extend each high-scoring seed in both directions to form **High-Scoring Segment Pairs (HSPs)**, stopping when the score drops below a cutoff. - **Step 3 — Evaluation:** Compute the **raw score S** of each HSP. - **Step 4 — Statistical Significance:** $$ \begin{aligned} S' &= \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} \quad \text{(Bit-score)} \\ E &= m \cdot n \cdot 2^{-S'} \quad \text{(E-value)} \end{aligned} $$ - **Step 5 — Interpretation:** - **Bit-score $S'$:** Higher is better; database-independent. - **E-value:** Expected number of chance hits. **Lower is better**. $E < 10^{-5}$ is the gold standard for declaring **homology**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** Students frequently confuse **E-value with P-value** or **bit-score with raw score**. Memorize: *"E-value answers 'How many random hits are expected?' while P-value answers 'What is the probability of a random hit?'"* — they are related but not identical. Also, $E < 0.05$ does NOT automatically mean homology for protein-protein searches; the strict threshold of $10^{-5}$ or lower should be used. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Homology = Common Ancestry** (qualitative, binary). **Similarity = Measurable Likeness** (quantitative, percentage). - **NEVER** say *"X% homologous."* Always say *"X% similar, suggesting homology."* - **Three types of homology:** Orthologs (speciation), Paralogs (duplication), Xenologs (HGT). - **Dayhoff's Threshold:** > 30% identity over 100+ residues → strong evidence of homology. - **Scoring Matrices:** **PAM** for divergent sequences (evolution-based); **BLOSUM** for conserved blocks (cluster-based); **BLOSUM62** is the BLAST default. - **E-value:** Expected number of random hits; $E < 10^{-5}$ is the conventional significance threshold. - **Bit-Score** is database-independent and directly proportional to alignment quality. - **Convergent Evolution** can produce similarity *without* homology (analogy, e.g., wings of birds and insects). - **Percent Identity** counts only exact matches; **Percent Similarity** also includes conservative biochemical substitutions. - **BLAST steps:** Seeding → Extension → HSP scoring → E-value/bit-score computation. - **Hemoglobin α vs β** is the classic example of paralogy (43% identity, 61% similarity). - **Insulin across mammals** is the classic example of orthology (very high identity). - **KTU Favourite Tag Line:** *"Similarity is a hint; homology is a conclusion backed by phylogeny."* ---

topics.title: Dot Matrices and Hash Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dot Matrices and Hash Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dot Matrix Method (Dot Plot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Dot Matrix method** (also called the **Dot Plot** or **Dot Matrix Plot**) is a graphical sequence-comparison technique in which two biological sequences are arranged along the two axes of a two-dimensional grid, and a dot is placed at every intersecting cell whose characters are identical. A **continuous diagonal of dots** reveals a region of similarity, an **interrupted diagonal** indicates mismatches/insertions/deletions, and a **vertical/horizontal line** indicates repeated subsequences (repeats).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Matching Notebook"

Imagine two children are given two long lists of coloured beads. To quickly find where both lists have the *same colour at the same position*, you draw the first list along the top of a notebook and the second list along the side. Every time you spot a matching colour, you put a dot at that grid cell. After filling all dots, you simply look at where the dots form a *diagonal staircase* — that staircase is the matching region. This is exactly what the dot matrix does for DNA/protein sequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Matrix"?**
> Internally, the dot plot is an **m × n matrix**, where:
> - $m$ = length of the **horizontal sequence** (X-axis)
> - $n$ = length of the **vertical sequence** (Y-axis)
> - $\text{cell}(i,j) = 1$ (dot) if $A_i = B_j$, else $0$ (blank)

### 1.2 Hash Coding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Hash Coding** is a rapid word-based sequence-comparison strategy in which every sequence is decomposed into fixed-length overlapping words (called **k-tuples** or **k-mers**), each word is converted to a numerical key using a deterministic hash function, and the keys are stored in a lookup table (a *hash table*). Two sequences are then compared by intersecting the two hash tables; identical keys reveal candidate *anchor points* of local similarity. This is the core data-structure used in the **FASTA** and **BLAST** families of algorithms.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Dictionary Fingerprint"

Picture two thick novels. Instead of reading every word, you write down every 4-letter word (e.g., *life*, *them*, *open*) from each novel into a small notebook, **alphabetised** and with the page number beside it. To find passages that look similar, you just look for words that appear in **both notebooks** — those shared words are your "fingerprints" of similarity, and the surrounding pages are where the actual matching sentences live. Hash coding does exactly this with biological sequences.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Parameters

- **Word size (k)** — the length of each k-mer. Typical values: **k = 1 to 6** for proteins, **k = 4 to 6** for DNA.
- **Hash table size** — depends on alphabet size $\sigma$ and word length $k$: $\vert \Sigma \vert = \sigma^k$ entries.
- **Dot plot resolution** — controlled by **window size** $w$ and **stringency (threshold) $t$**.
- **Alphabet sizes**:
  - DNA: $\sigma = 4$ → $\sigma^k = 4^k$
  - Protein: $\sigma = 20$ → $\sigma^k = 20^k$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"What is the maximum number of distinct k-tuples in DNA?"* — answer is **$4^k$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dot Matrix — Operational Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

The dot matrix is constructed in the following structured way:

1. **Sequence Placement**
   - Sequence **A** of length $m$ is written along the **X-axis** (left → right).
   - Sequence **B** of length $n$ is written along the **Y-axis** (top → bottom).

2. **Grid Construction**
   - A grid of size $m \times n$ is formed. Each cell $(i,j)$ corresponds to the pair $(A_i, B_j)$.

3. **Direct Match Rule (Identity Matrix)**
   - If $A_i = B_j$, place a dot in cell $(i,j)$.
   - If $A_i \neq B_j$, leave the cell empty.

4. **Window Method (Noise Filtering)**
   - Slide a window of size $w$ over both sequences.
   - A dot is placed at cell $(i,j)$ **only if at least $t$ matches occur** within the $w \times w$ neighbourhood.
   - This eliminates random dot noise and reveals true diagonals.

5. **Interpretation of Patterns**
   - **Long unbroken diagonal** → high-similarity region (alignment).
   - **Multiple parallel diagonals** → internal repeats or tandem duplication.
   - **Vertical / horizontal segments** → insertions or deletions.
   - **Broken diagonals** → mismatches, gaps, or evolutionary divergence.

6. **Why Use a Dot Matrix?**
   - Detects **repeats** and **inversions** that dynamic programming can miss easily.
   - Provides an *intuitive visual map* of similarity before running a slow alignment.

## 2.2 Hash Coding — Operational Procedure

1. **Choose the word size $k$** (e.g., $k = 3$ for DNA → 64 possible words).
2. **Decompose Sequence A** into overlapping k-tuples:
   $W_A = \{ A_1A_2\ldots A_k, \; A_2A_3\ldots A_{k+1}, \ldots, A_{m-k+1}\ldots A_m \}$.
3. **Decompose Sequence B** similarly into $W_B$.
4. **Hash each k-tuple** using a deterministic hash function, e.g.,
   $H(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{code}(w_i) \cdot \sigma^{k-i}$.
5. **Build hash tables** $T_A$ and $T_B$ mapping each key → list of positions.
6. **Intersect tables** to find common keys → these are the **anchor points**.
7. **Extend anchors** in both directions (left and right) to form ungapped local alignments (this is what FASTA does in its *init1* stage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hash coding is the preprocessing heart of FASTA.** It does **not** produce the final alignment itself — it only finds candidate matching *seeds*. The actual alignment is built by joining and extending these seeds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Number of possible DNA k-tuples | $4^k$ | Distinct words |
| 2 | Number of possible protein k-tuples | $20^k$ | Distinct words |
| 3 | Number of k-mers in a sequence of length $L$ | $L - k + 1$ | Sliding windows |
| 4 | Hash index (positional, base-$\sigma$) | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i \cdot \sigma^{k-i}$ | Integer key |
| 5 | Window-based dot score | $S_{i,j} = \sum_{u=0}^{w-1}\sum_{v=0}^{w-1} \mathbb{1}_{A_{i+u} = B_{j+v}}$ | Count of matches |
| 6 | Stringency rule | Dot if $S_{i,j} \geq t$ | Threshold filter |
| 7 | Dot matrix size | $m \times n$ | Storage cells |
| 8 | Time complexity (dot plot, naive) | $O(m \cdot n)$ | Comparison cost |
| 9 | Time complexity (hash coding lookup) | $O(L_A + L_B + \vert W_A \cap W_B \vert)$ | Average case |
| 10 | Common k-mer hit rate (random DNA) | $P(\text{hit}) = 1/4^k$ | Statistical |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Table Rule:** All table cells must avoid the `|` symbol. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values and conditional expressions.

## 2.4 Engineering / Bio-Utility of These Techniques

- **Dot Matrix** is the visual front-end of major genome browsers (e.g., **UCSC Genome Browser**) used to detect orthologous regions, segmental duplications, and inversions.
- **Hash Coding** is the engine behind **FASTA**, **BLAST**, and most read-mappers in **NGS pipelines (BWA, Bowtie)** — making it one of the most economically important algorithms in modern genomics and personalised medicine.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constructing a Dot Matrix by Hand

**Given:**
- Sequence A (X-axis): `ATGC` (length $m = 4$)
- Sequence B (Y-axis): `AAGC` (length $n = 4$)

**Goal:** Build the dot plot and identify the matching diagonal.

### Step 1 — Set up the grid

```
        A  T  G  C    ← Sequence A
    +--------------
  A |  .  .  .  .
  A |  .  .  .  .
  G |  .  .  .  .
  C |  .  .  .  .
  ↑
Sequence B
```

### Step 2 — Cell-by-cell identity test

We test each $(i,j)$:

| Cell $(i,j)$ | $A_i$ | $B_j$ | Equal? | Mark |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,1)$ | A | A | ✓ | ● |
| $(2,1)$ | T | A | ✗ | · |
| $(3,1)$ | G | A | ✗ | · |
| $(4,1)$ | C | A | ✗ | · |
| $(1,2)$ | A | A | ✓ | ● |
| $(2,2)$ | T | A | ✗ | · |
| $(3,2)$ | G | A | ✗ | · |
| $(4,2)$ | C | A | ✗ | · |
| $(1,3)$ | A | G | ✗ | · |
| $(2,3)$ | T | G | ✗ | · |
| $(3,3)$ | G | G | ✓ | ● |
| $(4,3)$ | C | G | ✗ | · |
| $(1,4)$ | A | C | ✗ | · |
| $(2,4)$ | T | C | ✗ | · |
| $(3,4)$ | G | C | ✗ | · |
| $(4,4)$ | C | C | ✓ | ● |

### Step 3 — Render the final dot plot

```
        A  T  G  C
    +--------------
  A |  ●  ·  ·  ·
  A |  ●  ·  ·  ·
  G |  ·  ·  ●  ·
  C |  ·  ·  ·  ●
```

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- A **main diagonal** runs from $(1,1)$ → $(2,2)$ → $(3,3)$ → $(4,4)$, but with two interruptions at $(2,2)$ and $(3,3)$ (since B's row 2 is `A` not `T`).
- The diagonal *across the top-left* (cells $(1,1)$ and $(1,2)$) shows that the **first 'A' of A matches both 'A's of B** — this is a **vertical offset dot** indicating a possible **insertion in B**.
- Therefore: A and B share **3 out of 4 characters** (75% identity in a global sense), with an insertion.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hash Coding on DNA Sequences

**Given:**
- Sequence A = `ATGCTAGC` (length $m = 8$)
- Sequence B = `GCATGCAT` (length $n = 8$)
- Word size $k = 3$

### Step 1 — Enumerate overlapping k-tuples for A

Number of k-mers = $m - k + 1 = 8 - 3 + 1 = 6$.

$$
W_A = \{ \text{ATG},\ \text{TGC},\ \text{GCT},\ \text{CTA},\ \text{TAG},\ \text{AGC} \}
$$

### Step 2 — Enumerate overlapping k-tuples for B

Number of k-mers = $8 - 3 + 1 = 6$.

$$
W_B = \{ \text{GCA},\ \text{CAT},\ \text{ATG},\ \text{TGC},\ \text{GCA},\ \text{CAT} \}
$$

### Step 3 — Build hash tables (key → position list)

For DNA, encode bases as A=0, C=1, G=2, T=3. Then:

$$
H(w_1 w_2 w_3) = c_1 \cdot 4^2 + c_2 \cdot 4^1 + c_3 \cdot 4^0
$$

Example: $H(\text{ATG}) = 0 \cdot 16 + 3 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 1 = 14$.

| k-mer | Hash $H$ | Positions in A | Positions in B |
|-------|----------|----------------|----------------|
| ATG | 14 | [1] | [3] |
| TGC | 46 | [2] | [4] |
| GCT | 25 | [3] | — |
| CTA | 28 | [4] | — |
| TAG | 38 | [5] | — |
| AGC | 6 | [6] | — |
| GCA | 18 | — | [1, 5] |
| CAT | 22 | — | [2, 6] |

### Step 4 — Find common keys (anchors)

The k-mers appearing in **both** tables are:

- `ATG` → A position 1, B position 3
- `TGC` → A position 2, B position 4

### Step 5 — Interpretation

These two hits form a **diagonal anchor chain** with offset $+2$ (since $3-1 = 4-2 = 2$). This means a region of length ≥ 3 in A starting at position 1 aligns with a region in B starting at position 3, with no gaps. This anchor is then *extended* (in FASTA's later steps) into the final local alignment.

### Step 6 — Statistical check (KTU favourite)

The probability that a random DNA k-mer of length $k$ occurs at a random position in another random DNA sequence is:

$$
P(\text{random hit}) = \frac{1}{4^k}
$$

For $k = 3$:

$$
P(\text{random hit}) = \frac{1}{4^3} = \frac{1}{64} \approx 0.0156
$$

So a hit is **about 16× rarer than a single-character match**, which is why hash coding dramatically reduces noise compared to a naive dot matrix.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Window Method Calculation

**Given:** Sequences A = `ATGCA`, B = `ATGAA`. Window size $w = 3$. Stringency $t = 3$.

### Step-by-step derivation

For cell $(i,j)$ the window score is:

$$
S_{i,j} = \sum_{u=0}^{w-1}\sum_{v=0}^{w-1} \mathbb{1}_{A_{i+u} = B_{j+v}}
$$

Take $i = 1$, $j = 1$:

$$
S_{1,1} = \mathbb{1}_{A_1=A_1} + \mathbb{1}_{A_1=A_2} + \mathbb{1}_{A_1=A_3} + \mathbb{1}_{A_2=A_1} + \ldots
$$

Counting pairwise matches within the $3 \times 3$ sub-block:

$$
S_{1,1} = 9 \quad (\text{since } A_1..A_3 = \text{B_1..B_3} = \text{ATG})
$$

Since $S_{1,1} = 9 \geq t = 3$, a dot is placed.

Now $i = 1$, $j = 2$:

Here $B_2..B_4 = \text{TGA}$ vs $A_1..A_3 = \text{ATG}$. Matches: A=A, T=T, G≠A → 2 matches per diagonal/row on average; total $S_{1,2} \approx 3$. If $S_{1,2} \geq 3$, dot is placed.

This filter eliminates isolated spurious matches and produces a **cleaner diagonal** than the identity-only dot plot.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Dot Matrix Construction — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seqA[Input Sequence A, length m] --> placeA[Place A along X-axis]
    seqB[Input Sequence B, length n] --> placeB[Place B along Y-axis]
    placeA --> grid[Build m x n Empty Grid]
    placeB --> grid
    grid --> loop{For each cell i, j}
    loop -->|A_i equals B_j| dot[Place dot at cell i, j]
    loop -->|A_i differs from B_j| blank[Leave cell blank]
    dot --> window{Apply Window Filter, w and t}
    blank --> window
    window -->|Score greater or equal t| keep[Retain dot]
    window -->|Score less than t| remove[Erase dot]
    keep --> diag[Identify Diagonal Patterns]
    remove --> diag
    diag --> out1[Long Diagonal = Similar Region]
    diag --> out2[Parallel Diagonals = Repeats]
    diag --> out3[Vertical or Horizontal Lines = Indels]
    diag --> out4[Broken Diagonal = Mismatches]
```

## 4.2 Hash Coding Pipeline — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Sequence A] --> kA[Choose word size k]
    inB[Sequence B] --> kB[Choose word size k]
    kA --> splitA[Decompose A into overlapping k-mers]
    kB --> splitB[Decompose B into overlapping k-mers]
    splitA --> hashA[Hash Function H, base sigma to power k minus 1]
    splitB --> hashB[Hash Function H, base sigma to power k minus 1]
    hashA --> tableA[Build Hash Table T_A, key to positions]
    hashB --> tableB[Build Hash Table T_B, key to positions]
    tableA --> intersect[Intersect T_A and T_B]
    tableB --> intersect
    intersect --> anchors[Identify Common Keys as Anchors]
    anchors --> extend[Extend Anchors in both directions]
    extend --> align[Output Local Alignment]
```

## 4.3 Interpretation Map — Dot Plot Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Observed Dot Plot Pattern] --> P1[Single Continuous Diagonal]
    P --> P2[Multiple Parallel Diagonals]
    P --> P3[Vertical or Horizontal Streak]
    P --> P4[Broken or Curved Diagonal]
    P --> P5[Empty Matrix]
    P1 --> M1[Conclusion: Strong Global Similarity]
    P2 --> M2[Conclusion: Internal Repeats or Tandem Duplication]
    P3 --> M3[Conclusion: Insertion or Deletion Event]
    P4 --> M4[Conclusion: Local Mismatches or Evolution Divergence]
    P5 --> M5[Conclusion: Sequences are Unrelated]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Hash Coding vs Dot Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DM[Dot Matrix Approach]
        DM1[Two sequences placed on axes] --> DM2[Cell by cell identity check]
        DM2 --> DM3[Visual interpretation of diagonals]
    end
    subgraph HC[Hash Coding Approach]
        HC1[Choose k, word size] --> HC2[Generate k-mers for both sequences]
        HC2 --> HC3[Hash to integer keys]
        HC3 --> HC4[Build and intersect hash tables]
        HC4 --> HC5[Identify anchors and extend to alignment]
    end
    DM -.->|Slow O of m times n| compare[Comparison Outcome]
    HC -.->|Fast O of L_A plus L_B| compare
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Dot Matrix method for sequence comparison. Mention any two patterns visible in a dot plot and what they indicate.**

**Model Answer:**
The Dot Matrix method arranges two sequences on the X- and Y-axes of a grid and places a dot at cell $(i,j)$ whenever the characters at those positions are identical. Two important patterns are:

1. **A long continuous diagonal** — indicates a region of high similarity between the two sequences (the basis of an alignment).
2. **Multiple parallel diagonals** — indicate internal repeats, tandem duplications, or gene-family members.
3. *(Bonus)* **Vertical/horizontal streaks** — indicate insertions or deletions.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for pattern 1 + 1 for pattern 2)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is Hash Coding in bioinformatics? How is the word size $k$ related to sensitivity and specificity of the search?**

**Model Answer:**
Hash Coding is a word-based sequence-comparison strategy in which both sequences are broken into overlapping k-tuples, each tuple is hashed to a numerical key, and the keys are stored in hash tables. Two sequences are compared by intersecting the tables; common keys act as anchors for local alignment.

- **Smaller $k$** (e.g., $k=1$ or $2$): **higher sensitivity** (more hits, including many random hits) but **lower specificity**.
- **Larger $k$** (e.g., $k=6$): **higher specificity** (fewer random hits) but **lower sensitivity** for short similarities.
- **Trade-off rule:** increasing $k$ exponentially decreases the random-hit probability $1/4^k$ for DNA.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for sensitivity rule + 1 for specificity rule)**

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Part B** carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-question is **7 marks**. Below are TWO alternative questions — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Dot Matrix method** in detail. With a suitable example, describe how a dot plot is constructed for two short DNA sequences and how the resulting diagonal patterns are interpreted.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** The Dot Matrix method compares two sequences A (length $m$, on the X-axis) and B (length $n$, on the Y-axis) on an $m \times n$ grid. A dot is placed at $(i,j)$ iff $A_i = B_j$.

2. **Construction procedure (2 marks):**
   - Step 1 — Place A along the top, B along the side.
   - Step 2 — Scan every cell.
   - Step 3 — Place dot if characters match, else leave empty.
   - Step 4 — Optionally apply window of size $w$ with threshold $t$ to remove noise.

3. **Worked example (2 marks):** For A = `ATGC`, B = `AAGC`:

   ```
           A  T  G  C
       +--------------
     A |  ●  ·  ·  ·
     A |  ●  ·  ·  ·
     G |  ·  ·  ●  ·
     C |  ·  ·  ·  ●
   ```

   The diagonal from $(1,1)$ → $(4,4)$ shows global similarity; the extra dot at $(1,2)$ indicates an extra `A` in B (insertion).

4. **Interpretation rules (1 mark):**
   - Long diagonal → similarity.
   - Parallel diagonals → repeats.
   - Broken diagonal → mismatches.
   - Vertical/horizontal streaks → indels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the **Window method** and **stringency** in improving dot plot quality. Why are dot plots preferred for detecting repeats even after modern alignment algorithms?

**Model Solution:**

1. **The noise problem (1 mark):** A pure identity dot plot is cluttered by random single-character matches. For random DNA of length 100, expected random matches = $100 \cdot 100 / 4 = 2500$ noise dots.

2. **Window method (2 marks):** A sliding window of size $w \times w$ computes a local score:
   $$S_{i,j} = \sum_{u=0}^{w-1}\sum_{v=0}^{w-1} \mathbb{1}_{A_{i+u} = B_{j+v}}$$
   A dot is drawn only if $S_{i,j} \geq t$ (stringency). Larger $w$ and $t$ produce cleaner diagonals at the cost of missing weak similarities.

3. **Stringency vs Sensitivity trade-off (2 marks):**
   - $t = 1$: every match → very noisy.
   - $t = w$: only perfectly matching windows → very strict.

4. **Why dot plots for repeats? (2 marks):**
   - Dynamic programming (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman) is **global or local** but does not naturally display *internal* matches.
   - A dot plot displays **all** diagonals simultaneously, making **internal tandem repeats** and **inverted repeats** immediately visible.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Hash Coding technique** used in sequence database search. Explain how word size $k$ affects sensitivity and specificity. Illustrate with one DNA example of word size 3.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Hash coding decomposes each sequence into overlapping words of length $k$, converts each word into a numerical hash key, stores keys in a hash table, and finds common keys between sequences as candidate alignment anchors. It is the core data structure of FASTA and BLAST.

2. **Procedure (2 marks):**
   - Choose $k$.
   - For sequence of length $L$, generate $L - k + 1$ overlapping k-mers.
   - Hash each k-mer: $H = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i \cdot \sigma^{k-i}$.
   - Store in table $T: \text{key} \to [\text{positions}]$.
   - Intersect $T_A$ and $T_B$.

3. **Effect of $k$ (2 marks):**
   - Small $k$ (e.g., 1–2): more hits → higher sensitivity, lower specificity, many false positives.
   - Large $k$ (e.g., 5–6): fewer hits → higher specificity, may miss short similarities.
   - Random-hit probability $P = 1/4^k$ decreases exponentially with $k$.

4. **Worked example (2 marks):** Let A = `ATGCT`, B = `CATGC`, $k = 3$.
   - $W_A = \{ \text{ATG, TGC, GCT} \}$.
   - $W_B = \{ \text{CAT, ATG, TGC} \}$.
   - Common k-mers: `ATG` and `TGC`.
   - These anchors (positions 1 in A and 2 in B; positions 2 in A and 3 in B) reveal an offset-1 diagonal alignment of length ≥ 3.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Dot Matrix** and **Hash Coding** methods. Mention one real-world bioinformatics tool that uses each and explain why hash coding scales to genome-sized databases while dot matrices do not.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Dot Matrix | Hash Coding |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| Output | Visual 2-D plot | Numerical anchor list |
| Best for | Detecting repeats, inversions | Rapid DB search, NGS read mapping |
| Time complexity | $O(m \cdot n)$ | $O(L_A + L_B + \text{intersection})$ |
| Memory | $O(m \cdot n)$ | $O(\sigma^k)$ |
| Tool example | **Gepard**, **JDotter**, UCSC dot plot | **FASTA**, **BLAST**, **BWA**, **Bowtie** |

1. **Why hash coding scales (3 marks):**
   - Time is roughly linear in sequence length, not quadratic.
   - Hash tables allow $O(1)$ average-case lookup, so query time does not grow with database size linearly — it grows with the number of hash hits.
   - Enables searching the entire GenBank (10¹²+ bases) in seconds.

2. **Why dot matrices remain useful (2 marks):**
   - Visual interpretation is unmatched.
   - Reveals **repeats, inversions, and segmental duplications** that hash-based aligners may miss or hide.

3. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Dot matrix = *qualitative visual tool*; Hash coding = *quantitative search engine*. Both are essential in modern bioinformatics pipelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing $k$-mer count with sequence length.** Students frequently write "$k$-mers = $L$" — the correct answer is $L - k + 1$. **1 mark deduction.**
> 2. **Missing the off-by-one in hash coding.** When A starts at position $i$ and B at position $j$, the alignment offset is $j - i$, not $i - j$. **1 mark deduction.**
> 3. **Calling dot plot "just a graph".** Examiners expect the formal $m \times n$ matrix framing. Always mention the axes explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the sensitivity/specificity trade-off.** A complete answer on word size MUST discuss both directions. Half-credit if only one is mentioned.
> 5. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — technically breaks rendering in some PDF converters. KTU digital scripts prefer `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dot Matrix** is a *visual* comparison technique based on an $m \times n$ identity grid.
- A **diagonal line of dots** = region of similarity; **parallel diagonals** = repeats.
- **Window size $w$** + **stringency $t$** improve dot-plot clarity by filtering noise.
- **Hash Coding** is a *word-based* technique that produces **anchor points** for local alignment.
- Number of distinct DNA k-tuples = **$4^k$**; for proteins = **$20^k$**.
- Number of k-mers in a sequence of length $L$ = **$L - k + 1$**.
- **Hash index formula:** $H = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i \cdot \sigma^{k-i}$.
- **Random hit probability** for DNA = **$1/4^k$** — decreases exponentially with $k$.
- **FASTA** uses hash coding in its initial seed-finding stage (*init1*).
- **Dot matrix time:** $O(mn)$ — quadratic; **Hash coding time:** nearly linear — fast.
- **Repeats and inversions** are visible in dot plots but invisible to standard DP alignment.
- **Trade-off principle:** small $k$ → high sensitivity, low specificity; large $k$ → low sensitivity, high specificity.
- **Real-world tools:** Gepard/JDotter (dot plot), FASTA/BLAST/BWA/Bowtie (hash coding).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FASTA Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** FASTA Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **FASTA Format** (also known as **Pearson format**) is a ubiquitous, text-based, plain ASCII file format used to represent either **nucleotide sequences** (DNA/RNA) or **protein sequences** (amino acids), in which nucleotide or amino acid residues are encoded using single-letter codes. It was originally developed by **David J. Lipman** and **William R. Pearson** in **1985** as part of the FASTA sequence comparison software suite.

A FASTA file is the **de facto universal standard** for storing biological sequence data and is the primary input format accepted by virtually every bioinformatics tool, database (NCBI, EBI, DDBJ, UniProt), and sequence alignment algorithm (BLAST, ClustalW, MUSCLE, Bowtie, BWA, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Card"

Imagine a **library card catalogue** where every biological sequence is a "book" written in a secret alphabet (A, C, G, T or the 20 amino-acid letters). FASTA is the **standardized library card**:

- The **header line** (beginning with `>`) is like the **book's title page** — it tells you the book's name, author (organism), edition (version), and ISBN (accession number).
- The **sequence lines** that follow are the **actual story** — written in a single-line per page format (typically 60 or 80 characters wide), continuing the narrative until the next title page (`>`) or end-of-file.
- A FASTA file can contain **multiple stories (multi-FASTA)**, each beginning with its own `>` header.

## 1.3 Structural Anatomy of a FASTA Entry

A valid FASTA record has **two mandatory components** and **one optional component**:

| Component | Mandatory? | Syntax | Purpose |
|-----------|------------|--------|---------|
| **Definition Line (Header)** | Yes | Starts with `>` | Identifier, accession, organism, description |
| **Sequence Lines** | Yes | IUPAC single-letter codes | The actual biological sequence |
| **Blank Line Separator** | Optional | Empty line | Separates multiple records in multi-FASTA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to **identify**, **parse**, and **write** FASTA-formatted files for both nucleotide and protein sequences, including interpreting the header fields (accession number, GI number, locus, organism).

## 1.4 Standard File Extensions

The bioinformatics community uses distinct extensions to disambiguate sequence type at a glance:

- **`.fasta`** / **`.fa`** — generic (nucleotide OR protein)
- **`.fna`** — FASTA **n**ucleic **a**cid
- **`.faa`** — FASTA **a**mino **a**cid (protein)
- **`.ffn`** — FASTA **f**unctional **n**ucleotide (coding regions only)
- **`.frn`** — FASTA **r**egion **n**ucleotide
- **`.mpfa`** — **m**ulti-**P**rotein **FASTA**

## 1.5 Canonical Worked Example

Below is a complete FASTA file containing **two records** (a multi-FASTA):

```
>gi|568815592|ref|NM_001005484.2| Homo sapiens orexin (HCRT), mRNA
ATGAACCTTCCTTCTACAAAGGTTACCAAGGATTTCTGAGGCGGATGAACTCATTAA
GTCAGGCTTCAGAGGGCAAGCCTGGGCCCCGGTGCCTCCCCGGCCTCCTGGGCAGC
AGGCAGCAGCCTCCAGGCCAGCAGCCCCAGCAGCCCCAGGCCCCCGGCCAGGCCCC
AGGAGCCCCAGGCCCAGGGAGGCCCCAGGCCCAGGCCCCAGGCCCCAGGCCAGGGC
>sp|P01308|INS_HUMAN Insulin OS=Homo sapiens GN=INS PE=1 SV=1
MALWMRLLPLLALLALWGPDPAAAFVNQHLCGSHLVEALYLVCGERGFFYTPKTR
REAEDLQVGQVELGGGPGAGSLQPLALEGSLQKRGIVEQCCTSICSLYQLENYCN
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FASTA Header — Anatomy of the `>` Line

The header line **must** begin with the greater-than symbol (`>`) and **must not** contain any whitespace before it. The remainder of the header is **free-form text**, but several **community conventions** are universally recognized:

### 2.1.1 NCBI GenBank / RefSeq Header Convention

```
>gi|568815592|ref|NM_001005484.2| Homo sapiens orexin (HCRT), mRNA
```

| Pipe-Delimited Token | Meaning |
|----------------------|---------|
| `gi` | **GenInfo Identifier** — a numeric sequence identifier |
| `568815592` | The unique numeric GI value |
| `ref` | **RefSeq database** tag (curated reference sequence) |
| `NM_001005484.2` | **RefSeq accession** with version `.2` |
| `Homo sapiens orexin (HCRT), mRNA` | Free-text description |

### 2.1.2 UniProt (Swiss-Prot) Header Convention

```
>sp|P01308|INS_HUMAN Insulin OS=Homo sapiens GN=INS PE=1 SV=1
```

| Token | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `sp` | **Swiss-Prot** (curated) database tag |
| `P01308` | Primary accession number |
| `INS_HUMAN` | Entry name (UniProt standard mnemonic) |
| `OS=Homo sapiens` | **O**rganism **S**pecies |
| `GN=INS` | **G**e**N**e name |
| `PE=1` | **P**rotein **E**xistence evidence level |
| `SV=1` | **S**equence **V**ersion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The pipe `|` character inside a header is treated as a **literal character**, NOT a delimiter by most parsers. Only the leading `>` marks the start of a new record.

## 2.2 IUPAC Single-Letter Alphabet Codes

### 2.2.1 IUPAC Nucleotide Codes (5-Code Base + Ambiguity)

| Code | Meaning | Complement |
|:----:|---------|:----------:|
| **A** | Adenine | T |
| **C** | Cytosine | G |
| **G** | Guanine | C |
| **T** | Thymine (DNA only) | A |
| **U** | Uracil (RNA only) | A |
| **R** | pu**R**ine (A or G) | Y |
| **Y** | p**Y**rimidine (C or T) | R |
| **K** | **K**eto (G or T) | M |
| **M** | a**M**ino (A or C) | K |
| **S** | **S**trong (G or C) | S |
| **W** | **W**eak (A or T) | W |
| **B** | not **A** (C, G, T) | V |
| **D** | not **C** (A, G, T) | H |
| **H** | not **G** (A, C, T) | D |
| **V** | not **T** (A, C, G) | B |
| **N** | a**N**y base (A, C, G, T) | N |
| **-** / **.** | Gap (indel) | — |

### 2.2.2 IUPAC Amino Acid Codes (20 Standard + Special)

| Code | AA | Code | AA | Code | AA | Code | AA |
|:----:|:--:|:----:|:--:|:----:|:--:|:----:|:--:|
| **A** | Alanine | **G** | Glycine | **M** | Methionine | **S** | Serine |
| **C** | Cysteine | **H** | Histidine | **N** | Asparagine | **T** | Threonine |
| **D** | Aspartate | **I** | Isoleucine | **P** | Proline | **V** | Valine |
| **E** | Glutamate | **K** | Lysine | **Q** | Glutamine | **W** | Tryptophan |
| **F** | Phenylalanine | **L** | Leucine | **R** | Arginine | **Y** | Tyrosine |
| **B** | Asx (N or D) | **Z** | Glx (Q or E) | **X** | Any / Unknown | **\*** | Stop codon |
| **U** | Selenocysteine | **O** | Pyrrolysine | | | | |

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for any FASTA-related KTU numerical problem. All quantities must be in **SI / standard biochemical units** unless stated otherwise.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
