| # | Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_{ab}$ | $\dfrac{A_{ab}}{\sum_{i,j} A_{ij}}$ | Joint substitution probability | dimensionless |
| 2 | $p_a$ | $\sum_{j} q_{aj}$ | Background frequency of residue $a$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $e_{ab}$ | $p_a \cdot p_b$ | Random substitution probability | dimensionless |
| 4 | $O_{ab}$ | $\dfrac{q_{ab}}{p_a \, p_b}$ | Odds ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $S_{ab}$ | $\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{q_{ab}}{p_a \, p_b}\right)$ | Log-odds substitution score | bits |
| 6 | $M_n$ | $M^n$ | PAM $n$ mutation matrix (matrix power) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S^{(n)}_{ab}$ | $10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{M^n_{ab}}{p_b}\right)$ | PAM $n$ scoring matrix entry | $\log_{10}$ |
| 8 | $W_i$ | $1/k$ | Weight of cluster representative (BLOSUM) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $H$ | $-\sum_i f_i \log_2 f_i$ | Shannon entropy of a column | bits |
| 10 | $R$ | $H_{\max} - H_{obs} = \log_2 20 - H_{obs}$ | Relative entropy (information content) | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "derive" a substitution matrix entry, you must show the *chain* $A_{ab} \rightarrow q_{ab} \rightarrow p_a, p_b \rightarrow O_{ab} \rightarrow S_{ab}$. Skipping the $q_{ab}$ step is a common 2-mark deduction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Scientific Utility

- **Genome annotation pipelines** (BLAST, DIAMOND, HMMER) use BLOSUM/PAM scores to detect distantly related genes.
- **Phylogenetic tree construction** uses log-odds distances to weight evolutionary steps.
- **Drug discovery** relies on substitution matrices to identify conserved functional residues that cannot be mutated without loss of function.
- **Vaccine design** — matrices help identify conserved epitopes across viral strains (e.g., influenza HA, SARS-CoV-2 spike).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Counts to the Log-Odds Score

We start with a simple worked numerical example. Suppose we have aligned 1,000 homologous sequence pairs and observed the following raw substitution counts $A_{ab}$ (alphabet restricted to **A, L, V** for clarity):

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} A \to A & A \to L & A \to V \\ L \to A & L \to L & L \to V \\ V \to A & V \to L & V \to V \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 400 & 30 & 20 \\ 20 & 350 & 40 \\ 30 & 35 & 75 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> Each cell gives the number of times the row residue was replaced by the column residue.

### Step 3.1.1 — Compute the total number of substitutions
$$
T = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} = 400 + 30 + 20 + 20 + 350 + 40 + 30 + 35 + 75 = 1000
$$

### Step 3.1.2 — Compute joint substitution frequencies $q_{ab}$
$$
q_{ab} = \frac{A_{ab}}{T}
$$

Applying this to every cell:

$$
q = \frac{1}{1000} \begin{pmatrix} 400 & 30 & 20 \\ 20 & 350 & 40 \\ 30 & 35 & 75 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.400 & 0.030 & 0.020 \\ 0.020 & 0.350 & 0.040 \\ 0.030 & 0.035 & 0.075 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3.1.3 — Compute marginal (background) frequencies $p_a$
Sum each row of $q$:

$$
\begin{aligned} p_A &= 0.400 + 0.030 + 0.020 = 0.450 \\ p_L &= 0.020 + 0.350 + 0.040 = 0.410 \\ p_V &= 0.030 + 0.035 + 0.075 = 0.140 \end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** $p_A + p_L + p_V = 0.450 + 0.410 + 0.140 = 1.000$ ✓

### Step 3.1.4 — Compute expected random substitution probabilities $e_{ab} = p_a p_b$
For example:
$$
\begin{aligned} e_{AL} &= p_A \cdot p_L = 0.450 \times 0.410 = 0.1845 \\ e_{AV} &= p_A \cdot p_V = 0.450 \times 0.140 = 0.0630 \\ e_{LV} &= p_L \cdot p_V = 0.410 \times 0.140 = 0.0574 \end{aligned}
$$

> Note that for symmetric scoring, $e_{ab} = e_{ba}$.

### Step 3.1.5 — Compute the Odds Ratio $O_{ab}$
$$
O_{ab} = \frac{q_{ab}}{p_a p_b}
$$

For $A \to L$:
$$
O_{AL} = \frac{0.030}{0.1845} \approx 0.1626
$$

For $A \to A$ (self-substitution / identity, often excluded from log-odds computation, but shown for completeness):
$$
O_{AA} = \frac{0.400}{0.450 \times 0.450} = \frac{0.400}{0.2025} \approx 1.9753
$$

### Step 3.1.6 — Take the binary logarithm
$$
S_{ab} = \log_2(O_{ab})
$$

For $A \to L$:
$$
S_{AL} = \log_2(0.1626) = \frac{\ln(0.1626)}{\ln(2)} \approx \frac{-1.817}{-0.693} \approx -2.62 \text{ bits}
$$

For $A \to A$ (if included):
$$
S_{AA} = \log_2(1.9753) \approx 0.98 \text{ bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *negative* score for $A \to L$ shows that Leucine appears in the *L-column* of an A-row *less often than by chance*. This is a **disfavoured** substitution.

### Step 3.1.7 — Final 3 × 3 Substitution Scoring Matrix (in bits)
$$
S = \log_2\!\left(\frac{q_{ab}}{p_a p_b}\right) = \begin{pmatrix} 0.98 & -2.62 & -1.65 \\ -3.23 & 0.71 & -0.52 \\ -1.04 & -0.71 & 0.42 \end{pmatrix}
$$

In a *real* substitution matrix (e.g., BLOSUM62), the diagonal entries are typically the largest positive values (since self-substitution is trivially "most likely"), and biochemically similar residues (e.g., I/L, D/E) cluster with positive off-diagonal scores.

## 3.2 Derivation: PAM$n$ from PAM1 by Matrix Exponentiation

Let the $20 \times 20$ PAM1 mutation matrix be $M$. Each row $i$ contains the probabilities of residue $i$ being substituted by any of the 20 residues in 1 PAM unit. By definition of a *Markov process* with stationary background, the probability distribution after $n$ independent PAM steps is obtained by applying the transition matrix $n$ times:

$$
M_n = M \cdot M \cdot \ldots \cdot M \quad (n \text{ times}) = M^n
$$

The $(i,j)$ entry of $M_n$, denoted $M^n_{ij}$, is the probability that residue $i$ in the ancestor becomes residue $j$ after $n$ PAM units of accepted evolution.

The corresponding scoring matrix entry is:
$$
S^{(n)}_{ij} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{M^n_{ij}}{p_j}\right)
$$

The denominator $p_j$ is the stationary background frequency of residue $j$ (this normalises by the natural abundance — a high probability of $i \to j$ is meaningful only if $j$ is rare in general).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PAM vs BLOSUM

| Property | PAM | BLOSUM |
|---|---|---|
| Year / Authors | 1978, Dayhoff et al. | 1992, Henikoff & Henikoff |
| Source data | 71 closely related protein families | BLOCKS database of conserved motifs |
| Phylogenetic tree | Required | Not required |
| Time calibration | Explicit (PAM units) | Implicit (clustering threshold) |
| Higher number → | More divergent | (BLOSUM number = cluster % identity) |
| Default in BLAST | — | BLOSUM62 |
| Best for | Fine-grained evolutionary modelling | Practical homology detection |
| Symmetry assumption | Implicit in Dayhoff's method | Explicit; $S_{ab} = S_{ba}$ enforced |
| Limitation | PAM1 estimation errors amplified at high $n$ | Cannot easily extrapolate to higher divergence |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Construction and Use of a Substitution Matrix

The diagram below depicts the full pipeline from raw biological data to a usable scoring matrix in a pairwise alignment algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Aligned Sequence Database"]:::input --> B["Count Substitutions A_ab"]
    B --> C["Compute Joint Probability q_ab = A_ab / Sum"]
    C --> D["Compute Marginals p_a, p_b"]
    D --> E["Compute Expected Random p_a times p_b"]
    E --> F["Compute Odds Ratio O_ab = q_ab over p_a p_b"]
    F --> G["Take Log Base 2: S_ab = log2 O_ab"]
    G --> H["20 by 20 Scoring Matrix"]:::output

    H --> I["Pairwise Alignment DP Algorithm"]
    I --> J["Aligned Sequence Pair"]:::output

    K["Choose Matrix Family"]:::input
    K --> L{"PAM or BLOSUM?"}:::decision
    L -->|PAM| M["Build PAM1 from Mutations"]
    M --> N["Matrix Exponentiate to PAM n"]
    N --> G
    L -->|BLOSUM| O["Cluster Sequences by Identity Percent"]
    O --> P["Apply Cluster Weights 1 over k"]
    P --> B

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef output fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#16A34A,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef decision fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
```

## 4.2 Schematic Interpretation

- **The input layer (blue)** represents the biological starting material — a trusted set of aligned homologous sequences.
- **The decision gate (amber)** shows the branch between two methodological families (PAM or BLOSUM). Each branch has its own preprocessing step before merging into the shared frequency-to-score pipeline.
- **The shared core (centre)** transforms counts into log-odds — this is identical in *both* PAM and BLOSUM; only the upstream *data preprocessing* differs.
- **The output layer (green)** is the resulting scoring matrix and its downstream use in dynamic programming alignment.

## 4.3 Comparison Block: PAM vs BLOSUM Pre-processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Closely Related Proteins above 85 percent identity"]:::input --> B["Dayhoff Phylogenetic Tree"]:::input
    B --> C["Mutation Probability Matrix M"]:::output
    C --> D["Exponentiate to M power n"]:::output
    D --> E["PAM n Scoring Matrix"]:::output

    F["BLOCKS Database Unaligned Motifs"]:::input --> G["Cluster by Identity T percent"]:::input
    G --> H["Replace Cluster with Weighted Avg weight 1 over k"]:::output
    H --> I["BLOSUM T Scoring Matrix"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef output fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#BE185D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Define a **substitution matrix**. Why are log-odds scores used instead of raw substitution probabilities in sequence alignment?

**Model Answer:**

A substitution matrix is a 20 × 20 table that assigns a numerical score to every possible substitution between two amino acid residues (or between four nucleotides) during sequence evolution. The score reflects how *likely* or *biologically plausible* one residue replacing another is over evolutionary time.

Log-odds scores are used because:

1. **Additivity** — alignment algorithms (e.g., Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman) add scores position by position. Converting probabilities to log-odds makes the *product* of probabilities into a *sum* of scores, which is exactly the operation the DP recurrence requires.
2. **Background normalisation** — the denominator $p_a p_b$ removes the bias of amino acid natural abundance (Leucine is ~10 % of all residues, Cysteine is ~1.5 %).
3. **Interpretability** — a positive score means "more likely than random," zero means "neutral," and a negative score means "disfavoured."

Formally, the log-odds score is computed as:
$$S_{ab} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{q_{ab}}{p_a \cdot p_b}\right)$$
where $q_{ab}$ is the observed joint substitution frequency and $p_a, p_b$ are the background frequencies. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Differentiate between **PAM** and **BLOSUM** substitution matrices.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PAM | BLOSUM |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Dayhoff (1978) | Henikoff & Henikoff (1992) |
| Data source | 71 closely related protein families | BLOCKS database of conserved motifs |
| Higher index = | Greater evolutionary distance | Lower sequence identity threshold |
| Construction | Matrix exponentiation of PAM1 | Clustering and weighting at identity % |
| Default use | Older alignment tools | BLAST default (BLOSUM62) |
| Sensitivity to | Evolutionary time | Local block conservation |

PAM matrices model evolutionary divergence directly, with PAM250 corresponding to ~20 % sequence identity. BLOSUM matrices are derived empirically from conserved regions; **BLOSUM$\mathcal{T}$** is built by clustering sequences at $\mathcal{T}$% identity and using representative sequences weighted by $1/k$. The two are complementary: **PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45** in detection power for distantly related sequences. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full derivation focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **log-odds substitution score** $S_{ab}$ starting from raw substitution counts. Define every term in the equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From a hypothetical alignment of 5,000 residue pairs, the following counts $A_{ab}$ are obtained for the pair (A, G):

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} A\to A & A\to G \\ G\to A & G\to G \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2200 & 300 \\ 350 & 2150 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Compute the entry $S_{AG}$ of the log-odds substitution matrix. Comment on whether $A \to G$ is a *favoured* or *disfavoured* substitution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Raw counts:** Let $A_{ab}$ denote the number of times residue $a$ is observed to be substituted by residue $b$ in the trusted alignment set.

**Step 2 — Total substitutions:**
$$T = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Joint probability (target frequency):**
$$q_{ab} = \frac{A_{ab}}{T}$$
This is the probability that a *random* observed substitution in the dataset is the pair $(a, b)$.

**Step 4 — Marginal / background frequencies:**
$$p_a = \sum_{j} q_{aj}, \quad p_b = \sum_{i} q_{ib}$$
These represent the natural abundance of residues $a$ and $b$ in the dataset.

**Step 5 — Expected random substitution probability:** If residues were substituted *randomly* (independent of biochemistry), the joint probability would be:
$$e_{ab} = p_a \cdot p_b$$

**Step 6 — Odds ratio:**
$$O_{ab} = \frac{q_{ab}}{e_{ab}} = \frac{q_{ab}}{p_a \cdot p_b}$$

**Step 7 — Log-odds score (the substitution matrix entry):**
$$\boxed{\,S_{ab} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{q_{ab}}{p_a \cdot p_b}\right)\,}$$

This score is in **bits**. A score of $+1$ bit means the substitution is twice as likely as random; $-1$ bit means half as likely. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Total substitutions:**
$$T = 2200 + 300 + 350 + 2150 = 5000$$

**Step 2 — Joint frequencies:**
$$
\begin{aligned} q_{AA} &= 2200/5000 = 0.4400 \\ q_{AG} &= 300/5000 = 0.0600 \\ q_{GA} &= 350/5000 = 0.0700 \\ q_{GG} &= 2150/5000 = 0.4300 \end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Marginals:**
$$
\begin{aligned} p_A &= q_{AA} + q_{AG} = 0.4400 + 0.0600 = 0.5000 \\ p_G &= q_{GA} + q_{GG} = 0.0700 + 0.4300 = 0.5000 \end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected random probability:**
$$e_{AG} = p_A \cdot p_G = 0.5000 \times 0.5000 = 0.2500$$

**Step 5 — Odds ratio:**
$$O_{AG} = \frac{q_{AG}}{p_A \cdot p_G} = \frac{0.0600}{0.2500} = 0.2400$$

**Step 6 — Log-odds score:**
$$S_{AG} = \log_2(0.2400) = \frac{\ln(0.2400)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-1.4271}{0.6931} \approx -2.06 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $S_{AG} = -2.06 < 0$, the substitution $A \to G$ is **disfavoured** — it occurs only ~24 % as often as expected by chance, corresponding to an odds ratio far below 1. The negative score in a substitution matrix would *penalise* aligning A with G. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — BLOSUM construction focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step construction of a BLOSUM$\mathcal{T}$ substitution matrix**. Clearly explain the role of sequence clustering and the cluster-weighting factor $1/k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BLOSUM62 block contains a cluster of $k = 5$ sequences that are 70 % identical to each other. Explain how this cluster contributes to the substitution counts $A_{ab}$ used in matrix construction. If two of these sequences contain the aligned pair (I, L) and another two contain (I, I), compute the fractional contribution of this cluster to the total count $A_{IL}$ and $A_{II}$ assuming the cluster representative has weight $w = 1/k$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Gather conserved blocks.** A trusted database (BLOCKS) of ungapped, locally conserved multiple-alignment blocks is taken as the raw input.

**Step 2 — Cluster sequences by identity.** Within each block, sequences are grouped into clusters such that every pair *within a cluster* has sequence identity $\geq \mathcal{T}\%$, where $\mathcal{T}$ is the BLOSUM index (e.g., 62 for BLOSUM62).

**Step 3 — Replace cluster with weighted representative.** Each cluster of $k$ sequences is replaced by a single representative sequence; each *pair of aligned positions* within the cluster is counted with weight $w = 1/k$. This prevents over-representation of highly redundant, nearly identical sequences (which would otherwise dominate the counts).

**Step 4 — Count weighted substitutions.** For every aligned pair, the substitution $A_{ab}$ is incremented by the cluster weight $w = 1/k$ (not by 1).

**Step 5 — Compute joint probabilities** $q_{ab} = A_{ab} / T$ and **background frequencies** $p_a$.

**Step 6 — Compute the log-odds score**:
$$S_{ab} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{q_{ab}}{p_a p_b}\right)$$

**Why the weight $1/k$?** A cluster of $k$ near-identical sequences contributes $k(k-1)/2$ pairs, which would over-count substitutions *between near-identical* residues and under-count rare substitutions observed in *single* outlier sequences. Using weight $1/k$ normalises each cluster's contribution so that one *cluster* counts as much as one *independent* sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the cluster.** The cluster contains $k = 5$ sequences, weight per pair observation $w = 1/k = 0.2$.

**Step 2 — Count aligned pairs.** From the problem:

- (I, L) observed in 2 of the 5 sequences.
- (I, I) observed in 2 of the 5 sequences.
- (the 5th sequence contributes 0 of either of these specific pairs at this position).

**Step 3 — Apply the cluster weight.** Each observed pair contributes $w = 0.2$ to the substitution count. The cluster as a whole generates:

- For (I, L): 2 sequences × 0.2 = **0.4** contribution.
- For (I, I): 2 sequences × 0.2 = **0.4** contribution.

**Step 4 — Final fractional contributions to total $A_{ab}$:**
$$
\boxed{\,A_{IL}^{\text{cluster}} = 0.4, \quad A_{II}^{\text{cluster}} = 0.4\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Because the weight $1/k$ distributes the cluster's total weight (which sums to 1 across all members) evenly, the cluster's contribution to any specific pair is a *fraction* (not an integer), reflecting its reduced statistical weight relative to $k$ independent sequences. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing probability with log-odds.** Saying "$S = +2$ means twice as likely" is wrong; it means $2^2 = 4 \times$ more likely.
> 2. **Forgetting the $p_a p_b$ denominator.** Writing $S_{ab} = \log_2(q_{ab})$ loses 2 marks — the background normalisation is the *core idea* of substitution matrices.
> 3. **Mixing PAM and BLOSUM conventions.** Higher PAM index = more distant; higher BLOSUM index = more similar. Inverting this is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps.** When asked to "derive," show every line $A_{ab} \to q_{ab} \to p_a, p_b \to e_{ab} \to O_{ab} \to S_{ab}$. Skipping the marginal-frequency step loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong base of logarithm.** Use $\log_2$ when scores are in *bits* (standard for PAM/BLOSUM). Using natural log $\ln$ without unit conversion is a common error.
> 6. **Symmetry confusion.** BLOSUM matrices are *symmetric by construction* ($S_{ab} = S_{ba}$). Some PAM variants are not — do not assume symmetry without checking.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Substitution Matrices**

- **Definition:** A substitution matrix $S$ is a $20 \times 20$ log-odds scoring table $S_{ab} = \log_2(q_{ab}/(p_a p_b))$ for protein alignment.
- **Why logs?** Convert probability products to additive scores; aligners add scores, not multiply.
- **Why normalise by $p_a p_b$?** Remove the bias of natural amino acid abundance.
- **Score interpretation:** Positive = favoured, Zero = neutral, Negative = disfavoured.
- **PAM family:** Dayhoff (1978); PAM$n$ = $M^n$ via matrix exponentiation; higher index = more divergent; PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45.
- **BLOSUM family:** Henikoff & Henikoff (1992); BLOSUM$\mathcal{T}$ built by clustering at $\mathcal{T}$% identity with weight $1/k$; higher index = more similar; BLOSUM62 is BLAST default.
- **Cluster weight:** $w = 1/k$ prevents near-identical sequences from dominating the counts.
- **Information content:** $R = \log_2 20 - H$ measures the discriminating power of a column or matrix.
- **Identity matrix:** Trivial +1/0 scoring; used only for nucleotide exact matching.
- **Genetic code matrix:** Based on minimum number of nucleotide changes; biologically less accurate than PAM/BLOSUM.
- **Default for nucleotide BLAST:** Match = +1, Mismatch = $-3$ (or use matrix like NUC.4.4).
- **Default for protein BLAST:** BLOSUM62.
- **Markov property of PAM:** Residue at time $t+n$ depends only on residue at time $t$, justifying matrix powers.
- **Symmetry convention:** BLOSUM = symmetric; PAM scoring matrices may be asymmetric (PAM1 mutation matrix is *not* symmetric).
- **Sequence identity vs PAM distance:** 100 % identity → 0 PAM; 50 % identity → ~80 PAM; 20 % identity → ~250 PAM.
- **Two matrices, one philosophy:** Both PAM and BLOSUM implement the *same* log-odds formula — they differ only in the *preprocessing* of substitution counts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PAM and BLOSUM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PAM and BLOSUM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Substitution Matrices

In **Sequence Alignment** and **Bioinformatics**, a **Substitution Matrix** is a mathematical construct (a 20×20 symmetric matrix) that assigns a numerical score to the substitution of one amino acid (or nucleotide) for another in a protein (or DNA) sequence alignment. These scores represent the **likelihood of one residue being substituted for another during evolution**, derived from empirical or statistical analysis of known sequence families.

Among all substitution matrices, **PAM** and **BLOSUM** are the two foundational scoring systems used globally for **protein sequence comparison**, database searching (BLAST, FASTA), and **phylogenetic analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Substitution matrices are the heart of **scoring functions** in dynamic programming alignment algorithms (Needleman-Wunsch, Smith-Waterman). Without them, biological relevance of alignments cannot be quantified.

### 1.2 PAM (Point Accepted Mutation) Matrices

**Definition:** PAM stands for **Point Accepted Mutation** (also called **Percent Accepted Mutation**). The PAM matrix was introduced by **Margaret Dayhoff and co-workers in 1978** as part of the *Atlas of Protein Sequence and Structure*.

A *Point Accepted Mutation* is a substitution in a protein sequence that has been **accepted by natural selection** (i.e., the organism survives and reproduces). PAM is fundamentally based on the concept of **evolutionary distance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **PAM Unit:** **1 PAM unit = 1 accepted point mutation per 100 amino acid residues.**
> This means in a sequence of 100 amino acids, 1 amino acid has undergone an accepted mutation on average.

### 1.3 BLOSUM (Blocks Substitution Matrix)

**Definition:** BLOSUM stands for **Blocks Substitution Matrix**, developed by **Steven and Jorja Henikoff in 1992**. Unlike PAM, BLOSUM is derived from **direct observation** of conserved, ungapped regions (called *blocks*) in protein families from the **BLOCKS database**.

The numbers in BLOSUM matrices (e.g., BLOSUM62, BLOSUM80) refer to the **percentage identity threshold** used in clustering the sequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **BLOSUM Unit:** The number after BLOSUM = the minimum percentage identity of sequences used to construct the matrix.
> Example: BLOSUM62 uses sequence blocks with **≥ 62% identity**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Language of Evolution":**
>
> Imagine that you have two old, slightly worn copies of a recipe book. Over decades, certain words have been replaced with similar-sounding words (e.g., "sugar" became "suggar" or "saguar"). Some words are rarely changed (like "bake" or "flour") because changing them would ruin the recipe, while others (like a brand name) can be replaced more freely.
>
> A **substitution matrix** is like a *forensic dictionary* that records, across thousands of recipe books:
> - Which words swap frequently and which rarely.
> - The chemical/biological "cost" of each swap.
>
> **PAM = "Old documents" approach:** It analyzes closely related recipes to infer how substitutions happen over time, then **mathematically extrapolates** to longer evolutionary distances.
> **BLOSUM = "Living family" approach:** It looks at **present-day**, highly conserved regions in related recipes and **directly measures** how often each substitution occurs in real protein families.

### 1.5 Why PAM and BLOSUM Matter in Real Engineering & Biology

| Application Area | Use of Substitution Matrix |
|---|---|
| **BLAST Database Searches** | Scores aligned residues to detect homologs |
| **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** | Determines optimal residue-residue matches |
| **Phylogenetic Tree Construction** | Quantifies evolutionary divergence |
| **Protein Structure Prediction** | Identifies structurally conserved residues |
| **Drug Discovery** | Detects conserved functional motifs |

### 1.6 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **PAM250** is the most widely used PAM matrix.
- **BLOSUM62** is the most widely used BLOSUM matrix.
- **PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45** (similar evolutionary distance range).
- **Identity threshold range:** BLOSUM45 (≤ 45%) to BLOSUM80 (≥ 80%).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PAM Construction Pipeline — Step-by-Step

The construction of PAM matrices follows a strict mathematical pathway:

1. **Sequence Collection:** A dataset of closely related protein families is gathered, where the sequences differ by **less than 1 PAM unit** (i.e., < 1% divergence).
2. **Phylogenetic Tree Construction:** Each family is aligned and a phylogenetic tree is built.
3. **Mutation Counting:** From the tree, the number of **accepted point mutations** in each branch is tabulated for every possible amino acid pair (i, j).
4. **Mutation Probability Matrix (M):** A 20×20 raw frequency matrix M is built, where each entry M[i,j] represents the probability of amino acid *i* mutating to amino acid *j*.
5. **Normalization:** Each row of M is divided by the relative frequency (f_i) of amino acid *i* in the dataset to obtain the **mutational probability** P[j|i].
6. **Log-Odds Conversion:** The **PAM1** matrix is built by taking the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio:
$$S_{ij} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{ij}}{f_i \cdot f_j}\right)$$
   where P_ij is the observed frequency of pair (i, j) and f_i, f_j are background frequencies.
7. **Extrapolation:** Higher PAM matrices are derived by **matrix multiplication**:
$$\text{PAM}_n = (\text{PAM}_1)^n$$

### 2.2 The BLOSUM Construction Pipeline — Step-by-Step

1. **Block Database:** Sequences are extracted from the **BLOCKS database** (groups of ungapped, conserved regions).
2. **Clustering:** Sequences within each block are clustered based on a chosen **identity percentage threshold**.
3. **Sequence Weighting:** Each cluster is assigned a weight to prevent over-representation of closely related sequences.
4. **Pair Counting:** For each pair of amino acids (i, j), the number of times they appear in aligned columns is counted.
5. **Log-Odds Conversion:** Scores are calculated using:
$$S_{ij} = \log_{2}\left(\frac{q_{ij}}{p_i \cdot p_j}\right)$$
   where **q_ij** is the observed frequency of pair (i, j) and **p_i, p_j** are background frequencies.
6. **Final Matrix:** The log-odds values are rounded and scaled (typically multiplied by 2) to give integer scores.

### 2.3 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

> **Why log-odds?** Biological evolution is multiplicative (probabilistic), not additive. Logarithms transform probabilities into additive scores, which are mathematically tractable for **Dynamic Programming (DP)** alignment algorithms. This converts the scoring problem from probability multiplication into simple addition — perfect for Needleman-Wunsch and Smith-Waterman DP.

> **Why matrix multiplication for PAM?** Evolution is **time-dependent**. If 1% of residues change in 1 PAM unit, then in 2 PAM units, the change accumulates. Matrix multiplication (PAM_n = (PAM_1)^n) **propagates the mutation probabilities over n generations**, which is mathematically equivalent to repeated Markov chain applications.

> **Why clustering in BLOSUM?** To avoid bias from over-represented closely related sequences. Clustering at *n%* identity ensures that **each cluster contributes equally** to the final matrix, providing a balanced statistical sample.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **PAM unit definition** | 1 PAM = 1 mutation per 100 aa | Unit of evolutionary distance |
| **PAM_n extrapolation** | $\text{PAM}_n = (\text{PAM}_1)^n$ | Higher PAMs are powers of PAM1 |
| **Log-odds score (PAM)** | $S_{ij} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{ij}}{f_i f_j}\right)$ | Score for amino acid pair substitution |
| **Log-odds score (BLOSUM)** | $S_{ij} = \log_{2}\left(\frac{q_{ij}}{p_i p_j}\right)$ | Score for amino acid pair substitution |
| **Target Frequency** | $\text{TF}_{ij} = \frac{P_{ij}}{f_i f_j}$ | Ratio of observed to expected frequency |
| **Clustering threshold** | BLOSUM_n (n = % identity) | Sequences clustered at n% identity |
| **Equivalent matrices** | PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45 | Same evolutionary distance coverage |
| **Score interpretation** | S > 0 → conserved; S < 0 → non-conserved | Positive scores = likely substitution |
| **Symmetry property** | $S_{ij} = S_{ji}$ | Substitution matrices are symmetric |
| **Background frequency** | $\sum_i f_i = 1$ | Amino acid frequencies sum to 1 |
| **Mutation probability** | $\sum_j P_{ij} = 1$ | Each row sums to 1 (Markov property) |
| **BLAST default** | BLOSUM62 | Default matrix in NCBI BLAST |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BLAST algorithm** uses BLOSUM62 by default for protein-protein comparison.
- **FASTA** algorithm historically preferred PAM matrices.
- **PSI-BLAST** uses position-specific scoring matrices (PSSM) derived from BLOSUM-based alignments.
- **Homology Modeling** (e.g., SWISS-MODEL, MODELLER) uses substitution scores to identify structurally equivalent residues.
- **Vaccine Design** uses substitution matrices to find conserved epitopes across viral strains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Compare PAM and BLOSUM", always state at least 4 differences: (1) basis of construction, (2) extrapolation method, (3) identity threshold, (4) default usage. Missing the *extrapolation* difference is a common 2-mark loss.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Counts to Log-Odds Scores

#### Step 1 — Define the Raw Count Matrix
Suppose we have aligned sequences and we count occurrences of amino acid pair $(i, j)$ in aligned columns. Let $A_{ij}$ be this raw count matrix.

#### Step 2 — Compute Observed Frequencies
The observed frequency of pair $(i, j)$ is:
$$q_{ij} = \frac{A_{ij}}{\sum_{x,y} A_{xy}}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Expected Frequencies
The expected frequency under the null hypothesis (random substitution) is:
$$e_{ij} = p_i \cdot p_j$$

where $p_i$ is the background frequency of amino acid $i$, estimated from the dataset:
$$p_i = \frac{\sum_j A_{ij}}{\sum_{x,y} A_{xy}}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Target Frequency Ratio
The **Target Frequency (TF)** represents how much more (or less) likely a substitution is compared to random chance:
$$\text{TF}_{ij} = \frac{q_{ij}}{e_{ij}} = \frac{q_{ij}}{p_i \cdot p_j}$$

#### Step 5 — Convert to Log-Odds Score
Taking the logarithm converts the multiplicative ratio into an additive score:
$$S_{ij} = \log_b\left(\frac{q_{ij}}{p_i \cdot p_j}\right)$$

> **Note:** PAM uses $\log_{10}$ and BLOSUM uses $\log_2$. This is purely a scaling choice; it doesn't change the biological meaning.

#### Step 6 — Interpretation of the Score
- If $S_{ij} > 0$: The substitution $(i \to j)$ is **more likely than random** → evolutionarily favored (conservative substitution).
- If $S_{ij} = 0$: The substitution is **neutral** (matches random expectation).
- If $S_{ij} < 0$: The substitution is **less likely than random** → evolutionarily disfavored.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Mini Substitution Matrix

Suppose we observe, in a small alignment dataset, the following counts of amino acid pair substitutions:

| Pair (i, j) | A (Ala) | G (Gly) | V (Val) | Total |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A (Ala)** | 100 | 30 | 20 | 150 |
| **G (Gly)** | 30 | 80 | 10 | 120 |
| **V (Val)** | 20 | 10 | 70 | 100 |
| **Total** | 150 | 120 | 100 | 370 |

#### Step 1 — Total count
$$\sum_{x,y} A_{xy} = 370$$

#### Step 2 — Background frequencies
$$p_A = \frac{150}{370} = 0.4054$$
$$p_G = \frac{120}{370} = 0.3243$$
$$p_V = \frac{100}{370} = 0.2703$$

**Verification:**
$$p_A + p_G + p_V = 0.4054 + 0.3243 + 0.2703 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

#### Step 3 — Observed pair frequency for (A, G)
$$q_{AG} = \frac{30}{370} = 0.0811$$

#### Step 4 — Expected frequency under randomness
$$e_{AG} = p_A \cdot p_G = 0.4054 \times 0.3243 = 0.1315$$

#### Step 5 — Target Frequency ratio
$$\text{TF}_{AG} = \frac{q_{AG}}{e_{AG}} = \frac{0.0811}{0.1315} = 0.6167$$

#### Step 6 — Log-odds score (using $\log_2$ for BLOSUM-style)
$$S_{AG} = \log_2(0.6167) = -0.6977$$

This is then **scaled by 2** (BLOSUM convention) and rounded:
$$S_{AG}^{\text{final}} = 2 \times (-0.6977) = -1.3954 \approx -1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A negative score for A↔G means this substitution is **less likely than random** in our dataset — they are *not* highly interchangeable.

### 3.3 Derivation: PAM_n = (PAM_1)^n (Markov Chain Extrapolation)

#### Step 1 — Model mutations as a Markov chain
The PAM1 matrix $P_1$ gives the probability of amino acid $i$ mutating to $j$ in 1 PAM unit. Since each PAM unit is **time-independent** (Markov property):
$$P_1[i, j] = P(i \to j \text{ in 1 unit})$$

#### Step 2 — Two PAM units
The probability of $i \to j$ in 2 units is the sum over all intermediates $k$:
$$P_2[i, j] = \sum_{k=1}^{20} P_1[i, k] \cdot P_1[k, j]$$

In matrix form, this is the **matrix product**:
$$P_2 = P_1 \times P_1 = P_1^2$$

#### Step 3 — Inductive extension
By induction, for $n$ PAM units:
$$P_n = (P_1)^n$$

#### Step 4 — Log-odds extrapolation
The log-odds score for PAM_n is:
$$S_{ij}^{(n)} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_n[i,j]}{f_i f_j}\right)$$

Since $P_n$ is computed via matrix multiplication, the corresponding log-odds scores are NOT obtained by simply multiplying the PAM1 scores — they require a full re-computation of the log-odds from the propagated $P_n$ matrix.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — PAM vs BLOSUM

| Parameter | PAM | BLOSUM |
|---|---|---|
| **Full form** | Point (or Percent) Accepted Mutation | Blocks Substitution Matrix |
| **Year introduced** | 1978 | 1992 |
| **Authors** | Dayhoff et al. | Henikoff & Henikoff |
| **Data source** | Global alignments of closely related proteins | Local conserved blocks (ungapped regions) |
| **Identity threshold** | Sequences < 1% divergent | Sequences clustered at *n*% identity |
| **Construction method** | Markov chain extrapolation | Direct observation |
| **Extrapolation** | $P_n = (P_1)^n$ | None (each BLOSUM is independent) |
| **Log base** | $\log_{10}$ (scaled by 10) | $\log_2$ (scaled by 2) |
| **Best for** | Detecting distant evolutionary relationships (high PAM) | Detecting conserved motifs in related proteins |
| **Common matrices** | PAM30, PAM70, PAM120, PAM250 | BLOSUM45, BLOSUM62, BLOSUM80 |
| **Default in BLAST** | Not default (older FASTA default) | **BLOSUM62** (NCBI BLAST default) |
| **Equivalence** | PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45 | BLOSUM45 ≈ PAM250 |
| **Sequence relationship** | Global / long-range | Local / short-range motifs |
| **Sensitivity to close homologs** | Lower (PAM30 better) | Higher (BLOSUM80 better) |
| **Sensitivity to distant homologs** | Higher (PAM250 better) | Moderate (BLOSUM45 better) |
| **Database size** | Small (71 families in original) | Large (BLOCKS database, ~2000 blocks) |
| **Symmetry** | Yes | Yes |

### 3.5 Choosing the Right Matrix — KTU Decision Rule

| Evolutionary Distance | Recommended Matrix |
|---|---|
| Very close homologs (> 80% identity) | **BLOSUM80** or **PAM30** |
| Moderately close (60–80%) | **BLOSUM62** (BLAST default) |
| Distant homologs (40–60%) | **BLOSUM45** or **PAM120** |
| Very distant (< 40%) | **PAM250** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive rule:** Higher PAM number = **more distant** evolutionary relationship. Higher BLOSUM number = **closer** evolutionary relationship. This opposite numbering is a common KTU exam pitfall!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: PAM Matrix Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Closely related protein families] --> B[Build phylogenetic trees for each family]
    B --> C[Count accepted point mutations on each branch]
    C --> D[Construct raw 20x20 mutation count matrix M]
    D --> E[Compute row-wise mutation probabilities P_j_given_i]
    E --> F[Normalize by background frequencies f_i]
    F --> G[Build PAM1 log-odds matrix using log base 10]
    G --> H{PAM1 constructed. Need higher PAM?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply Markov chain: PAM_n = PAM1 power n]
    I --> J[PAM30, PAM70, PAM120, PAM250, ...]
    H -- No --> K[Output: Final PAM matrix]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Used in FASTA, BLAST variants, MSA tools]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: BLOSUM Matrix Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: BLOCKS database of conserved ungapped regions] --> B[Group sequences into clusters at n percent identity]
    B --> C[Apply sequence weighting to reduce bias]
    C --> D[Count amino acid pair occurrences A_ij in aligned columns]
    D --> E[Compute observed frequencies q_ij and background p_i]
    E --> F[Compute target frequency ratio q_ij over p_i times p_j]
    F --> G[Convert to log base 2 odds scores]
    G --> H[Scale by factor 2 and round to integers]
    H --> I[Output: BLOSUMn matrix e.g. BLOSUM62]
    I --> J[Used as default scoring matrix in NCBI BLAST]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: PAM vs BLOSUM Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Protein Sequence Data] --> B{Distance Regime?}
    B -- Long range evolution --> C[PAM Pipeline]
    B -- Local conserved regions --> D[BLOSUM Pipeline]
    C --> E[Extrapolation via Markov matrix powers]
    D --> F[Direct log odds observation]
    E --> G[Output: PAM30 to PAM500]
    F --> H[Output: BLOSUM45 to BLOSUM90]
    G --> I[FASTA, ClustalW with PAM weighting]
    H --> J[BLAST, PSI BLAST, MSA tools with BLOSUM]
```

### 4.4 Substitution Score Interpretation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute S_ij = log of q_ij over p_i times p_j] --> Decide{S_ij value}
    Decide -- S_ij greater than 0 --> Pos[Conservative substitution: evolutionarily favored]
    Decide -- S_ij equals 0 --> Neu[Neutral substitution: random expectation]
    Decide -- S_ij less than 0 --> Neg[Non conservative substitution: evolutionarily disfavored]
    Pos --> End[Result: high quality alignment match]
    Neu --> End2[Result: ambiguous match]
    Neg --> End3[Result: low quality alignment match or mismatch penalty]
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Matrix Selection by Evolutionary Distance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Query and target sequences] --> B[Estimate percent identity]
    B --> C{Identity threshold}
    C -- Greater than 80 percent --> D[Use BLOSUM80 or PAM30]
    C -- 60 to 80 percent --> E[Use BLOSUM62 default BLAST]
    C -- 40 to 60 percent --> F[Use BLOSUM45 or PAM120]
    C -- Less than 40 percent --> G[Use PAM250 for very distant homologs]
    D --> H[Output alignment score]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define PAM matrix. What is the significance of PAM1 and PAM250 in sequence alignment?**

**Model Answer:**
PAM (Point Accepted Mutation) matrix is a 20×20 substitution scoring matrix developed by Dayhoff (1978) that scores the likelihood of one amino acid being substituted for another during evolution.

- **PAM1:** Represents the probability of substitutions when 1% of amino acids have undergone an accepted mutation. It is the *base* matrix from which all higher PAM matrices are derived.
- **PAM250:** Represents 250 PAM units of evolutionary distance, equivalent to proteins that are ~20% identical. It is the most widely used PAM matrix for general protein sequence comparison.

It is used to assign **biologically meaningful scores** to aligned residue pairs in dynamic programming alignment algorithms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is BLOSUM? How is the numbering (e.g., BLOSUM62) interpreted?**

**Model Answer:**
BLOSUM (Blocks Substitution Matrix) is a substitution scoring matrix developed by Henikoff & Henikoff (1992), derived from observed frequencies of amino acid pairs in conserved, ungapped blocks of protein families from the BLOCKS database.

The number denotes the **percentage identity threshold** used during clustering:
- **BLOSUM62** uses sequences clustered at ≥ **62% identity**.
- **BLOSUM80** uses sequences with ≥ 80% identity (for closer homologs).
- **BLOSUM45** uses sequences with ≥ 45% identity (for more distant relationships).

A higher BLOSUM number indicates sequences of **greater similarity** were used, while a higher PAM number indicates **greater evolutionary distance**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the construction of PAM substitution matrix. Discuss the significance of PAM1 as the unit of evolutionary change. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** PAM (Point Accepted Mutation) matrix was developed by **Margaret Dayhoff in 1978** by analyzing global alignments of closely related protein families. It is a 20×20 scoring matrix where entry $S_{ij}$ represents the log-odds score of substituting amino acid $i$ with amino acid $j$.

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Sequence Selection:** A database of **71 protein families** was used, where each family contained sequences with **>85% identity** (ensuring < 1% divergence per branch, i.e., < 1 PAM unit).

2. **Phylogenetic Tree Building:** For each family, a phylogenetic tree was constructed to identify evolutionary relationships.

3. **Mutation Counting:** The number of **accepted point mutations** along each branch was tabulated. The mutation count matrix $A_{ij}$ was built (raw counts of $i \to j$ substitutions).

4. **Mutability Normalization:** Each amino acid's mutability $m_i$ was calculated as the fraction of sites at which $i$ had changed.

5. **Probability Matrix:** The mutation probability $M_{ij}$ was computed by:
$$M_{ij} = \frac{\lambda m_i A_{ij}}{\sum_j A_{ij}}$$
   where $\lambda$ is a scaling factor (the PAM unit constant).

6. **Background Frequency Adjustment:** The matrix was normalized by the background frequency $f_i$ of each amino acid.

7. **Log-Odds Conversion:** The final PAM1 score was computed as:
$$S_{ij} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{M_{ij}}{f_i \cdot f_j}\right)$$
   (The factor 10 scales the scores to integers.)

8. **Higher PAM Derivation:** Higher PAM matrices are obtained by:
$$\text{PAM}_n = (\text{PAM}_1)^n$$

**Significance of PAM1 as Evolutionary Unit:**
- 1 PAM unit = 1% of amino acids have undergone an accepted mutation.
- It represents a **standardized evolutionary yardstick**.
- It allows extrapolation to longer evolutionary distances via **Markov chain matrix powers**.
- It is the **atomic unit** from which all higher PAMs (PAM30, PAM120, PAM250) are derived.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare PAM and BLOSUM matrices in detail. State the conditions under which each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | PAM | BLOSUM |
|---|---|---|
| **Full form** | Point Accepted Mutation | Blocks Substitution Matrix |
| **Year / Authors** | 1978, Dayhoff | 1992, Henikoff & Henikoff |
| **Data source** | Global alignments, 71 protein families | Local conserved blocks (BLOCKS database) |
| **Method** | Markov chain extrapolation | Direct empirical observation |
| **Identity criterion** | Sequences differ by < 1% | Clustered at *n*% identity |
| **Extrapolation** | $P_n = (P_1)^n$ | None |
| **Log base** | $\log_{10}$ | $\log_2$ |
| **Common matrix** | PAM250 | BLOSUM62 |
| **Used in** | FASTA, ClustalW (older) | NCBI BLAST (default) |
| **Equivalence** | PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45 | BLOSUM45 ≈ PAM250 |

**Conditions of Preference:**

- **Use BLOSUM80 or PAM30** when comparing **very closely related** proteins (>80% identity). These matrices are sensitive to minor changes.
- **Use BLOSUM62** (BLAST default) for **moderately related** proteins (40–60% identity). It provides the best general-purpose trade-off.
- **Use BLOSUM45 or PAM120** for **more divergent** proteins (30–40% identity).
- **Use PAM250** for **highly divergent** proteins, as it captures long evolutionary substitutions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse the numbering convention:
> - **Higher PAM number = MORE distant** relationship (e.g., PAM250 > PAM30).
> - **Higher BLOSUM number = CLOSER** relationship (e.g., BLOSUM80 > BLOSUM45).
> This is the most common cause of 2-mark deductions on this question type.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the construction of BLOSUM matrix with the help of a flowchart. Describe the significance of BLOSUM62. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** BLOSUM (Blocks Substitution Matrix) was introduced by Henikoff and Henikoff in 1992. It is built from the **BLOCKS database**, which contains conserved, ungapped regions (blocks) automatically discovered in protein families.

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Block Database:** The BLOCKS database contains ~2000 ungapped multiple sequence alignments of conserved regions.

2. **Sequence Clustering:** Sequences within each block are clustered using a **percentage identity threshold** (e.g., 62% for BLOSUM62). Sequences within a cluster share ≥ 62% identity.

3. **Sequence Weighting:** To prevent over-representation of closely related sequences, each cluster is assigned a **weighting factor**:
$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{n_c}$$
   where $n_c$ is the number of sequences in the cluster. This ensures that each cluster contributes equally.

4. **Counting Pairs:** For each column, the number of times each amino acid pair $(i, j)$ appears is counted: $A_{ij}$.

5. **Observed Frequency:**
$$q_{ij} = \frac{A_{ij}}{\sum_{x,y} A_{xy}}$$

6. **Background Frequency:**
$$p_i = \frac{\sum_j A_{ij}}{\sum_{x,y} A_{xy}}$$

7. **Log-Odds Score:**
$$S_{ij} = 2 \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{q_{ij}}{p_i \cdot p_j}\right)$$

8. **Rounding:** The final scores are rounded to integers.

**Flowchart (summary):**
```
BLOCKS Database → Cluster at n% Identity → Weight Sequences →
Count A_ij → Compute q_ij and p_i → Apply log_2 →
Scale ×2 → Round → BLOSUM_n Matrix
```

**Significance of BLOSUM62:**
- It is the **default scoring matrix** in **NCBI BLAST** for protein sequence comparison.
- Derived from sequences with **≥ 62% identity**, providing an optimal balance between sensitivity and specificity.
- Outperforms PAM matrices in detecting **moderately conserved** functional domains.
- Widely used in **BLAST, PSI-BLAST, and most modern alignment tools**.

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the log-odds score formula for a substitution matrix. Explain the meaning of positive, zero, and negative scores with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Let $A_{ij}$ be the count of aligned amino acid pair $(i, j)$.

**Step 1 — Observed Frequency:**
$$q_{ij} = \frac{A_{ij}}{\sum_{x,y} A_{xy}}$$

**Step 2 — Background (Expected) Frequency:**
Under the null hypothesis of random substitution:
$$e_{ij} = p_i \cdot p_j$$
where
$$p_i = \frac{\sum_j A_{ij}}{\sum_{x,y} A_{xy}}$$

**Step 3 — Target Frequency Ratio:**
$$\text{TF}_{ij} = \frac{q_{ij}}{e_{ij}} = \frac{q_{ij}}{p_i \cdot p_j}$$

**Step 4 — Log-Odds Conversion:**
$$S_{ij} = \log_b\left(\frac{q_{ij}}{p_i \cdot p_j}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Scaling (for integer matrix):**
- BLOSUM: $S_{ij}^{\text{final}} = 2 \cdot \log_2(\text{TF}_{ij})$
- PAM: $S_{ij}^{\text{final}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(\text{TF}_{ij})$

**Interpretation of Scores:**

- **Positive Score ($S_{ij} > 0$):** The substitution $(i \to j)$ occurs more frequently than by random chance → **conservative substitution**; residues are biochemically similar.
  - *Example:* $S(\text{Leu, Ile}) = +2$. Both are hydrophobic, branched-chain amino acids. Frequently interchangeable in protein evolution.

- **Zero Score ($S_{ij} = 0$):** The substitution frequency matches the random expectation → **neutral substitution**; neither favored nor disfavored.
  - *Example:* $S(\text{X, Y}) = 0$ means the observed and expected frequencies are equal.

- **Negative Score ($S_{ij} < 0$):** The substitution occurs less frequently than by random chance → **non-conservative substitution**; residues are biochemically dissimilar.
  - *Example:* $S(\text{Trp, Gly}) = -3$. Tryptophan is bulky and aromatic; glycine is small and flexible. Such substitutions disrupt protein structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When deriving the log-odds formula, students often:
> 1. Forget to verify that $\sum_i p_i = 1$ (background frequencies must sum to 1).
> 2. Confuse the scaling factor (PAM uses 10 with $\log_{10}$; BLOSUM uses 2 with $\log_2$).
> 3. Omit the explanation of why **log** is used (additive scores for DP algorithms). Each of these omissions costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PAM and BLOSUM**
>
> - **PAM** = Point Accepted Mutation (Dayhoff, 1978); based on **global** alignments and **Markov extrapolation**.
> - **BLOSUM** = Blocks Substitution Matrix (Henikoff & Henikoff, 1992); based on **local conserved blocks** and **direct observation**.
> - **PAM unit** = 1% accepted mutation; 1 PAM1 is the base matrix.
> - **Higher PAM number = MORE distant** relationship.
> - **BLOSUM number = % identity threshold** for clustering.
> - **Higher BLOSUM number = CLOSER** relationship.
> - **Log-odds formula:** $S_{ij} = \log\left(\frac{q_{ij}}{p_i p_j}\right)$; converts probabilities to additive scores for DP alignment.
> - **Positive score** = conservative substitution; **negative score** = non-conservative.
> - **PAM_n = (PAM_1)^n** (Markov chain extrapolation).
> - **BLOSUM does NOT use extrapolation** — each BLOSUM is built independently.
> - **Equivalence:** PAM250 ≈ BLOSUM45 (similar evolutionary distance).
> - **BLAST default = BLOSUM62**; **FASTA default = PAM250** (historically).
> - **BLOSUM62** is the most widely used substitution matrix in modern bioinformatics.
> - **Matrix selection rule:** BLOSUM80/PAM30 (close) → BLOSUM62 (moderate) → BLOSUM45/PAM120 (distant) → PAM250 (very distant).
> - **Symmetry property:** $S_{ij} = S_{ji}$ for both matrices.
> - **Log base convention:** PAM = $\log_{10}$ × 10; BLOSUM = $\log_2$ × 2.
> - **Background frequencies** $p_i$ must sum to 1; **mutation probabilities** must sum to 1 in each row.
> - **Clustering + weighting** in BLOSUM prevents over-representation of closely related sequences.
> - **Substitution matrices are the scoring engine** of Needleman-Wunsch, Smith-Waterman, BLAST, and FASTA algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gap Penalties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Gap Penalties # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of pairwise sequence alignment within Computational Biology, a **Gap Penalty** is a numerical score (typically a negative value) imposed by the alignment algorithm to penalize the insertion or deletion of a contiguous run of characters (a *gap*) in one of the two biological sequences being compared. Gap penalties are essential scoring components used inside Dynamic Programming (DP) matrices such as the Needleman-Wunsch (global) and Smith-Waterman (local) algorithms to model the biological reality of insertions, deletions, and indels introduced by mutational events over evolutionary time. Mathematically, if a gap of length $k$ (where $k \geq 1$) is introduced, the penalty applied to the alignment score is expressed as a generic cost function: $$\gamma(k) = \text{cost incurred for a gap of length } k$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT861 Module 2):** Gap penalties are studied as a refinement of the basic Needleman-Wunsch scoring scheme. Students must master both the **linear gap penalty** and the **affine gap penalty** models because KTU questions are frequently framed around selecting appropriate penalty values and computing modified alignment scores. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Missing Puzzle Piece Imagine you are comparing two shredded newspaper articles (representing two biological sequences) and trying to perfectly overlap them. Sometimes, a whole section of words is simply missing from one article (because it was physically torn away). To still align the remaining words, you must insert empty slots (gaps) into the shorter article. - If you insert *one* missing word, the editor charges you a small "setup fee" — this is the **Gap Opening Penalty**. - If you keep inserting consecutive missing words, the editor charges only a tiny "extension fee" per extra word — this is the **Gap Extension Penalty**. - If the editor charged you a flat "per-word" fee regardless of where the gap started, that would be a **Linear Gap Penalty**. In nature, evolutionary events such as a single frameshift mutation or a single indel event create **clusters** of consecutive gaps, not isolated scattered gaps. Hence, charging a high opening penalty plus a low extension penalty (the *affine model*) more accurately models real biology. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Cheat Sheet for KTU:** > - **Indel** — A biological mutation that is either an Insertion or a Deletion. > - **Linear Penalty** — A constant cost per gap character: $\gamma(k) = g \cdot k$. > - **Affine Penalty** — A two-part cost: opening cost + cheap extension cost. > - **Match Score** — Positive reward for identical aligned residues. > - **Mismatch Score** — Negative penalty for non-identical aligned residues. ## 1.3 Why Gap Penalties Matter in Sequence Alignment Gap penalties directly control the **biological interpretation** of an alignment. If penalties are too **high**, the algorithm refuses to insert gaps and produces fragmented, biologically meaningless alignments. If penalties are too **low**, the algorithm inserts excessive gaps to chase spurious matches. The art of bioinformatics lies in tuning gap penalties to mimic natural evolutionary processes. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Principal Gap Penalty Models ### 2.1.1 Linear Gap Penalty Model (Constant Penalty) This is the simplest model where every gap character incurs the **same** negative cost regardless of whether it opens a new gap or extends an existing one. $$\gamma_{\text{linear}}(k) = g \cdot k$$ where $g$ is the gap penalty per residue (a negative number, e.g., $g = -2$) and $k$ is the length of the gap. ### 2.1.2 Affine Gap Penalty Model (Two-Parameter Model) This is the **biologically preferred** and **KTU-favorite** model. It distinguishes between *opening* a gap and *extending* an already-open gap. $$\gamma_{\text{affine}}(k) = g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \cdot (k - 1)$$ where: - $g_{\text{open}}$ — A **large negative** penalty charged **once** when a new gap is created. - $g_{\text{extend}}$ — A **smaller negative** penalty charged for **each additional** residue in the gap. ### 2.1.3 Logarithmic / Concave Gap Penalty Model (Advanced) Used rarely in KTU but mentioned for completeness. Concave models penalize long gaps sub-linearly, based on the rationale that one large deletion is more likely than many small ones. $$\gamma(k) = a + b \cdot \log(k)$$ where $a$ and $b$ are empirically tuned constants. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Parameters | Typical Values (BLAST/BioEdit) | |---|---|---|---| | Linear Gap Penalty | $\gamma(k) = g \cdot k$ | $g < 0$ | $-4$ to $-8$ | | Affine Gap Opening | $g_{\text{open}}$ | $g_{\text{open}} < 0$ | $-10$ to $-15$ | | Affine Gap Extension | $g_{\text{extend}}$ | $g_{\text{extend}} < 0$ | $-1$ to $-2$ | | Alignment Score (Global) | $S = \sum M_i + \sum \gamma(k_j)$ | sum over all aligned positions | — | | Modified DP Recurrence | $F(i,j) = \max\{M(i,j),\, I_x(i,j),\, I_y(i,j)\}$ | three auxiliary matrices | Gotoh (1982) | | Gap Open / Ext Ratio | $\rho = g_{\text{open}} / g_{\text{extend}}$ | ratio constraint | often $\geq 5$ | > [!NOTE] > **Constraint Used in KTU Problems:** The opening penalty is always **more negative** than the extension penalty, i.e., $\vert g_{\text{open}} \vert > \vert g_{\text{extend}} \vert$. A standard rule-of-thumb used by tools like EMBOSS and ClustalW is $g_{\text{open}} \approx 10 \times g_{\text{extend}}$. ## 2.3 Engineering and Biological Utility - **Phylogenetic Analysis:** Accurate gap penalties prevent over-counting evolutionary distance between divergent species. - **Gene Prediction & Homology Search:** Tools such as BLAST use affine gap penalties by default to model intron-exon splice events where gaps occur in long clusters. - **Protein Structure Modeling:** Long gaps correspond to surface loops; tuning affine penalties prevents misaligning conserved alpha-helix or beta-sheet core regions. - **Database Search Sensitivity:** A wrongly-tuned gap penalty reduces both *sensitivity* (true homologs missed) and *specificity* (false homologs accepted). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Linear to Affine Penalty Recurrence In the classical Needleman-Wunsch algorithm, every gap cell is treated identically. Let $F(i,j)$ denote the optimal alignment score of the prefixes $A[1..i]$ and $B[1..j]$. Under a **linear** gap penalty, the recurrence is: $$F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1,\, j-1) + s(A_i, B_j) \\ F(i-1,\, j) + g \\ F(i,\, j-1) + g \end{cases}$$ where $s(A_i, B_j)$ is the substitution score for matching $A_i$ against $B_j$, and $g$ is the per-residue linear gap penalty. **Step 1 — Identify the limitation.** Both horizontal moves $F(i-1,j)$ and vertical moves $F(i,j-1)$ apply the **same** cost $g$ whether or not the gap is being newly opened or already extended. Biologically, this means the algorithm cannot reward the grouping of consecutive gaps. **Step 2 — Introduce auxiliary gap matrices.** Following Gotoh (1982), we split the state into two auxiliary matrices: - $I_x(i,j)$ — optimal score ending in a **gap in sequence A** (vertical gap). - $I_y(i,j)$ — optimal score ending in a **gap in sequence B** (horizontal gap). **Step 3 — Write the three affine recurrences.** For a gap in sequence A (vertical movement): $$I_x(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1,\, j) + g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \\ I_x(i-1,\, j) + g_{\text{extend}} \end{cases}$$ For a gap in sequence B (horizontal movement): $$I_y(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i,\, j-1) + g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \\ I_y(i,\, j-1) + g_{\text{extend}} \end{cases}$$ For the main alignment matrix: $$F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1,\, j-1) + s(A_i, B_j) \\ I_x(i,j) \\ I_y(i,j) \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 — Verify that affine collapses to linear.** If we set $g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} = g$ (i.e., $g_{\text{open}} = 0$), then the affine recurrence degenerates into the linear recurrence. This is the **boundary condition** often asked in KTU viva questions. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example (Affine Penalty) **Given Sequences:** - Sequence A (length 5): `A T C G A` - Sequence B (length 5): `A C G A` **Given Scoring Scheme:** - Match: $+2$ - Mismatch: $-1$ - Gap opening: $g_{\text{open}} = -5$ - Gap extension: $g_{\text{extend}} = -1$ **Step 1 — Compute $\gamma(k)$ for various gap lengths using the affine formula.** $$\gamma(1) = -5 + (-1)(1-1) = -5$$ $$\gamma(2) = -5 + (-1)(2-1) = -6$$ $$\gamma(3) = -5 + (-1)(3-1) = -7$$ $$\gamma(4) = -5 + (-1)(4-1) = -8$$ $$\gamma(5) = -5 + (-1)(5-1) = -9$$ > [!NOTE] > Observation: A linear model with $g = -1.8$ would also give $\gamma(5) = -9$, but the affine model charges $-5$ upfront for just a single gap, which discourages short isolated gaps but tolerates long contiguous gaps cheaply. This is exactly the biological signature of an indel event. **Step 2 — Compute the optimal alignment manually.** Let us enumerate candidate alignments and compute their scores using $\gamma_{\text{affine}}$. **Alignment Candidate 1:** ``` A T C G A - A - C G A T ``` - Pairwise scores: $+2$, $-1$ (T vs gap), $+2$, $+2$, $+2$, $-5$ (open + 1 extend on bottom) - Wait — let us re-do this systematically. Traceback: Let us build the recurrence table for the simpler problem $A = \text{ATGA}$, $B = \text{ACGA}$: Recurrence with affine gap cost: - $F(0,0) = 0$ - $F(0,j) = j \cdot g_{\text{open}} + (j-1) \cdot g_{\text{extend}}$ — but for simplicity in this worked example, we focus on the score formula. **Step 3 — Final alignment score under affine model:** Let us consider the best alignment: ``` A T C G A A - C G A ``` Match columns: - A vs A: $+2$ - T vs gap: $\gamma(1) = -5$ - C vs C: $+2$ - G vs G: $+2$ - A vs A: $+2$ $$S_{\text{affine}} = 2 + (-5) + 2 + 2 + 2 = 3$$ **Step 4 — Compare with the linear penalty equivalent** (using $g_{\text{lin}} = -2$ for instance): $$S_{\text{linear}} = 2 + (-2) + 2 + 2 + 2 = 6$$ The linear model gives a higher score because it does not punish the isolated single gap as harshly. This illustrates why **the choice of penalty model changes the optimal alignment itself**, not merely its score. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Linear Penalty | Affine Penalty | |---|---|---| | Number of parameters | 1 | 2 | | Computational cost | $O(nm)$ | $O(nm)$ | | Treats isolated vs grouped gaps equally? | Yes | No | | Biologically realistic? | Low | High | | Used by BLAST? | No | Yes (default) | | KTPE861 emphasis | Foundational | **High-Yield** | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing a Gap Penalty Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Two Biological Sequences to Align"] --> B{"Are sequences
closely related
same length?"} B -- "Yes, homologs" --> C["Use Linear Gap Penalty
g approx -2 to -4"] B -- "No, divergent
with indels" --> D{"Are long clusters
of gaps expected?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["Use Affine Gap Penalty
g_open = -10, g_ext = -1"] D -- "No, scattered" --> F["Use Linear Gap Penalty
with high g"] C --> G["Run Needleman-Wunsch
Single DP Matrix"] E --> H["Run Gotoh Algorithm
Three DP Matrices F, Ix, Iy"] F --> G G --> I["Output Alignment
and Score"] H --> I ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Architecture of Gotoh's Affine Gap Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs SeqA["Sequence A
length m"] SeqB["Sequence B
length n"] Scorer["Substitution
Scoring Matrix
e.g. BLOSUM62"] Penalties["Penalty Parameters
g_open, g_extend"] end subgraph ProcessingUnit["DP Engine - Gotoh 1982"] MatF["Matrix F
Best Score"] MatIx["Matrix Ix
Gap in A"] MatIy["Matrix Iy
Gap in B"] Recurrence["Recurrence Update
Cell by Cell"] end subgraph Outputs Traceback["Traceback
Path Reconstruction"] FinalAlign["Optimal Aligned
Sequences"] FinalScore["Optimal
Alignment Score"] end SeqA --> Recurrence SeqB --> Recurrence Scorer --> Recurrence Penalties --> Recurrence Recurrence --> MatF Recurrence --> MatIx Recurrence --> MatIy MatF --> Traceback MatIx --> Traceback MatIy --> Traceback Traceback --> FinalAlign Traceback --> FinalScore ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Components of the Affine Gap Penalty ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["Affine Gap Penalty
gamma of k"] --> Open["Gap Opening Component
g_open
charged ONCE"] Root --> Ext["Gap Extension Component
g_extend times k minus 1
charged REPEATEDLY"] Open --> OpenBio["Models initiation of
an indel mutation event"] Ext --> ExtBio["Models slippage of
DNA polymerase during
replication"] Root --> Final["gamma k = g_open + g_extend times k minus 1"] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Define the term "gap penalty" in the context of pairwise sequence alignment. Differentiate between linear and affine gap penalty models.** **Model Answer:** A **gap penalty** is a negative numerical score assigned by a sequence alignment algorithm to represent the biological cost of inserting or deleting residues in one sequence to align it with another. It appears as a parameter in Dynamic Programming recurrences used by algorithms such as Needleman-Wunsch and Smith-Waterman. **Linear Gap Penalty:** Charges a constant cost $g$ per residue regardless of whether the gap is newly opened or being extended. The total cost for a gap of length $k$ is $\gamma(k) = g \cdot k$. **Affine Gap Penalty:** Distinguishes between the cost of *opening* a new gap ($g_{\text{open}}$, a large negative value) and *extending* an already-open gap ($g_{\text{extend}}$, a smaller negative value). The total cost is $\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \cdot (k-1)$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write the affine formula incorrectly as $\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} \cdot k + g_{\text{extend}}$. The correct form is $\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}}(k-1)$. Mark deduction of 1 mark for this mistake. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Why is the affine gap penalty biologically more realistic than a simple linear gap penalty? Give one example where a linear penalty would suffice.** **Model Answer:** The affine gap penalty is biologically more realistic because in nature, mutations such as insertions or deletions tend to occur as **single evolutionary events** that produce **clusters** of consecutive gaps rather than isolated, scattered gaps. The affine model charges a high "opening" cost once and then a small "extension" cost per additional residue, thereby rewarding alignments where gaps are grouped together. A **linear gap penalty would suffice** when aligning two sequences of nearly equal length and very high similarity (e.g., two isoforms of the same protein), where the few differences are point mutations and not indels. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Internal Choice]` **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the affine gap penalty model in detail. Derive the three Dynamic Programming recurrences required to incorporate affine gap penalties into the global alignment algorithm. **(b) (7 Marks)** Given the sequences `A = GATTACA` and `B = GCATGCU` (note: U in B is a typo for T, ignore it — use `B = GCATGCA`), compute the optimal global alignment score using: - Match score: $+2$ - Mismatch score: $-1$ - Gap opening penalty: $g_{\text{open}} = -5$ - Gap extension penalty: $g_{\text{extend}} = -1$ Show all DP matrix steps. #### Model Solution for (a): The affine gap penalty is given by: $$\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \cdot (k-1)$$ To incorporate this into the DP, we define three matrices $F(i,j)$, $I_x(i,j)$, $I_y(i,j)$: **Matrix $F(i,j)$** — best alignment score ending in a match/mismatch at $(i,j)$: $$F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1,\, j-1) + s(A_i, B_j) \\ I_x(i,j) \\ I_y(i,j) \end{cases}$$ **Matrix $I_x(i,j)$** — best score ending in a gap in sequence A (i.e., aligned with $B_j$): $$I_x(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1,\, j) + g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \\ I_x(i-1,\, j) + g_{\text{extend}} \end{cases}$$ **Matrix $I_y(i,j)$** — best score ending in a gap in sequence B (i.e., aligned with $A_i$): $$I_y(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i,\, j-1) + g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \\ I_y(i,\, j-1) + g_{\text{extend}} \end{cases}$$ Boundary conditions: $F(0,0) = 0$; $F(i,0) = I_x(i,0) = g_{\text{open}} + (i-1) \cdot g_{\text{extend}}$; $F(0,j) = I_y(0,j) = g_{\text{open}} + (j-1) \cdot g_{\text{extend}}$. The optimal score is $F(m,n)$. #### Model Solution for (b): For brevity in this model solution we provide the **scoring logic and final alignment**, since a full 7×7 matrix would consume considerable space. Students should construct the matrix in exam conditions. **Step 1:** Build $7 \times 7$ DP matrices initialized with $F(0,0)=0$, first row/column filled using $g_{\text{open}} + (k-1)g_{\text{extend}}$. For row 1, col 1 (G vs G): match score $+2$, so $F(1,1)=2$. For row 1, col 2 (G vs C): mismatch $-1$, best is $F(0,1)+(-1) = -4 + (-1) = -5$ vs vertical gap $-6$. So $F(1,2) = -5$. Continuing the entire table (students to fill) gives: | | - | G | C | A | T | G | C | A | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **-** | 0 | -5 | -6 | -7 | -8 | -9 | -10 | -11 | | **G** | -5 | 2 | -3 | -4 | -5 | -6 | -7 | -8 | | **A** | -6 | -3 | 1 | 0 | -1 | -2 | -3 | -4 | | **T** | -7 | -4 | -2 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0 | -1 | | **T** | -8 | -5 | -3 | -1 | 2 | 1 | 0 | -1 | | **A** | -9 | -6 | -4 | -2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | | **C** | -10 | -7 | -5 | -3 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 2 | | **A** | -11 | -8 | -6 | -4 | -1 | -1 | 2 | **5** | **Step 2:** Final optimal alignment score = $\mathbf{5}$. **Step 3:** Traceback yields: ``` G A T T A C A G C A T G C A ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to apply the affine penalty correctly during traceback. If a vertical move extends an already-open gap, the cost is **only $g_{\text{extend}}$**, not $g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}}$. A 2-mark deduction is standard for misclassifying extension vs opening during traceback reconstruction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2 Internal Choice – Alternative]` **(a) (7 Marks)** Compare and contrast linear and affine gap penalty models. With a neat sketch of the Dynamic Programming matrix, explain how the three-matrix Gotoh algorithm extends the Needleman-Wunsch algorithm. **(b) (7 Marks)** A bioinformatics lab is aligning two homologous protein sequences using the following parameters: Match $= +3$, Mismatch $= -1$, $g_{\text{open}} = -8$, $g_{\text{extend}} = -1$. Compute the total penalty cost for the following proposed alignment: ``` Sequence 1: K V - - L M N T Sequence 2: K V F G L M N T ``` Is this an "affine-friendly" or "linear-friendly" alignment, and why? #### Model Solution for (a): **Comparison Table (students must write):** - Linear uses one DP matrix; Affine uses three ($F$, $I_x$, $I_y$). - Linear has one parameter $g$; Affine has two ($g_{\text{open}}$, $g_{\text{extend}}$). - Linear treats isolated vs grouped gaps identically; Affine rewards grouping. - Both run in $O(nm)$ time and $O(nm)$ space (with optimization $O(m)$). **Sketch of Three Matrices:** Draw three grids of size $(m+1)\times(n+1)$. Cell $(i,j)$ of $F$ depends on its diagonals (match/mismatch); $I_x$ depends on $F(i-1,j)$ for opening and $I_x(i-1,j)$ for extension; $I_y$ depends on $F(i,j-1)$ and $I_y(i,j-1)$ analogously. Arrows during traceback distinguish whether a step extends a previously opened gap. #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1:** Identify gaps in Sequence 1. There is **one gap of length $k = 2$** at positions 3 and 4 (residues $F$ and $G$ are missing from Sequence 1). **Step 2:** Compute the affine gap cost using $\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \cdot (k-1)$: $$\gamma(2) = -8 + (-1)(2-1) = -8 + (-1) = -9$$ **Step 3:** Compute the substitution score (match + match + match + match + match + match = six matches): $$S_{\text{sub}} = 6 \times (+3) = 18$$ **Step 4:** Compute the linear gap cost for comparison using $g_{\text{lin}} = -3$ (a typical linear penalty): $$\gamma_{\text{linear}}(2) = 2 \times (-3) = -6$$ **Step 5:** Final scores: - Affine model: $18 + (-9) = \mathbf{9}$ - Linear model: $18 + (-6) = \mathbf{12}$ **Conclusion:** This alignment is **affine-friendly** because the two missing residues form a **single contiguous gap**, which is exactly the biological scenario affine penalties are designed to reward. The high opening cost is paid only once, and the extension cost is minimal. A linear penalty would unfairly treat these two residues as if they were two independent mutations. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often mistakenly compute the gap cost as $g_{\text{open}} \cdot k$ instead of $g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}}(k-1)$. This is the most common 2-mark loss in Part B gap-penalty questions. Memorize the formula as "one opening, plus extensions." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Gap Penalty =** A negative score applied to align mismatched-length sequences. - **Linear Model:** $\gamma(k) = g \cdot k$. One parameter. Used for closely related sequences. - **Affine Model:** $\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}} \cdot (k-1)$. Two parameters. Biologically realistic. - **Default Affine Ratio:** $g_{\text{open}}$ is roughly **10 times** more negative than $g_{\text{extend}}$ (e.g., $-10 / -1$). - **Gotoh's Algorithm:** Implements affine penalties using **three** DP matrices: $F$, $I_x$, $I_y$. - **Boundary Conditions:** First row/column require cumulative penalty $g_{\text{open}} + (k-1)g_{\text{extend}}$, not $g_{\text{open}} \cdot k$. - **Traceback Rule:** Distinguish between *opening* a gap (cost $g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}}$) and *extending* an existing gap (cost $g_{\text{extend}}$ only). - **Computational Complexity:** Both linear and affine Needleman-Wunsch run in $O(nm)$ time — affine does **not** increase asymptotic complexity. - **Tool Default:** BLAST, ClustalW, and EMBOSS all use affine gap penalties by default. - **Examiner's Favorite Traps:** (i) Using $\vert$ in markdown tables; (ii) writing $\gamma(k) = g_{\text{open}} \cdot k$; (iii) forgetting that a single opening event charges $g_{\text{open}} + g_{\text{extend}}$ for the *first* residue.

topics.title: CLUSTAL and Its Variants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CLUSTAL and Its Variants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CLUSTAL** is a family of widely-used computer programs for performing **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** of nucleotide or amino acid sequences. The name is a play on "CLUSTER" + "Alignment," reflecting its core strategy of hierarchically clustering sequences before progressively aligning them.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CLUSTAL is classified as a **progressive (hierarchical) global multiple sequence alignment tool** that uses a three-stage pipeline: (i) pairwise distance estimation, (ii) guide tree (phylogenetic) construction, and (iii) progressive alignment of sequences according to the guide tree topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 — Sequence Alignment):**
> *"CLUSTAL is a progressive multiple sequence alignment algorithm that uses pairwise alignment scores to build a guide tree, which then dictates the order in which sequences/profiles are aligned."*

## 1.2 Variants of CLUSTAL — A Concise Catalog

| Variant | Year | Key Innovation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CLUSTALV** | 1992 | First version with weighted sequences | Small alignments (≤ 50 seqs) |
| **CLUSTALW** | 1994 | Introduced **W**eighted sequences & position-specific gap penalties; command-line | Standard benchmark tool |
| **CLUSTALX** | 1997 | **X** = GUI front-end for CLUSTALW (Windows/Linux) | Teaching & interactive use |
| **CLUSTALW2** | 2007 | Bug fixes, improved accuracy | Legacy pipelines |
| **CLUSTAL OMEGA (ω)** | 2011 | Replaces progressive with **HMM-HMM alignment** via HHalign; mBed clustering | Large-scale (100,000+ sequences) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kebab-case Naming Convention:** Lowercase ("clustalo", "clustalw") is the modern command-line invocation; the tools still respond to capitalized names in literature.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Reunion Seating Problem"

Imagine a large Indian wedding where **100 cousins** from different branches of a family must be seated at one long dinner table. The host (the algorithm) doesn't know each cousin's "closeness" directly. So the host:

1. **Step 1 — Pairwise Introductions:** Asks each pair of cousins how related they feel → builds a *distance* between every two.
2. **Step 2 — Family Tree:** Joins the closest cousins first, then their immediate families, then larger branches — building a *family tree* (guide tree).
3. **Step 3 — Progressive Seating:** Now seats the family in the order of the tree — closest relatives sit next to each other first, then merges happen between already-formed groups.

The **family tree is the guide tree**, the **closeness metric is the sequence identity**, and the **final seating chart is the multiple sequence alignment**. This is exactly what CLUSTAL does — and explains why errors made at the tree stage *propagate* to the final alignment (a phenomenon called the **"once a gap, always a gap"** problem).

## 1.4 Why CLUSTAL Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CLUSTAL is the **canonical teaching example** for progressive alignment in Module 2. Questions typically test: (a) the three-stage algorithm, (b) the role of the guide tree, (c) variants and their differences, and (d) gap penalty strategies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CLUSTALW Algorithm — Three-Stage Pipeline

CLUSTALW (and its direct descendants) follows a rigorously defined three-stage workflow. We dissect each stage.

### Stage 1 — Pairwise Alignment & Distance Matrix

For every pair of sequences $(S_i, S_j)$ in the input set of $N$ sequences:

1. Perform a **global Needleman–Wunsch pairwise alignment** using a substitution matrix (PAM or BLOSUM for proteins; identity/match for DNA).
2. Convert the resulting alignment score $\sigma_{ij}$ into a pairwise **distance** $d_{ij}$.

**Two conversion formulae are used by CLUSTALW:**

**Formula A — Kimura-style (for proteins with PAM250):**

$$d_{ij} = -\ln\left(1 - s_{ij}\right) - s_{ij}$$

**Formula B — Identity-based (raw fraction):**

$$d_{ij} = 1 - \frac{\text{number of identical positions}}{\text{aligned length}}$$

where $s_{ij}$ is the *fractional similarity score* (normalized raw score).

The result is a symmetric $N \times N$ distance matrix $\mathbf{D}$ with zeros on the diagonal.

### Stage 2 — Guide Tree Construction
