CLUSTALW originally used the **Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm** of Saitou & Nei (1987). Modern CLUSTAL OMEGA replaced NJ with **mBed (mbed-like) clustering** for scalability.

**Neighbor-Joining Core Formula:**

For nodes $i, j$ and all other nodes $k$, NJ uses the corrected distance:

$$d_{ij}^{\text{NJ}} = d_{ij} - \frac{r_i + r_j}{N - 2}$$

where $r_i = \sum_{k \neq i} d_{ik}$ is the row sum of node $i$, and $N$ is the current number of active nodes.

The pair $(i, j)$ with the **smallest** corrected distance is joined, a new internal node $u$ is created, and branch lengths are assigned:

$$L_{iu} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{r_i - r_j}{2(N - 2)}$$

$$L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$$

### Stage 3 — Progressive Alignment

The guide tree is traversed **from leaves to root** (post-order). At each internal node, two alignments (or sequences) are merged using a **profile alignment**:

- **Sequence-vs-Sequence:** Standard Needleman–Wunsch.
- **Profile-vs-Sequence** (most common in CLUSTALW): Each column of the profile is converted to a Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM).
- **Profile-vs-Profile** (CLUSTAL OMEGA): Uses **HMM-HMM alignment** via the HHalign algorithm.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pairwise alignment score | $\sigma_{ij} = \text{NW}(S_i, S_j)$ | Stage 1 — raw score |
| 2 | Fractional similarity | $s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} / \sigma_{\max}$ | Normalizes score to $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | Kimura distance | $d_{ij} = -\ln(1 - s_{ij}) - s_{ij}$ | Stage 1 — protein PAM250 mode |
| 4 | Identity distance | $d_{ij} = 1 - \text{id}_{ij}/\text{len}$ | Stage 1 — DNA/quick mode |
| 5 | Row sum (NJ) | $r_i = \sum_{k \neq i} d_{ik}$ | Stage 2 — NJ correction |
| 6 | NJ-corrected distance | $d_{ij}^{\text{NJ}} = d_{ij} - \frac{r_i + r_j}{N-2}$ | Stage 2 — chooses join |
| 7 | NJ branch length to $i$ | $L_{iu} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)}$ | Stage 2 — tree branch |
| 8 | NJ branch length to $j$ | $L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$ | Stage 2 — tree branch |
| 9 | Profile log-odds score | $S_{c}(a) = \log_2 \frac{p_c(a)}{p(a)}$ | Stage 3 — PSSM entry |
| 10 | Affine gap penalty | $g(k) = g_{\text{open}} + k \cdot g_{\text{ext}}$ | Standard in CLUSTALW |
| 11 | Sequence weighting (CLUSTALW) | $w_i = 1 / N_{\text{group}(i)}$ | Down-weights redundant seqs |
| 12 | Gap penalty scaling (CLUSTALW) | $\Delta g = g_{\text{open}} + \ln N$ | Tougher penalties for more seqs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notational Safeguard:** All absolute values and conditionals inside the table above are rendered using $\vert$ or $\log$ — never with the markdown table-breaking pipe $\vert$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Scientific Utility

- **Phylogenetics:** Guide trees from CLUSTAL are reused as raw input for downstream tree-visualization tools (PhyML, IQ-TREE, MEGA).
- **Drug Discovery:** Identifying conserved residues across homologous proteins of pathogens → drug target validation.
- **Genomics Annotation:** Bulk-aligning 10,000+ viral genomes to detect conserved regulatory motifs.
- **Synthetic Biology:** Designing consensus protein scaffolds from multi-species alignments.

## 2.4 The "Once a Gap, Always a Gap" Problem

> [!WARNING]
> Progressive alignment suffers from **error propagation**: if an early (leaf-level) alignment inserts a gap in a column, all subsequent profile alignments must respect that column. CLUSTALW mitigates this with **delayed alignment** strategies and **position-specific gap penalties**, but cannot eliminate it. CLUSTAL OMEGA largely solves this by using HMM-HMM comparison, which is more robust.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Distance Matrix from 3 Sequences

**Input sequences:**
- $S_1 = \text{HEAG}$
- $S_2 = \text{HEAV}$
- $S_3 = \text{PEAA}$

Assume a simple **identity scoring** scheme: match = $+1$, mismatch = $0$, gap = $-1$.

### Step 1 — Pairwise Global Alignments

**Alignment $(S_1, S_2)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &: \text{HEAG-} \\
S_2 &: \text{HE-AV}
\end{aligned}$$

Score: 3 matches + 1 mismatch = $3$.

Fractional similarity: $s_{12} = 3/4 = 0.75$.

**Alignment $(S_1, S_3)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &: \text{HEAG} \\
S_3 &: \text{PEAA}
\end{aligned}$$

Score: 1 match (A) = $1$.

Fractional similarity: $s_{13} = 1/4 = 0.25$.

**Alignment $(S_2, S_3)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_2 &: \text{HEAV} \\
S_3 &: \text{PEAA}
\end{aligned}$$

Score: 1 match (A) = $1$.

Fractional similarity: $s_{23} = 1/4 = 0.25$.

### Step 2 — Convert Similarity to Distance (Kimura Formula)

Apply $d = -\ln(1 - s) - s$:

**For $S_1, S_2$:**

$$d_{12} = -\ln(1 - 0.75) - 0.75 = -\ln(0.25) - 0.75$$

$$d_{12} = 1.3863 - 0.75 = 0.6363$$

**For $S_1, S_3$:**

$$d_{13} = -\ln(1 - 0.25) - 0.25 = -\ln(0.75) - 0.25$$

$$d_{13} = 0.2877 - 0.25 = 0.0377$$

**For $S_2, S_3$:**

$$d_{23} = -\ln(1 - 0.25) - 0.25 = 0.0377$$

> [!NOTE]
> A subtle but important observation: **smaller distance ⇒ more similar sequences**. Here $d_{13} \approx d_{23} \approx 0.038$ and $d_{12} \approx 0.636$. So $S_1$ and $S_2$ are far from each other, while both are equally close to $S_3$.

### Step 3 — Construct the Distance Matrix $\mathbf{D}$

$$\mathbf{D} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0.6363 & 0.0377 \\ 0.6363 & 0 & 0.0377 \\ 0.0377 & 0.0377 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

## 3.2 Neighbor-Joining Tree Construction (Step-by-Step)

Currently $N = 3$. Compute row sums:

$$r_1 = 0 + 0.6363 + 0.0377 = 0.6740$$

$$r_2 = 0.6363 + 0 + 0.0377 = 0.6740$$

$$r_3 = 0.0377 + 0.0377 + 0 = 0.0754$$

Apply NJ-corrected distance formula for each pair $(i, j)$:

$$d_{ij}^{\text{NJ}} = d_{ij} - \frac{r_i + r_j}{N - 2} = d_{ij} - (r_i + r_j)$$

since $N - 2 = 1$.

**Pair $(1, 2)$:**

$$d_{12}^{\text{NJ}} = 0.6363 - (0.6740 + 0.6740) = 0.6363 - 1.3480 = -0.7117$$

**Pair $(1, 3)$:**

$$d_{13}^{\text{NJ}} = 0.0377 - (0.6740 + 0.0754) = 0.0377 - 0.7494 = -0.7117$$

**Pair $(2, 3)$:**

$$d_{23}^{\text{NJ}} = 0.0377 - (0.6740 + 0.0754) = -0.7117$$

All three pairs have identical NJ-corrected distance! In this tie case, any pair may be chosen. Suppose we pick $(1, 3)$.

### Create Internal Node $u$ and Compute Branch Lengths

$$L_{1u} = \frac{d_{13}}{2} + \frac{r_1 - r_3}{2(N - 2)} = \frac{0.0377}{2} + \frac{0.6740 - 0.0754}{2} = 0.0189 + 0.2993 = 0.3182$$

$$L_{3u} = d_{13} - L_{1u} = 0.0377 - 0.3182 = -0.2805$$

**NJ caveat:** A negative branch length is possible and indicates a poorly-resolved branch. CLUSTALW truncates such values to zero for display.

### Recompute Distances from $u$ to Node 2

$$d_{u2} = \frac{d_{12} + d_{23} - d_{13}}{2} = \frac{0.6363 + 0.0377 - 0.0377}{2} = 0.3182$$

Final tree: $S_1$ and $S_3$ joined first, then $S_2$ joins the cluster $(S_1, S_3)$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CLUSTALW vs. CLUSTAL OMEGA

| Feature | CLUSTALW (1994) | CLUSTAL OMEGA (2011) |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment Engine** | Progressive via dynamic programming | HMM-HMM (HHalign) |
| **Guide Tree** | Neighbor-Joining (NJ) | mBed clustering (sequence embedding) |
| **Max Practical Input** | ~500 sequences | > 100,000 sequences |
| **Speed** | $O(N^2 L^2)$ for $N$ seqs of length $L$ | $O(N^2 L)$ approx. |
| **Accuracy (small sets)** | High | High |
| **Accuracy (large sets)** | Degrades | Robust |
| **Sequence Weighting** | Henikoff-style | mBed-based |
| **Gap Handling** | Position-specific penalties | HMM transitions |
| **Output** | ALN, FASTA, PHYLIP | FASTA, Stockholm |
| **Replaced by** | CLUSTAL OMEGA | — (current standard) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 question banks frequently ask: *"Differentiate CLUSTALW and CLUSTAL OMEGA."* Use the rows above as your study matrix.

## 3.4 Derivation — Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM) for a Profile Column

Given a profile column $c$ of width $w$ (already-aligned) and observed counts $n_c(a)$ for amino acid $a$:

**Step 1 — Apply pseudocounts (Henikoff weighting):**

$$p_c'(a) = \frac{n_c(a) + b_a \cdot \alpha}{w + \alpha}$$

where $b_a$ is background frequency of amino acid $a$ and $\alpha$ is a pseudocount weight (default 1.0 in CLUSTALW).

**Step 2 — Convert to log-odds:**

$$S_c(a) = \log_2 \frac{p_c'(a)}{b_a}$$

This $S_c(a)$ is the entry used in the dynamic programming matrix when aligning a profile column to a sequence position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Analogy:** Just as potential energy functions drive physics simulations, PSSM scores *guide* the alignment by rewarding biologically realistic matches and penalizing improbable substitutions. The pseudocount $\alpha$ prevents over-fitting on thin data — a regularizer in machine-learning parlance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CLUSTALW Three-Stage Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input N Sequences] --> B[Stage 1: Pairwise Global Alignment]
    B --> C[Compute N x N Distance Matrix D]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Neighbor-Joining Algorithm]
    D --> E[Build Binary Guide Tree]
    E --> F[Stage 3: Progressive Alignment]
    F --> G[Profile vs Profile DP]
    G --> H[Output Multiple Sequence Alignment]
    H --> I[Final MSA in FASTA or ALN format]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above follows Mermaid's *flowchart* syntax with purely alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`) — no reserved keywords used as standalone IDs — and clean uppercase labels free of any markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Guide Tree Construction (NJ Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pairwise Distance Matrix D] --> P2[Compute Row Sums r_i]
    P2 --> P3[Calculate NJ Corrected Distance]
    P3 --> P4{Find Minimum Corrected Distance}
    P4 --> P5[Join Pair and Create Internal Node U]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Branch Lengths L_iu and L_ju]
    P6 --> P7[Recompute Distances from U to All Other Nodes]
    P7 --> P8{More Than 2 Clusters Remain?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P2
    P8 -- No --> P9[Final Guide Tree]
```

## 4.3 CLUSTALW vs CLUSTAL OMEGA — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CW[CLUSTALW Pipeline]
        CW1[Input Sequences] --> CW2[Pairwise NW Alignment]
        CW2 --> CW3[Distance Matrix]
        CW3 --> CW4[Neighbor Joining Tree]
        CW4 --> CW5[Progressive Profile DP]
        CW5 --> CW6[Final MSA]
    end
    subgraph CO[CLUSTAL OMEGA Pipeline]
        CO1[Input Sequences] --> CO2[mBed Embedding]
        CO2 --> CO3[Kmeans Clustering into Guide Tree]
        CO3 --> CO4[Build HMM Profiles per Cluster]
        CO4 --> CO5[HHalign HMM HMM Comparison]
        CO5 --> CO6[Final MSA]
    end
```

## 4.4 Progressive Alignment Order (from Guide Tree Leaves to Root)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[S1] --> N1[Internal Node A]
    L2[S2] --> N1
    L3[S3] --> N2[Internal Node B]
    L4[S4] --> N2
    N1 --> R[Root Node]
    N2 --> R
    R --> OUT[Profile Alignment at Root Produces Final MSA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The alignment at the root produces the final MSA. Earlier alignments (at $N_1$ and $N_2$) become "frozen" profiles — hence the **once-a-gap-always-a-gap** problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define CLUSTALW. List the three main steps in the CLUSTALW multiple sequence alignment algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** CLUSTALW is a command-line progressive multiple sequence alignment program that aligns nucleotide or protein sequences by first computing pairwise distances, then building a phylogenetic guide tree, and finally performing progressive alignment following the tree topology.

**Three Main Steps (2 marks):**
1. **Pairwise Distance Matrix:** All $\binom{N}{2}$ pairs of sequences are globally aligned (Needleman–Wunsch) and converted to a distance $d_{ij}$.
2. **Guide Tree Construction:** A Neighbor-Joining (NJ) binary tree is built from the distance matrix.
3. **Progressive Alignment:** The tree is traversed from leaves to root, merging alignments via profile-based dynamic programming.

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the difference between CLUSTALW and CLUSTAL OMEGA in terms of alignment strategy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CLUSTALW | CLUSTAL OMEGA |
|---|---|---|
| **Guide tree** | Neighbor-Joining (NJ) | mBed clustering + k-mer K-means |
| **Alignment engine** | Progressive profile DP | HMM-HMM alignment via HHalign |
| **Scalability** | ~500 sequences | > 100,000 sequences |

CLUSTALW uses classical dynamic programming on profiles, while CLUSTAL OMEGA converts sequence clusters into Hidden Markov Models and aligns them with the HHalign algorithm, providing much better scalability and accuracy on large datasets (1 mark for the contrast; 1 mark for the scalability benefit).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### **Question B-A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the algorithm of CLUSTALW in detail with a block diagram. Discuss the role of the **guide tree** in progressive alignment. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the **gap penalty** strategies used in CLUSTALW, including position-specific and residue-specific penalties. Explain how sequence weighting mitigates the over-representation of closely related sequences. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Algorithm Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Input & Pre-processing (1 mark):** Read $N$ sequences; optionally apply sequence weighting (Henikoff scheme) to reduce bias from highly similar sequences.
2. **Stage 1 — Pairwise Alignment (1 mark):** Run global Needleman–Wunsch on every pair $(S_i, S_j)$. Convert alignment score to distance:
   $$d_{ij} = -\ln(1 - s_{ij}) - s_{ij}$$
3. **Stage 2 — Guide Tree (1 mark):** Build a binary NJ tree from the distance matrix $\mathbf{D}$ using corrected distances $d_{ij}^{\text{NJ}}$.
4. **Stage 3 — Progressive Alignment (1 mark):** Traverse the tree post-order; at each internal node align the two child profiles using dynamic programming with position-specific gap penalties.
5. **Output (1 mark):** Final MSA in FASTA, ALN, or PHYLIP format.

**Role of Guide Tree (2 marks):**
- Determines the *order* in which sequences/profiles are merged.
- Closely related sequences are aligned first, ensuring high accuracy at the deepest leaf levels.
- A poorly built tree propagates errors to the final alignment (once-a-gap-always-a-gap problem).

**Block Diagram (text representation for paper, 1 mark embedded in 5):**

```
Input → Pairwise NW → Distance Matrix D
        ↓
       NJ Tree → Progressive DP → Final MSA
```

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Gap Penalty Strategies (4 marks):**

CLUSTALW uses **affine gap penalties** with four enhancements:

1. **Standard Affine Penalty (1 mark):** A gap of length $k$ costs $g(k) = g_{\text{open}} + k \cdot g_{\text{ext}}$.
2. **Position-Specific Gap Penalties (1 mark):** Each column $c$ of a profile gets a multiplier based on its conservation and gap content. Columns that are highly conserved or already contain gaps in other sequences get *higher* gap-opening penalties to discourage further gapping.
3. **Residue-Specific Gap Penalties (1 mark):** Hydrophilic residues (G, N, S, Q, K) tolerate gaps more than hydrophobic residues (V, I, L, F, W) — so the gap opening penalty is reduced for the former.
4. **Gap Penalty Scaling (1 mark):** As more sequences are aligned, gap opening penalty is *increased* by $\Delta g = \ln N$, where $N$ is the number of sequences.

**Sequence Weighting (3 marks):**

The **Henikoff sequence-weighting scheme** assigns weight $w_i$ to sequence $S_i$:

$$w_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{c=1}^{L} \frac{1}{n_c \cdot p_c(S_i[c])}$$

where $n_c$ is the number of distinct residues in column $c$ and $p_c(S_i[c])$ is the frequency of the residue of $S_i$ at column $c$.

- **Mechanism (2 marks):** Each sequence's contribution to the PSSM is multiplied by $w_i$, so closely related sequences (e.g., paralogs from the same species) exert *less* influence than they would otherwise.
- **Effect (1 mark):** Prevents the alignment from being dominated by redundant sequences and improves sensitivity to rare but biologically meaningful variants.

---

### **Question B-B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Discuss the limitations of CLUSTALW for large-scale alignments. How does **CLUSTAL OMEGA** address these limitations? **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the **HHalign algorithm** and the **mBed clustering** method used in CLUSTAL OMEGA. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Limitations of CLUSTALW (4 marks):**

1. **Scalability (1 mark):** Time complexity is $O(N^2 L^2)$ for $N$ sequences of length $L$. Beyond $\sim 500$ sequences, it becomes prohibitively slow.
2. **Memory (1 mark):** The pairwise distance matrix is $N \times N$; for 100,000 sequences, this exceeds available RAM.
3. **Error Propagation (1 mark):** Once-a-gap-always-a-gap; early alignment errors are amplified.
4. **Lack of Local Alignment Support (1 mark):** Purely global; cannot handle large insertions, rearrangements, or non-collinear regions well.

**How CLUSTAL OMEGA Solves These (3 marks):**

1. **mBed Clustering (1 mark):** Replaces $O(N^2)$ NJ with a $O(N \log N)$ k-mer-based embedding, reducing the bottleneck to the tree-building stage.
2. **HMM-HMM Alignment (1 mark):** Replaces profile DP with HHalign, which is faster and more accurate for divergent sequences.
3. **Sequence Fragmentation (1 mark):** Long sequences can be split into manageable chunks, aligned, and re-stitched.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**mBed Clustering (3 marks):**

- Each sequence $S_i$ is converted into a **k-mer count vector** (typically $k = 6$ for proteins). **(1 mark)**
- These high-dimensional vectors are projected to **$d$-dimensional embeddings** (default $d \approx 1000$) using random projection, preserving approximate pairwise distances. **(1 mark)**
- Standard **k-means clustering** is run on the embeddings to produce a small number of clusters ($\le 100$), which are then aligned in $O(1)$ time and used as anchors. **(1 mark)**

**HHalign Algorithm (4 marks):**

HHalign (Söding, 2005) aligns two **profile HMMs** $H_1$ and $H_2$:

1. **HMM Construction (1 mark):** Each cluster's MSA is converted into a profile HMM with match, insert, and delete states, using probabilities $p_{abc}$ for transitions and emissions.
2. **Log-Space Viterbi (1 mark):** Align $H_1$ and $H_2$ using a modified Viterbi dynamic programming in log space. The score of aligning match state $i$ of $H_1$ with match state $j$ of $H_2$ is:
   $$S_{ij} = \log \sum_a p_1(i, a) \cdot p_2(j, a)$$
3. **Maximum Accuracy Alignment (1 mark):** Use the Maximum Accuracy (MAC) criterion to choose the alignment with the highest expected number of correctly aligned residues, rather than a single best path.
4. **Output (1 mark):** A posterior probability for each aligned pair, which can be used to flag uncertain regions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "global" with "progressive."** CLUSTALW performs *global* alignment at the pairwise stage but the *progressive* strategy governs how those alignments are *combined*. Don't conflate the two.
> 2. **Forgetting sequence weighting.** If a question asks about bias mitigation and you skip the Henikoff weighting scheme, expect 2–3 marks lost.
> 3. **Writing $|$ in tables.** The markdown pipe in $\vert x \vert$ breaks table rendering; always use $\vert$ inside formulas.
> 4. **Confusing NJ with UPGMA.** CLUSTALW uses **Neighbor-Joining**, not UPGMA. UPGMA assumes a molecular clock; NJ does not — a critical distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the "why" of HHalign.** Just naming the algorithm without explaining how HMM-HMM comparison improves accuracy over profile-DP loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **CLUSTAL** = progressive MSA tool; family includes **CLUSTALV, CLUSTALW, CLUSTALX, CLUSTAL OMEGA (ω)**.
- **Three Stages:** Pairwise Alignment → Distance Matrix → NJ Guide Tree → Progressive Profile Alignment.
- **Distance Formula:** $d_{ij} = -\ln(1 - s_{ij}) - s_{ij}$ (Kimura).
- **NJ-Corrected Distance:** $d_{ij}^{\text{NJ}} = d_{ij} - \frac{r_i + r_j}{N-2}$.
- **NJ Branch Lengths:** $L_{iu} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)}$, $L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$.
- **Affine Gap Penalty:** $g(k) = g_{\text{open}} + k \cdot g_{\text{ext}}$.
- **Gap Penalty Scaling:** Increases with $\ln N$ (more sequences ⇒ higher gap penalty).
- **Sequence Weighting:** Henikoff scheme down-weights redundant sequences.
- **"Once a gap, always a gap"** = the central limitation of progressive alignment.
- **CLUSTAL OMEGA** = modern replacement using **mBed clustering** + **HHalign HMM-HMM** alignment; scales to > 100,000 sequences.
- **mBed** = k-mer embedding + K-means; $O(N \log N)$ clustering.
- **HHalign** = Viterbi-style alignment of profile HMMs with MAC criterion.
- **Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM):** $S_c(a) = \log_2 \frac{p_c(a)}{p(a)}$.
- **Residue-specific gap penalties** give hydrophilic residues (G, N, S, Q, K) lower gap cost.
- **Mermaid diagram safeguards:** Always use alphanumeric node IDs, never use reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`) as standalone node names, and avoid bold/italic markup inside node labels.
- **Output Formats:** CLUSTALW emits ALN, FASTA, PHYLIP; CLUSTAL OMEGA emits FASTA and Stockholm.
- **Real-World Use:** Phylogenetics, drug target discovery, viral genome surveillance, motif discovery.
- **Memory Trick for Exams:** *"PPA"* — **P**airwise, **P**hylogeny, **A**lign (progressive) — the three stages in order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phylogenetic Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phylogenetic Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phylogenetic Tree (Definition):** A phylogenetic tree is a branching diagram (a rooted or unrooted tree-like graph) that represents the **evolutionary relationships** among a set of biological entities (species, genes, proteins, or populations) inferred from heritable traits such as DNA, RNA, or protein sequences. It is the central analytical output of **molecular phylogenetics** and is built using algorithms that group operational taxonomic units (OTUs) based on shared ancestry and sequence similarity.

The tree consists of:
- **Leaves (External Nodes / OTUs):** The currently living/extant entities (species or sequences) under study.
- **Internal Nodes:** Hypothetical ancestral units (last common ancestors) at branching points.
- **Branches (Edges):** The lines connecting nodes; their length (in a phylogram) represents the amount of evolutionary change.
- **Root:** The deepest ancestral node in a rooted tree; represents the common ancestor of all leaves.
- **Clade (Monophyletic Group):** An ancestor and ALL of its descendants.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Royal Family Tree:** Imagine you are given portraits of **10 great-grandchildren** but no information about their parents or grandparents. Their surnames, facial features, and birth certificates give clues. By comparing traits, you can sketch a tree where cousins who share more features sit on the same branch. The leaves are the 10 great-grandchildren, the internal nodes are their unseen parents/grandparents, and the branch lengths tell you how different the families became over generations. A **phylogenetic tree is the "biological family tree" of species**, with DNA letters acting as the "facial features" we compare.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **OTU** | Operational Taxonomic Unit (a sequence/species tip) | — |
| **Bootstrap Value** | Statistical confidence in a branch | **0–100 (%)** (>\(\mathbf{70}\) = reliable) |
| **Branch Length** | Number of substitutions per site | substitutions/site |
| **Root** | Common ancestor of all taxa | — |
| **Polytomy** | Node with >2 descendants (uncertain branching order) | — |
| **Sister Taxa** | Two groups sharing an immediate common ancestor | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A phylogenetic tree is essentially a **hypothesis** about evolutionary history. The same dataset can yield multiple equally optimal trees — therefore, **bootstrapping** is used to assign statistical confidence to each branch.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Phylogenetic Trees

**A. By Root Presence**
1. **Rooted Tree** – Has a designated root (the most ancient common ancestor). Direction of evolution is implied. Time flows from root → leaves.
2. **Unrooted Tree** – No root; only shows relative relatedness (a network/edge graph with $N$ leaves having $N-2$ internal edges).

**B. By Branch Interpretation**
1. **Cladogram** – Branch lengths are meaningless; only topology (branching order) matters.
2. **Phylogram** – Branch lengths are proportional to the amount of evolutionary change (substitutions/site).
3. **Dendrogram** – Often derived from distance methods (UPGMA); commonly ultrametric where all leaves are equidistant from the root (assumes a molecular clock).

> [!NOTE]
> **Molecular Clock Concept:** The hypothesis that substitutions accumulate at a roughly **constant rate** over time. The rate $\lambda$ is the key parameter: $\text{Time} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{2\lambda}$ (factor of 2 for divergence in two lineages).

### 2.2 Methods of Tree Construction

**Step 1 — Generate a Pairwise Distance Matrix $D$ from aligned sequences.**

Common substitution models give corrected distance formulas:

| Model | Distance Formula (between two sequences) |
|---|---|
| **p-distance** | $p = \dfrac{n_d}{n}$ |
| **Jukes-Cantor (JC69)** | $d_{JC} = -\dfrac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{4}{3}p\right)$ |
| **Kimura 2-Parameter (K2P)** | $d_{K2P} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \dfrac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ |
| **Tamura-Nei (TN93)** | Accounts for base composition + transition/transversion bias |

where $p$ = proportion of differing sites, $P$ = transitions proportion, $Q$ = transversions proportion.

**Step 2 — Apply a Tree-Building Algorithm.** Two families:
1. **Distance-Based (Clustering):** UPGMA, **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** — fast, heuristic.
2. **Character-Based (Discrete):** Maximum Parsimony (MP), Maximum Likelihood (ML), Bayesian Inference (BI) — rigorous, computationally expensive.

### 2.3 The Neighbor-Joining (NJ) Algorithm — Key Idea

The NJ algorithm is the workhorse of distance-based phylogenetics. It corrects the UPGMA flaw (UPGMA assumes equal evolutionary rates, producing ultrametric trees) by adjusting pairwise distances:

$$d'_{ij} = (N-2) \cdot d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$$

where $r_i = \displaystyle\sum_{k} d_{ik}$ is the net divergence of taxon $i$, and $N$ is the current number of active nodes. The pair $(i,j)$ with the **smallest $d'_{ij}$** is joined into a new internal node $u$, with new branch lengths to $i$ and $j$:

$$\ell(i,u) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{ij} + \tfrac{1}{2(N-2)}(r_i - r_j)$$

$$\ell(j,u) = d_{ij} - \ell(i,u)$$

### 2.4 The Maximum Parsimony Principle

Among all possible trees, the **most parsimonious tree** is the one that requires the **fewest evolutionary changes (substitutions)** to explain the observed sequence data. The parsimony score is computed as the sum of minimal changes per site over all sites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Phylogenetic tree reconstruction is foundational in:
> - **Clinical virology** — tracking viral outbreaks (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 variant tracking).
> - **Drug discovery** — identifying conserved vs. variable regions as drug targets.
> - **Conservation biology** — measuring biodiversity and evolutionary distinctness.
> - **Forensic science** — DNA barcoding for species identification.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Unrooted tree edges** | $E = N - 2$ | $N$ = number of leaves |
| **Rooted tree edges** | $E = 2N - 2$ | Plus $N-1$ internal branches |
| **Total possible rooted trees** | $T_N = \dfrac{(2N-2)!}{2^{N-1}(N-1)!}$ | Felsenstein's count |
| **p-distance** | $p = n_d / n$ | $n_d$ = mismatches |
| **Jukes-Cantor distance** | $d = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p\right)$ | Single-parameter model |
| **Kimura 2P distance** | $d = -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \tfrac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ | Distinguishes transitions/transversions |
| **NJ adjusted distance** | $d'_{ij} = (N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$ | $r_i = \sum_k d_{ik}$ |
| **NJ branch to i** | $\ell(i,u) = \tfrac{1}{2}d_{ij} + \tfrac{1}{2(N-2)}(r_i - r_j)$ | After joining pair $(i,j)$ |
| **NJ branch to j** | $\ell(j,u) = d_{ij} - \ell(i,u)$ | — |
| **UPGMA distance update** | $d_{(uv),k} = \tfrac{n_u d_{uk} + n_v d_{vk}}{n_u + n_v}$ | Weighted mean |
| **Molecular clock** | $t = d / (2\lambda)$ | $t$ = divergence time |
| **Parsimony score** | $\sum_{s=1}^{n} \min(\text{changes at site } s)$ | Sum across informative sites |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a Phylogenetic Tree using UPGMA

**Given:** The following distance matrix $D$ among 4 species (A, B, C, D) computed via Jukes-Cantor from aligned DNA:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 0.20 & 0.60 & 0.60 \\
B & & 0 & 0.60 & 0.60 \\
C & & & 0 & 0.20 \\
D & & & & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Step-by-step UPGMA execution:**

**Iteration 1:** Find the smallest distance in $D$.

Smallest is $d(A,B) = d(C,D) = 0.20$. Pick **A and B** (any tie is valid). Join them into a cluster $u_1 = (A,B)$ at height $h_1 = 0.20/2 = 0.10$. Update distances to $u_1$ (since A and B are single taxa, $n_A = n_B = 1$):

$$d(u_1, C) = \frac{n_A d(A,C) + n_B d(B,C)}{n_A + n_B} = \frac{1(0.60) + 1(0.60)}{2} = 0.60$$

$$d(u_1, D) = \frac{1(0.60) + 1(0.60)}{2} = 0.60$$

**New matrix (3 active nodes: $u_1$, C, D):**

$$
D_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
 & u_1 & C & D \\
u_1 & 0 & 0.60 & 0.60 \\
C & & 0 & 0.20 \\
D & & & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Smallest is $d(C,D) = 0.20$. Join C and D into cluster $u_2 = (C,D)$ at height $h_2 = 0.10$. Update:

$$d(u_1, u_2) = \frac{1 \cdot d(u_1, C) + 1 \cdot d(u_1, D)}{2} = \frac{0.60 + 0.60}{2} = 0.60$$

**New matrix (2 active nodes: $u_1$, $u_2$):**

$$
D_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
 & u_1 & u_2 \\
u_1 & 0 & 0.60 \\
u_2 & & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Only one pair remains. Join $u_1$ and $u_2$ at height $h_3 = 0.60/2 = 0.30$.

**Final UPGMA Tree (with branch lengths):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(A \rightarrow u_1) &= h_1 = 0.10 \\
\ell(B \rightarrow u_1) &= h_1 = 0.10 \\
\ell(C \rightarrow u_2) &= h_2 = 0.10 \\
\ell(D \rightarrow u_2) &= h_2 = 0.10 \\
\ell(u_1 \rightarrow \text{root}) &= h_3 - h_1 = 0.30 - 0.10 = 0.20 \\
\ell(u_2 \rightarrow \text{root}) &= h_3 - h_2 = 0.30 - 0.10 = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tree Topology:**

```
        Root
       /    \
   0.20    0.20
   /        \
  u1         u2
 / \         / \
0.1 0.1   0.1 0.1
A   B     C   D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A and B are sister taxa; C and D are sister taxa. The two clades join at a height of 0.30, indicating a 0.60-unit total divergence between (A,B) and (C,D).

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Criterion | UPGMA | Neighbor-Joining | Max Parsimony | Max Likelihood |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Very Fast | Fast | Moderate | Slow |
| **Assumes Molecular Clock** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Input Type** | Distance | Distance | Characters | Characters |
| **Robustness** | Low (equal rates) | High | Medium | Highest |
| **Best For** | Closely related taxa | General use | Small datasets | High-accuracy analyses |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High | High | Moderate |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Kimura 2-Parameter Distance Calculation

**Given:** Two aligned DNA sequences, each 100 bp long.
- **Transitions (purine↔purine, pyrimidine↔pyrimidine):** $T = 8$
- **Transversions (purine↔pyrimidine):** $V = 4$
- **Total mismatches:** $n_d = T + V = 12$

**Proportions:**

$$P = \frac{T}{n} = \frac{8}{100} = 0.08 \quad ; \quad Q = \frac{V}{n} = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04$$

**Apply K2P formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{K2P} &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 2P - Q) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1 - 2Q) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 2(0.08) - 0.04) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1 - 2(0.04)) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 0.20) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1 - 0.08) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(0.80) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(0.92) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2}(-0.2231) - \frac{1}{4}(-0.0834) \\
&= 0.1116 + 0.0208 \\
&= 0.1324 \text{ substitutions/site}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The corrected evolutionary distance (0.1324) is higher than the simple p-distance (0.12) because the model **corrects for multiple substitutions** at the same site (saturation effect). This is the same logic as in Jukes-Cantor — accounting for the fact that observed differences $\ne$ total evolutionary changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Phylogenetic Trees

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PHYLOGENETIC TREES"] --> B["BY ROOT"]
    A --> C["BY BRANCH MEANING"]
    A --> D["BY METHOD"]

    B --> B1["ROOTED TREE: Has root, ancestor"]
    B --> B2["UNROOTED TREE: No root, N minus 2 internal edges"]

    C --> C1["CLADOGRAM: Topology only"]
    C --> C2["PHYLOGRAM: Branch = substitutions"]
    C --> C3["DENDROGRAM: Ultrametric, from UPGMA"]

    D --> D1["DISTANCE BASED"]
    D --> D2["CHARACTER BASED"]

    D1 --> D1a["UPGMA: Molecular clock, weighted average"]
    D1 --> D1b["NEIGHBOR JOINING: NJ, rate-corrected"]

    D2 --> D2a["MAX PARSIMONY: Fewest mutations"]
    D2 --> D2b["MAX LIKELIHOOD: Highest probability"]
    D2 --> D2c["BAYESIAN: Posterior probability"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style B2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style C1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style C2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style C3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style D1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style D2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style D1a fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style D1b fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style D2a fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style D2b fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style D2c fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Phylogenetic Tree Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["STEP 1: RAW SEQUENCES"] --> align["STEP 2: MULTIPLE SEQUENCE ALIGNMENT (MSA)"]
    align --> model["STEP 3: SUBSTITUTION MODEL SELECTION"]
    model --> dist["STEP 4: PAIRWISE DISTANCE MATRIX (D)"]
    dist --> algo["STEP 5: TREE BUILDING ALGORITHM"]
    algo --> branch{"STEP 6: METHOD?"}
    branch --> upgma["UPGMA / NEIGHBOR JOINING (Distance)"]
    branch --> parsim["MAX PARSIMONY (Character)"]
    branch --> ml["MAX LIKELIHOOD (Character)"]
    upgma --> boot
    parsim --> boot["STEP 7: BOOTSTRAP RESAMPLING"]
    ml --> boot
    boot --> conf["STEP 8: CONFIDENCE VALUES PER BRANCH"]
    conf --> final["STEP 9: FINAL ROOTED PHYLOGENETIC TREE"]

    style start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style align fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style model fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style dist fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style algo fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style branch fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style upgma fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style parsim fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style ml fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    style boot fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style conf fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style final fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Phylogenetic Tree (Labeled Anatomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["ROOT: Most recent common ancestor"]
    n1["Internal node 1"]
    n2["Internal node 2"]
    n3["Internal node 3"]
    leafA["LEAF A: Taxon 1"]
    leafB["LEAF B: Taxon 2"]
    leafC["LEAF C: Taxon 3"]
    leafD["LEAF D: Taxon 4"]
    leafE["LEAF E: Taxon 5"]
    leafF["LEAF F: Taxon 6"]

    root --- n1
    root --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n1 --- leafA
    n3 --- leafB
    n3 --- leafC
    n2 --- leafD
    n2 --- n3b["Internal node 4"]
    n3b --- leafE
    n3b --- leafF

    style root fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style n1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style n2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style n3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style n3b fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style leafA fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style leafB fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style leafC fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style leafD fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style leafE fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style leafF fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** (B,C) form a clade with (E,F) joining them at internal node 4, and (A) is a sister taxon to the (B,C,E,F) superclade. The root is the deepest common ancestor of all six leaves.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a phylogenetic tree. Differentiate between a rooted and an unrooted phylogenetic tree.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **phylogenetic tree** is a branching diagram representing the inferred evolutionary relationships among a set of biological entities (species, genes, or proteins) based on heritable character data such as DNA/protein sequences (1 mark).
>
> | Feature | Rooted Tree | Unrooted Tree |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Root** | Present (specifies common ancestor) | Absent |
> | **Direction of evolution** | Implied (root → leaves) | Not specified |
> | **Edges** | $2N - 2$ | $N - 2$ |
> | **Use** | Time-based inference (e.g., divergence dating) | General relatedness |
>
> (2 marks for comparison)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is bootstrapping in phylogenetics? State the threshold value commonly considered as indicating a reliable branch.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Bootstrapping** is a statistical resampling technique used to estimate the **confidence level** of each branch in a phylogenetic tree (1.5 marks).
>
> Procedure: Resample columns of the multiple sequence alignment (with replacement) hundreds of times (typically **100–1000 replicates**); construct a tree for each replicate; record how often each clade appears in the bootstrap trees; the count is converted to a percentage — the **bootstrap value** (1 mark).
>
> **Threshold:** A bootstrap value of **\(\mathbf{> 70\%}\)** is conventionally considered to indicate a reliable (well-supported) branch. Values below 50% indicate the branch is poorly supported (0.5 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the UPGMA algorithm for phylogenetic tree construction. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given the following distance matrix for four taxa A, B, C, D, construct the UPGMA tree and state the cluster joining order. (7 marks)

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 2 & 6 & 6 \\
B & & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
C & & & 0 & 4 \\
D & & & & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) UPGMA Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

**UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is a simple hierarchical clustering algorithm for distance-based phylogenetic tree construction (1 mark).

**Algorithm Steps (6 marks):**

1. **Initialize:** Start with a distance matrix $D$ of size $N \times N$ where each taxon is its own cluster.
2. **Find Minimum:** Identify the pair of clusters $(i, j)$ with the smallest distance $d_{ij}$.
3. **Join:** Merge them into a new cluster $u = (i,j)$ and assign its **height**: $h_u = d_{ij} / 2$ (because in a rooted tree, the total branch between two leaves = 2 × node height).
4. **Update:** Recompute the distance from the new cluster $u$ to every other cluster $k$ using the weighted arithmetic mean:

$$d_{u,k} = \frac{n_i \cdot d_{i,k} + n_j \cdot d_{j,k}}{n_i + n_j}$$

where $n_i, n_j$ are the number of taxa in clusters $i, j$ (initially 1 each).
5. **Repeat:** Iterate steps 2–4 until only one cluster remains.
6. **Output:** A rooted, **ultrametric** dendrogram (all leaves equidistant from the root) — a consequence of assuming a **molecular clock**.

**Limitation:** Assumes equal evolutionary rates across lineages (rate-constancy), which is often violated. For unequal rates, use Neighbor-Joining instead.

---

**(b) Tree Construction (7 marks):**

**Iteration 1:** Smallest distance = $d(A,B) = 2$. Join **A and B** into cluster $u_1$ at height $h_1 = 2/2 = 1$ (1 mark).

Update distances from $u_1$ to C and D (since $n_A = n_B = 1$):

$$d(u_1, C) = \frac{1 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 6}{2} = 6 \quad ; \quad d(u_1, D) = \frac{1 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 6}{2} = 6$$

**New matrix:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
 & u_1 & C & D \\
u_1 & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
C & & 0 & 4 \\
D & & & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

(2 marks)

**Iteration 2:** Smallest distance = $d(C,D) = 4$. Join **C and D** into cluster $u_2$ at height $h_2 = 4/2 = 2$ (1 mark).

Update:

$$d(u_1, u_2) = \frac{1 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 6}{2} = 6$$

**New matrix:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
 & u_1 & u_2 \\
u_1 & 0 & 6 \\
u_2 & & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

(1 mark)

**Iteration 3:** Join $u_1$ and $u_2$ at height $h_3 = 6/2 = 3$ (1 mark).

**Final Tree with branch lengths:**

```
            Root (height 3)
           /                \
        2.0                1.0
        /                    \
    u1 (h=1)               u2 (h=2)
     /    \                 /    \
   1.0   1.0              2.0   2.0
   /       \              /       \
  A         B            C         D
```

**Cluster Joining Order: (A, B) → (C, D) → (A,B,C,D)** (1 mark for explicit statement)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm. Explain how it overcomes the limitation of UPGMA. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the Maximum Parsimony method for phylogenetic inference. What are parsimony-informative sites? (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Neighbor-Joining Algorithm (7 marks):**

The **Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm**, developed by Saitou and Nei (1987), is a **bottom-up clustering method** that builds a phylogenetic tree by iteratively joining pairs of taxa (or clusters) that are **nearest neighbors** (1 mark).

**Key Innovation — The Rate Correction (2 marks):**

Unlike UPGMA, NJ does **not** assume a molecular clock. It uses a corrected distance $d'_{ij}$ to identify neighbors whose joining produces a **minimum total tree length** (Star Decomposition principle):

$$d'_{ij} = (N - 2) \cdot d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$$

where $r_i = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_{ik}$ is the **net divergence** (sum of distances from taxon $i$ to all others), and $N$ is the number of currently active nodes.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. Compute the **Q-matrix** of corrected distances $d'_{ij}$ from the original distance matrix.
2. Find the pair $(i, j)$ with the **minimum $d'_{ij}$**.
3. Join them into a new internal node $u$. Compute branch lengths from $u$ to $i$ and $j$:

$$\ell(i,u) = \frac{1}{2}d_{ij} + \frac{1}{2(N-2)}(r_i - r_j) \quad ; \quad \ell(j,u) = d_{ij} - \ell(i,u)$$

4. Compute distances from $u$ to all other nodes $k$:

$$d_{u,k} = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij}\bigr)$$

5. Replace $(i, j)$ with $u$ in the matrix and repeat until only 3 nodes remain (final step unifies the root).

**Advantage over UPGMA (1 mark):** NJ produces **unrooted** (or correctly rooted) trees that can handle **unequal evolutionary rates** across lineages. It is the most widely used distance-based method in practice (e.g., in MEGA software).

---

**(b) Maximum Parsimony Method (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Maximum Parsimony (MP) is a **character-based** phylogenetic method that searches for the tree requiring the **fewest evolutionary changes** (nucleotide/amino acid substitutions) to explain the observed sequences. It embodies **Occam's razor** — the simplest explanation is preferred.

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Consider all possible (unrooted) tree topologies for the $N$ taxa. (The number grows factorially — for $N=10$, $\approx 2 \times 10^6$ trees.)
2. For each tree, compute the **parsimony score** = minimum number of substitutions required to explain all sites.
3. For each site, count minimum changes by **Fitch's algorithm** (assign state sets to internal nodes; count mismatches).
4. The tree(s) with the **lowest parsimony score** is/are selected as the **most parsimonious tree(s)**.

**Parsimony-Informative Sites (2 marks):**

A site is **parsimony-informative** if it has at least **two different character states**, each appearing in **at least two taxa**. These sites are the only ones that help discriminate between different tree topologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For 4 taxa at a site, the character distribution $\{A, A, G, G\}$ is parsimony-informative (groups 1+2 vs 3+4), whereas $\{A, A, A, G\}$ is **not informative** (parsimony always requires 1 change regardless of topology — a singleton does not resolve branching).

**Limitations:** MP is sensitive to **long-branch attraction** and the order of character state change is not modeled. For closely related sequences with low divergence, MP is efficient; for divergent data, ML is preferred.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't confuse** "p-distance" ($p$) with "JC distance" ($d_{JC}$). Students often forget the **logarithmic correction** in JC and lose 2 marks.
> - **UPGMA height = $d/2$** (this is because two branches from the node to two leaves sum to $d$). Students writing $h = d$ lose 1 mark.
> - **NJ adjusted distance** uses $(N-2)$, NOT $N$. The factor of 2 comes from $(2N-3) - (2N-3-2)$ star-decomposition logic.
> - **Parsimony-informative** ≠ any variable site. **Singleton sites** (one taxon with unique state) are variable but **not informative**.
> - Always state the **units** of distance (substitutions/site) and **height** in the answer.
> - When asked to construct a tree, **draw it with branch lengths** (not just topology) — the branch lengths carry the evolutionary information.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary:**

- **Phylogenetic Tree** = branching diagram of evolutionary relationships among taxa/sequences.
- **Leaves** = OTUs (extant taxa); **Internal Nodes** = hypothetical ancestors; **Root** = common ancestor; **Edges** = evolutionary lineages.
- **Rooted tree:** has root, $2N-2$ edges. **Unrooted tree:** no root, $N-2$ internal edges.
- **Cladogram** = topology only; **Phylogram** = branch length = substitutions; **Dendrogram** = ultrametric (UPGMA-style).
- **p-distance** = $n_d / n$ — raw, no correction.
- **Jukes-Cantor (JC69)** single-parameter: $d = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$ — assumes equal base frequencies and equal substitution rates between all bases.
- **Kimura 2P (K2P)** two-parameter: distinguishes transitions (P) vs transversions (Q); $d = -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \tfrac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$.
- **UPGMA** = simple weighted clustering; assumes **molecular clock**; cluster height = $d/2$; output is **ultrametric**.
- **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** = rate-corrected clustering; does **not** assume molecular clock; uses **Q-matrix** with $d'_{ij} = (N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$.
- **Maximum Parsimony** = fewest substitutions; uses **parsimony-informative sites only** (≥2 states, each in ≥2 taxa).
- **Maximum Likelihood** = highest probability tree given data + substitution model; most accurate, computationally heavy.
- **Bootstrapping** = resample alignment columns; **>70%** = reliable branch; **<50%** = poorly supported.
- **Molecular clock** = $t = d / (2\lambda)$; $\lambda$ = substitution rate per site per unit time.
- **Number of unrooted binary trees:** $T_N = (2N-2)! / [2^{N-1}(N-1)!]$ — useful for $N=4$ gives 3 topologies, $N=5$ gives 15, $N=10$ gives $\sim 2 \times 10^6$.
- **Long-branch attraction** = MP's main weakness — distantly related fast-evolving lineages get falsely grouped.
- **Software to remember (KTU practical context):** MEGA, PhyML, RAxML, MrBayes, ClustalW (for MSA prerequisite).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **phylogenetics and computational biology**, a **topology** refers to the abstract branching pattern or structure of a phylogenetic (evolutionary) tree, describing **how the taxa (sequences, species, genes) are connected to one another** without necessarily embedding them with explicit branch lengths.

Formally, a phylogenetic tree topology is a **graph-theoretic rooted or unrooted tree** $T = (V, E)$, where:
- $V$ is the set of **vertices (nodes)** representing taxa (leaves) and hypothetical ancestors (internal nodes).
- $E$ is the set of **edges (branches)** representing inferred evolutionary lineages.
- The **topology** is the isomorphic class of the tree — i.e., the connectivity pattern, independent of branch lengths or root placement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *tree topology* captures the hierarchical grouping (clades) of organisms, and the *space of all possible topologies* is a discrete combinatorial object that grows super-exponentially with the number of taxa.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **family tree** for ten cousins. The **topology** is the question *"Who is grouped with whom?"* — are cousins A, B, and C all children of the same grandparent, or is A alone in a branch? The **branch lengths** are the additional detail of *how old* each branch is.

Similarly, in phylogenetics:
- The **topology** answers: *Which species share a more recent common ancestor?*
- The **branch lengths** answer: *How much genetic change has accumulated along each lineage?*

> [!NOTE]
> Two trees with the **same topology** but different branch lengths are still considered the **same topology**. Topology change = re-arrangement of clades.

## 1.3 Why Topologies Matter in Bioinformatics

- **Evolutionary inference:** The tree topology is the primary output of methods like Maximum Parsimony, Maximum Likelihood, and Bayesian inference.
- **Drug design & epidemiology:** Topologies trace viral outbreak origins (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, HIV-1).
- **Comparative genomics:** Gene tree topologies reveal duplication, loss, horizontal transfer.
- **Standard metric:** The number of distinct possible topologies for $n$ taxa is a **combinatorial function**, and the KTU syllabus explicitly requires students to compute this for small $n$ (e.g., $n = 3, 4, 5, 6$).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded)

- The set of all unrooted binary topologies for $n$ taxa is denoted $U_n$.
- The set of all rooted binary topologies is denoted $R_n$.
- The number of **distinct topologies** is **discrete** — it is a count, not a continuous quantity.
- The standard **edge-labeled** vs **edge-unlabeled** distinction is fundamental: $U_n$ counts unlabeled topologies; labeled topologies are obtained by permuting taxon labels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of a Phylogenetic Topology

A topology is constructed from the following structural primitives:

1. **Leaves (External Nodes / Taxa):** The endpoints representing the observed biological units (e.g., species, genes, proteins). For $n$ taxa, there are exactly $n$ leaves.
2. **Internal Nodes:** Hypothetical ancestors. In a **binary (bifurcating)** tree, every internal node has degree **3** (in unrooted) or **2 children + 1 parent** (in rooted).
3. **Edges / Branches:** Connections representing lineage descent.
4. **Root:** The single most ancestral node (present only in **rooted** topologies). The root is typically placed using an outgroup.
5. **Clades / Monophyletic Groups:** Subsets of taxa that share a common ancestor exclusive to them.

## 2.2 Classification of Topologies

Topologies are classified along **three independent axes**:

### Axis A — Rooted vs Unrooted
- **Rooted topology:** Has a defined ancestor node (root); direction of evolution is implied. Branches are oriented (parent → child).
- **Unrooted topology:** No designated root; only the branching pattern is specified. Direction of evolution is not implied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key conversion rule:** A single unrooted binary topology with $n \ge 3$ leaves can give rise to **(n − 2)** distinct rooted binary topologies, by placing the root on any of the (2n − 3) branches (for binary trees, 2n − 3 internal branches; for n ≥ 3, exactly (n − 2) **internal** branches).

### Axis B — Binary (Bifurcating) vs Multifurcating
- **Binary / Bifurcating:** Every internal node has exactly **3 neighbors (unrooted)** or **2 children (rooted)**. This is the most common assumption in maximum likelihood and Bayesian methods.
- **Multifurcating (Polytomous):** Some internal nodes have **≥ 3** children/descendants. These represent uncertainty or hard polytomies (simultaneous speciation events).

### Axis C — Cladogram vs Phylogram vs Ultrametric
- **Cladogram:** Only topology, no branch lengths.
- **Phylogram:** Topology + branch lengths (substitutions/site).
- **Ultrametric:** Topology + branch lengths where all tips are equidistant from the root (suitable for molecular-clock dating).

## 2.3 Counting the Number of Topologies — The Central Combinatorial Question

This is the **most heavily tested calculation in KTU examinations** on this topic. The KTU board expects students to:

- Derive the formula
- Apply it to small $n$
- Explain what super-exponential growth implies for tree search algorithms

### 2.3.1 Number of Rooted Binary (Bifurcating) Topologies

For $n$ taxa ($n \ge 2$), the number of distinct **rooted binary** topologies is:

$$
R_n = (2n - 3)!! = (2n - 3) \cdot (2n - 5) \cdot (2n - 7) \cdots 3 \cdot 1
$$

where the **double factorial** $k!!$ means: $k \cdot (k-2) \cdot (k-4) \cdots$ until reaching 1 or 2.

### 2.3.2 Number of Unrooted Binary Topologies

For $n$ taxa ($n \ge 3$), the number of distinct **unrooted binary** topologies is:

$$
U_n = (2n - 5)!! = (2n - 5) \cdot (2n - 7) \cdot (2n - 9) \cdots 3 \cdot 1
$$

### 2.3.3 Relationship

The ratio relationship between rooted and unrooted counts is:

$$
R_n = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n
$$

because the relationship is $R_n = U_n \cdot (n-2) \cdot 2 / 1$ for some factor — more precisely, the standard identity $R_n = (2n-3)!! = (2n-3) \cdot (2n-5)!! = (2n-3) \cdot U_n$. So $(2n-3)$ rooted topologies are obtained from each unrooted binary tree by placing the root on any of the $(2n-3)$ branches.

### 2.3.4 Number of Labeled Topologies (with taxon names)

If we additionally consider permutations of taxon labels:

$$
\text{Labeled rooted binary} = n! \cdot \frac{R_n}{2^{n-2}}
$$

For unlabeled, every internal branching is counted once, and the factor of $2^{n-2}$ accounts for the fact that swapping two clades at an internal node yields the same unlabeled tree.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Reference Table (memorize for Part A & Part B):**

| Concept | Formula | Domain / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Unrooted binary topologies | $U_n = (2n-5)!!$ | $n \ge 3$ |
| Rooted binary topologies | $R_n = (2n-3)!!$ | $n \ge 2$ |
| Relationship | $R_n = (2n-3) \cdot U_n$ | All $n \ge 3$ |
| Edges in unrooted binary tree | $E = 2n - 3$ | $n \ge 3$ |
| Edges in rooted binary tree | $E = 2n - 2$ | $n \ge 2$ |
| Internal nodes (unrooted binary) | $n - 2$ | $n \ge 3$ |
| Internal nodes (rooted binary) | $n - 1$ | $n \ge 2$ |
| Number of rooted topologies from one unrooted | $n - 2$ | $n \ge 3$ |
| Multifurcating rooted (Cavender–Farris) | $\dfrac{(2n-3)!}{2^{n-2}(n-2)!}$ | $n \ge 2$ |
| Multifurcating unrooted | $\dfrac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$ | $n \ge 3$ |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing the double factorial $k!!$ in your answer sheet, **always expand it fully** for small $n$. The KTU examiner awards the method mark only if the expansion is visible.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Tree search algorithms** (heuristics like NNI, SPR, TBR) operate on the discrete space of topologies because exhaustive search becomes infeasible beyond ~12 taxa.
- **Bayesian MCMC** (MrBayes, BEAST) samples topology space proportionally to posterior probability.
- **Bootstrap support** on a topology edge measures how often that **clade (i.e., a particular topology feature)** is recovered across resampled datasets.
- **Consensus topologies** (strict, majority-rule, ML-consensus) summarize competing topologies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Unrooted Binary Topologies $U_n$

### Step 1 — Start with the smallest meaningful case
For $n = 3$ taxa, there is exactly **one** unrooted binary topology (a 3-leaf star). So $U_3 = 1$.

### Step 2 — Recursive growth
To add the $(n+1)$-th taxon to an existing unrooted binary tree with $n$ leaves:
- The new taxon must attach to one of the **existing edges**.
- A tree with $n$ leaves has $2n - 3$ edges (see derivation below).
- Each edge placement yields a distinct new unrooted binary topology.

### Step 3 — Build the recurrence

$$
U_{n+1} = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n
$$

### Step 4 — Prove the edge count
For any unrooted tree (connected, acyclic graph) with $n$ leaves and $n - 2$ internal nodes, the number of edges is:

$$
E = (n) + (n - 2) - 1 = 2n - 3
$$

using the tree property $E = V - 1$ where $V = n + (n - 2) = 2n - 2$.

### Step 5 — Unroll the recurrence

$$
U_{n+1} = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n
$$

Unrolling from $U_3 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_4 &= (2 \cdot 3 - 3) \cdot U_3 = 3 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
U_5 &= (2 \cdot 4 - 3) \cdot U_4 = 5 \cdot 3 = 15 \\
U_6 &= (2 \cdot 5 - 3) \cdot U_5 = 7 \cdot 15 = 105 \\
U_7 &= (2 \cdot 6 - 3) \cdot U_6 = 9 \cdot 105 = 945 \\
U_8 &= (2 \cdot 7 - 3) \cdot U_7 = 11 \cdot 945 = 10395
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Recognize the double factorial pattern

$$
U_n = (2n - 5)!! = (2n - 5) \cdot (2n - 7) \cdot (2n - 9) \cdots 3 \cdot 1
$$

This is verified by induction: at each step, multiplying the previous result by the next odd integer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Rooted Binary Topologies $R_n$

### Step 1 — Base case
For $n = 2$ taxa, there is exactly **one** rooted binary topology (a single edge with a root at one end). So $R_2 = 1$.

### Step 2 — Recurrence
A rooted binary tree with $n$ leaves has $2n - 2$ edges (one more than the unrooted version, due to the root's extra parent edge). Each new $(n+1)$-th taxon can be inserted on any of $2n - 2$ edges:

$$
R_{n+1} = (2n - 2) \cdot R_n
$$

### Step 3 — Unroll the recurrence

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3 &= (2 \cdot 2 - 2) \cdot R_2 = 2 \cdot 1 = 2 \\
R_4 &= (2 \cdot 3 - 2) \cdot R_3 = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \\
R_5 &= (2 \cdot 4 - 2) \cdot R_4 = 6 \cdot 8 = 48 \\
R_6 &= (2 \cdot 5 - 2) \cdot R_5 = 8 \cdot 48 = 384 \\
R_7 &= (2 \cdot 6 - 2) \cdot R_6 = 10 \cdot 384 = 3840
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Double factorial form

$$
R_n = (2n - 3)!! = (2n - 3) \cdot (2n - 5) \cdot (2n - 7) \cdots 3 \cdot 1
$$

### Step 5 — Verify the relation $R_n = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n$

$$
R_n = (2n - 3) \cdot (2n - 5)!! = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n
$$

This makes intuitive sense: every rooted binary tree with $n$ leaves can be obtained from a unique unrooted binary tree with $n$ leaves by placing the root on one of the $(2n - 3)$ internal edges.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Rooted vs Unrooted

| Property | Rooted Binary | Unrooted Binary |
|---|---|---|
| Number of topologies | $(2n-3)!!$ | $(2n-5)!!$ |
| Number of internal nodes | $n - 1$ | $n - 2$ |
| Number of edges | $2n - 2$ | $2n - 3$ |
| Internal node degree | 3 (2 children + 1 parent) | 3 (1 parent + 2 children OR 3 neighbors) |
| Time direction implied | **Yes** | No |
| Rooting requires | Outgroup or molecular clock | Not required |
| Smallest valid $n$ | 2 | 3 |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Q: How many unrooted binary topologies exist for 6 taxa?**

**Solution:**

$$
U_6 = (2 \cdot 6 - 5)!! = 7!! = 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 105
$$

**Q: How many rooted binary topologies exist for 6 taxa?**

**Solution:**

$$
R_6 = (2 \cdot 6 - 3)!! = 9!! = 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 945
$$

**Q: For 10 taxa, approximately how many topologies exist?**

$$
U_{10} = (15)!! = 15 \cdot 13 \cdot 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 2027025
$$

$$
R_{10} = (17)!! = 17 \cdot 15 \cdot 13 \cdot 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 34459425
$$

This super-exponential growth (called **tree-space explosion**) is the reason **heuristic search** (NNI, SPR, TBR) is required for moderate-to-large datasets.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Unrooted vs Rooted Topology (n = 5)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Taxon A] --- N1((Internal Node 1))
    L2[Taxon B] --- N1
    N1 --- N2((Internal Node 2))
    L3[Taxon C] --- N2
    L4[Taxon D] --- N2
    N2 --- N3((Internal Node 3))
    L5[Taxon E] --- N3
```

*This represents a single unrooted binary topology with 5 leaves and 3 internal nodes, total edges = 2(5) - 3 = 7.*

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Same Topology, Rooted (Root Placed on Edge)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT((Root)) --- N1((Internal Node 1))
    N1 --- N2((Internal Node 2))
    N1 --- L1[Taxon A]
    N2 --- N3((Internal Node 3))
    N2 --- L2[Taxon B]
    N3 --- L3[Taxon C]
    N3 --- L4[Taxon D]
    L5[Taxon E] --- N3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Two Distinct Unrooted Topologies for n = 4

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph T1[Topology 1 - Pairwise Grouping]
        a1[A] --- n1a((N1))
        b1[B] --- n1a
        n1a --- n2a((N2))
        c1[C] --- n2a
        d1[D] --- n2a
    end

    subgraph T2[Topology 2 - Ladder]
        a2[A] --- n1b((N1))
        n1b --- n2b((N2))
        n2b --- n3b((N3))
        b2[B] --- n1b
        c2[C] --- n2b
        d2[D] --- n3b
    end
```

*For n = 4, there are exactly $U_4 = 3!! = 3$ unrooted binary topologies. The third one is the mirror image of Topology 2 (which is treated as identical in unlabeled counting).*

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Block Diagram: Topology Search Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Aligned Sequences] --> B[Initialize Random Topology T0]
    B --> C[Compute Score of T0]
    C --> D[Apply NNI / SPR / TBR Rearrangement]
    D --> E[New Topology Candidate T1]
    E --> F[Compute Score of T1]
    F --> G{Score Improved?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Accept T1 as Current Tree]
    G -- No --> I{Use Simulated Annealing?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Accept with Probability p]
    I -- No --> K[Reject T1]
    H --> L{Convergence Reached?}
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -- No --> D
    L -- Yes --> M[Output Best Topology + Branch Lengths]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Schematic — Topology Space Growth Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    N3[n = 3, U = 1] --> N4[n = 4, U = 3]
    N4 --> N5[n = 5, U = 15]
    N5 --> N6[n = 6, U = 105]
    N6 --> N7[n = 7, U = 945]
    N7 --> N8[n = 8, U = 10395]
    N8 --> N9[n = 9, U = 135135]
    N9 --> N10[n = 10, U = 2027025]
    N10 --> N11[n = 11, U = 34459425]
    N11 --> N12[n = 12, U = 654729075]
```

*Each step multiplies by an odd integer: $2n-5$. The super-exponential growth justifies heuristic search algorithms over exhaustive enumeration.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a phylogenetic tree topology and distinguish between rooted and unrooted topologies.**

**Model Answer:**

A phylogenetic tree topology is the **branching pattern** that describes the hierarchical relationships among a set of taxa, represented as a graph $T = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of nodes (leaves + internal ancestors) and $E$ is the set of edges (lineages).

- A **rooted** topology has a designated ancestor node, with branches oriented from parent to child, implying direction of evolution.
- An **unrooted** topology has no designated ancestor; only the connectivity pattern is specified, and direction of evolution is not implied.

Rooted topologies are obtained from unrooted ones by choosing a root location (e.g., via outgroup), and one unrooted binary tree corresponds to $(n-2)$ rooted binary trees for $n$ taxa.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**How many unrooted binary topologies are possible for 5 taxa? Show your calculation.**

**Model Answer:**

The number of unrooted binary topologies for $n$ taxa is:

$$
U_n = (2n - 5)!!
$$

For $n = 5$:

$$
U_5 = (2 \cdot 5 - 5)!! = 5!! = 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 15
$$

Therefore, **15 distinct unrooted binary topologies** are possible for 5 taxa.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the number of unrooted binary topologies $U_n$ for $n$ taxa. Calculate $U_6$ and $U_{10}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a diagram the difference between rooted and unrooted topologies. How many rooted binary topologies exist for 6 taxa, and how does this relate to the unrooted count? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation:**

A binary phylogenetic tree with $n$ leaves and $n - 2$ internal nodes has $E = V - 1 = (2n - 2) - 1 = 2n - 3$ edges.

Starting from $U_3 = 1$ (the unique 3-leaf star tree), the $(n+1)$-th taxon can be inserted on any of the existing $2n - 3$ edges, giving the recurrence:

$$
U_{n+1} = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n
$$

Unrolling from $U_3 = 1$:

$$
U_n = (2n - 5)!! = (2n - 5) \cdot (2n - 7) \cdot (2n - 9) \cdots 3 \cdot 1
$$

**Calculation for $U_6$:**

$$
U_6 = (2 \cdot 6 - 5)!! = 7!! = 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 105
$$

**Calculation for $U_{10}$:**

$$
U_{10} = 15!! = 15 \cdot 13 \cdot 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1
$$

Computing step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
15 \cdot 13 &= 195 \\
195 \cdot 11 &= 2145 \\
2145 \cdot 9 &= 19305 \\
19305 \cdot 7 &= 135135 \\
135135 \cdot 5 &= 675675 \\
675675 \cdot 3 &= 2027025 \\
2027025 \cdot 1 &= 2027025
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
U_{10} = 2{,}027{,}025
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Rooted vs Unrooted Topologies (Diagram):**

```
Unrooted (n = 4):              Rooted (n = 4):

      A                              Root
       \                            /    \
        N1 -- N2                  N1      N2
       /      \                  / \     / \
      B        C                B   C   D   E
              |                      
       N3 -- (D-E branch)
      /
     D
      \
       E
```

In an **unrooted** topology, no node is privileged; the tree shows only the **cladistic relationships** among taxa.

In a **rooted** topology, a specific node (root) is chosen (typically by an outgroup or molecular clock), and branches acquire a direction (parent → child).

**Counting rooted binary topologies for $n = 6$:**

$$
R_6 = (2 \cdot 6 - 3)!! = 9!! = 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 945
$$

**Relation to unrooted count:**

Each unrooted binary topology with $n$ leaves can be rooted in $(n - 2) = 4$ ways for $n = 6$ (by placing the root on any of the $2n - 3 = 9$ branches and choosing an orientation; for binary, distinct rooted topologies from one unrooted is $2n - 3 = 9$ in the most general case, but typically $(n-2)$ for distinguishing by topology of rooted form):

The exact relation is:

$$
R_n = (2n - 3) \cdot U_n
$$

For $n = 6$:

$$
R_6 = (2 \cdot 6 - 3) \cdot U_6 = 9 \cdot 105 = 945
$$

This confirms the calculation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** What is a phylogenetic topology? Compare and contrast bifurcating and multifurcating topologies with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the number of rooted and unrooted binary topologies for 7 taxa. Explain the practical implication of this combinatorial explosion for phylogenetic tree-search algorithms. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition of Phylogenetic Topology:**

A phylogenetic topology is the abstract branching structure of an evolutionary tree, representing the pattern of descent among a set of taxa, independent of branch lengths.

**Bifurcating Topologies:**

- Every internal node has **exactly 2 descendants** (in a rooted tree) or **exactly 3 neighbors** (in an unrooted tree).
- They represent the **default assumption** of speciation via binary splitting.
- The number of bifurcating topologies for $n$ taxa is $(2n-3)!!$ rooted or $(2n-5)!!$ unrooted.

**Example (Bifurcating Unrooted, $n = 4$):**

$$
\text{A—((B,C),D)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{(A,(B,(C,D)))}
$$

**Multifurcating Topologies:**

- Internal nodes may have **≥ 3** children simultaneously.
- Represents either **soft polytomies** (uncertain resolution due to insufficient data) or **hard polytomies** (true simultaneous speciation).
- Number of multifurcating topologies is much larger than the bifurcating subset.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Bifurcating | Multifurcating |
|---|---|---|
| Internal node degree (rooted) | 3 (2 children + 1 parent) | ≥ 3 children + 1 parent |
| Number of topologies | $(2n-3)!!$ | $\dfrac{(2n-3)!}{2^{n-2}(n-2)!}$ |
| Implied speciation pattern | Sequential binary splits | Simultaneous multi-way splits |
| Statistical resolution | High (fully resolved) | Low / uncertain |
| Example method | Maximum Likelihood | Consensus trees from incompatible gene trees |

**Example (Multifurcating, $n = 4$):**

$$
\text{A,((B,C,D))} \quad \text{— a 3-way polytomy}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**For $n = 7$ taxa:**

**Unrooted binary topologies:**

$$
U_7 = (2 \cdot 7 - 5)!! = 9!! = 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 945
$$

**Rooted binary topologies:**

$$
R_7 = (2 \cdot 7 - 3)!! = 11!! = 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1
$$

Computing step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
11 \cdot 9 &= 99 \\
99 \cdot 7 &= 693 \\
693 \cdot 5 &= 3465 \\
3465 \cdot 3 &= 10395 \\
10395 \cdot 1 &= 10395
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
R_7 = 10{,}395
$$

**Verification of relation:**

$$
R_7 = (2n - 3) \cdot U_7 = (2 \cdot 7 - 3) \cdot 945 = 11 \cdot 945 = 10{,}395
$$

The relation holds.

**Practical Implication — Tree-Space Explosion:**

The number of topologies grows **super-exponentially** with $n$, not merely exponentially. To illustrate:

| $n$ | $U_n$ | $R_n$ |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | 1 | 3 |
| 4 | 3 | 15 |
| 5 | 15 | 105 |
| 6 | 105 | 945 |
| 7 | 945 | 10,395 |
| 10 | 2,027,025 | 34,459,425 |
| 20 | $\approx 2.2 \times 10^{20}$ | $\approx 8.2 \times 10^{21}$ |
| 50 | $\approx 2.8 \times 10^{74}$ | $\approx 2.8 \times 10^{76}$ |

For $n = 20$ taxa, the number of possible unrooted topologies exceeds the number of atoms on Earth (~$10^{50}$), and for $n = 50$ it exceeds the number of atoms in the observable universe (~$10^{80}$). Therefore, **exhaustive enumeration** of all topologies is **computationally infeasible** beyond $n \approx 12$ taxa.

This forces the use of **heuristic tree-search algorithms**:

- **NNI (Nearest Neighbor Interchange):** Local rearrangement, swaps two subtrees across an internal edge.
- **SPR (Subtree Pruning and Regrafting):** Prunes a subtree and regrafts it on a different branch.
- **TBR (Tree Bisection and Reconnection):** Bisects the tree at an edge, then reconnects the two halves in a new way.

These heuristics explore topology space efficiently, but do not guarantee the global optimum. **Bootstrap resampling** and **Bayesian MCMC** are used to assess confidence in the recovered topology.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $U_n$ and $R_n$ formulas.** A very common student error is using $(2n-3)!!$ when the question asks for unrooted topologies. **Always re-read the question — "unrooted" → $(2n-5)!!$, "rooted" → $(2n-3)!!$.**
> 2. **Do not forget to fully expand the double factorial.** Writing "7!!" without expansion loses the working mark. Show: $7!! = 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 105$.
> 3. **For $n = 2$ unrooted, the formula breaks down** ($U_2$ is undefined or trivial). State explicitly that the formula applies for $n \ge 3$.
> 4. **Branch lengths are NOT part of the topology.** A common confusion: "two trees with different branch lengths are different topologies" — **wrong**. Same topology + different lengths = same topology class.
> 5. **Rooted/unrooted diagram must show direction of root.** Examiner deducts 1 mark if the root is shown but no arrow/direction is indicated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Topologies**

- ✅ **Topology** = branching pattern of a phylogenetic tree, independent of branch lengths.
- ✅ A **rooted** topology has a designated ancestor node; an **unrooted** topology does not.
- ✅ **Bifurcating (binary)** trees have all internal nodes of degree 3 (unrooted) or 2 children (rooted).
- ✅ **Multifurcating** trees have polytomies (internal nodes with ≥ 3 descendants).
- ✅ **Unrooted binary** count: $U_n = (2n-5)!!$ for $n \ge 3$.
- ✅ **Rooted binary** count: $R_n = (2n-3)!!$ for $n \ge 2$.
- ✅ **Identity:** $R_n = (2n-3) \cdot U_n$.
- ✅ **Edge counts:** Unrooted = $2n - 3$, Rooted = $2n - 2$.
- ✅ **Internal node counts:** Unrooted = $n - 2$, Rooted = $n - 1$.
- ✅ **Tree space grows super-exponentially**, motivating heuristic search (NNI, SPR, TBR).
- ✅ **Rooting** is achieved by outgroup species or molecular-clock assumptions.
- ✅ **Standard tree file formats** for storing topology: Newick, NEXUS, NHX.
- ✅ **Topology equivalence** disregards branch lengths; only the clade structure matters.
- ✅ **Clades (monophyletic groups)** = subsets of taxa sharing a unique common ancestor; they are the building blocks of any topology.
- ✅ **For $n \ge 12$ taxa, exhaustive search is infeasible** — always state the practical implication in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Matrix Based and Character Based Tree Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distance Matrix Based and Character Based Tree Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Phylogenetic Tree Reconstruction — The Foundational Problem

In bioinformatics, the central goal of molecular phylogenetics is to reconstruct the **evolutionary history** of a set of biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) and represent it as a **bifurcating tree** (a tree in which every internal node has exactly three branches when rooted, or two branches when unrooted). This tree is called a **phylogenetic tree**, and the leaves of the tree represent the **operational taxonomic units (OTUs)** — typically the contemporary species, genes, or sequences being compared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A *phylogenetic tree* is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of degree ≥ 3 in which leaves represent extant taxa and internal nodes represent hypothetical ancestral sequences. The branch lengths represent the amount of evolutionary change (substitutions per site) along that lineage.

Two fundamentally different mathematical philosophies exist for reconstructing these trees from sequence data:

1. **Distance-Based Methods** — Convert the sequence alignment into a matrix of pairwise evolutionary distances, then build a tree whose branch lengths best fit that distance matrix.
2. **Character-Based Methods** — Operate directly on the aligned characters (columns) of the alignment, searching for the tree that best explains the observed character states under an explicit optimality criterion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction**
> The two method families differ in *what they optimize*. Distance methods optimize a function of pairwise distances (an algebraic criterion). Character-based methods optimize a function of individual character transformations (a combinatorial or probabilistic criterion).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Reunion Map"

Imagine you have 5 photographs of cousins taken at different times in their lives. You want to draw a **family tree** showing who is most closely related.

- **Distance-Based Approach (the "similarity" map):** You measure how similar each pair of faces is, fill a 5×5 table with those similarity scores, and then group the closest pairs first, then the next closest, building outward. You never actually look at individual features — only the *aggregate* distance. → This is **UPGMA / Neighbor-Joining**.

- **Character-Based Approach (the "feature" map):** You examine *specific* heritable features — "has dimples," "freckles," "left-handed" — and you trace each feature back through generations to find the tree that requires the fewest assumed independent origins of those features. → This is **Maximum Parsimony / Maximum Likelihood**.

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods answer the same biological question ("who evolved from whom?"), but they ask the *mathematical* question in different ways. Distance methods are **faster** but throw away the per-character information. Character-based methods are **slower** but more biologically informative because they retain the per-site evidence.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Distance Methods

The most common evolutionary distance metric used in KTU-typical exam problems is the **Jukes–Cantor (JC69) distance**, given by:

$$d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$$

where $p$ is the observed **proportion of differing sites** between two aligned sequences.

The simpler **p-distance** (Hamming proportion) is defined as:

$$d_p = \frac{\text{number of differing sites}}{\text{total aligned sites}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why JC69 instead of raw p-distance?**
> Raw p-distance **saturates** at $p = 0.75$ (because at high divergence a site can be "corrected" multiple times, masking true evolutionary distance). JC69 applies a logarithmic correction. The constant **−3/4** is a **physical/mathematical normalization constant** arising from the JC69 model of nucleotide substitution, where all 4 nucleotides are assumed equally frequent and all 6 substitution types are equiprobable.
> **Standard Reference Metric:** JC69 assumes substitution rate $\alpha$ per site per unit time, with 4 states.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distance-Based Methods — Operational Logic

Distance-based methods operate in two distinct phases:

### Phase A — Distance Calculation
