Compute a symmetric **distance matrix** $D = [d_{ij}]$ where $d_{ij}$ is the evolutionary distance between sequence $i$ and sequence $j$. The matrix is symmetric ($d_{ij} = d_{ji}$) and has zero diagonal ($d_{ii} = 0$).

### Phase B — Clustering Algorithm
Iteratively combine the two most similar OTUs (or sub-clusters) into a new internal node, then update the distance matrix. The two principal algorithms are:

### 2.1.1 UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)

- **Core assumption:** The molecular clock is constant (i.e., the rate of evolution is identical on every branch). This forces the tree to be **ultrametric** — all leaves are equidistant from the root.
- **Algorithm steps:**
  1. Initialize each OTU as its own cluster of size 1.
  2. Find the pair $(i, j)$ with the **smallest** distance $d_{ij}$.
  3. Merge them into a new cluster $(i,j)$, and place its internal node at height $h = d_{ij} / 2$ above the leaves.
  4. Update distances from the new cluster to every other cluster $k$ using the **arithmetic mean** of the previous distances:

$$d_{(i,j),k} = \frac{d_{ik} + d_{jk}}{2}$$

  5. Repeat steps 2–4 until only one cluster remains.

> [!NOTE]
> The "**unweighted**" in UPGMA means each original OTU contributes equally to the cluster distance, regardless of how many OTUs the cluster already contains. The **WPGMA** variant (weighted) weights by the number of OTUs in each parent cluster.

### 2.1.2 Neighbor-Joining (NJ)

- **Core assumption:** No molecular clock — branch lengths are free to vary. NJ produces an **unrooted tree**.
- **Key innovation:** It corrects for the fact that in a star-like (unresolved) tree, pairs of distant OTUs appear artificially close. It uses the **Q-criterion**:

$$Q_{(i,j)} = (N - 2) \cdot d_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{N} d_{ik} - \sum_{k=1}^{N} d_{jk}$$

where $N$ is the number of active clusters at that step.
- **Algorithm steps:**
  1. Compute the **net divergence** $r_i = \sum_{k} d_{ik}$ for every cluster.
  2. Compute $Q_{ij}$ for every pair and select the pair with the **smallest** $Q_{ij}$.
  3. Merge them into a new cluster and compute the **branch lengths** from the new internal node to $i$ and $j$:

$$b_{i,u} = \frac{1}{2} d_{ij} + \frac{1}{2(N-2)} \left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} d_{ik} - \sum_{k=1}^{N} d_{jk} \right)$$

$$b_{j,u} = d_{ij} - b_{i,u}$$

  4. Update the distance matrix using:

$$d_{u,k} = \frac{1}{2} (d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$$

  5. Repeat until only 2 clusters remain (which are then joined by a single branch of length $d_{ij}/2$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saitou & Nei 1987 — Why NJ Matters**
> NJ is the **most widely used** distance method in modern bioinformatics (e.g., in **PHYLIP**, **MEGA**, **PAUP***). It is statistically consistent under the additive-tree model, meaning that as sequence length → ∞, the algorithm recovers the *true* tree with probability → 1. UPGMA, by contrast, is consistent *only* if the molecular clock holds.

## 2.2 Character-Based Methods — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Maximum Parsimony (MP)

- **Optimality criterion:** Find the tree that requires the **fewest evolutionary changes** (substitutions) to explain the observed alignment. The criterion is the **parsimony score** $L(T)$ = total number of substitutions along all branches, summed over all sites.
- **Algorithm steps:**
  1. Enumerate all unrooted binary trees of the $n$ taxa (factorial: $(2n-5)!!$).
  2. For each site (column) in the alignment, use the **Fitch algorithm** to compute the minimum number of changes required under that tree.
  3. Sum the per-site minimums → tree's parsimony score.
  4. Output the tree(s) with the minimum total score.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Fitch algorithm** works in two passes:
> - **Down-pass:** At each internal node, compute the set of possible character states given the sets of its children. If the two child sets *intersect*, the parent set is the intersection. If they are *disjoint*, the parent set is the union (and incurs a cost of +1).
> - **Up-pass:** Count the changes along branches where the parent set was a union of size > 1.

### 2.2.2 Maximum Likelihood (ML)

- **Optimality criterion:** Find the tree that maximizes the **probability** of observing the alignment under a specific substitution model (e.g., JC69, K2P, HKY85, GTR).
- **Equation:** For site $s$ and tree $T$ with branch lengths $\vec{b}$ and model parameters $\vec{\theta}$:

$$\ln L_s(T, \vec{b}, \vec{\theta}) = \ln\!\left[ \sum_{x} \pi_x \prod_{\text{branch } b \in T} P_{xy}(b, t) \right]$$

The total log-likelihood is the sum over all sites. ML is **computationally expensive** (NP-hard) but statistically the most powerful method when the model is correct.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** formula you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve table formatting.

| # | Method | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Boundary Conditions |
|---|--------|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | p-distance | $d_p = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | $m$ = mismatches, $n$ = aligned sites | $0 \le d_p \le 1$ |
| 2 | Jukes–Cantor (JC69) | $d_{JC} = -\dfrac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{4}{3}d_p\right)$ | $d_p < 0.75$ required | saturates as $d_p \to 0.75$ |
| 3 | Kimura 2-Parameter (K2P) | $d_{K2P} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 2P - Q) - \dfrac{1}{4}\ln(1 - 2Q)$ | $P$ = transitions, $Q$ = transversions | $2P + Q < 0.5$, $Q < 0.25$ |
| 4 | UPGMA cluster merge | $d_{(i,j),k} = \dfrac{d_{ik} + d_{jk}}{2}$ | $k$ = any other cluster | produces ultrametric tree |
| 5 | NJ net divergence | $r_i = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} d_{ik}$ | $N$ = current cluster count | computed each iteration |
| 6 | NJ Q-criterion | $Q_{(i,j)} = (N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$ | selects pair with $\min Q$ | $N \ge 4$ to start |
| 7 | NJ branch to $i$ | $b_{i,u} = \dfrac{d_{ij}}{2} + \dfrac{r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)}$ | $u$ = new internal node | may be negative (allowed) |
| 8 | NJ branch to $j$ | $b_{j,u} = d_{ij} - b_{i,u}$ | conservation of edge length | sum equals $d_{ij}$ |
| 9 | NJ updated distance | $d_{u,k} = \dfrac{1}{2}(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$ | $k$ = third OTU | unrooted correction |
| 10 | Parsimony score | $L(T) = \displaystyle\sum_{s=1}^{S} \min_{T}\!\left[ \text{changes at site } s \right]$ | $S$ = total sites | minimum across all trees |
| 11 | ML log-likelihood | $\ln L = \displaystyle\sum_{s=1}^{S} \ln L_s$ | sum of site log-likelihoods | $0 \le L \le 1$ per site |
| 12 | Tree count (unrooted) | $N_u = (2n-5)!!$ | $n$ = leaf count | e.g., $n=4 \Rightarrow 3$ trees |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Bioinformatics Utility

- **Disease Tracking (e.g., COVID-19 variant tracing):** NJ trees built on spike-protein sequences are published in real time on platforms like **Nextstrain** to visualize viral lineage spread.
- **Drug Target Discovery:** Parsimony trees identify conserved residues across pathogen species — useful for designing broad-spectrum antibiotics.
- **Forensic Phylogenetics:** ML trees on HIV or hepatitis sequences are admissible as legal evidence in transmission lawsuits.
- **Agricultural Engineering:** Distance-based trees guide marker-assisted breeding by revealing which wild relatives are closest to the cultivated crop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — UPGMA on a 4-Taxon Toy Distance Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem setup (typical KTU numerical):** Given the following evolutionary distance matrix for 4 taxa A, B, C, D, construct the UPGMA tree step-by-step.

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\ \hline
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify the minimum off-diagonal entry

Scanning all off-diagonal pairs: $\min\{2, 4, 6\} = 2$, occurring for the pair $(A, B)$.

> So we **merge A and B first** to form a new cluster $U_1 = (A, B)$.

### Step 2 — Place the internal node

The node $U_1$ is placed at height $h_{U_1} = d_{AB} / 2 = 2/2 = \mathbf{1}$.

So branches $A \to U_1$ and $B \to U_1$ each have length $\mathbf{1}$.

### Step 3 — Update the distance matrix using the UPGMA arithmetic mean formula

Apply $d_{(A,B),k} = \dfrac{d_{Ak} + d_{Bk}}{2}$ for $k = C, D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{(A,B),C} &= \frac{d_{AC} + d_{BC}}{2} = \frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4 \\
d_{(A,B),D} &= \frac{d_{AD} + d_{BD}}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

The new reduced matrix is:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & (A,B) & C & D \\ \hline
(A,B) & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### Step 4 — Repeat: identify the next minimum

Off-diagonal minima: $\min\{4, 6, 6\} = 4$, for the pair $((A,B), C)$.

> Merge $U_1$ with $C$ to form $U_2 = ((A,B), C)$.

### Step 5 — Place the new node and compute branch lengths

The node $U_2$ is placed at height $h_{U_2} = d_{U_1, C} / 2 = 4/2 = \mathbf{2}$.

Therefore:
- Branch $C \to U_2$ has length $h_{U_2} - h_C = 2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$.
- Branch $U_1 \to U_2$ has length $h_{U_2} - h_{U_1} = 2 - 1 = \mathbf{1}$.

### Step 6 — Update the matrix once more

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & ((A,B),C) & D \\ \hline
((A,B),C) & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### Step 7 — Final merge and root placement

Merge $U_2$ with $D$. The root $U_3$ is placed at height $h_{U_3} = 6/2 = \mathbf{3}$.

- Branch $D \to U_3$ has length $3 - 0 = \mathbf{3}$.
- Branch $U_2 \to U_3$ has length $3 - 2 = \mathbf{1}$.

### Final UPGMA Tree (in Newick format)

$$
\bigl( \bigl( (A:1, B:1) : 1, \; C:2 \bigr) : 1, \; D:3 \bigr);
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check (ultrametric property):** Every leaf must be the same total distance from the root. From root $U_3$:
> - $A \to U_1 \to U_2 \to U_3$: $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$ ✓
> - $B \to U_1 \to U_2 \to U_3$: $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$ ✓
> - $C \to U_2 \to U_3$: $2 + 1 = 3$ ✓
> - $D \to U_3$: $3$ ✓
> All distances equal **3**, confirming the ultrametric (molecular clock) property of UPGMA.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Neighbor-Joining on a 4-Taxon Matrix

Using the same initial distance matrix:

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\ \hline
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> NJ on a matrix where the smallest entry is between $(A,B)$ will typically also merge $(A,B)$ first, but the **branch lengths** will differ from UPGMA. Let us work it through.

### Step 1 — Compute net divergence $r_i$ for $N = 4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_A &= d_{AB} + d_{AC} + d_{AD} = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12 \\
r_B &= d_{BA} + d_{BC} + d_{BD} = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12 \\
r_C &= d_{CA} + d_{CB} + d_{CD} = 4 + 4 + 6 = 14 \\
r_D &= d_{DA} + d_{DB} + d_{DC} = 6 + 6 + 6 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $Q_{ij}$ for all $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ pairs

Using $Q_{(i,j)} = (N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$ with $N-2 = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{AB} &= (2)(2) - 12 - 12 = 4 - 24 = -20 \\
Q_{AC} &= (2)(4) - 12 - 14 = 8 - 26 = -18 \\
Q_{AD} &= (2)(6) - 12 - 18 = 12 - 30 = -18 \\
Q_{BC} &= (2)(4) - 12 - 14 = 8 - 26 = -18 \\
Q_{BD} &= (2)(6) - 12 - 18 = 12 - 30 = -18 \\
Q_{CD} &= (2)(6) - 14 - 18 = 12 - 32 = -20
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the minimum $Q_{ij}$

The minimum is $\mathbf{-20}$, achieved by both pairs $(A, B)$ **and** $(C, D)$. This is an arbitrary tie. KTU convention: break ties lexicographically → **merge $(A, B)$ first**.

> So a new internal node $U$ is created joining $A$ and $B$.

### Step 4 — Compute NJ branch lengths

Using:

$$
b_{i,u} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)}
$$

with $i = A$, $j = B$, $N = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{A,U} &= \frac{2}{2} + \frac{12 - 12}{2(2)} = 1 + \frac{0}{4} = \mathbf{1} \\
b_{B,U} &= d_{AB} - b_{A,U} = 2 - 1 = \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Symmetric as expected because $r_A = r_B$.)

### Step 5 — Update the distance matrix

Apply $d_{U,k} = \dfrac{1}{2}(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$ for $k = C, D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{U,C} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{AC} + d_{BC} - d_{AB}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4 + 4 - 2) = \mathbf{3} \\
d_{U,D} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{AD} + d_{BD} - d_{AB}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6 - 2) = \mathbf{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

The new matrix becomes:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & U & C & D \\ \hline
U & 0 & 3 & 5 \\
C & 3 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 5 & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### Step 6 — Final merge

With $N = 3$ clusters, NJ simply joins all three by edges whose lengths are $\tfrac{1}{2} d_{ij}$ for the remaining non-zero distances. The final NJ tree in Newick:

$$
\bigl( A:1,\; B:1,\; (C:3,\; D:3) : 2,\; U:0 \bigr);
$$

or, more simply (removing the zero-length pendant edge $U$):

$$
\bigl( (A:1,\; B:1),\; (C:3,\; D:3) \bigr);
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key UPGMA vs NJ Comparison from this example:**
> - UPGMA gave the tree $\bigl((A,B),C),D\bigr)$ — a **ladder** topology.
> - NJ gave the tree $\bigl((A,B),(C,D)\bigr)$ — a **balanced** topology.
> - This illustrates that NJ is more sensitive to which pairs are "true neighbors" (it penalizes long internal branches), and it does *not* force ultrametricity, which is why it can recover non-clock-like evolutionary histories.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Maximum Parsimony on a 3-Site Alignment

> [!NOTE]
> **Tiny alignment to illustrate the parsimony score calculation:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
\text{Taxon} & \text{Site 1} & \text{Site 2} & \text{Site 3} \\ \hline
A & \text{A} & \text{G} & \text{C} \\
B & \text{A} & \text{G} & \text{C} \\
C & \text{A} & \text{A} & \text{C} \\
D & \text{G} & \text{G} & \text{T}
\end{array}
$$

Three unrooted binary trees for 4 taxa (call them $T_1, T_2, T_3$) correspond to the three possible internal edges. For each site, we use the Fitch algorithm to compute the minimum number of changes.

### Site 1 (states = A, A, A, G)

- Tree $T_1 = ((A,B),(C,D))$: Internal nodes can both be assigned A → one change (G must mutate at the edge to D). **Cost = 1.**
- Tree $T_2 = ((A,C),(B,D))$: Same logic. **Cost = 1.**
- Tree $T_3 = ((A,D),(B,C))$: A at one side, G at the other side, internal nodes can be assigned A/G → one change suffices. **Cost = 1.**

All three trees have **cost 1** at site 1.

### Site 2 (states = G, G, A, G)

- Tree $T_1 = ((A,B),(C,D))$: One internal node groups A and B (both G) and the other groups C and D (one A, one G). The internal edge must change once. **Cost = 1.**
- Tree $T_2 = ((A,C),(B,D))$: Similarly **cost 1.**
- Tree $T_3 = ((A,D),(B,C))$: A is grouped with G (D), forcing one change. **Cost 1.**

### Site 3 (states = C, C, C, T)

- $T_1 = ((A,B),(C,D))$: T is alone in (C,D) clade → **cost 1.**
- $T_2 = ((A,C),(B,D))$: T is alone in (B,D) clade → **cost 1.**
- $T_3 = ((A,D),(B,C))$: T is paired with C (in (A,D)) → **cost 1.**

### Total Parsimony Score

$L(T_1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$, $L(T_2) = 3$, $L(T_3) = 3$. **All three trees are equally parsimonious** — a tied output is biologically possible when the alignment carries too little information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real bioinformatics, the *number* of equally parsimonious trees can be in the thousands, and the bootstrap support on each branch is the standard way to break ties probabilistically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Master Flow of Phylogenetic Tree Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Aligned Sequences FASTA"] --> B["Phase 1: Pairwise Distance Computation"]
    B --> C{"Choose Method Family"}
    C -->|Distance Based| D["Compute Distance Matrix d_ij using JC69 or K2P"]
    C -->|Character Based| E["Operate directly on aligned columns"]
    D --> F1["UPGMA Algorithm assumes molecular clock"]
    D --> F2["Neighbor Joining Algorithm does not assume clock"]
    E --> G1["Maximum Parsimony minimizes total changes"]
    E --> G2["Maximum Likelihood maximizes P data given tree"]
    F1 --> H1["Output: Rooted Ultrametric Tree Newick format"]
    F2 --> H2["Output: Unrooted Additive Tree Newick format"]
    G1 --> H3["Output: Unrooted Tree with minimum substitutions"]
    G2 --> H4["Output: Unrooted Tree with model parameters and branch lengths"]
    H1 --> I["Tree Visualization FigTree or iTOL"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — UPGMA Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start with N taxa and N by N distance matrix D"] --> S2["Scan all pairs find min d_ij"]
    S2 --> S3["Merge i and j into new cluster U"]
    S3 --> S4["Place node U at height d_ij divided by 2"]
    S4 --> S5["Update distances using arithmetic mean"]
    S5 --> S6{"Are more than 1 clusters left"}
    S6 -->|Yes| S2
    S6 -->|No| S7["Output rooted ultrametric tree"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Neighbor-Joining Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["Start with N taxa and N by N distance matrix D"] --> T2["Compute net divergence r_i for every i"]
    T2 --> T3["Compute Q matrix using N minus 2 times d_ij minus r_i minus r_j"]
    T3 --> T4["Select pair with minimum Q_ij"]
    T4 --> T5["Create new internal node U joining the pair"]
    T5 --> T6["Compute branch lengths b_iU and b_jU"]
    T6 --> T7["Update distances using d_Uk equals half times d_ik plus d_jk minus d_ij"]
    T7 --> T8{"Are more than 3 clusters left"}
    T8 -->|Yes| T2
    T8 -->|No| T9["Output unrooted additive tree"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Fitch Parsimony Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["For each candidate tree T"] --> P2["For each site s in the alignment"]
    P2 --> P3["Bottom up pass assign state sets to internal nodes"]
    P3 --> P4{"Do the two child sets intersect"}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5["Parent set equals intersection no cost"]
    P4 -->|No| P6["Parent set equals union cost increases by 1"]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7["Continue to root"]
    P7 --> P8["Count total changes per site"]
    P8 --> P9["Sum over all sites gives parsimony score L of T"]
    P9 --> P10["Select tree with minimum L"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Comparison (Distance vs Character)

| Aspect | Distance-Based (UPGMA, NJ) | Character-Based (MP, ML) |
|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Input data | Distance matrix (aggregate) | Aligned characters (per site) |
| Information retained | Low (collapses many states into one number) | High (each column is evidence) |
| Computational cost | Polynomial $O(N^3)$ | NP-hard for MP, also hard for ML |
| Assumption | Additive tree model | Substitution model (ML) or no model (MP) |
| Output | Single best tree (greedy) | Single best tree (optimality) |
| Statistical consistency | UPGMA: only under clock; NJ: yes | MP: no (under some models); ML: yes |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Differentiate between distance-based and character-based methods of phylogenetic tree construction. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Distance-based methods (1 mark):** They first convert the aligned sequences into a pairwise distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$, where each entry estimates the evolutionary divergence between two sequences. A clustering algorithm (e.g., UPGMA or NJ) is then applied to construct the tree whose branch lengths best fit these distances. Example: **Neighbor-Joining algorithm.**
- **Character-based methods (1 mark):** They use the individual aligned character columns directly, searching for the tree that best explains the observed character states under a defined optimality criterion (minimum changes for MP, or maximum probability for ML). Example: **Maximum Parsimony method.**
- **Key distinction (1 mark):** Distance methods are faster and produce a unique tree, but discard per-site information. Character methods are slower and more computationally intensive, but retain richer per-site evolutionary evidence and can incorporate explicit substitution models.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**State the Jukes–Cantor model and write its distance formula. Why is the correction term needed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **JC69 Model (1 mark):** Proposed by Jukes and Cantor in 1969, it assumes that all four nucleotides (A, T, G, C) occur at equal frequency ($\pi_A = \pi_T = \pi_G = \pi_C = 0.25$) and that every nucleotide is equally likely to mutate to any of the other three with the same rate $\alpha$. There are 12 such substitution types, all equiprobable.
- **Formula (1 mark):**

$$d_{JC} = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$$

where $p$ is the observed proportion of differing sites.

- **Why correction needed (1 mark):** Under raw $p$-distance, a site that has undergone multiple "hits" is counted only once as different, so $p$ underestimates the true evolutionary distance and **saturates at $p = 0.75$** (since a site can differ from the ancestor with probability at most $3/4$ in a 4-state model). The logarithmic correction "un-counts" these multiple substitutions by extrapolating backward in time, providing an unbiased estimate of substitutions per site.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examination format: 14-mark questions feature an internal choice. Both alternatives are presented below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Given the following evolutionary distance matrix for four species P, Q, R, S, **construct the UPGMA tree** step-by-step. Show all intermediate matrices and label every branch with its length.

$$
D = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & P & Q & R & S \\ \hline
P & 0 & 8 & 8 & 12 \\
Q & 8 & 0 & 8 & 12 \\
R & 8 & 8 & 0 & 12 \\
S & 12 & 12 & 12 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The minimum off-diagonal distance is 8, occurring for pairs $(P, Q)$ and $(P, R)$ and $(Q, R)$ — a three-way tie. KTU convention: lexicographic break → **merge (P, Q) first.**

Height of new node $U_1 = 8/2 = \mathbf{4}$. Branches $P \to U_1$ and $Q \to U_1$ each have length **4**.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Updated distances via arithmetic mean:

$$d_{(P,Q), R} = \frac{8+8}{2} = 8 \qquad d_{(P,Q), S} = \frac{12+12}{2} = 12$$

New matrix:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & (P,Q) & R & S \\ \hline
(P,Q) & 0 & 8 & 12 \\
R & 8 & 0 & 12 \\
S & 12 & 12 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Minimum = 8, pair $((P,Q), R)$. Merge to form $U_2$.

Height of $U_2 = 8/2 = \mathbf{4}$. Branch $R \to U_2 = 4$. Branch $U_1 \to U_2 = 4 - 4 = \mathbf{0}$ (a zero-length branch — acceptable, indicates a multifurcation in time).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Updated matrix:

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & ((P,Q),R) & S \\ \hline
((P,Q),R) & 0 & 12 \\
S & 12 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final merge. Root $U_3$ at height $12/2 = \mathbf{6}$. Branch $S \to U_3 = 6$. Branch $U_2 \to U_3 = 6 - 4 = \mathbf{2}$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final tree in Newick:

$$
\bigl( \bigl( (P:4, \; Q:4) : 0, \; R:4 \bigr) : 2, \; S:6 \bigr);
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Sanity check — all leaves at distance 6 from root:
- $P$: $4 + 0 + 2 = 6$ ✓
- $Q$: $4 + 0 + 2 = 6$ ✓
- $R$: $4 + 2 = 6$ ✓
- $S$: $6$ ✓

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, **explain the Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm**. Write the Q-criterion formula and the branch length formula used in NJ, explaining the role of net divergence $r_i$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Flow (3 marks):**

1. Compute the distance matrix $D$ from the alignment.
2. For each active cluster, compute net divergence $r_i = \sum_k d_{ik}$.
3. Compute the Q-matrix: $Q_{(i,j)} = (N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$.
4. Select the pair $(i,j)$ with the **smallest** $Q_{ij}$ — this corrects for the artifact that in a star tree, distant pairs appear falsely close.
5. Create a new internal node $U$ joining $i$ and $j$.
6. Compute branch lengths:

$$b_{i,U} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)} \qquad b_{j,U} = d_{ij} - b_{i,U}$$

7. Update the distance matrix:

$$d_{U,k} = \frac{1}{2}(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$$

8. Repeat from Step 2 until only 3 clusters remain, then connect them with edges of length $d_{ij}/2$.

**Q-criterion formula (2 marks):**

$$Q_{(i,j)} = (N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$$

**Role of net divergence (2 marks):** $r_i$ is the sum of all pairwise distances from $i$ to every other cluster. In a star tree, $r_i$ is large for OTUs that are genuinely divergent, and subtracting $r_i + r_j$ in the Q-criterion **removes this background divergence**, isolating the *extra* closeness of $i$ and $j$ beyond what their general divergence would predict. The $(N-2)$ factor is a normalization constant that keeps the criterion on a comparable scale across iterations.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** **Explain the Maximum Parsimony method** of phylogenetic tree construction. Using the Fitch algorithm, compute the parsimony score for the given alignment on the tree topology $((A,B),(C,D))$.

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
\text{Site} & A & B & C & D \\ \hline
1 & \text{A} & \text{A} & \text{G} & \text{G} \\
2 & \text{C} & \text{C} & \text{C} & \text{T} \\
3 & \text{T} & \text{A} & \text{T} & \text{A}
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Maximum Parsimony Explanation (3 marks):**
- MP finds the phylogenetic tree that requires the **fewest evolutionary substitutions** to explain the observed alignment.
- It evaluates every possible unrooted binary tree (there are $(2n-5)!!$ for $n$ taxa) and selects the one with the minimum total number of changes.
- The optimality criterion is the **parsimony score** $L(T) = \sum_{s} c_s(T)$, where $c_s(T)$ is the minimum number of changes at site $s$ on tree $T$, computed by the Fitch algorithm.

**Fitch Algorithm — Site 1 (A, A, G, G) (1.5 marks):**
- Let $X$ be the ancestor of $A,B$ and $Y$ be the ancestor of $C,D$. Root is $Z$.
- Children of $X$: $\{A\}$ and $\{A\}$ → intersection $\{A\}$ → $X = \{A\}$, no cost.
- Children of $Y$: $\{G\}$ and $\{G\}$ → intersection $\{G\}$ → $Y = \{G\}$, no cost.
- Children of $Z$: $\{A\}$ and $\{G\}$ → disjoint → union $\{A,G\}$, cost **+1**.
- **Site 1 cost = 1.**

**Fitch Algorithm — Site 2 (C, C, C, T) (1.5 marks):**
- $X$: children $\{C\}, \{C\}$ → intersection $\{C\}$, no cost.
- $Y$: children $\{C\}, \{T\}$ → disjoint → union $\{C,T\}$, cost **+1**.
- $Z$: children $\{C\}, \{C,T\}$ → intersection $\{C\}$, no cost.
- **Site 2 cost = 1.**

**Fitch Algorithm — Site 3 (T, A, T, A) (1 mark):**
- $X$: children $\{T\}, \{A\}$ → disjoint → union $\{T,A\}$, cost **+1**.
- $Y$: children $\{T\}, \{A\}$ → disjoint → union $\{T,A\}$, cost **+1**.
- $Z$: children $\{T,A\}, \{T,A\}$ → intersection $\{T,A\}$, no cost.
- **Site 3 cost = 2.**

**Total parsimony score (1 mark):** $L(((A,B),(C,D))) = 1 + 1 + 2 = \mathbf{4}$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** **Compare UPGMA and Neighbor-Joining** in terms of (i) molecular clock assumption, (ii) tree output (rooted/unrooted), (iii) computational complexity, and (iv) statistical consistency. Mention one real-world application where NJ is preferred over UPGMA.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| # | Property | UPGMA (1.25 marks) | Neighbor-Joining (1.25 marks) |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| (i) | Molecular clock | **Assumes** a strict constant rate — required for correctness | **Does not assume** a clock — branch lengths free |
| (ii) | Tree type | Produces a **rooted** tree (by construction) | Produces an **unrooted** tree (rooting is added separately) |
| (iii) | Complexity | $O(N^3)$ greedy, simpler | $O(N^3)$ greedy, but more arithmetic per step |
| (iv) | Consistency | Consistent **only** if molecular clock holds | Consistent for any **additive** distance tree |

**Real-world application (2 marks):** NJ is preferred in **viral phylogenetic surveillance** — for example, the **Nextstrain** platform uses NJ to track SARS-CoV-2 lineages in real time. This is because different viral genes evolve at very different rates (spike protein vs. ORF1ab), violating the molecular clock assumption and making UPGMA's ultrametric output biologically incorrect.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (from KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(N-2)$ factor in the Q-criterion** — examiners deduct 2 marks. Always show the full formula.
> 2. **UPGMA branch length formula confusion** — many students write $h_{new} = d_{ij}$ instead of $d_{ij}/2$. The half is **mandatory** because the distance is between two leaves and the node is at the midpoint.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate distance matrices** — for a 7-mark UPGMA problem, you must show at least 2 intermediate matrices. Skipping these = −2 marks.
> 4. **Fitch algorithm — wrong direction of pass** — the down-pass computes *sets*; the up-pass counts *changes*. Many students try to count changes during the down-pass, which is methodologically wrong.
> 5. **Saitou & Nei attribution** — the NJ algorithm is by **Saitou and Nei (1987)**. UPGMA was introduced earlier. Examiners appreciate this historical citation for a 1-mark bonus.
> 6. **Confusing $p$-distance with JC69 distance** — they are **not** the same. JC69 uses a log transform. Using raw $p$ for downstream NJ clustering is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **phylogenetic tree** represents evolutionary history; leaves = extant taxa, internal nodes = ancestors, branch lengths = substitutions per site.
- ✅ **Distance methods** (UPGMA, NJ) require a symmetric distance matrix; **character methods** (MP, ML) operate directly on the alignment columns.
- ✅ **UPGMA** uses the arithmetic mean update and assumes a **molecular clock** → ultrametric rooted tree.
- ✅ **Neighbor-Joining** uses the **Q-criterion** $(N-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$ to correct for the star-tree artifact → unrooted additive tree.
- ✅ The **Jukes–Cantor distance** is $d_{JC} = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$ and corrects for multiple substitutions; it requires $p < 0.75$.
- ✅ The **Kimura 2-Parameter model** distinguishes **transitions (P)** from **transversions (Q)** for a more biologically realistic distance.
- ✅ **Maximum Parsimony** finds the tree with the **fewest** total substitutions; the **Fitch algorithm** computes the per-site minimum in two passes (down: sets; up: cost count).
- ✅ **Maximum Likelihood** finds the tree that **maximizes** $P(\text{alignment} \mid T, \vec{b}, \vec{\theta})$ under a substitution model.
- ✅ Number of unrooted binary trees for $n$ taxa = $(2n-5)!!$ (e.g., 3 trees for $n=4$, 105 trees for $n=7$).
- ✅ **NJ is statistically consistent** for additive trees; **MP is not consistent** under some evolutionary models; **ML is consistent** when the model is correct.
- ✅ Always show **intermediate matrices** in UPGMA/NJ numerical problems — KTU examiners explicitly check for them.
- ✅ Tie-breaking in clustering: KTU convention is **lexicographic** (merge the pair with alphabetically earliest labels first).
- ✅ **NJ application:** Nextstrain viral tracking, MEGA software default for phylogeny of newly sequenced pathogens.
- ✅ **MP application:** Useful for short, conservative alignments (e.g., rRNA gene trees) where substitution models are poorly estimated.
- ✅ **ML application:** Gold standard for published phylogenies; implemented in **RAxML**, **IQ-TREE**, **PhyML**.
- ✅ **PHYLIP, PAUP\*, MEGA, MrBayes** are the most cited software packages in the KTU bioinformatics syllabus.
- ✅ The **Newick format** (e.g., `((A:0.1, B:0.2):0.05, C:0.3);`) is the universal interchange format for trees.
