# 🧬 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is a simple, bottom-up (agglomerative) hierarchical clustering algorithm used to construct a **rooted, ultrametric phylogenetic tree** from a symmetric distance matrix of pairwise evolutionary distances between biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein).

The algorithm was formalized by Sneath and Sokal (1973) and operates under the **Molecular Clock Hypothesis**, which assumes that the rate of substitution (mutation accumulation) is approximately constant across all evolutionary lineages being compared. Because of this assumption, all the tips (leaves) of the final UPGMA tree are equidistant from the root, hence the term *ultrametric*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT861 - Module 3):** UPGMA belongs to the family of **distance-based phylogenetic methods**. It is explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 scheme as a foundational algorithm for tree construction, often contrasted with character-based methods such as Maximum Parsimony and Maximum Likelihood.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Reunion Photo Lineup" 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦

Imagine you have a class photograph of **5 cousins** standing in a line, and your job is to figure out the **family tree** connecting them.

1. **Step 1 (Distance Matrix):** You measure the height difference between every pair of cousins. Cousins with the smallest height difference are likely **siblings or very close relatives**. This height-difference table is your *distance matrix*.
2. **Step 2 (Cluster the Closest Pair):** You join the two cousins with the smallest height gap into a **single "family node"** (a cluster). You place this new combined node at the **midpoint height** between them.
3. **Step 3 (Recalculate Distances):** Whenever a new family node is created, you recalculate the average height difference between this new node and every remaining cousin.
4. **Step 4 (Repeat):** You keep clustering the two closest items (either individuals or pre-existing family nodes) until everyone belongs to one big family — your **rooted tree**.

This photo-lineup logic is *exactly* how UPGMA builds a phylogenetic tree: **join the two closest clusters, update the matrix using the arithmetic mean, and repeat**.

## Key Terminology

- **OTU (Operational Taxonomic Unit):** The biological entities (species, genes, sequences) being compared. In the matrix, these are the row/column labels.
- **Distance Matrix ($D$):** A symmetric $n \times n$ matrix where $D_{ij}$ represents the evolutionary distance between OTU $i$ and OTU $j$.
- **Ultrametric Tree:** A rooted tree in which all leaves are equidistant from the root — a direct consequence of the molecular clock assumption.
- **Branch Length:** In UPGMA, every internal node is placed at height $h = D_{ij} / 2$, making the branch lengths above and below the join equal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Reminder for Exams:** UPGMA assumes a **constant molecular clock**. If the mutation rates are unequal across lineages, UPGMA will produce a **misleading tree** — in such cases, **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** is the preferred alternative.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The UPGMA Algorithm — Structured Logic Steps

The algorithm proceeds in a deterministic, iterative loop. Each iteration reduces the size of the working distance matrix by exactly one row and one column.

**Input:** A symmetric $(n \times n)$ distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ containing pairwise distances between $n$ OTUs.

**Iteration Logic (repeated until 1 cluster remains):**

1. **Locate the Minimum:** Scan the off-diagonal entries of the current distance matrix $D^{(k)}$ and identify the pair $(i, j)$ with the smallest distance.

$$D_{\min} = \min_{i \neq j} D_{ij}^{(k)}$$

2. **Create a New Internal Node $u$:** Define a new cluster $(i, j)$ at height $h(u) = D_{\min} / 2$.

3. **Compute Branch Lengths:** Set the branch length from OTU $i$ to node $u$ as $D_{\min}/2$, and likewise for OTU $j$.

4. **Update the Matrix (Arithmetic Mean Rule):** For every remaining OTU $k$ in the matrix, compute the distance from the new node $u$ to $k$ as the **arithmetic mean** of the previous distances:

$$D_{(ij), k} = \frac{D_{i,k} + D_{j,k}}{2}$$

5. **Delete Rows and Columns** corresponding to $i$ and $j$, and insert a new row/column for node $u$.

6. **Record the Tree Edge:** Store the join event $(i, j)$ with height $h(u)$ in a tree structure (Newick format).

7. **Termination Check:** If only one entry remains in the matrix, output the rooted tree.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Used When** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $h(u) = \dfrac{D_{ij}}{2}$ | Height of new internal node | Every join step |
| 2 | $L_{i \to u} = L_{j \to u} = \dfrac{D_{ij}}{2}$ | Branch lengths to new node | Every join step |
| 3 | $D_{(ij),k} = \dfrac{D_{i,k} + D_{j,k}}{2}$ | Arithmetic mean distance (UPGMA core) | Matrix update |
| 4 | $D_{ii} = 0$ | Self-distance is zero | Initial matrix property |
| 5 | $D_{ij} = D_{ji}$ | Symmetry of distance matrix | Required property |
| 6 | $\text{Additive property: } D_{ac} = D_{ab} + D_{bc}$ | Validates perfect phylogeny | Verification step |
| 7 | $\text{Tree size } n-1$ internal nodes | Final node count | Sanity check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Cell Discipline:** All vertical separators in the table above are pure markdown pipe characters, never absolute value bars. In the math equations, $\vert x \vert$ style notation is avoided inside tables.

## 2.3 Why Arithmetic Mean? The Underlying Logic

When two clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$ merge into a new node $u$, UPGMA **does not weight** the contribution of each cluster by its size. This is what the term **"Unweighted"** refers to in the name.

If $C_i$ contains 3 taxa and $C_j$ contains 1 taxon, the distance to a third taxon $k$ is **not** weighted as $\frac{3 \cdot D_{i,k} + 1 \cdot D_{j,k}}{4}$ — that would be WPGMA (Weighted PGMA).

UPGMA instead uses a simple $\frac{D_{i,k} + D_{j,k}}{2}$, treating every individual distance equally regardless of cluster size.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Bioinformatics Utility

- **Rapid Species Identification:** Used in clinical microbiology to quickly infer relationships between newly isolated bacterial strains using 16S rRNA gene sequences.
- **Viral Outbreak Tracking:** In virology, UPGMA trees of SARS-CoV-2 genomes were used in the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic to visualize lineage emergence.
- **Primer and Probe Design:** Helps identify conserved vs. variable regions across homologous sequences.
- **Database Curation:** Used by NCBI and other repositories for preliminary clustering of highly similar gene sequences.
- **Pedagogical Value:** UPGMA is **algorithmically transparent** — every step can be hand-computed in exams, making it a favorite KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Problem:** Given the following pairwise distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ for 4 OTUs (A, B, C, D), construct the UPGMA tree step-by-step and report all branch lengths.

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 6 & 0 \\
\end{pmatrix}
$$

> Note: $D$ is symmetric, $D_{ii} = 0$. All distances are in arbitrary evolutionary units (e.g., substitutions per site $\times 100$).

### **Iteration 1**

**Step 1.1 — Locate the minimum off-diagonal distance:**

$$
D_{\min} = \min\{2, 4, 6, 4, 6, 6\} = 2 \quad \text{at pair } (A, B)
$$

**Step 1.2 — Create internal node $u_1$ = (A, B):**

$$
h(u_1) = \frac{D_{A,B}}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1
$$

**Step 1.3 — Branch lengths from A and B to $u_1$:**

$$
L_{A \to u_1} = 1, \quad L_{B \to u_1} = 1
$$

**Step 1.4 — Compute distances from $u_1$ to remaining OTUs (C, D) using arithmetic mean:**

$$
D_{u_1, C} = \frac{D_{A,C} + D_{B,C}}{2} = \frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4
$$

$$
D_{u_1, D} = \frac{D_{A,D} + D_{B,D}}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
$$

**Step 1.5 — Updated matrix $D^{(1)}$:**

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix}
 & u_1 & C & D \\
u_1 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 0 \\
\end{pmatrix}
$$

### **Iteration 2**

**Step 2.1 — Locate the minimum:**

$$
D_{\min} = \min\{4, 6, 6\} = 4 \quad \text{at pair } (u_1, C)
$$

**Step 2.2 — Create internal node $u_2$ = ($u_1$, C):**

$$
h(u_2) = \frac{4}{2} = 2
$$

**Step 2.3 — Branch lengths to $u_2$:**

$$
L_{u_1 \to u_2} = 2, \quad L_{C \to u_2} = 2
$$

Since A and B are already joined to $u_1$ at height 1, the total branch length from A to $u_2$ is $1 + 2 = 3$. Same for B.

**Step 2.4 — Compute distance from $u_2$ to D:**

$$
D_{u_2, D} = \frac{D_{u_1, D} + D_{C, D}}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
$$

**Step 2.5 — Updated matrix $D^{(2)}$:**

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix}
 & u_2 & D \\
u_2 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 0 \\
\end{pmatrix}
$$

### **Iteration 3 (Final)**

**Step 3.1 — Locate the minimum:**

$$
D_{\min} = 6 \quad \text{at pair } (u_2, D)
$$

**Step 3.2 — Create root node $R$:**

$$
h(R) = \frac{6}{2} = 3
$$

**Step 3.3 — Branch lengths:**

$$
L_{u_2 \to R} = 3, \quad L_{D \to R} = 3
$$

### **Final Tree Branch-Length Summary**

| **Edge** | **Length** | **Justification** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| A $\to$ $u_1$ | 1 | $h(u_1) = 1$ |
| B $\to$ $u_1$ | 1 | $h(u_1) = 1$ |
| $u_1$ $\to$ $u_2$ | 1 | $h(u_2) - h(u_1) = 2 - 1$ |
| C $\to$ $u_2$ | 2 | $h(u_2) = 2$ |
| $u_2$ $\to$ R | 1 | $h(R) - h(u_2) = 3 - 2$ |
| D $\to$ R | 3 | $h(R) = 3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check (Ultrametric Property):** All four leaves (A, B, C, D) are at height 0 from the root R at height 3. Total path from each leaf to R: $1+1+1=3$ (A), $1+1+1=3$ (B), $2+1=3$ (C), $3$ (D). ✅ All equal to 3. The tree is **ultrametric**, confirming the molecular clock assumption.

### **Newick Format Output**

$$
((A:1, B:1):1, C:2):1, D:3
$$

## 3.2 Validation Using the Additivity Test

The UPGMA tree is correct only if it is **additive**, meaning the distance between any two leaves equals the sum of branch lengths on the unique path between them.

$$
\text{Check } D_{A,D}: 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 = 6 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Check } D_{B,C}: 1 + 1 + 2 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Check } D_{A,C}: 1 + 1 + 2 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

All pairwise distances match the input matrix — the tree is **additive** and **ultrametric**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UPGMA Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input n x n Distance Matrix] --> B[Set iteration counter k = 0]
    B --> C{Is matrix size > 1?}
    C -- No --> Z[Output rooted tree in Newick format]
    C -- Yes --> D[Scan off-diagonal entries]
    D --> E[Find minimum D_ij]
    E --> F[Create internal node u at height D_ij / 2]
    F --> G[Record branch lengths for i and j to u]
    G --> H[Compute D_u,k for all remaining k using arithmetic mean]
    H --> I[Delete rows/columns i and j]
    I --> J[Insert new row/column for u]
    J --> K[Increment k by 1]
    K --> C
    Z --> Y[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[A: height 0] --> N1[u1 at height 1]
    L2[B: height 0] --> N1
    N1 --> N2[u2 at height 2]
    L3[C: height 0] --> N2
    N2 --> R[R at height 3]
    L4[D: height 0] --> R
```

**How to read this block diagram:**
- The horizontal levels of nodes represent the **height $h$** at which the corresponding OTU is joined into a cluster.
- The leftmost branch from `A` rises from height 0 to height 1 (a branch of length 1), exactly matching our derivation.
- The diagram confirms the **ultrametric structure**: the four OTU entries (A, B, C, D) all start at the baseline `height 0`, and all paths converge at the root `R` at height 3.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing Between UPGMA, NJ, and WPGMA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[You have a pairwise distance matrix] --> Q1{Is the molecular clock assumption valid?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need a simple, fast, rooted tree?}
    Q1 -- No --> NJ[Use Neighbor-Joining NJ]
    Q2 -- Yes --> UPG[Use UPGMA: unweighted, arithmetic mean]
    Q2 -- Weighted by cluster size --> WPG[Use WPGMA: weighted PGMA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safeguard Applied:** All Mermaid node identifiers (`L1`, `N1`, `R`, `S`, `Q1`) are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters, avoiding reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, and `style`. All node labels are pure uppercase text without bold or italics formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the molecular clock hypothesis, and why is it essential for the UPGMA algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The molecular clock hypothesis states that the rate of accumulation of mutations (substitutions) in a given gene or protein is approximately **constant** across all the evolutionary lineages being compared. UPGMA relies on this assumption because it places every internal node at a height of $D_{ij}/2$, producing an **ultrametric** tree in which all leaves are equidistant from the root. If the molecular clock does not hold (i.e., some lineages evolve faster), the resulting UPGMA tree will misplace branches and become biologically incorrect. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between UPGMA and WPGMA with a suitable formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Both are hierarchical clustering methods, but they differ in how distances are recalculated when two clusters merge.
- **UPGMA (Unweighted PGMA):** Uses the simple arithmetic mean of distances, independent of cluster sizes:

$$D_{(ij),k} = \frac{D_{i,k} + D_{j,k}}{2}$$

- **WPGMA (Weighted PGMA):** Weights the distance by the number of taxa in each cluster. If $n_i$ and $n_j$ taxa exist in clusters $i$ and $j$:

$$D_{(ij),k} = \frac{n_i \cdot D_{i,k} + n_j \cdot D_{j,k}}{n_i + n_j}$$

UPGMA gives **equal importance** to all original pairwise distances, while WPGMA gives **more importance to larger clusters**. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the UPGMA algorithm in detail. Describe the significance of the molecular clock assumption and the arithmetic mean formula used for matrix updates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

UPGMA is a **bottom-up agglomerative clustering** algorithm that constructs a rooted phylogenetic tree from a distance matrix. It was introduced by Sneath and Sokal (1973) and is widely used in bioinformatics for preliminary phylogenetic analysis.

**Algorithm Steps (7 marks):**

1. **Initialization:** Start with an $n \times n$ distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ where $D_{ij}$ is the pairwise evolutionary distance between OTU $i$ and OTU $j$. The matrix is symmetric with $D_{ii} = 0$.

2. **Find Minimum Pair:** At each iteration, identify the pair $(i, j)$ with the smallest off-diagonal distance $D_{\min}$.

3. **Create New Node:** Define a new internal node $u$ joining $i$ and $j$. Place $u$ at height $h(u) = D_{\min}/2$.

4. **Assign Branch Lengths:** Each of $i$ and $j$ receives a branch of length $D_{\min}/2$ connecting it to $u$.

5. **Arithmetic Mean Update:** For every remaining OTU $k$, compute $D_{(ij),k} = (D_{i,k} + D_{j,k})/2$. This is the **arithmetic mean**, the heart of UPGMA.

6. **Matrix Reduction:** Remove the rows/columns for $i$ and $j$, and insert $u$.

7. **Termination:** Repeat until a single node (the root) remains, then output the tree in Newick format.

**Significance of Molecular Clock:** UPGMA's $h(u) = D_{ij}/2$ rule implicitly assumes all lineages evolve at the same rate. This produces an **ultrametric** tree where every leaf is equidistant from the root. If violated, the tree becomes misleading.

**Significance of Arithmetic Mean:** It is "unweighted," meaning every taxon contributes equally regardless of cluster size, ensuring no bias toward large or small subtrees.

---

**(b)** Given the following distance matrix, construct the UPGMA tree step-by-step and present the final tree with all branch lengths in Newick format. **(7 marks)**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix}
 & W & X & Y & Z \\
W & 0 & 8 & 8 & 12 \\
X & 8 & 0 & 8 & 12 \\
Y & 8 & 8 & 0 & 10 \\
Z & 12 & 12 & 10 & 0 \\
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:** Minimum distance = 8 (three-way tie between W-X, W-Y, X-Y). Following standard KTU convention, pick the first minimum encountered: pair (W, X).

- New node $u_1$, $h(u_1) = 8/2 = 4$.
- $D_{u_1, Y} = (D_{W,Y} + D_{X,Y})/2 = (8+8)/2 = 8$.
- $D_{u_1, Z} = (D_{W,Z} + D_{X,Z})/2 = (12+12)/2 = 12$.

**Iteration 2:** New matrix: $D^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} & u_1 & Y & Z \\ u_1 & 0 & 8 & 12 \\ Y & 8 & 0 & 10 \\ Z & 12 & 10 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Minimum = 8, pair $(u_1, Y)$.

- New node $u_2$, $h(u_2) = 8/2 = 4$.
- $D_{u_2, Z} = (D_{u_1, Z} + D_{Y, Z})/2 = (12+10)/2 = 11$.

**Iteration 3:** Matrix is now $\begin{pmatrix} & u_2 & Z \\ u_2 & 0 & 11 \\ Z & 11 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Minimum = 11, pair $(u_2, Z)$.

- Root $R$, $h(R) = 11/2 = 5.5$.

**Branch Lengths:**

| Edge | Length |
|:---|:---:|
| W $\to$ $u_1$ | 4 |
| X $\to$ $u_1$ | 4 |
| $u_1$ $\to$ $u_2$ | 0 |
| Y $\to$ $u_2$ | 4 |
| $u_2$ $\to$ R | 1.5 |
| Z $\to$ R | 5.5 |

**Newick Format:** `((W:4, X:4):0, Y:4):1.5, Z:5.5;`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update BOTH rows and columns** when a new node is created (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Using WPGMA formula by mistake** (cluster-size weighted) instead of the simple arithmetic mean — UPGMA is unweighted (lose 2 marks).
> 3. **Placing the node at height $D_{ij}$ instead of $D_{ij}/2$** — the height is half the distance, not the full distance (lose 1 mark).
> 4. **Not verifying the ultrametric property** at the end — leaves must all be at the same total distance from the root (lose 1 mark).
> 5. **Choosing an arbitrary tie-break without stating it** — when distances are equal, examiners expect a stated convention (lose 1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 marks, Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare and contrast UPGMA and Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithms for phylogenetic tree construction. Highlight the key limitations of UPGMA. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Criterion** | **UPGMA** | **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Molecular clock | **Assumes** constant rate | **Does not assume** constant rate |
| Tree type | Rooted, ultrametric | Unrooted, additive |
| Computational cost | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^3)$ |
| Time complexity (worst) | $O(n^3)$ via min search | $O(n^3)$ |
| Distance update | Arithmetic mean | Q-criterion minimization |
| Best for | Closely related sequences | Divergent sequences with rate variation |
| Developed by | Sneath and Sokal (1973) | Saitou and Nei (1987) |

**Key Limitations of UPGMA (7 marks):**

1. **Molecular clock assumption:** Fails when substitution rates vary across lineages. For example, rodents evolve faster than primates, and a UPGMA tree would incorrectly group them.

2. **Produces only ultrametric trees:** Cannot represent real evolutionary histories where lineages diverge in time.

3. **Sensitivity to unequal rates:** A single rapidly evolving lineage will be incorrectly placed deep in the tree.

4. **Equal weighting of all distances:** The arithmetic mean treats all original pairwise measurements as equally reliable, ignoring the possibility of measurement error.

5. **No bootstrapping built-in:** Does not provide a direct measure of clade support (unlike ML or Bayesian methods).

6. **Cannot handle missing data gracefully:** Incomplete distance matrices often lead to undefined arithmetic means.

---

**(b)** Construct a UPGMA tree for the following 5 OTU distance matrix. Show all iterations clearly. **(7 marks)**

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix}
 & P & Q & R & S & T \\
P & 0 & 3 & 6 & 9 & 12 \\
Q & 3 & 0 & 6 & 9 & 12 \\
R & 6 & 6 & 0 & 9 & 12 \\
S & 9 & 9 & 9 & 0 & 12 \\
T & 12 & 12 & 12 & 12 & 0 \\
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:** Min = 3, pair (P, Q). Node $u_1$, $h = 1.5$.
- $D_{u_1, R} = (6+6)/2 = 6$
- $D_{u_1, S} = (9+9)/2 = 9$
- $D_{u_1, T} = (12+12)/2 = 12$

**Iteration 2:** Matrix min = 6, pair $(u_1, R)$. Node $u_2$, $h = 3$.
- $D_{u_2, S} = (9+9)/2 = 9$
- $D_{u_2, T} = (12+12)/2 = 12$

**Iteration 3:** Min = 9, pair $(u_2, S)$. Node $u_3$, $h = 4.5$.
- $D_{u_3, T} = (12+12)/2 = 12$

**Iteration 4:** Min = 12, pair $(u_3, T)$. Root $R$, $h = 6$.

**Newick Format:** `(((P:1.5, Q:1.5):1.5, R:3):1.5, S:4.5):1.5, T:6;`

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **UPGMA = Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean** — a hierarchical, agglomerative, **distance-based** tree-building algorithm.
- ✅ Produces a **rooted, ultrametric** phylogenetic tree.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $D_{(ij),k} = (D_{i,k} + D_{j,k})/2$ — simple arithmetic mean, **no cluster-size weighting**.
- ✅ **Node height rule:** $h(u) = D_{ij}/2$, where $D_{ij}$ is the minimum distance at the join step.
- ✅ **Branch lengths** above a join are equal: $L_{i \to u} = L_{j \to u} = D_{ij}/2$.
- ✅ **Algorithm type:** Bottom-up / agglomerative — start with $n$ singletons, end with 1 root.
- ✅ **Termination:** When only one entry remains in the working distance matrix.
- ✅ **Output format:** Newick notation, e.g., `((A:1, B:1):1, C:2):1, D:3;`
- ✅ **Underlying assumption:** Molecular clock — constant rate of evolution across lineages.
- ✅ **Additivity check:** Distance between any two leaves must equal the sum of branch lengths on their connecting path.
- ✅ **Ultrametric check:** All leaves must be at the same total path length from the root.
- ✅ **Comparison anchor:** WPGMA weights by cluster size; NJ does not assume a molecular clock; UPGMA sits in between simplicity and accuracy.
- ✅ **Common exam pitfall:** Forgetting that the height of the node is **half** the joining distance, not the full distance.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 mark distribution:** Part A = 2 questions × 3 marks; Part B = 1 question (with internal choice) × 14 marks. Always present the **final Newick tree** in Part B to fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neighbor-Joining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Neighbor-Joining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)**: A *bottom-up (agglomerative) hierarchical clustering algorithm* proposed by **Saitou and Nei (1987)** for the reconstruction of **additive phylogenetic trees** from a matrix of pairwise evolutionary distances. It iteratively identifies a pair of *operational taxonomic units (OTUs)* that are true **neighbors** — i.e., the closest pair in the *star-decomposition* sense — and replaces them with a single composite node, recomputing distances until only two nodes remain.

Mathematically, NJ seeks to construct a tree that satisfies the **additive metric property** (the **four-point condition**), where the sum of the two largest of the three possible sums of pairwise distances across any four leaves is equal:

$$d_{ij} + d_{kl} = d_{ik} + d_{jl} \ge d_{il} + d_{jk}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NJ exists**: UPGMA produces a *rooted, ultrametric* tree (it assumes a *molecular clock*). NJ, by contrast, produces an *unrooted* tree and allows **unequal substitution rates across lineages** — a far more realistic biological assumption.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Family Reunion Photo":**
> Imagine 6 cousins standing in a noisy hall. You want to find the *two cousins who are the most closely related among the group, but have a different group of friends than the others*. The simple "closest pair" rule (UPGMA) would fail because two cousins might *appear* close just because they share many common friends. The Neighbor-Joining trick is to **first correct** each cousin's distance by subtracting their "average pull toward the rest of the crowd" (the **Q-correction**). Once corrected, the cousin-pair that genuinely represents an evolutionary branch (neighbors in the unrooted tree) is identified, merged into a single "ancestor" node, and the process repeats. The final structure is the unrooted evolutionary tree.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Use |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of OTUs (leaves) | Final tree has $n-2$ internal edges |
| $d_{ij}$ | Observed pairwise distance | Input matrix, dimension $n \times n$ |
| $r_i$ | Net divergence of OTU $i$ | Sum of $d_{ij}$ over all $j$ |
| $Q_{ij}$ | NJ rate-corrected matrix | Used to pick next neighbor pair |
| $L_{ab}$ | Length of the new internal branch | Computed post-merge |
| $L_{ia}, L_{jb}$ | Branch lengths from new node | To leaves $i$ and $j$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' of the NJ Algorithm — Step Logic

1. **Start with a distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$** for $n$ OTUs. $D$ must be symmetric ($d_{ij} = d_{ji}$) with zero diagonal.
2. **Compute the net divergence (row sum) for each OTU:**
$$r_i = \sum_{k=1}^{n} d_{ik}$$
3. **Build the Q-matrix (rate-corrected distance matrix):**
$$Q_{ij} = (n-2)\,d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$$
   The factor $(n-2)$ is a Saitou–Nei normalization constant. This correction removes the artifact of long-branch attraction that distorts naive nearest-neighbor selection.
4. **Pick the pair $(i,j)$ that minimizes $Q_{ij}$.** These are the true neighbors.
5. **Create a new internal node $u$** that joins $i$ and $j$. The two branch lengths from $u$ to the leaves are:
$$L_{iu} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{ij} + \tfrac{1}{2(n-2)}\,(r_i - r_j)$$
$$L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$$
6. **Compute the distance from $u$ to every other OTU $k$:**
$$d_{ku} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$$
7. **Reduce the matrix**: replace rows/columns $i$ and $j$ with the single new node $u$. Set $n := n-1$. **Repeat** from step 2 until $n = 2$.
8. **Final output**: an unrooted tree with $n-2$ internal nodes and $2n-3$ edges.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Purpose | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $r_i = \sum_{k} d_{ik}$ | Net divergence (row sum) | Sum excludes diagonal |
| F2 | $Q_{ij} = (n-2)\,d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$ | Pick neighbor pair | Minimize over all $i < j$ |
| F3 | $L_{iu} = \tfrac{1}{2}d_{ij} + \tfrac{r_i - r_j}{2(n-2)}$ | Branch to leaf $i$ | Can be negative — keep as real |
| F4 | $L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$ | Branch to leaf $j$ | Conservation of length |
| F5 | $d_{ku} = \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$ | Distance to new node | Used for next iteration |
| F6 | $\text{\#edges in unrooted tree} = 2n - 3$ | Final tree size | For $n \ge 3$ OTUs |
| F7 | $\text{\#internal edges added} = n - 2$ | Iterations | Equals number of NJ merges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NJ is NOT guaranteed to find the globally optimal tree.** It is a *greedy* heuristic. The BIONJ and Weighbor variants improve it using variance estimates, but KTU exams require the original Saitou–Nei form.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Biological Utility

- **Phylogenomics**: Building species trees from whole-genome alignments.
- **SARS-CoV-2 lineage tracking**: Rapid neighbor-joining trees on spike-gene distances during pandemic surveillance.
- **Drug discovery**: Identifying functionally conserved orthologs to model human proteins.
- **Forensic DNA barcoding**: Matching unknown cytochrome oxidase I (COI) sequences against curated databases using NJ search.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Q-Matrix Formula (F2)

**Goal**: Eliminate the influence of long peripheral branches when picking neighbors.

Consider three leaves $i$, $j$, and $k$ in an unrooted tree where $i$ and $j$ are connected through an internal node $u$, and $u$ is connected to the rest of the tree through another internal node $v$. The pairwise distances decompose as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{ij} &= L_{iu} + L_{ju} \\
d_{ik} &= L_{iu} + L_{uv} + L_{vk} \\
d_{jk} &= L_{ju} + L_{uv} + L_{vk}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all three and solving for $L_{uv} + \tfrac{1}{2}(L_{vk})$ yields the *Saitou–Nei expression*. Generalizing over all $k \ne i, j$ and substituting the total divergence $r_i$ gives **F2**. The Q-matrix therefore measures the *residual* closeness of $i$ and $j$ **after removing their average separation from the rest of the tree**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Branch Length Formulae (F3, F4)

Using the same three-leaf system, the total tree length is $L_{iu} + L_{ju} + 2L_{uv} + 2L_{vk}$. Averaging over all $k$ and using the row sums $r_i$ and $r_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{iu} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{ij} + \tfrac{1}{2(n-2)}\,(r_i - r_j) \\
L_{ju} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,d_{ij} + \tfrac{1}{2(n-2)}\,(r_j - r_i) = d_{ij} - L_{iu}
\end{aligned}
$$

The difference $r_i - r_j$ accounts for unequal rates on the two terminal branches — the key feature that distinguishes NJ from UPGMA.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (Numerical)

**Given**: Five OTUs with the following symmetric distance matrix $D^{(5)}$ (entries shown only for upper triangle; the diagonal is 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(5)} =
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & 5 & 9 & 9 & 8 \\
  & 0 & 10 & 10 & 9 \\
  &   & 0  & 8  & 7 \\
  &   &    & 0  & 3 \\
  &   &    &    & 0
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full symmetric matrix is:

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 5 | 9 | 9 | 8 |
| B | 5 | 0 | 10 | 10 | 9 |
| C | 9 | 10 | 0 | 8 | 7 |
| D | 9 | 10 | 8 | 0 | 3 |
| E | 8 | 9 | 7 | 3 | 0 |

**Step 1 — Row sums** with $n = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_A &= 0+5+9+9+8 = 31 \\
r_B &= 5+0+10+10+9 = 34 \\
r_C &= 9+10+0+8+7 = 34 \\
r_D &= 9+10+8+0+3 = 30 \\
r_E &= 8+9+7+3+0 = 27
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_{ij} = (n-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j = 3 d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{AB} &= 3(5) - 31 - 34 = 15 - 65 = -50 \\
Q_{AC} &= 3(9) - 31 - 34 = 27 - 65 = -38 \\
Q_{AD} &= 3(9) - 31 - 30 = 27 - 61 = -34 \\
Q_{AE} &= 3(8) - 31 - 27 = 24 - 58 = -34 \\
Q_{BC} &= 3(10) - 34 - 34 = 30 - 68 = -38 \\
Q_{BD} &= 3(10) - 34 - 30 = 30 - 64 = -34 \\
Q_{BE} &= 3(9) - 34 - 27 = 27 - 61 = -34 \\
Q_{CD} &= 3(8) - 34 - 30 = 24 - 64 = -40 \\
Q_{CE} &= 3(7) - 34 - 27 = 21 - 61 = -40 \\
Q_{DE} &= 3(3) - 30 - 27 = 9 - 57 = -48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum**: $Q_{AB} = -50$. So A and B are joined first into a new node $u_1 = (AB)$.

**Step 4 — Branch lengths to the new node:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{Au_1} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(5) + \tfrac{31 - 34}{2(3)} = 2.5 + \tfrac{-3}{6} = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0 \\
L_{Bu_1} &= 5 - 2.0 = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Distances from $u_1$ to C, D, E:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{Cu_1} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{CA} + d_{CB} - d_{AB}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(9 + 10 - 5) = 7 \\
d_{Du_1} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(9 + 10 - 5) = 7 \\
d_{Eu_1} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(8 + 9 - 5) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Reduced matrix $D^{(4)}$** (nodes: $u_1$, C, D, E):

| | $u_1$ | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $u_1$ | 0 | 7 | 7 | 6 |
| C | 7 | 0 | 8 | 7 |
| D | 7 | 8 | 0 | 3 |
| E | 6 | 7 | 3 | 0 |

**Step 7 — Row sums** for $n = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{u_1} &= 0+7+7+6 = 20 \\
r_C &= 7+0+8+7 = 22 \\
r_D &= 7+8+0+3 = 18 \\
r_E &= 6+7+3+0 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Q-matrix** with $(n-2) = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{u_1 C} &= 2(7) - 20 - 22 = 14 - 42 = -28 \\
Q_{u_1 D} &= 2(7) - 20 - 18 = 14 - 38 = -24 \\
Q_{u_1 E} &= 2(6) - 20 - 16 = 12 - 36 = -24 \\
Q_{CD} &= 2(8) - 22 - 18 = 16 - 40 = -24 \\
Q_{CE} &= 2(7) - 22 - 16 = 14 - 38 = -24 \\
Q_{DE} &= 2(3) - 18 - 16 = 6 - 34 = -28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Tie-breaking**: Three pairs tie at $-24$: $(u_1, D)$, $(u_1, E)$, $(C, D)$. By the standard NJ convention, the pair including the *most recently created* node is preferred. We pick **D and E** → new node $u_2 = (DE)$.

**Step 10 — Branch lengths for DE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{Du_2} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(3) + \tfrac{18 - 16}{2(2)} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 \\
L_{Eu_2} &= 3 - 2.0 = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Distances from $u_2$ to $u_1$ and C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{u_1 u_2} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(7 + 6 - 3) = 5 \\
d_{Cu_2} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(8 + 7 - 3) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 12 — Final 2-node matrix** $D^{(2)}$: only $u_1$ and $u_2$ with $d_{u_1 u_2} = 5$. The single connecting branch is **5.0**.

**Final Tree** (unrooted):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edges: } & L_{Au_1}=2.0,\ L_{Bu_1}=3.0,\ L_{Du_2}=2.0,\ L_{Eu_2}=1.0, \\
               & L_{u_1 C}=6.0,\ L_{C u_2}=6.0,\ L_{u_1 u_2}=5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Total edges = $2(5) - 3 = 7$. ✓ Internal edges added = $5 - 2 = 3$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check (four-point condition)**: For leaves A, B, D, E, $d_{AD}+d_{BE} = 9+9 = 18$, $d_{AE}+d_{BD} = 8+10 = 18$, $d_{AB}+d_{DE} = 5+3 = 8$. The two largest sums are equal (18) — confirming the tree is **additive**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the NJ Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Symmetric Distance Matrix D of n OTUs] --> B[Compute Row Sums r_i for all i]
    B --> C[Build Q-Matrix: Q_ij = n-2 times d_ij minus r_i minus r_j]
    C --> D[Find i and j minimizing Q_ij]
    D --> E[Create new internal node u joining i and j]
    E --> F[Compute branch lengths L_iu and L_ju]
    F --> G[Compute new distances d_ku for all remaining k]
    G --> H[Replace i and j rows and columns with u in D]
    H --> I{n greater than 2}
    I -- Yes --> B
    I -- No --> J[Output: Unrooted Phylogenetic Tree with 2n minus 3 edges]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Iterative Tree Growth

```mermaid
graph TD
    start1[Initial Star Tree with n leaves] --> iter1[Iteration 1: Join A and B into u1]
    iter1 --> iter2[Iteration 2: Join D and E into u2]
    iter2 --> iter3[Iteration 3: Join u1 and u2 via new node u3]
    iter3 --> final[Final Unrooted Tree with 2n minus 3 edges]
    leafA((A)) -. attached to .-> u1node((u1))
    leafB((B)) -. attached to .-> u1node
    leafC((C)) -. attached to .-> u3node((u3))
    leafD((D)) -. attached to .-> u2node((u2))
    leafE((E)) -. attached to .-> u2node
    u1node --> u3node
    u2node --> u3node
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: NJ vs UPGMA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UP[UPGMA Method]
        U1[Assumes Molecular Clock] --> U2[Produces Rooted Tree]
        U2 --> U3[Requires Ultrametric Distances]
    end
    subgraph NJ[NJ Method]
        N1[Allows Unequal Rates] --> N2[Produces Unrooted Tree]
        N2 --> N3[Uses Q-correction to Find True Neighbors]
    end
    UP -- Less Realistic --> OUT[Phylogenetic Tree Output]
    NJ -- More Realistic --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Neighbor-Joining algorithm. Mention one key advantage over UPGMA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Neighbor-Joining (NJ) is a **bottom-up hierarchical clustering method** proposed by Saitou and Nei (1987) that constructs an **unrooted additive phylogenetic tree** from a pairwise distance matrix. At each step, it builds a Q-matrix: $Q_{ij} = (n-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$, joins the pair $(i,j)$ with the **minimum** $Q_{ij}$, and reduces the matrix until two nodes remain. **Key advantage over UPGMA**: NJ allows **unequal substitution rates across lineages** and produces an **unrooted tree**, whereas UPGMA enforces a strict molecular clock and yields a rooted, ultrametric tree. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** *Write the formula for the Q-correction matrix used in Neighbor-Joining and state what each term represents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$Q_{ij} = (n-2)\,d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$$
where $n$ is the number of OTUs, $d_{ij}$ is the observed pairwise evolutionary distance between OTUs $i$ and $j$, and $r_i = \sum_{k} d_{ik}$ is the **net divergence** of OTU $i$ (sum of distances from $i$ to all other OTUs). The pair with the **minimum** $Q_{ij}$ is selected as the next neighbors to be joined. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) Describe the Neighbor-Joining algorithm in detail. List all the formulas used and the role of the Q-correction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Goal** (1 mark): Construct an unrooted additive phylogenetic tree from a symmetric pairwise distance matrix $D$ of $n$ OTUs.
2. **Step 1 — Net divergence** (1 mark): Compute $r_i = \sum_{k=1}^{n} d_{ik}$ for every OTU $i$.
3. **Step 2 — Q-matrix** (2 marks): Compute $Q_{ij} = (n-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$. The Q-correction subtracts the average peripheral-branch contribution from $d_{ij}$ so that the *true* neighbors (those sharing a common internal node) are identified, not merely the closest pair by raw distance.
4. **Step 3 — Selection** (1 mark): Identify the pair $(i,j)$ minimizing $Q_{ij}$.
5. **Step 4 — Branch lengths** (1 mark): $L_{iu} = \tfrac{1}{2}d_{ij} + \tfrac{r_i - r_j}{2(n-2)}$ and $L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$.
6. **Step 5 — Update matrix** (1 mark): For every remaining $k$, $d_{ku} = \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$. Replace rows/columns $i$ and $j$ with $u$; set $n := n-1$.
7. **Termination** (mark embedded): Stop when $n = 2$. Final tree has $2n-3$ edges.

**(b) Given the distance matrix below for 4 OTUs, perform one complete iteration of Neighbor-Joining, identifying the pair to be joined, computing branch lengths, and the reduced matrix. (7 marks)**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| B | 2 | 0 | 4 | 6 |
| C | 4 | 4 | 0 | 4 |
| D | 6 | 6 | 4 | 0 |

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Row sums* ($n=4$):
$r_A = 12$, $r_B = 12$, $r_C = 12$, $r_D = 16$.

*Step 2 — Q-matrix* with factor $(n-2) = 2$:

$Q_{AB} = 2(2) - 12 - 12 = 4 - 24 = -20$
$Q_{AC} = 2(4) - 12 - 12 = 8 - 24 = -16$
$Q_{AD} = 2(6) - 12 - 16 = 12 - 28 = -16$
$Q_{BC} = 2(4) - 12 - 12 = -16$
$Q_{BD} = 2(6) - 12 - 16 = -16$
$Q_{CD} = 2(4) - 12 - 16 = 8 - 28 = -20$

*Step 3 — Minima*: $Q_{AB} = -20$ and $Q_{CD} = -20$ tie. By convention, choose the pair including the alphabetically/numerically first pair → **A and B joined** into node $u$.

*Step 4 — Branch lengths*:

$L_{Au} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2) + \tfrac{12-12}{2(2)} = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0$
$L_{Bu} = 2.0 - 1.0 = 1.0$

*Step 5 — Distances to $u$*:

$d_{Cu} = \tfrac{1}{2}(4 + 4 - 2) = 3.0$
$d_{Du} = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6 - 2) = 5.0$

*Step 6 — Reduced matrix*:

| | $u$ | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| $u$ | 0 | 3 | 5 |
| C | 3 | 0 | 4 |
| D | 5 | 4 | 0 |

(Final 7 marks distributed across steps 1–6 above.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Style]**

**(a) Compare and contrast the Neighbor-Joining and UPGMA algorithms with respect to (i) tree type produced, (ii) assumption of rate constancy, and (iii) computational complexity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | UPGMA | Neighbor-Joining |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Tree type | **Rooted** | **Unrooted** |
| (ii) Rate assumption | **Strict molecular clock** (equal rates along all lineages) | **No molecular clock** (rates may vary) |
| (iii) Time complexity | $O(n^2 \log n)$ (with a heap) | $O(n^3)$ naïve; reducible to $O(n^2)$ with neighbour search |
| Selection rule | Smallest $d_{ij}$ | Smallest $Q_{ij}$ (rate-corrected) |
| Output branch lengths | Average linkage distances | Saitou–Nei weighted lengths |
| Root placement | Rooted at midpoint of the last two clusters | Requires outgroup for rooting |

UPGMA is a special case of NJ only when the additive metric is *ultrametric* (the molecular clock holds). With realistic data showing rate heterogeneity, NJ gives a more accurate tree. (7 marks)

**(b) The Q-matrix computed for five OTUs in a study is shown below (only upper triangle shown). Identify the next neighbor pair to be joined, compute their branch lengths to the new node, and the distance from the new node to OTU 3. (7 marks)**

| | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | $-30$ | $-20$ | $-25$ | $-22$ |
| 2 | | — | $-24$ | $-28$ | $-26$ |
| 3 | | | — | $-23$ | $-21$ |
| 4 | | | | — | $-27$ |
| 5 | | | | | — |

Original distance values (from the same dataset): $d_{12} = 4$, $d_{13} = 5$, $d_{23} = 5$. Row sums of the original distance matrix: $r_1 = 13$, $r_2 = 14$, $r_3 = 12$.

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1*: Minimum $Q_{ij}$ from the table is $Q_{12} = -30$. So OTUs 1 and 2 are joined into node $u$.

*Step 2*: With $n=5$, branch lengths:

$L_{1u} = \tfrac{1}{2}d_{12} + \tfrac{r_1 - r_2}{2(n-2)} = \tfrac{1}{2}(4) + \tfrac{13 - 14}{2(3)} = 2.0 - \tfrac{1}{6} \approx 1.833$
$L_{2u} = 4.0 - 1.833 \approx 2.167$

*Step 3*: Distance from $u$ to OTU 3:

$d_{3u} = \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{13} + d_{23} - d_{12}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(5 + 5 - 4) = 3.0$

(Final 7 marks across these three steps.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Using raw $d_{ij}$ instead of $Q_{ij}$** for neighbor selection — this is UPGMA's rule, not NJ's. (Penalty: full 2 marks lost in (a) and the answer is logically wrong.)
> 2. **Forgetting the $(n-2)$ factor** in $Q_{ij}$. Many students write $Q_{ij} = d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$ and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up $L_{iu}$ and $L_{ju}$**: always recompute $L_{ju} = d_{ij} - L_{iu}$ to verify; the two branch lengths must add to $d_{ij}$.
> 4. **Tie-breaking silence**: when two $Q_{ij}$ values are equal, KTU expects an *explicit* tie-breaking statement (e.g., "by lexicographic order" or "by the most recently created node"). Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the four-point condition** check at the end of a worked example — examiners often award 1 mark for verifying additivity.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **NJ was proposed by Saitou & Nei in 1987** — quote it correctly in 1-mark questions.
- ✅ **NJ produces an UNROOTED tree** — a high-frequency MCQ trap.
- ✅ **Q-matrix formula**: $Q_{ij} = (n-2)d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$; **minimize**, not maximize.
- ✅ **Branch length to leaf $i$**: $L_{iu} = \tfrac{1}{2}d_{ij} + \tfrac{r_i - r_j}{2(n-2)}$.
- ✅ **Distance to new node**: $d_{ku} = \tfrac{1}{2}(d_{ik} + d_{jk} - d_{ij})$.
- ✅ **Stop when $n=2$**; final tree has $2n-3$ edges.
- ✅ **NJ does NOT assume a molecular clock** — unlike UPGMA.
- ✅ **NJ is a greedy heuristic** — does not guarantee the globally optimal tree.
- ✅ **Negative branch lengths are allowed** in NJ — they indicate unequal rates, not a calculation error.
- ✅ **Final sanity check**: verify the four-point condition for any quartet of leaves.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Parsimony
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maximum Parsimony

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Maximum Parsimony (MP)** is a character-based, optimality-criterion method for **phylogenetic tree reconstruction** that selects the evolutionary tree requiring the *fewest* number of character-state changes (e.g., nucleotide substitutions, amino acid replacements) to explain the observed biological data. The tree topology that minimizes the total number of evolutionary changes across all sites is declared the **Most Parsimonious Tree (MPT)**.

Mathematically, given a set of aligned sequences $D$ and the universe of all unrooted binary trees $\mathcal{T}$, MP finds:

$$T_{MP} = \arg\min_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i(T)$$

where $L_i(T)$ is the **parsimony score** (tree length) contributed by site $i$ to tree $T$, and $N$ is the number of aligned columns (sites) in the dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** MP belongs to the family of **cladistic methods** introduced by Willi Hennig (1966) and operationalized computationally by Farris (1970), Fitch (1971), and Kluge & Farris (1969). It is a **discrete, non-probabilistic** approach — it does not assume any explicit model of substitution (unlike Maximum Likelihood or Bayesian methods).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Detective's Shortest Story"

Imagine a crime scene with four suspects, each holding a different coloured glove. A detective wants to reconstruct who stole the glove by assuming the thief *changed* gloves the fewest possible times.

- The detective tries every possible "family tree" linking the suspects.
- For each tree, she counts how many glove-changes (mutations) are required to explain the current arrangement.
- The tree needing the **fewest changes** is accepted as the true story.

Maximum Parsimony applies identical logic to **DNA/protein sequences** at **aligned columns** — each column is a "crime scene" of nucleotides, and the tree requiring the fewest substitutions wins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle (Occam's Razor):** *Entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter necessitatem* — "Entities must not be multiplied beyond necessity." The simplest hypothesis that fits the data is preferred.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Visibility)

| Term | Symbol | Standard Value / Meaning |
|------|--------|--------------------------|
| Number of Taxa | $n$ | Typically 4–100 in MP analyses |
| Number of Sites | $N$ | Aligned columns considered |
| Tree Length | $L(T)$ | **Minimum total number of changes** on tree $T$ |
| Consistency Index | $CI$ | $\mathbf{CI = M / S}$ where $M$ = minimum possible changes, $S$ = observed changes |
| Retention Index | $RI$ | $\mathbf{RI = (G - S) / (G - M)}$ where $G$ = maximum possible changes |
| Homoplasy | $H$ | $H = L(T) - M$ (extra changes due to convergence/reversal) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Four Phases of Maximum Parsimony

Maximum Parsimony is executed in **four sequential phases**. Each phase has a clear "why" and "how."

#### Phase 1: Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)
- **Why?** MP requires homologous characters to be stacked in vertical columns; misaligned sites produce fictitious mutations.
- **How?** Tools such as **ClustalW**, **MAFFT**, **MUSCLE**, or **T-Coffee** generate the alignment.

#### Phase 2: Site Classification
Each column is classified into one of three categories:

- **Invariant sites** — only one character state present; contribute **0** to tree length. *(Uninformative)*
- **Singleton (autapomorphic) sites** — exactly one taxon differs from the rest; contribute exactly **1** to tree length regardless of topology. *(Uninformative for topology)*
- **Phylogenetically informative sites** — at least **two different character states**, each appearing in **at least two taxa**. *These are the only sites that discriminate between competing topologies.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** A site is informative **iff** it contains $\geq 2$ character states with each state occurring $\geq 2$ times. For 4 taxa, the minimum parsimony-informative pattern is *2+2* (e.g., A, A, G, G).

#### Phase 3: Tree-Space Search (Optimization Problem)
The number of unrooted binary trees on $n$ taxa is:

$$N_{unrooted} = \frac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$$

$$N_{rooted} = \frac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-2}(n-2)!}$$

- For $n=10$: $N_{unrooted} = 2,027,025$
- For $n=20$: $N_{unrooted} \approx 2.2 \times 10^{20}$ — search becomes NP-hard.

**Three search strategies are used:**

| Strategy | Complexity | Use Case |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Exhaustive (Branch & Bound)** | $O(N_{unrooted})$ | $n \leq 12$ |
| **Heuristic (Stepwise / NNI / SPR / TBR)** | Sub-exponential | $n > 12$ |
| **Branch and Bound** | Guaranteed optimal, bounded | $n \leq 25$ |

#### Phase 4: Tree Scoring — The Small Parsimony Problem
Given a **fixed** tree topology with leaves labelled, compute the minimum number of substitutions. Two algorithms dominate:

- **Fitch Algorithm (1971)** — for **unweighted**, **unordered** characters (nucleotides).
- **Sankoff Algorithm (1975)** — generalized DP allowing **weighted** costs and **step matrices**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Application |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Unrooted tree count | $N_{unrooted} = \dfrac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$ | Calculate search-space size |
| Rooted tree count | $N_{rooted} = \dfrac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-2}(n-2)!}$ | Clock-like trees |
| Consistency Index | $CI = M / S$ | Measures homoplasy; $CI=1$ → no homoplasy |
| Retention Index | $RI = (G-S) / (G-M)$ | Measures synapomorphy retention; $0 \leq RI \leq 1$ |
| Rescaled CI | $RC = CI \times RI$ | Combined fit measure |
| Fitch union rule | $S_v = S_{v_L} \cup S_{v_R}$ | Construct ancestral state set |
| Fitch intersection rule | $S_v = S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R}$ | Detect informative intersections |
| Sankoff recursion | $S_v(k) = \min_{j} \left[ S_{v_L}(j) + w(j,k) \right] + \min_{m} \left[ S_{v_R}(m) + w(k,m) \right]$ | Weighted parsimony |
| Tree length (unweighted) | $L(T) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i(T)$ | Sum of minimum changes per site |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Maximum Parsimony is the workhorse of **molecular systematics** (e.g., HIV subtyping, viral origin tracing, paleogenomic placement of Neanderthal/Denisovan sequences), **cladistic biogeography**, and **forensic DNA lineage analysis**. Software: **PAUP\***, **MEGA**, **TNT**, **PHYLIP (DNAPARS)**, **RAxML-parsimony**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Fitch Algorithm — Full Derivation (Unweighted MP)

The Fitch algorithm solves the **Small Parsimony Problem** in $O(n \cdot k)$ time, where $n$ is taxa count and $k$ is the number of character states.

#### 3.1.1 Setup
- Each leaf $v$ is assigned a set $S_v \subseteq \{A, C, G, T\}$ reflecting the observed state.
- For unambiguous nucleotides, $\vert S_v \vert = 1$; for ambiguous (e.g., N, IUPAC codes), $\vert S_v \vert > 1$.

#### 3.1.2 Bottom-Up Pass (Postorder Traversal)

**Rule 1 (Intersection ≠ ∅):** If $S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R} \neq \emptyset$, then
$$S_v = S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R}, \quad \text{incremental cost } c_v = 0$$

**Rule 2 (Intersection = ∅):** If $S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R} = \emptyset$, then
$$S_v = S_{v_L} \cup S_{v_R}, \quad \text{incremental cost } c_v = 1$$

#### 3.1.3 Top-Down Pass (Preorder Back-Tracing)

Starting at the root with an arbitrary state from $S_{root}$:
- If the assigned state $k \in S_{v_L}$, propagate $k$ unchanged.
- Otherwise, pick any state from $S_{v_L}$ and add a substitution along the edge $(v \to v_L)$.

#### 3.1.4 Worked Example — Fitch on a Single Site

Given the unrooted tree with four taxa and the site states:
- $S_A = \{A\}$, $S_B = \{A\}$, $S_C = \{G\}$, $S_D = \{G\}$

Tree: $((A,B),(C,D))$

**Bottom-up pass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AB} &= S_A \cap S_B = \{A\} \cap \{A\} = \{A\}, \quad c_{AB} = 0 \\
S_{CD} &= S_C \cap S_D = \{G\} \cap \{G\} = \{G\}, \quad c_{CD} = 0 \\
S_{root} &= S_{AB} \cap S_{CD} = \{A\} \cap \{G\} = \emptyset \\
\Rightarrow S_{root} &= S_{AB} \cup S_{CD} = \{A, G\}, \quad c_{root} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total tree length at this site:** $L = c_{AB} + c_{CD} + c_{root} = 0 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{1}$.

### 3.2 The Sankoff Algorithm — Full DP Derivation (Weighted MP)

When transition/transversion costs differ, the **Sankoff algorithm** is required. Let $S_v(k)$ denote the minimum cost of subtree rooted at $v$ given that $v$ is assigned state $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$.

#### 3.2.1 Initialization (Leaves)
For each leaf $v$ with observed state $i$:
$$
S_v(k) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } k = i \\ \infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

#### 3.2.2 Recursion (Postorder)
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_v(k) = \min_{j} \left[ S_{v_L}(j) + w(j,k) \right] + \min_{m} \left[ S_{v_R}(m) + w(k,m) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $w(j,k)$ is the cost of changing from state $j$ to state $k$ along a branch.

#### 3.2.3 Termination (Root Assignment)
$$
L(T) = \min_{k} S_{root}(k)
$$

#### 3.2.4 Worked Example — Weighted Sankoff (TV:TS = 2:1)

Cost matrix $w(j,k)$ (Ts = 1, Tv = 2):

| From \ To | A | C | G | T |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| C | 2 | 0 | 2 | 1 |
| G | 1 | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| T | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0 |

Taxa states: A, C, G, T (one each). Tree: $((A,C),(G,T))$.

**Step 1 — Internal node $X$ above $(A,C)$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X(A) &= \min_j[S_A(j)+w(j,A)] + \min_m[S_C(m)+w(A,m)] \\
&= [S_A(A)+0] + \min[S_C(A)+2,\; S_C(C)+0,\; S_C(G)+2,\; S_C(T)+1] \\
&= 0 + \min[\infty+2,\; 0+0,\; \infty+2,\; \infty+1] \\
&= 0 + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X(C) &= \min[S_A(j)+w(j,C)] + \min[S_C(m)+w(C,m)] \\
&= \min[0+2,\; \infty+0,\; \infty+2,\; \infty+1] + \min[\infty+2,\; 0+0,\; \infty+2,\; \infty+1] \\
&= 2 + 0 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X(G) &= \min[S_A(j)+w(j,G)] + \min[S_C(m)+w(C,G)] \\
&= \min[0+1,\; \infty+2,\; \infty+0,\; \infty+2] + \min[\infty+2,\; 0+2,\; \infty+0,\; \infty+2] \\
&= 1 + 2 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X(T) &= \min[S_A(j)+w(j,T)] + \min[S_C(m)+w(C,T)] \\
&= \min[0+2,\; \infty+1,\; \infty+2,\; \infty+0] + \min[\infty+2,\; 0+1,\; \infty+2,\; \infty+0] \\
&= 2 + 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Internal node $Y$ above $(G,T)$ (by symmetry, $S_Y(G)=0$, $S_Y(T)=2$, $S_Y(A)=3$, $S_Y(C)=3$).**

**Step 3 — Root $R$ above $(X,Y)$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R(A) &= \min_j[S_X(j)+w(j,A)] + \min_m[S_Y(m)+w(A,m)] \\
&= \min[0+0,\; 2+2,\; 3+1,\; 3+2] + \min[3+0,\; 3+2,\; 0+1,\; 2+2] \\
&= 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R(C) &= 2 + 0 = 2 \\
S_R(G) &= 1 + 0 = 1 \\
S_R(T) &= 2 + 0 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum Tree Length:**
$$
L(T) = \min\{1, 2, 1, 2\} = \mathbf{1}
$$

A most parsimonious assignment places the root in state **A** *or* **G** (both yield $L=1$).

### 3.3 Homoplasy Metrics — Explicit Derivation

Given an MP tree with length $L_{obs}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI &= \frac{M}{L_{obs}} \\
RI &= \frac{L_{max} - L_{obs}}{L_{max} - M} \\
RC &= CI \times RI = \frac{M (L_{max} - L_{obs})}{L_{obs}(L_{max} - M)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $M$ = minimum possible changes (sum of minimum state changes per site, $\sum_i m_i$)
- $L_{max}$ = maximum possible changes on the *worst* tree
- $L_{obs}$ = observed length of the MP tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Homoplasy** = $L_{obs} - M$ counts convergent evolution, parallel evolution, and reversal events.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End Maximum Parsimony Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Aligned Sequences] --> B[Phase 1: Multiple Sequence Alignment Verification]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Site Classification]
    C --> D{Is Site Phylogenetically Informative?}
    D -->|No| E[Add 0 or 1 to Tree Length Constantly]
    D -->|Yes| F[Contribute to Topology Discrimination]
    E --> G[Phase 3: Tree Space Search]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Search Strategy}
    H -->|n le 12| I[Exhaustive Enumeration]
    H -->|12 lt n le 25| J[Branch and Bound]
    H -->|n gt 25| K[Heuristic: NNI, SPR, TBR]
    I --> L[Phase 4: Compute Tree Length per Topology]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Apply Fitch Algorithm or Sankoff Algorithm]
    M --> N{Tree Length Minimum?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Mark as Most Parsimonious Tree MPT]
    N -->|No| P[Discard Topology]
    O --> Q[Compute CI, RI, RC Homoplasy Metrics]
    P --> G
    Q --> R[Output MP Tree with Bootstrap Support]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Tree Topology — Worked Example Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    root((Root R)) --> nA((A))
    root --> nX((Internal X))
    nX --> nA1((A: 0))
    nX --> nC1((C: 0))
    nX --> nY((Internal Y))
    nY --> nG1((G: 0))
    nY --> nT1((T: 0))
    root -.->|edge cost 1| nX
    nX -.->|edge cost 0| nA1
    nX -.->|edge cost 0| nC1
    nX -.->|edge cost 1| nY
    nY -.->|edge cost 0| nG1
    nY -.->|edge cost 0| nT1
```

*Caption: Schematic of Sankoff-tree $((A,C),(G,T))$ with weighted edge costs; final $L=1$ when Ts:Tv = 1:2.*

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Fitch Algorithm Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Leaf Node Input S_v] --> pass1[Bottom Up Postorder Traversal]
    pass1 --> cond1{S left child cap S right child neq empty set}
    cond1 -->|Yes| op1[Assign S_v equal Intersection cost 0]
    cond1 -->|No| op2[Assign S_v equal Union cost 1]
    op1 --> accum[Accumulate Costs Across Sites]
    op2 --> accum
    accum --> pass2[Top Down Preorder Back Tracing]
    pass2 --> out1[Reconstruct Ancestral States]
    out1 --> out2[Final Tree Length L of T]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams follow the **KTU 2024 diagram-rendering standard** for the *Bioinformatics* course — every node ID is alphanumeric, every label is clean uppercase text, and no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define Maximum Parsimony. What is a "phylogenetically informative site"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Maximum Parsimony (1 mark):** A character-based phylogenetic method that selects the tree requiring the **fewest evolutionary changes** to explain the observed sequence data.
- **Most Parsimonious Tree (MPT) (1 mark):** The tree topology with the minimum total tree length $L(T)$ across all informative sites.
- **Phylogenetically informative site (1 mark):** A column in the alignment containing **at least two different character states**, each occurring in **at least two taxa** (e.g., the 2+2 pattern AAGG).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*State the formula for the number of unrooted binary trees on $n$ taxa. For how many taxa does exhaustive search become infeasible?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Formula (2 marks):**
$$N_{unrooted} = \frac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$$
- **Feasibility threshold (1 mark):** Exhaustive enumeration is infeasible beyond **$n \approx 12$** taxa (the count exceeds $\sim 2 \times 10^6$ trees at $n=10$ and explodes combinatorially thereafter). Beyond this, heuristic search (NNI/SPR/TBR) is used.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Fitch algorithm** for the small parsimony problem with a suitable example. Compute the parsimony score for the site pattern $S_1 = \{A\}, S_2 = \{C\}, S_3 = \{G\}, S_4 = \{T\}$ on the unrooted tree $((1,2),(3,4))$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Consistency Index (CI)** and **Retention Index (RI)**. A dataset of 50 aligned sites yields a most parsimonious tree of length 78. The minimum possible tree length $M = 70$ and the maximum possible tree length $L_{max} = 120$. Calculate CI, RI, and RC. Comment on the homoplasy level. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer A:**

**(a) Fitch Algorithm (7 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Fitch algorithm is a two-pass, bottom-up-then-top-down dynamic-programming procedure that computes the minimum number of substitutions on a fixed tree in $O(n \cdot k)$ time, where $k$ = number of character states.
- **Phase 1 — Bottom-up (2 marks):** Postorder traversal.
  * Rule 1: If $S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R} \neq \emptyset$, then $S_v = S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R}$ and incremental cost = 0.
  * Rule 2: If $S_{v_L} \cap S_{v_R} = \emptyset$, then $S_v = S_{v_L} \cup S_{v_R}$ and incremental cost = 1.
- **Phase 2 — Top-down (1 mark):** Choose any state from $S_{root}$ and assign greedily to children; cost 1 incurred whenever a child lacks the assigned state.
- **Numerical computation (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{12} &= \{A\} \cap \{C\} = \emptyset \Rightarrow S_{12} = \{A,C\},\; c_{12}=1 \\
S_{34} &= \{G\} \cap \{T\} = \emptyset \Rightarrow S_{34} = \{G,T\},\; c_{34}=1 \\
S_{root} &= \{A,C\} \cap \{G,T\} = \emptyset \Rightarrow S_{root} = \{A,C,G,T\},\; c_{root}=1 \\
L &= 1 + 1 + 1 = \mathbf{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) CI, RI, RC (7 marks):**
- **Definitions (2 marks):** $CI$ measures how well the data fit the tree without homoplasy; $RI$ measures the fraction of possible synapomorphies actually retained. $RC = CI \times RI$ combines both.
- **CI calculation (1.5 marks):**
$$CI = \frac{M}{S} = \frac{70}{78} = 0.897$$
- **RI calculation (1.5 marks):**
$$RI = \frac{L_{max} - S}{L_{max} - M} = \frac{120 - 78}{120 - 70} = \frac{42}{50} = 0.840$$
- **RC calculation (1 mark):**
$$RC = CI \times RI = 0.897 \times 0.840 = 0.753$$
- **Comment (1 mark):** High CI (> 0.8) and high RI (> 0.8) indicate **low homoplasy**; the dataset fits the MP tree with little convergent/reversal noise, suggesting strong phylogenetic signal.

---

**Question B (14 marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** With a clear diagram, describe the **four operational phases** of Maximum Parsimony phylogenetic reconstruction. What role do **autapomorphies** and **synapomorphies** play in parsimony analysis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Sankoff algorithm** to compute the weighted parsimony score for the tree $((A,C),(G,T))$ using the cost matrix where transitions = 1 and transversions = 2. Show all working steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer B:**

**(a) Four Phases + Apomorphy Types (7 marks):**
- **Phase 1 — MSA (1.5 marks):** Use ClustalW/MAFFT to align homologs. Misalignment introduces spurious mutations.
- **Phase 2 — Site Classification (1.5 marks):** Classify columns as invariant, singleton (autapomorphic), or parsimony-informative.
- **Phase 3 — Tree Search (2 marks):** Exhaustive (small $n$), branch-and-bound (medium $n$), or heuristic NNI/SPR/TBR (large $n$).
- **Phase 4 — Tree Scoring (1 mark):** Fitch (unweighted) or Sankoff (weighted) algorithm applied to each candidate topology.
- **Apomorphies (1 mark):** *Autapomorphies* (derived characters unique to one taxon) add 1 to tree length but **do not help topology choice**; *synapomorphies* (shared derived characters) are the **primary source of phylogenetic signal** in MP, since they group taxa into clades.

**(b) Sankoff Algorithm (7 marks):** (Worked fully in Section 3.2.4 above.)
- **Initialization (1 mark):** $S_A(A)=0$, else $\infty$; $S_C(C)=0$, else $\infty$; $S_G(G)=0$, else $\infty$; $S_T(T)=0$, else $\infty$.
- **Internal node X (2.5 marks):** $S_X(A)=0$, $S_X(C)=2$, $S_X(G)=3$, $S_X(T)=3$.
- **Internal node Y (1.5 marks):** $S_Y(G)=0$, $S_Y(T)=2$, $S_Y(A)=3$, $S_Y(C)=3$.
- **Root R (1 mark):** $S_R(A)=1$, $S_R(C)=2$, $S_R(G)=1$, $S_R(T)=2$.
- **Minimum (1 mark):** $L(T) = \min = \mathbf{1}$. Root can be A or G.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Maximum Parsimony Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing informative vs singleton sites** — A 1+3 pattern (e.g., A, G, G, G) is *not* informative because only one character state appears twice; it adds 1 to every tree and cannot discriminate topologies. Examiners deduct 1 mark if students count it as informative.
> 2. **Forgetting the union rule's cost increment** — In Fitch, when intersection is empty, students often assign the union but **forget the +1 cost**, leading to an undercounted tree length. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Mixing rooted vs unrooted tree counts** — The formula $N_{unrooted}$ uses $(2n-5)!$ and $2^{n-3}$, while $N_{rooted}$ uses $(2n-5)!$ and $2^{n-2}$. One-fact confusion = full mark loss on tree-count questions.
> 4. **Sankoff: missing the two-child minimization** — The Sankoff recursion has **two** $\min(\cdot)$ terms (one per child). Writing only one is a 2-mark error.
> 5. **Claiming MP "assumes a molecular clock"** — MP makes **no clock assumption**; it is model-free at the substitution level. This conceptual error loses 1 mark in 7-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Maximum Parsimony**

- ✅ **MP is a character-based, optimality-criterion, non-parametric tree-building method** based on Occam's razor.
- ✅ **Goal:** Find the tree that requires the **minimum number of evolutionary changes** to explain aligned sequences.
- ✅ **Tree length $L(T)$** is the total parsimony score; the **Most Parsimonious Tree (MPT)** has $L_{min}$.
- ✅ **Parsimony-informative site rule:** $\geq 2$ character states AND each state in $\geq 2$ taxa. The classic 2+2 pattern is the smallest informative configuration.
- ✅ **Uninformative sites:** invariant columns and singletons — counted in $L(T)$ but **topology-blind**.
- ✅ **Fitch algorithm:** two-pass DP; intersection → cost 0, union → cost 1. Unweighted, unordered.
- ✅ **Sankoff algorithm:** weighted DP using a cost matrix $w(j,k)$; supports Ts/Tv asymmetry.
- ✅ **Tree-space size:** $N_{unrooted} = \frac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$; NP-hard for $n > 25$.
- ✅ **Search strategies:** Exhaustive ($n \le 12$), Branch-and-Bound ($n \le 25$), Heuristic NNI/SPR/TBR ($n > 25$).
- ✅ **Homoplasy metrics:** $CI = M/S$, $RI = (G-S)/(G-M)$, $RC = CI \times RI$. $CI=1$ implies no homoplasy.
- ✅ **Software:** PAUP\*, MEGA, TNT, PHYLIP (DNAPARS), NONA.
- ✅ **MP makes no model assumption** about substitution rates; it is model-free but sensitive to long-branch attraction (LBA) — caveat for examiners.
- ✅ **Key historical references to mention:** Hennig (1966), Kluge & Farris (1969), Farris (1970), Fitch (1971), Sankoff (1975). *Do not fabricate editions.*

---

**End of Module 3 — Maximum Parsimony Notes**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PEITT861 — Bioinformatics standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Representations - Deterministic and Probabilistic Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pattern Representations - Deterministic and Probabilistic Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pattern** in bioinformatics is a short, recurring nucleotide or amino-acid sequence of biological significance that is hypothesized to be functionally or structurally important. Patterns are mathematical abstractions of conserved regions (motifs) found across a set of related biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or proteins).

**Pattern Representation** refers to the formal encoding scheme used to describe these conserved motifs in a manner that allows:

1. Storage in biological databases,
2. Search and discovery within large sequence collections,
3. Mathematical scoring and comparison of new sequences against known motifs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Pattern representations fall into two principal families:
> - **Deterministic Patterns** (also called *Consensus Patterns* or *Exact Patterns*)
> - **Probabilistic Patterns** (also called *Statistical Patterns* or *Weight-Matrix Patterns*)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian and you have **100 poems written by the same poet**. You notice that the *first word* of every poem is either "The", "A", or "An". You could represent this observation in two ways:

- **Deterministic Approach:** You write a rigid rule — *"The first word is EXACTLY 'The'."* This is precise, but it will fail to recognize poems that start with "A" or "An".
- **Probabilistic Approach:** You write a flexible rule — *"There is a 60% chance the first word is 'The', a 30% chance it is 'A', and a 10% chance it is 'An'."* This is more realistic, tolerates variation, and captures biological reality better.

In biology, **DNA sequences evolve and mutate**, so a "Transcription Factor Binding Site" is rarely identical in every occurrence. The **deterministic approach** demands a perfect match, while the **probabilistic approach** allows soft matching based on observed frequencies — exactly mirroring the biological reality of sequence conservation with variation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - Deterministic = exact, rigid, character-by-character match (uses IUPAC codes, regular expressions, consensus strings).
> - Probabilistic = statistical, flexible, frequency-based match (uses Position Weight Matrices, PSSMs, HMM profiles).

### 1.3 IUPAC Ambiguity Codes (Foundational Vocabulary)

A set of standard nucleotide codes used to represent positions where multiple bases are acceptable in deterministic patterns:

| Symbol | Meaning | Bases Represented |
|:------:|:--------|:------------------|
| A | Adenine | A |
| C | Cytosine | C |
| G | Guanine | G |
| T | Thymine | T |
| **R** | puRine | A, G |
| **Y** | pYrimidine | C, T |
| **S** | Strong (3 H-bonds) | G, C |
| **W** | Weak (2 H-bonds) | A, T |
| **K** | Keto | G, T |
| **M** | aMino | A, C |
| **B** | not A | C, G, T |
| **D** | not C | A, G, T |
| **H** | not G | A, C, T |
| **V** | not T (not U) | A, C, G |
| **N** | aNy base | A, C, G, T |
| **X** | aNy amino acid | Any |

These IUPAC codes are critical for **deterministic** representation because they permit compact description of weakly conserved positions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deterministic Pattern Representations

Deterministic patterns describe a motif using a single fixed string, where each position is specified either as an exact nucleotide/amino acid or as an IUPAC ambiguity code.

#### 2.1.1 Types of Deterministic Patterns

**A. Simple Consensus String (Most-Reliable Base)**

Given an aligned set of sequences, the consensus is built by selecting, at each column, the most frequent base.

> Example Alignment (5 sequences):
>
> ```
> A T G C A T
> A C G T A C
> A T G C A T
> A T G C A T
> G T G C A T
> ```
> Consensus: **ATG CAT** (column-wise majority vote)

**B. Regular Expression (Regex / PROSITE-style patterns)**

A formal language pattern using:
- `[ ]` for character classes
- `{n}` for exact repetition
- `x` for any amino acid
- `( )` for grouped choices

Example PROSITE pattern for the N-glycosylation site:
`N-{P}-[ST]-{P}`

Meaning: N, any residue except P, S or T, any residue except P.

**C. Exact String / Subsequence Pattern**

A literal substring (e.g., `TATAAA` for the TATA box), requiring a strict character-by-character match.

**D. Word-Based / Dictionary Patterns**

A list of valid tokens at each position, e.g., the TATA-box variant set `{TATAAA, TATAAT, TATATA, TATAAC}`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pros of Deterministic Patterns:**
> - Simple, fast to search (using algorithms like Aho-Corasick or Knuth-Morris-Pratt).
> - Human-readable and compact.
>
> **Cons of Deterministic Patterns:**
> - Cannot describe variable-strength conservation.
> - High false-negative rate when sequences diverge.
> - A single mismatch fails the match.

### 2.2 Probabilistic Pattern Representations

Probabilistic patterns describe a motif using the *observed frequency* of each nucleotide at each position. This is the dominant approach in modern bioinformatics (e.g., JASPAR, TRANSFAC databases).

#### 2.2.1 Position Frequency Matrix (PFM)

For an aligned set of $N$ sequences of length $L$, the PFM $M$ is a $4 \times L$ (DNA) or $20 \times L$ (protein) matrix where:

$$M_{i,j} = \text{count of nucleotide/amino-acid } i \text{ at position } j$$

Example (4 aligned sequences of length 5):

| Pos | A | C | G | T |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1   | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2   | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 |
| 3   | 0 | 0 | 4 | 0 |
| 4   | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4 |
| 5   | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

> Note that at each position the column sum equals $N$ (the number of sequences).

#### 2.2.2 Position Probability Matrix (PPM)

The PFM is normalized by dividing each entry by $N$ to obtain frequencies (probabilities):

$$P_{i,j} = \frac{M_{i,j}}{N}$$

For the example above with $N=4$:

| Pos | A   | C   | G   | T   |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1   | 1.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2   | 0.25 | 0.50 | 0.00 | 0.25 |
| 3   | 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 | 0.00 |
| 4   | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 |
| 5   | 0.25 | 0.25 | 0.25 | 0.25 |

**Background Probability** $b_i$ is the genome-wide frequency of base $i$ (often: $A=0.25, C=0.25, G=0.25, T=0.25$).

#### 2.2.3 Position Weight Matrix (PWM) / Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM)

The PWM converts probabilities to log-likelihood scores relative to a background model. This is the **most important** probabilistic representation for KTU.

The score of base $b$ at position $j$ is:

$$S_{b,j} = \log_2 \left( \frac{P_{b,j}}{b_b} \right)$$

The total score of a candidate sequence $S = s_1 s_2 \dots s_L$ is the sum of positional scores:

$$\text{Score}(S) = \sum_{j=1}^{L} S_{s_j, j}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of PWM score:**
> - **Positive score** $\rightarrow$ base is more conserved than background.
> - **Negative score** $\rightarrow$ base is depleted at this position.
> - **Score = 0** $\rightarrow$ base frequency equals background (no information).
> - A higher total score means a stronger match to the motif.

#### 2.2.4 Pseudocounts (Laplace Smoothing)

To avoid $\log(0)$ when a base never appears at a position, a **pseudocount** $\alpha$ is added:

$$P_{i,j} = \frac{M_{i,j} + \alpha}{N + 4\alpha}$$

Typical choice: $\alpha = 0.5$ (small-sample correction).

#### 2.2.5 Information Content & Sequence Logos

The **Information Content (IC)** at position $j$ (in bits) measures how much the motif constraint reduces uncertainty relative to random background:

$$IC_j = 2 - H_j = 2 + \sum_{b \in \{A,C,G,T\}} P_{b,j} \cdot \log_2(P_{b,j})$$

where $H_j$ is the Shannon entropy at column $j$:

$$H_j = -\sum_{b} P_{b,j} \cdot \log_2(P_{b,j})$$

Maximum IC = 2 bits per position (perfect conservation in DNA).

**Sequence Logo** is the visual representation: stack heights proportional to $IC_j$, letter heights proportional to frequency at that position.

#### 2.2.6 Profile Hidden Markov Models (Profile HMMs)

The most expressive probabilistic model. A profile HMM has three-state-per-position architecture:

- **Match state (M):** emits a conserved residue.
- **Insert state (I):** emits a residue inserted relative to the consensus.
- **Delete state (D):** silent state for skipping this position.

Transition probabilities $a_{kl}$ and emission probabilities $e_k(b)$ are learned from aligned training sequences using Baum-Welch or directly from a multiple alignment. Tools: **HMMER**, **SAM**, **PSI-BLAST**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|:--------|:--------|:--------------|
| PFM Entry | $M_{i,j} = \text{count}(i,j)$ | Integer; row sum over $i$ = $N$ |
| PPM Entry | $P_{i,j} = \dfrac{M_{i,j}}{N}$ | $\sum_i P_{i,j} = 1$ for each $j$ |
| Pseudocount PPM | $P_{i,j} = \dfrac{M_{i,j}+\alpha}{N+4\alpha}$ | $\alpha \in [0.5, 1]$ typical |
| PWM (PSSM) Entry | $S_{b,j} = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{P_{b,j}}{b_b} \right)$ | Units: **bits** |
| PWM Sequence Score | $\text{Score}(S) = \sum_{j=1}^{L} S_{s_j,j}$ | Higher = better match |
| Shannon Entropy (per pos.) | $H_j = -\sum_{b} P_{b,j}\log_2 P_{b,j}$ | $0 \le H_j \le 2$ for DNA |
| Information Content (per pos.) | $IC_j = 2 - H_j$ | $0 \le IC_j \le 2$ bits |
| Motif Total IC | $IC_{\text{motif}} = \sum_j IC_j$ | $0 \le IC \le 2L$ bits |
| Log-Odds Threshold | $T = \sum_j \log_2(1/N_b)$ | Common empirical cutoff |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All PWM scores are in **log base 2 (bits)** unless otherwise stated. The natural-log form (log-odds in nats) is mathematically equivalent but conventionally written as $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Bioinformatics Utility

| Application | Pattern Type Used | Tool / Database |
|:------------|:------------------|:----------------|
| Transcription factor binding site discovery | PWM (PSSM) | JASPAR, TRANSFAC |
| Protein domain detection | Profile HMM | Pfam, HMMER |
| Restriction enzyme site search | Deterministic regex | REBASE, NEBcutter |
| Gene regulatory element (TATA, CAAT) | Deterministic consensus + PWM | EPD, PlantPAN |
| Motif discovery (de novo) | PWM via EM / Gibbs sampling | MEME, Gibbs Motif Sampler |
| Protein motif (PROSITE) | Deterministic regex patterns | PROSITE / ScanProsite |
| Sequence alignment seeding | PWM / HMM | BLAST (PSI-BLAST), HMMER |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
