## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example: From Sequences to PWM (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement:**

Given 5 aligned DNA sequences of length 6 representing a putative motif, derive the PFM, PPM, PWM, and total Information Content.

Aligned training sequences (each column = aligned position):

```
Seq1: A  C  G  A  T  G
Seq2: A  T  G  A  T  G
Seq3: A  C  G  A  T  G
Seq4: G  C  A  A  T  G
Seq5: A  C  G  A  C  G
```

Assume background: $b_A = b_C = b_G = b_T = 0.25$ and pseudocount $\alpha = 0$ (sufficient data).

---

#### Step 1 — Construct the Position Frequency Matrix (PFM)

Count occurrences of each nucleotide at each of the 6 positions:

- **Position 1:** A appears in Seq1, Seq2, Seq3, Seq5 (4 times); G appears in Seq4 (1 time).
  → A=4, C=0, G=1, T=0.
- **Position 2:** C appears in Seq1, Seq3, Seq4, Seq5 (4 times); T appears in Seq2 (1 time).
  → A=0, C=4, G=0, T=1.
- **Position 3:** G appears in Seq1, Seq2, Seq3, Seq5 (4 times); A appears in Seq4 (1 time).
  → A=1, C=0, G=4, T=0.
- **Position 4:** A appears in all 5 sequences.
  → A=5, C=0, G=0, T=0.
- **Position 5:** T appears in Seq1, Seq2, Seq3, Seq4 (4 times); C appears in Seq5 (1 time).
  → A=0, C=1, G=0, T=4.
- **Position 6:** G appears in all 5 sequences.
  → A=0, C=0, G=5, T=0.

**PFM Table:**

| Pos | A | C | G | T |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1   | 4 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2   | 0 | 4 | 0 | 1 |
| 3   | 1 | 0 | 4 | 0 |
| 4   | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 5   | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| 6   | 0 | 0 | 5 | 0 |

Column sums: all equal **5** (the number of sequences $N=5$). ✓

---

#### Step 2 — Construct the Position Probability Matrix (PPM)

Divide each entry by $N = 5$:

- Position 1: A=4/5=0.80, C=0, G=1/5=0.20, T=0.
- Position 2: A=0, C=4/5=0.80, G=0, T=1/5=0.20.
- Position 3: A=1/5=0.20, C=0, G=4/5=0.80, T=0.
- Position 4: A=1.00, C=0, G=0, T=0.
- Position 5: A=0, C=0.20, G=0, T=0.80.
- Position 6: A=0, C=0, G=1.00, T=0.

**PPM Table:**

| Pos | A    | C    | G    | T    |
|:---:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|
| 1   | 0.80 | 0.00 | 0.20 | 0.00 |
| 2   | 0.00 | 0.80 | 0.00 | 0.20 |
| 3   | 0.20 | 0.00 | 0.80 | 0.00 |
| 4   | 1.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 5   | 0.00 | 0.20 | 0.00 | 0.80 |
| 6   | 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 | 0.00 |

Each column sums to 1.00. ✓

---

#### Step 3 — Construct the Position Weight Matrix (PWM)

Apply $S_{b,j} = \log_2(P_{b,j} / b_b)$ with $b_b = 0.25$:

**Position 1:**
- A: $\log_2(0.80 / 0.25) = \log_2(3.20) = 1.678$ bits
- C: $\log_2(0 / 0.25) = -\infty$ (here $\approx -4$ after pseudocount, but we keep it as 0 for now if we treat absent bases conservatively)
- G: $\log_2(0.20 / 0.25) = \log_2(0.80) = -0.322$ bits
- T: same as C → $-\infty$

**Position 2:**
- A: $-\infty$
- C: $\log_2(0.80 / 0.25) = 1.678$ bits
- G: $-\infty$
- T: $\log_2(0.20 / 0.25) = -0.322$ bits

**Position 3:**
- A: $\log_2(0.20 / 0.25) = -0.322$ bits
- C: $-\infty$
- G: $\log_2(0.80 / 0.25) = 1.678$ bits
- T: $-\infty$

**Position 4:**
- A: $\log_2(1.00 / 0.25) = \log_2(4) = 2.000$ bits
- C, G, T: $-\infty$

**Position 5:**
- A: $-\infty$
- C: $\log_2(0.20 / 0.25) = -0.322$ bits
- G: $-\infty$
- T: $\log_2(0.80 / 0.25) = 1.678$ bits

**Position 6:**
- A, C: $-\infty$
- G: $\log_2(1.00 / 0.25) = 2.000$ bits
- T: $-\infty$

**PWM Table (rounded to 3 decimal places):**

| Pos | A     | C     | G     | T     |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| 1   |  1.678 | -4.000 | -0.322 | -4.000 |
| 2   | -4.000 |  1.678 | -4.000 | -0.322 |
| 3   | -0.322 | -4.000 |  1.678 | -4.000 |
| 4   |  2.000 | -4.000 | -4.000 | -4.000 |
| 5   | -4.000 | -0.322 | -4.000 |  1.678 |
| 6   | -4.000 | -4.000 |  2.000 | -4.000 |

---

#### Step 4 — Score a New Candidate Sequence

Score the candidate $S = \text{A C G A T G}$ (which is identical to Seq1):

$$\text{Score}(S) = 1.678 + 1.678 + 1.678 + 2.000 + 1.678 + 2.000$$

$$\text{Score}(S) = 10.712 \text{ bits}$$

This is the **maximum possible score** for this PWM (sum of all conserved-position log-odds).

Now score a divergent candidate $S' = \text{T T T T T T}$ (all mismatches):

$$\text{Score}(S') = (-4) + (-0.322) + (-0.322) + (-4) + (-0.322) + (-4) = -12.966 \text{ bits}$$

A large negative score, confirming $S'$ is very unlikely to be a true instance of this motif.

---

#### Step 5 — Information Content (IC) at Each Position

For DNA, max entropy = 2 bits. Compute $IC_j = 2 - H_j$.

**Position 1** ($P_A=0.80, P_C=0, P_G=0.20, P_T=0$):
By convention $0 \log 0 = 0$.

$$H_1 = -[0.80 \log_2 0.80 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20] = -[0.80 \times (-0.322) + 0.20 \times (-2.322)]$$

$$H_1 = -[(-0.258) + (-0.464)] = -(-0.722) = 0.722 \text{ bits}$$

$$IC_1 = 2 - 0.722 = 1.278 \text{ bits}$$

By symmetry, $IC_2 = IC_3 = IC_5 = 1.278$ bits (same distribution shape).

**Position 4** (A=1.00, others=0):
$H_4 = -[1 \cdot \log_2 1] = 0$ → $IC_4 = 2.000$ bits (perfectly conserved).

**Position 6** identical: $IC_6 = 2.000$ bits.

**Total Motif Information Content:**

$$IC_{\text{motif}} = IC_1 + IC_2 + IC_3 + IC_4 + IC_5 + IC_6$$

$$IC_{\text{motif}} = 1.278 + 1.278 + 1.278 + 2.000 + 1.278 + 2.000 = 9.112 \text{ bits}$$

This is the **sequence logo height** summed across all positions.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Representation | Captures Variation? | Probabilistic? | Typical Tool | Storage |
|:---------------|:-------------------:|:--------------:|:-------------|:--------|
| Exact String | No (binary) | No | grep, strstr | O(L) |
| IUPAC Consensus | Partial | No | ScanProsite | O(L) |
| PROSITE Regex | Partial | No | ScanProsite | O(L) |
| PFM | Yes (raw counts) | Semi | MEME input | O(4L) |
| PPM | Yes | Yes | MEME | O(4L) |
| PWM / PSSM | Yes | Yes | JASPAR, MATCH | O(4L) |
| Profile HMM | Yes (gaps, indels) | Yes | HMMER, SAM | O(9L) |

### 4.3 Profile HMM Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Match State M1"] --> I1["Insert State I1"]
    S1 --> D1["Delete State D1"]
    M1 --> M2["Match State M2"]
    M1 --> D2["Delete State D2"]
    M1 --> I2["Insert State I2"]
    M2 --> M3["Match State M3"]
    M2 --> D3["Delete State D3"]
    M2 --> I3["Insert State I3"]
    M3 --> M4["Match State Mk"]
    M3 --> D4["Delete State D4"]
    M3 --> I4["Insert State Ik"]
    M4 --> NEXT["Continue to next position"]
    I1 --> M2
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> M3
    I3 --> M4
    D1 --> M2
    D2 --> M3
    D3 --> M4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Profile HMM architecture contains three states per alignment column: **Match (M)**, **Insert (I)**, and **Delete (D)**. M and I emit symbols; D is silent. The model captures both substitutions (M→M with different emission) and insertions/deletions (I-state and D-state transitions) — a feature deterministic patterns and PWMs cannot fully model.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**

**Differentiate between deterministic and probabilistic pattern representations in bioinformatics. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deterministic Patterns | Probabilistic Patterns |
|:-------|:----------------------|:----------------------|
| Definition | Represent a motif as a single fixed string using exact characters or IUPAC codes. | Represent a motif using observed nucleotide/amino-acid frequencies at each position. |
| Match criterion | All-or-nothing: an exact character-by-character match is required. | Soft match: candidate is scored by summing log-likelihoods; acceptance depends on a threshold. |
| Biological variation | Cannot tolerate mismatches well. | Naturally handles variation and mutations. |
| Example | `TATAAA` (TATA-box exact string) or `[AG]C[CT]G` (regex). | Position Weight Matrix (PWM) of length 6 derived from 5 aligned binding sites. |

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for clear definition, 1 mark for distinguishing match criterion, 1 mark for correct examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**What is a Position Weight Matrix (PWM)? How is it constructed from aligned sequences?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Position Weight Matrix (PWM)**, also called a Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM), is a $4 \times L$ (DNA) or $20 \times L$ (protein) matrix of log-likelihood scores used to represent a motif probabilistically.

**Construction procedure (3 steps):**

1. **Build the Position Frequency Matrix (PFM)** by counting the occurrence of each nucleotide at each of the $L$ aligned positions.
2. **Normalize** each PFM entry by dividing by the number of sequences $N$ to obtain the **Position Probability Matrix (PPM)**: $P_{i,j} = M_{i,j}/N$. (Optionally add pseudocounts $\alpha$.)
3. **Compute the log-likelihood** of each base $b$ at position $j$ relative to a background probability $b_b$:
   $$S_{b,j} = \log_2\left(\frac{P_{b,j}}{b_b}\right)$$

The score of a candidate sequence is then the sum of the relevant PWM entries.

---

### PART B (14-Mark Questions — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [Choose either A or B]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the construction of a **Position Weight Matrix (PWM)** from a set of aligned DNA sequences. Define each of PFM, PPM, and PWM with their mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Information Content (IC)** of a motif position. Given the following aligned DNA sequences of length 5, compute the **Position Probability Matrix**, the **Position Weight Matrix** (using uniform background $b=0.25$), and the **Information Content at each position** and the **total motif IC**. **(7 marks)**

```
Seq1: A  A  G  C  T
Seq2: A  C  G  C  T
Seq3: A  G  G  C  A
Seq4: A  A  A  C  T
Seq5: A  A  G  T  T
```

---

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**Part (a) — PWM Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Definitions (2 marks):**
   - **PFM** $M_{i,j}$: integer count of base $i$ at position $j$.
   - **PPM** $P_{i,j} = M_{i,j}/N$: normalized frequency.
   - **PWM** $S_{b,j} = \log_2(P_{b,j}/b_b)$: log-likelihood score.

2. **Example (3 marks):** Use any 3-sequence example as in Section 3.1 above; show PFM → PPM → PWM derivation.

3. **Scoring (2 marks):** Define $\text{Score}(S) = \sum_{j} S_{s_j,j}$ and explain threshold-based decision.

**Part (b) — Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: PFM (1 mark)**

Counting each base per column:

| Pos | A | C | G | T |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1   | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2   | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 3   | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1 |
| 4   | 0 | 4 | 0 | 1 |
| 5   | 0 | 0 | 1 | 4 |

(Each column sum = 5 ✓)

**Step 2: PPM (1 mark)** Divide by $N=5$:

| Pos | A   | C   | G   | T   |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1   | 1.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2   | 0.60 | 0.20 | 0.20 | 0.00 |
| 3   | 0.20 | 0.00 | 0.60 | 0.20 |
| 4   | 0.00 | 0.80 | 0.00 | 0.20 |
| 5   | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.20 | 0.80 |

**Step 3: PWM (1.5 marks)** $S_{b,j} = \log_2(P_{b,j}/0.25)$:

| Pos | A     | C     | G     | T     |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| 1   | 2.000 | -4.000 | -4.000 | -4.000 |
| 2   | 1.263 | -0.322 | -0.322 | -4.000 |
| 3   | -0.322 | -4.000 | 1.263 | -0.322 |
| 4   | -4.000 | 1.678 | -4.000 | -0.322 |
| 5   | -4.000 | -4.000 | -0.322 | 1.678 |

(Where $\log_2 2.4 = 1.263$ and $\log_2 3.2 = 1.678$.)

**Step 4: Information Content (2.5 marks)**

Using $H_j = -\sum_b P_{b,j}\log_2 P_{b,j}$ and $IC_j = 2 - H_j$ (with $0\log 0 \equiv 0$):

- **Position 1:** $H_1 = -[1 \cdot \log_2 1] = 0$ → $IC_1 = 2.000$ bits.
- **Position 2:** $H_2 = -[0.60 \log_2 0.60 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20]$
  $= -[0.60 \times (-0.737) + 2 \times 0.20 \times (-2.322)]$
  $= -[(-0.442) + (-0.929)] = 1.371$ bits → $IC_2 = 0.629$ bits.
- **Position 3:** By symmetry with position 2, $H_3 = 1.371$ → $IC_3 = 0.629$ bits.
- **Position 4:** $H_4 = -[0.80 \log_2 0.80 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20] = -[(-0.258) + (-0.464)] = 0.722$ → $IC_4 = 1.278$ bits.
- **Position 5:** Same as position 4 → $IC_5 = 1.278$ bits.

**Total motif IC:**

$$IC_{\text{motif}} = 2.000 + 0.629 + 0.629 + 1.278 + 1.278 = 5.814 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** Positions 1, 4, 5 are highly conserved; positions 2, 3 tolerate more variation.

**Final score for Part (b):** 7 marks (1 PFM + 1 PPM + 1.5 PWM + 2.5 IC + 1 final sum/interpretation).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the deterministic pattern representations in bioinformatics. Discuss IUPAC ambiguity codes, consensus strings, and PROSITE-style regular expressions with at least one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **Profile Hidden Markov Models**? Explain the three-state architecture (Match, Insert, Delete) and discuss how they differ from PWMs in representing biological motifs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**Part (a) — Deterministic Patterns (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Deterministic patterns describe a motif as a single fixed string of characters, where each position is specified as an exact nucleotide/amino acid or an IUPAC ambiguity code; matches are all-or-nothing.

2. **IUPAC Codes (2 marks):** A standardized alphabet for degenerate positions. Example: `R` = A or G (puRine), `Y` = C or T (pYrimidine), `N` = any base. Full table as in Section 1.3.

   *Example:* TATA box consensus `TATAWA` (W = A or T) represents both `TATAAA` and `TATATA`.

3. **Consensus Strings (2 marks):** Built by column-wise majority vote from a multiple alignment. Example: from 5 aligned TATA-box variants, choose the most frequent base at each position.

4. **PROSITE Regular Expressions (2 marks):** Formal language patterns using `[ ]`, `{ }`, `x`, `( )` syntax.

   *Example:* N-glycosylation site pattern `N-{P}-[ST]-{P}` means:
   - Position 1: N (asparagine)
   - Position 2: any residue **except** P
   - Position 3: S or T
   - Position 4: any residue **except** P

   Search tool: **ScanProsite** (https://prosite.expasy.org/scanprosite/).

**Part (b) — Profile HMMs (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A Profile Hidden Markov Model is a probabilistic graphical model that represents a multiple sequence alignment as a stochastic finite-state machine. It captures position-specific conservation **and** insertion/deletion patterns using Markov-chain transition and emission probabilities.

2. **Three-state architecture per column (3 marks):**
   - **Match state (M):** emits a conserved residue according to position-specific emission probabilities.
   - **Insert state (I):** emits residues inserted relative to the consensus (random insertions).
   - **Delete state (D):** silent state representing a gap at this position in a particular sequence.

   Transitions $a_{kl}$ between M, I, D states are learned from the training alignment.

3. **Comparison with PWM (2 marks):**

   | Feature | PWM | Profile HMM |
   |:--------|:----|:------------|
   | Length | Fixed | Variable (handles indels) |
   | Gaps | Cannot model | Models via D states |
   | Insertions | Cannot model | Models via I states |
   | Training | Direct frequency counts | Baum-Welch or direct from alignment |
   | Search | Sliding window sum | Viterbi / Forward algorithm |
   | Tools | MATCH, JASPAR | HMMER, SAM, PSI-BLAST |

   **Conclusion:** Profile HMMs are more expressive and handle biologically realistic length variation, while PWMs are simpler and faster for fixed-length motifs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PFM and PWM:** Many students write PWM entries as raw counts (PFM) instead of log-likelihoods. PWM is in **log space** (bits), not probability space.
> 2. **Forgetting background probability $b_b$:** PWM requires division by background, not just the PPM. Using $b_b = 0.25$ uniformly is the most common assumption; state it explicitly.
> 3. **Information Content formula errors:** The maximum IC for DNA is **2 bits/position**, not 1 bit or $\log_2(4)$ written without the minus sign. Use $IC_j = 2 - H_j$ and remember the negative sign in $H$.
> 4. **Column vs. row confusion:** A PWM has $4 \times L$ (or $20 \times L$ for proteins) entries; rows = bases, columns = positions. Reversing this layout is a common mistake.
> 5. **Deterministic vs. probabilistic match semantics:** A PWM does *not* give a yes/no answer; it gives a *score*. State a threshold $T$ explicitly when discussing motif scanning.
> 6. **Pseudocounts:** When computing log-odds, $\log_2(0)$ is undefined. Always mention pseudocounts when one base has zero counts.
> 7. **Units:** PWM scores are in **bits** (log base 2) by KTU convention; stating "log" without base is incomplete.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Pattern = a recurring, biologically meaningful short sequence** (motif).
- **Two families:** Deterministic (fixed string) and Probabilistic (statistical matrix).
- **Deterministic subtypes:** exact string, IUPAC consensus, PROSITE regex, word list.
- **IUPAC codes:** R (A,G), Y (C,T), S (G,C), W (A,T), K (G,T), M (A,C), B (not A), D (not C), H (not G), V (not T), N (any).
- **PFM:** raw counts; column sum = $N$.
- **PPM:** PFM divided by $N$ (or $N + 4\alpha$ if pseudocounts used); each column sums to 1.
- **PWM/PSSM:** $\;S_{b,j} = \log_2(P_{b,j}/b_b)\;$ — log-likelihood score in **bits**.
- **Sequence score:** $\;\text{Score}(S) = \sum_{j=1}^{L} S_{s_j,j}\;$ — higher = stronger match.
- **Pseudocount $\alpha$:** added to avoid $\log(0)$; typical $\alpha = 0.5$.
- **Shannon entropy:** $\;H_j = -\sum_b P_{b,j} \log_2 P_{b,j}\;$ — 0 (perfect conservation) to 2 (random, for DNA).
- **Information Content:** $\;IC_j = 2 - H_j\;$ for DNA; $\;IC_{\text{motif}} = \sum_j IC_j\;$.
- **Sequence Logo:** visual plot of $IC_j$ with letter heights proportional to base frequency.
- **Profile HMM:** 3-state per position (Match, Insert, Delete); handles gaps and indels.
- **Tools:** MEME (motif discovery), JASPAR/TRANSFAC (PWM databases), Pfam/HMMER (Profile HMMs), ScanProsite (regex search).
- **Threshold:** PWM scoring requires a cutoff $T$ to convert scores into yes/no hits.
- **Background assumption:** equal base frequencies ($b=0.25$) unless stated otherwise; in real genomes $b_A \neq b_C \neq b_G \neq b_T$.
- **KTU high-frequency keywords:** PFM, PPM, PWM, PSSM, IUPAC, consensus, regex, profile HMM, information content, log-odds, pseudocounts, sequence logo, motif scanning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Pattern Discovery - HMM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Pattern Discovery - HMM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a doubly stochastic, finite-state probabilistic automaton in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a **Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states**, and where each hidden state emits an observable symbol according to a defined emission probability distribution. Formally, an HMM is defined by the **5-tuple λ = (S, V, A, B, π)**:

- **S** = A finite set of *N* hidden states, $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N\}$
- **V** = A finite set of *M* observable symbols (the alphabet), $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_M\}$
- **A** = The state transition probability matrix, $A = \{a_{ij}\}$, where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$
- **B** = The emission (observation) probability matrix, $B = \{b_j(k)\}$, where $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = s_j)$
- **π** = The initial state distribution, $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$, where $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** HMMs form the mathematical backbone of computational gene prediction, protein family modeling (Pfam/HMMER), and motif discovery. The syllabus explicitly stresses the three canonical HMM problems — *Evaluation, Decoding, and Learning* — and their algorithmic solutions (Forward, Viterbi, Baum-Welch).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Underground Weather Game"

Imagine a tourist in **Kerala** who lives in a soundproof underground bunker and is only told what the *outdoor weather* is each day (Sunny, Rainy, Cloudy). The tourist **cannot directly observe** the season (Monsoon, Post-Monsoon, Summer, Winter) — these are the **hidden states**. The tourist only sees the *weather* (Sunny/Rainy) — these are the **emitted observations**.

- The **hidden states** transition between each other with certain probabilities (the season rarely jumps directly from Monsoon to Summer — there is a Markov chain governing transitions).
- From each hidden state, a **weather symbol** is "emitted" with a particular probability (e.g., during Monsoon, Rainy is emitted 80% of the time).

The HMM **infers** the most likely sequence of hidden seasons (states) given only the observed weather sequence. In bioinformatics, the **observed sequence is DNA/Protein**, and the **hidden states are biological features** such as *exons, introns, promoters, CpG islands,* or *conserved residues in a protein family*.

## 1.3 The Markov Assumption and the Output Independence Assumption

For a sequence of length *T* with hidden path $Q = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_T)$ and observed sequence $O = (o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T)$:

$$P(q_t \mid q_{t-1}, q_{t-2}, \dots, q_1) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$$

$$P(o_t \mid q_1, q_2, \dots, q_T, o_1, \dots, o_{t-1}, o_{t+1}, \dots, o_T) = P(o_t \mid q_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first equation is the **first-order Markov property** (memory of one step). The second is the **output independence assumption** (emission depends only on the current state, not on history or future). These two assumptions are what make HMMs computationally tractable.

## 1.4 Why HMMs Matter in Bioinformatics

| Application | Hidden State | Observation | Classical Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gene Finding | Exon / Intron / Intergenic | Nucleotide (A,C,G,T) | GENSCAN, GeneMark |
| Protein Family Modeling | Match / Insert / Delete | Amino acid | HMMER / Pfam |
| Sequence Alignment | Match / Mismatch / Gap | Residue pair | Profile HMM |
| Transmembrane Topology | Helix / Loop / TM-region | Amino acid | TMHMM |
| Signal Peptide Prediction | Signal / Cleavage / Mature | Amino acid | SignalP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical HMM Problems (Rabiner's Framework)

Lawrence Rabiner's 1989 tutorial formalized three fundamental problems for any HMM. Every HMM application in bioinformatics maps to one or more of these:

### Problem 1 — **Evaluation (Scoring)**
*Given a model λ and an observation sequence O, compute* $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithmic Solution:** **Forward Algorithm** (dynamic programming) or **Backward Algorithm**.
- **Bioinformatics Use:** Decide whether a sequence belongs to a protein family (HMMER bit-score vs threshold).

### Problem 2 — **Decoding (Path Finding)**
*Given a model λ and observation O, find the optimal hidden state sequence Q\* that best "explains" O.*
- **Algorithmic Solution:** **Viterbi Algorithm** (dynamic programming, max instead of sum).
- **Bioinformatics Use:** Predict the gene structure (which positions are exons, which are introns).

### Problem 3 — **Learning (Parameter Estimation)**
*Given the topology and an observation sequence O, find the model parameters λ\* that maximize* $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithmic Solution:** **Baum-Welch Algorithm** (a special case of **Expectation-Maximization, EM**).
- **Bioinformatics Use:** Train Pfam profile HMMs from unaligned sequence databases.

## 2.2 The Forward Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

**Goal:** Compute $\alpha_t(i) = P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t, q_t = s_i \mid \lambda)$ — the probability of being in state $s_i$ at time $t$ after seeing the first *t* observations.

**Why dynamic programming?** Direct computation of $P(O \mid \lambda)$ over all $N^T$ paths is exponentially infeasible. The Forward algorithm recurses in $O(N^2 T)$ time.

### Logical Steps:
1. **Initialization (t=1):** For each state $s_i$, the joint probability of starting in $s_i$ AND emitting $o_1$ is $\pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1)$.
2. **Induction (t = 1 → T-1):** Each forward variable at time *t+1* is built by summing over all predecessor states at time *t* — this "fans in" the contributions.
3. **Termination:** The total probability of the observation sequence is the sum of the final forward variables over all states.

## 2.3 The Viterbi Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

**Goal:** Find $Q^* = \arg\max_{Q} P(Q, O \mid \lambda)$ — the single most likely path.

**Why different from Forward?** Forward uses $\sum$ (probabilities of all paths); Viterbi uses $\max$ (probability of the *best* path). The two algorithms are structurally identical except for the $\sum \rightarrow \max$ substitution and the addition of a **back-pointer matrix** to recover the optimal path.

## 2.4 The Baum-Welch (EM) Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

**Goal:** Iteratively re-estimate λ = (A, B, π) to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$.

**Why iterative?** No closed-form solution exists for arbitrary HMMs. EM alternates between:
- **E-step:** Compute expected counts of state occupancies and transitions given current λ.
- **M-step:** Re-estimate λ to maximize the expected likelihood.

**Convergence:** Monotonically non-decreasing, but converges only to a **local maximum** (EM is not globally optimal).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE FOR ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State transition | $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1}=s_j \mid q_t=s_i)$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1$ |
| 2 | Emission probability | $b_j(k) = P(o_t=v_k \mid q_t=s_j)$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{M} b_j(k) = 1$ |
| 3 | Initial distribution | $\pi_i = P(q_1=s_i)$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i = 1$ |
| 4 | Joint probability (Forward init) | $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1)$ | $1 \le i \le N$ |
| 5 | Forward induction | $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})$ | $1 \le t \le T-1$ |
| 6 | Forward termination | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$ | — |
| 7 | Backward init | $\beta_T(i) = 1$ | — |
| 8 | Backward induction | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | $t = T-1, \dots, 1$ |
| 9 | Viterbi init | $\delta_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1)$ | — |
| 10 | Viterbi recursion | $\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_i \left[\delta_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})$ | — |
| 11 | Viterbi termination | $P^* = \max_i \delta_T(i)$ | — |
| 12 | Posterior state prob. | $\gamma_t(i) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i)\beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | $\sum_i \gamma_t(i) = 1$ |
| 13 | Posterior transition prob. | $\xi_t(i,j) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i)\,a_{ij}\,b_j(o_{t+1})\,\beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | — |
| 14 | Baum-Welch re-est. of A | $\bar{a}_{ij} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | Row stochastic |
| 15 | Baum-Welch re-est. of B | $\bar{b}_j(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{t: o_t=v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$ | Column stochastic |
| 16 | Baum-Welch re-est. of π | $\bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i)$ | $\sum_i \bar{\pi}_i = 1$ |
| 17 | Log-space computation | $\log P(O \mid \lambda) = \log \sum_i \alpha_T(i)$ | Prevents underflow |

## 2.6 Profile HMMs — Architecture Used by Pfam/HMMER

A **profile HMM** for a protein family models the consensus length, conserved columns, and variable insertions/deletions using three types of states per position:

- **Match state (M):** Consensus column — likely to emit a conserved residue.
- **Insert state (I):** Position where an insertion relative to the consensus is observed.
- **Delete state (D):** Position where a column is skipped (gap in this sequence).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Profile HMMs are the foundation of the Pfam database (curated by EMBL-EBI), containing **20,000+ protein families**. Each family is represented by one profile HMM trained via Baum-Welch on seed alignments.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Life Sciences

- **Personalized Medicine:** HMMs predict pathogenic variants in patient genomes.
- **Phylogenetics:** HMM-based alignment is more sensitive than pairwise methods for divergent sequences.
- **Synthetic Biology:** HMMs guide the design of codon-optimized genes.
- **Biodefense & Forensics:** HMMER identifies pathogen sequences from metagenomic shotgun data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — The "Casino" Two-State HMM (Adapted from Durbin et al.)

This is the canonical tutorial example from the *Biological Sequence Analysis* textbook. We will compute **Forward, Backward, Viterbi, and Baum-Welch re-estimation** by hand on a 3-symbol observation.

### 3.1.1 Model Definition (λ)

Suppose we have a **dishonest casino** that uses a *fair coin* and a *loaded coin*:

- **Hidden states:** $S = \{F, L\}$ where $F$ = Fair, $L$ = Loaded ($N = 2$).
- **Observable alphabet:** $V = \{H, T\}$ (Heads, Tails) ($M = 2$).
- **Initial distribution:** $\pi = (0.5, 0.5)$ — equally likely to start with either coin.
- **Transition matrix A:**

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} a_{FF} & a_{FL} \\ a_{LF} & a_{LL} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 \end{pmatrix}$$

- **Emission matrix B:**

$$B = \begin{pmatrix} b_F(H) & b_F(T) \\ b_L(H) & b_L(T) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.9 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

- **Observation sequence:** $O = (H, T, H)$ — i.e., $o_1 = H, o_2 = T, o_3 = H$. ($T = 3$)

> **Goal:** Compute (a) $P(O \mid \lambda)$ using Forward, (b) the Viterbi path $Q^*$, (c) one Baum-Welch re-estimation step.

### 3.1.2 Derivation — Forward Algorithm Computation

**Step 1 — Initialization (t=1, observation $o_1 = H$):**

$$\alpha_1(F) = \pi_F \cdot b_F(H) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$$

$$\alpha_1(L) = \pi_L \cdot b_L(H) = 0.5 \times 0.9 = 0.45$$

**Step 2 — Induction to t=2 (observation $o_2 = T$):**

$$\alpha_2(F) = \left[\alpha_1(F)\,a_{FF} + \alpha_1(L)\,a_{LF}\right] \cdot b_F(T)$$

$$= \left[0.25 \times 0.9 + 0.45 \times 0.1\right] \times 0.5$$

$$= \left[0.225 + 0.045\right] \times 0.5 = 0.270 \times 0.5 = 0.135$$

$$\alpha_2(L) = \left[\alpha_1(F)\,a_{FL} + \alpha_1(L)\,a_{LL}\right] \cdot b_L(T)$$

$$= \left[0.25 \times 0.1 + 0.45 \times 0.9\right] \times 0.1$$

$$= \left[0.025 + 0.405\right] \times 0.1 = 0.430 \times 0.1 = 0.043$$

**Step 3 — Induction to t=3 (observation $o_3 = H$):**

$$\alpha_3(F) = \left[\alpha_2(F)\,a_{FF} + \alpha_2(L)\,a_{LF}\right] \cdot b_F(H)$$

$$= \left[0.135 \times 0.9 + 0.043 \times 0.1\right] \times 0.5$$

$$= \left[0.1215 + 0.0043\right] \times 0.5 = 0.1258 \times 0.5 = 0.0629$$

$$\alpha_3(L) = \left[\alpha_2(F)\,a_{FL} + \alpha_2(L)\,a_{LL}\right] \cdot b_L(H)$$

$$= \left[0.135 \times 0.1 + 0.043 \times 0.9\right] \times 0.9$$

$$= \left[0.0135 + 0.0387\right] \times 0.9 = 0.0522 \times 0.9 = 0.04698$$

**Step 4 — Termination (compute total probability $P(O \mid \lambda)$):**

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_3(F) + \alpha_3(L) = 0.0629 + 0.04698 = 0.10988$$

Thus, the probability of observing the sequence $HTH$ under this casino model is approximately **0.1099**.

### 3.1.3 Derivation — Viterbi Algorithm Computation

We repeat the same skeleton but use **max** instead of **sum**, and record the back-pointer.

**Step 1 — Initialization (t=1, $o_1 = H$):**

$$\delta_1(F) = \pi_F \cdot b_F(H) = 0.25 \quad \psi_1(F) = 0$$

$$\delta_1(L) = \pi_L \cdot b_L(H) = 0.45 \quad \psi_1(L) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion (t=2, $o_2 = T$):**

$$\delta_2(F) = \max\left[\delta_1(F)\,a_{FF},\; \delta_1(L)\,a_{LF}\right] \cdot b_F(T)$$

$$= \max\left[0.25 \times 0.9,\; 0.45 \times 0.1\right] \times 0.5$$

$$= \max\left[0.225,\; 0.045\right] \times 0.5 = 0.225 \times 0.5 = 0.1125$$

$$\psi_2(F) = F \quad \text{(since } F \text{ gave the max)}$$

$$\delta_2(L) = \max\left[\delta_1(F)\,a_{FL},\; \delta_1(L)\,a_{LL}\right] \cdot b_L(T)$$

$$= \max\left[0.25 \times 0.1,\; 0.45 \times 0.9\right] \times 0.1$$

$$= \max\left[0.025,\; 0.405\right] \times 0.1 = 0.405 \times 0.1 = 0.0405$$

$$\psi_2(L) = L$$

**Step 3 — Recursion (t=3, $o_3 = H$):**

$$\delta_3(F) = \max\left[0.1125 \times 0.9,\; 0.0405 \times 0.1\right] \times 0.5$$

$$= \max\left[0.10125,\; 0.00405\right] \times 0.5 = 0.10125 \times 0.5 = 0.050625$$

$$\psi_3(F) = F$$

$$\delta_3(L) = \max\left[0.1125 \times 0.1,\; 0.0405 \times 0.9\right] \times 0.9$$

$$= \max\left[0.01125,\; 0.03645\right] \times 0.9 = 0.03645 \times 0.9 = 0.032805$$

$$\psi_3(L) = L$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$P^* = \max\left[\delta_3(F),\, \delta_3(L)\right] = \max\left[0.050625,\; 0.032805\right] = 0.050625$$

The optimal state is $q_3^* = F$.

**Step 5 — Backtrace:**

$q_3^* = F \rightarrow \psi_3(F) = F \rightarrow q_2^* = F \rightarrow \psi_2(F) = F \rightarrow q_1^* = F$.

$$Q^* = (F, F, F) \quad \text{(the entire sequence was generated using the fair coin)}$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation — One Step of Baum-Welch Re-estimation

**Step 1 — Compute the Backward variables for t=2 and t=1.**

Backward initialization: $\beta_3(F) = 1$, $\beta_3(L) = 1$.

Backward induction for t=2:

$$\beta_2(F) = a_{FF}\,b_F(o_3)\,\beta_3(F) + a_{FL}\,b_L(o_3)\,\beta_3(L)$$

$$= 0.9 \times 0.5 \times 1 + 0.1 \times 0.9 \times 1 = 0.45 + 0.09 = 0.54$$

$$\beta_2(L) = a_{LF}\,b_F(o_3)\,\beta_3(F) + a_{LL}\,b_L(o_3)\,\beta_3(L)$$

$$= 0.1 \times 0.5 \times 1 + 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 1 = 0.05 + 0.81 = 0.86$$

Backward induction for t=1 (using $o_2 = T$):

$$\beta_1(F) = a_{FF}\,b_F(T)\,\beta_2(F) + a_{FL}\,b_L(T)\,\beta_2(L)$$

$$= 0.9 \times 0.5 \times 0.54 + 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.86 = 0.243 + 0.0086 = 0.2516$$

$$\beta_1(L) = a_{LF}\,b_F(T)\,\beta_2(F) + a_{LL}\,b_L(T)\,\beta_2(L)$$

$$= 0.1 \times 0.5 \times 0.54 + 0.9 \times 0.1 \times 0.86 = 0.027 + 0.0774 = 0.1044$$

**Sanity check:** $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_i \alpha_3(i)\beta_3(i) = 0.0629 \times 1 + 0.04698 \times 1 = 0.10988$ ✓ (matches Section 3.1.2).

**Step 2 — Compute γ (posterior state probabilities) at each time.**

At t=1:

$$\gamma_1(F) = \frac{\alpha_1(F)\,\beta_1(F)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.2516}{0.10988} = \frac{0.0629}{0.10988} \approx 0.5724$$

$$\gamma_1(L) = \frac{0.45 \times 0.1044}{0.10988} = \frac{0.04698}{0.10988} \approx 0.4276$$

At t=2:

$$\gamma_2(F) = \frac{0.135 \times 0.54}{0.10988} = \frac{0.0729}{0.10988} \approx 0.6635$$

$$\gamma_2(L) = \frac{0.043 \times 0.86}{0.10988} = \frac{0.03698}{0.10988} \approx 0.3365$$

At t=3:

$$\gamma_3(F) = \frac{0.0629 \times 1}{0.10988} \approx 0.5724$$

$$\gamma_3(L) = \frac{0.04698 \times 1}{0.10988} \approx 0.4276$$

**Step 3 — Compute ξ (posterior transition probabilities).**

For t=1, transition $F \to F$:

$$\xi_1(F,F) = \frac{\alpha_1(F)\,a_{FF}\,b_F(o_2)\,\beta_2(F)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.9 \times 0.5 \times 0.54}{0.10988}$$

$$= \frac{0.06075}{0.10988} \approx 0.5529$$

For t=1, transition $F \to L$:

$$\xi_1(F,L) = \frac{0.25 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.86}{0.10988} = \frac{0.00215}{0.10988} \approx 0.0196$$

For t=1, transition $L \to F$:

$$\xi_1(L,F) = \frac{0.45 \times 0.1 \times 0.5 \times 0.54}{0.10988} = \frac{0.0243}{0.10988} \approx 0.2212$$

For t=1, transition $L \to L$:

$$\xi_1(L,L) = \frac{0.45 \times 0.9 \times 0.1 \times 0.86}{0.10988} = \frac{0.06966}{0.10988} \approx 0.6339$$

(At t=2, the four $\xi_2(\cdot,\cdot)$ values are computed analogously using $o_3 = H$.)

**Step 4 — Re-estimate parameters.**

Numerator for $\bar{a}_{FF}$: $\xi_1(F,F) + \xi_2(F,F) = 0.5529 + 0.4\text{(approx)} = 0.9529$
Denominator for $\bar{a}_{FF}$: $\gamma_1(F) + \gamma_2(F) = 0.5724 + 0.6635 = 1.2359$

$$\bar{a}_{FF} = \frac{0.9529}{1.2359} \approx 0.7710$$

(This new value replaces the old $a_{FF} = 0.9$. The process iterates until convergence.)

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which HMM Algorithm

| Use Case | Required Problem | Algorithm | Complexity | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Score a sequence vs. model | Evaluation (1) | Forward | $O(N^2 T)$ | Log-likelihood |
| Find gene structure | Decoding (2) | Viterbi | $O(N^2 T)$ | Optimal path |
| Train model from unaligned seqs | Learning (3) | Baum-Welch | $O(N^2 T \cdot \text{iter})$ | New λ |
| Compute state posteriors | Posterior decoding | Forward + Backward | $O(N^2 T)$ | $\gamma_t(i)$ matrix |
| Posterior decoding (alt) | Soft decoding | Posterior decoding | $O(N^2 T)$ | Per-base prob. of exon/intron |

## 3.3 Profile HMM — Posterior Probability Formulation

For a profile HMM, the probability of an amino acid $a$ at match position $j$ is computed from emission scores and transition weights. The log-odds score used by HMMER is:

$$\text{Score} = \log_2 \frac{P(\text{seq} \mid \text{HMM})}{P(\text{seq} \mid \text{Random})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerator uses the model emission + transition probabilities; the denominator uses background amino acid frequencies (e.g., from Swiss-Prot). A positive score indicates the sequence is **more likely** under the family HMM than by chance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM Architecture — Three-State Left-to-Right Profile HMM

> The following Mermaid block diagrams a profile HMM of length $L = 3$ (Match/Insert/Delete states per position, plus Begin/End), as used by HMMER/Pfam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BEGIN([Begin]) --> M1
    BEGIN --> I0((I0))
    I0 --> M1
    M1 -- a_M1_M1 --> M1
    M1 -- a_M1_I1 --> I1((I1))
    M1 -- a_M1_D2 --> D2[/D2\]
    I1 -- a_I1_M2 --> M2
    I1 -- a_I1_I1 --> I1
    M2 -- a_M2_M2 --> M2
    M2 -- a_M2_I2 --> I2((I2))
    M2 -- a_M2_D3 --> D3[/D3\]
    I2 -- a_I2_M3 --> M3
    I2 -- a_I2_I2 --> I2
    M3 -- a_M3_M3 --> M3
    M3 -- a_M3_END --> END([End])
    D2 -- a_D2_M3 --> M3
    D3 -- a_D3_END --> END
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each box `Mk` emits a consensus residue; `Ik` is a position where insertions are emitted (at cost); `Dk` is a "silent" state that skips a column. The `a_ij` labels represent entries of the transition matrix A.

## 4.2 Forward Algorithm — Trellis Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph t1["t = 1  (init: pi_i b_i o1)"]
        F1["alpha_1 F = 0.25"]
        L1["alpha_1 L = 0.45"]
    end
    subgraph t2["t = 2  (induction)"]
        F2["alpha_2 F = 0.135"]
        L2["alpha_2 L = 0.043"]
    end
    subgraph t3["t = 3  (induction)"]
        F3["alpha_3 F = 0.0629"]
        L3["alpha_3 L = 0.04698"]
    end
    F1 -- a_FF bF o2 --> F2
    L1 -- a_LF bF o2 --> F2
    F1 -- a_FL bL o2 --> L2
    L1 -- a_LL bL o2 --> L2
    F2 -- a_FF bF o3 --> F3
    L2 -- a_LF bF o3 --> F3
    F2 -- a_FL bL o3 --> L3
    L2 -- a_LL bL o3 --> L3
    F3 --> SUM[/"P O lambda = 0.10988"/]
    L3 --> SUM
```

## 4.3 Three Canonical HMM Problems — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Given Model lambda and Observation O"] --> B1["Evaluation Problem"]
    A --> B2["Decoding Problem"]
    A --> B3["Learning Problem"]
    B1 --> C1["Forward Algorithm"]
    B1 --> C2["Backward Algorithm"]
    C1 --> D1["Output P O lambda"]
    C2 --> D1
    B2 --> E1["Viterbi Algorithm"]
    E1 --> D2["Output Best Path Q star"]
    B3 --> F1["Baum-Welch EM"]
    F1 --> D3["Output Updated lambda bar"]
    D1 --> G["Bioinformatics Apps"]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    G --> G1["HMMER Pfam"]
    G --> G2["GENSCAN Gene Finding"]
    G --> G3["TMHMM Topology"]
```

## 4.4 Viterbi Trellis with Backpointers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph col1["t = 1"]
        F1n["F delta = 0.25"]
        L1n["L delta = 0.45"]
    end
    subgraph col2["t = 2"]
        F2n["F delta = 0.1125"]
        L2n["L delta = 0.0405"]
    end
    subgraph col3["t = 3"]
        F3n["F delta = 0.050625"]
        L3n["L delta = 0.032805"]
    end
    F1n -- 0.225 max --> F2n
    L1n -- 0.045 discard --> F2n
    F1n -- 0.025 discard --> L2n
    L1n -- 0.405 max --> L2n
    F2n -- 0.10125 max --> F3n
    L2n -- 0.00405 discard --> F3n
    F2n -- 0.01125 discard --> L3n
    L2n -- 0.03645 max --> L3n
    F3n -- backtrace F F F --> ENDN([Q star = F F F])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(3 Marks)** *Define a Hidden Markov Model. List the five elements of an HMM and state the Markov property used in its construction.*

**Model Answer:**

A Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is a probabilistic model for sequences in which the system is assumed to follow a Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states, and each hidden state emits an observable symbol.

**Five elements (λ = {S, V, A, B, π}):**
- **S** — Set of N hidden states
- **V** — Set of M observable symbols (alphabet)
- **A** — State transition probability matrix
- **B** — Emission probability matrix
- **π** — Initial state distribution

**First-order Markov property:** $P(q_t \mid q_{t-1}, q_{t-2}, \dots, q_1) = P(q_t \mid q_{t-1})$ — the next state depends only on the current state, not on the full history.

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(3 Marks)** *Differentiate between the Forward algorithm and the Viterbi algorithm in HMMs.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Algorithm | Viterbi Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Evaluation: compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Decoding: find the best state path $Q^*$ |
| **Aggregation** | Uses $\sum$ over all predecessor states | Uses $\max$ over all predecessor states |
| **Output** | Scalar probability | Optimal state sequence + its probability |
| **Backtrace** | None (no pointer matrix) | Yes — uses back-pointers $\psi$ to recover $Q^*$ |
| **Complexity** | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N^2 T)$ |

Both share the same trellis structure; the only differences are the $\sum \rightarrow \max$ substitution and the back-pointer bookkeeping.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs as formulated by Rabiner. For each problem, name the algorithm used to solve it and a bioinformatics application that uses that algorithm.*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider a 2-state HMM with $S = \{s_1, s_2\}$, $V = \{A, B\}$, initial distribution $\pi = (0.6, 0.4)$, transition matrix* 

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$$

*and emission matrix* 

$$B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$

*For the observation sequence $O = (A, B, A)$, compute the total probability $P(O \mid \lambda)$ using the Forward algorithm.*

---

### **Model Solution — Part B Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Rabiner's Three HMM Problems**

| # | Problem | Statement | Algorithm | Bioinformatics Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Evaluation** | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ for given model and observation. | **Forward / Backward** | Scoring a candidate sequence against a Pfam HMM (HMMER bit-score). |
| 2 | **Decoding** | Find optimal hidden state sequence $Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$. | **Viterbi** | Gene structure prediction in GENSCAN (exon/intron labeling). |
| 3 | **Learning** | Find $\lambda^* = \arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$ from observations alone. | **Baum-Welch (EM)** | Training a profile HMM from unaligned seed sequences for a new Pfam family. |

**Note on importance:** All three are interlinked — evaluation (1) is the subroutine used by learning (3), and decoding (2) requires the trellis structure of (1) with a $\max$ operator.

#### **Part (b) — Forward Algorithm on O = (A, B, A)**

**Step 1 — Initialization (t = 1, $o_1 = A$):**

$$\alpha_1(s_1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(A) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$$

$$\alpha_1(s_2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(A) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$$

**Step 2 — Induction to t = 2 (observation $o_2 = B$):**

$$\alpha_2(s_1) = \left[\alpha_1(s_1)\,a_{11} + \alpha_1(s_2)\,a_{21}\right] \cdot b_1(B)$$

$$= \left[0.54 \times 0.7 + 0.08 \times 0.4\right] \times 0.1 = \left[0.378 + 0.032\right] \times 0.1 = 0.041$$

$$\alpha_2(s_2) = \left[\alpha_1(s_1)\,a_{12} + \alpha_1(s_2)\,a_{22}\right] \cdot b_2(B)$$

$$= \left[0.54 \times 0.3 + 0.08 \times 0.6\right] \times 0.8 = \left[0.162 + 0.048\right] \times 0.8 = 0.168$$

**Step 3 — Induction to t = 3 (observation $o_3 = A$):**

$$\alpha_3(s_1) = \left[\alpha_2(s_1)\,a_{11} + \alpha_2(s_2)\,a_{21}\right] \cdot b_1(A)$$

$$= \left[0.041 \times 0.7 + 0.168 \times 0.4\right] \times 0.9 = \left[0.0287 + 0.0672\right] \times 0.9 = 0.08631$$

$$\alpha_3(s_2) = \left[\alpha_2(s_1)\,a_{12} + \alpha_2(s_2)\,a_{22}\right] \cdot b_2(A)$$

$$= \left[0.041 \times 0.3 + 0.168 \times 0.6\right] \times 0.2 = \left[0.0123 + 0.1008\right] \times 0.2 = 0.02262$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_3(s_1) + \alpha_3(s_2) = 0.08631 + 0.02262 = 0.10893$$

$$\boxed{P(O \mid \lambda) \approx 0.1089}$$

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the architecture of a profile Hidden Markov Model. Explain the roles of Match, Insert, and Delete states with the help of a diagram.*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** *For the profile HMM in part (a) of length $L = 2$, let* 

- $A = \begin{pmatrix} a_{MM} & a_{MI} & a_{MD} \\ a_{IM} & a_{II} & a_{IE} \\ a_{DM} & a_{DI} & a_{DE} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 & 0.1 & 0.1 \\ 0.5 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.7 & 0.1 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$

- *Emissions: $b_M(A) = 0.6, b_M(C) = 0.2, b_M(G) = 0.1, b_M(T) = 0.1$* 
- *$b_I(A) = b_I(C) = b_I(G) = b_I(T) = 0.25$* 
- *Initial: $\pi_M = 1.0, \pi_I = 0, \pi_D = 0$.* 
- *For $O = (A, C)$, compute the Viterbi path and its probability.*

---

### **Model Solution — Part B Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Profile HMM Architecture**

A **profile HMM** models a multiple sequence alignment (MSA) of a protein family. The architecture is a **left-to-right** model with three parallel state types per consensus column:

- **Match states ($M_k$):** Emit a residue corresponding to the *k*-th consensus column. Emits conserved positions with high probability.
- **Insert states ($I_k$):** Emit residues that are *insertions* relative to the consensus (i.e., residues appearing in some sequences but not in the consensus column). They act as a "loop" for variable regions.
- **Delete states ($D_k$):** Silent (do not emit any residue) — they represent a column *skipped* in the current sequence, modeling a gap relative to the consensus.

**Topological rules:**
- From $M_k$, one can self-loop to $M_{k+1}$, branch to $I_k$, or skip to $D_{k+1}$.
- From $I_k$, one can return to $M_{k+1}$ or self-loop.
- $D_k$ is silent — it transitions to $M_{k+1}$ or $D_{k+1}$ without emission.
- Special **Begin** (no emission, $\pi = 1.0$ for $M_1$) and **End** (terminal) states handle sequence entry/exit.

**Diagram (text representation):**
```
Begin → M1 → M2 → ... → ML → End
        ↑↓    ↑↓
        I0    I1
        ↑↓    ↑↓           (Insert states self-loop; can insert multiple residues)
        D1    D2           (Delete states are silent and skip a column)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In HMMER, the probability of *not* taking an insert or delete from a match is bundled into $a_{MM}$. The architecture above is the basis of all Pfam family models.

#### **Part (b) — Viterbi on Profile HMM (O = AC, L=2)**

**Step 1 — Initialization (t = 1, $o_1 = A$):**

Allowed states at t=1: $M_1, I_0$ (Delete $D_1$ emits nothing, so it cannot be the "current emitting state" at t=1 in this model — though transitions can pass through it; for Viterbi we track it via its position in the trellis).

For emitting states at t=1:

$$\delta_1(M_1) = \pi_{M_1} \cdot b_{M_1}(A) = 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.6, \quad \psi_1(M_1) = \text{Begin}$$

$$\delta_1(I_0) = \pi_{I_0} \cdot b_{I_0}(A) = 0 \times 0.25 = 0, \quad \psi_1(I_0) = \text{Begin}$$

(If $\delta_1(I_0) = 0$, the path through $I_0$ is killed; we keep it for completeness.)

$$\delta_1(D_1) = 0 \text{ (silent; does not emit at t=1; entry handled via $\pi_{D_1} = 0$)}.$$

**Step 2 — Recursion to t = 2 (observation $o_2 = C$):**

For $M_2$ (allowed predecessors: $M_1, I_1, D_1$):

$$\delta_2(M_2) = \max\left[\delta_1(M_1)\,a_{MM},\; \delta_1(I_0)\,a_{IM},\; \delta_1(D_1)\,a_{DM}\right] \cdot b_{M_2}(C)$$

$$= \max\left[0.6 \times 0.8,\; 0 \times 0.5,\; 0 \times 0.7\right] \times 0.2 = \max\left[0.48,\; 0,\; 0\right] \times 0.2 = 0.48 \times 0.2 = 0.096$$

$$\psi_2(M_2) = M_1$$

For $I_1$ (allowed predecessors: $M_1, I_0, I_1$ — and $I_1$ can self-loop):

$$\delta_2(I_1) = \max\left[\delta_1(M_1)\,a_{MI},\; \delta_1(I_0)\,a_{II},\; \delta_1(I_1)\,a_{II}\right] \cdot b_{I_1}(C)$$

$$= \max\left[0.6 \times 0.1,\; 0 \times 0.4,\; \text{(no prior } I_1 \text{ emission)}\right] \times 0.25 = 0.06 \times 0.25 = 0.015$$

$$\psi_2(I_1) = M_1$$

**Step 3 — Termination & Backtrace:**

$$P^* = \max\left[\delta_2(M_2),\, \delta_2(I_1)\right] = \max\left[0.096,\, 0.015\right] = 0.096$$

The optimal final state is $q_2^* = M_2$. Backtracing:
- $\psi_2(M_2) = M_1 \Rightarrow q_1^* = M_1$.
- $\psi_1(M_1) = \text{Begin}$.

$$\boxed{Q^* = (\text{Begin} \to M_1 \to M_2), \quad P^* = 0.096}$$

**Interpretation:** The observation $A, C$ is most likely generated by **two match states** with no insertions or deletions — a clean alignment against the consensus.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Forward and Viterbi:** Forward uses $\sum$ (probability mass over *all* paths); Viterbi uses $\max$ (the single *best* path). Marks deducted if you write $\sum$ in Viterbi or $\max$ in Forward.
> 2. **Forgetting the emission factor:** The recursion is $[\;] \times b_j(o_{t+1})$, not just the sum/max term. This is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not initializing properly:** Forgetting to set $\beta_T(i) = 1$ in the Backward algorithm loses full marks on termination.
> 4. **Baum-Welch confusion:** Students often write $P(O \mid \lambda)$ is maximized globally — it is NOT; EM guarantees only a **local** maximum. Examiners love this trap.
> 5. **Profile HMM topology:** Drawing 3 parallel states is not enough — you must explain the transition structure (M→M, M→I, M→D, I→I, I→M, D→M, D→D). Half-marks for incomplete diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **HMM = 5-tuple (S, V, A, B, π).** Memorize: N states, M symbols, A is row-stochastic, B is column-stochastic, π sums to 1.
- [ ] **Markov Assumption:** First-order — next state depends only on the current state.
- [ ] **Output Independence:** Emission at position *t* depends only on the hidden state at position *t*.
- [ ] **Three problems:** Evaluation (Forward), Decoding (Viterbi), Learning (Baum-Welch/EM).
- [ ] **Forward Algorithm:** $O(N^2 T)$, uses $\sum$, computes $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- [ ] **Viterbi Algorithm:** $O(N^2 T)$, uses $\max$ + back-pointers $\psi$, returns $Q^*$.
- [ ] **Backward Algorithm:** $\beta_T(i) = 1$, $\beta_t(i) = \sum_j a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$.
- [ ] **Baum-Welch Re-estimation:** Uses $\gamma_t(i)$ (state posteriors) and $\xi_t(i,j)$ (transition posteriors); EM is iterative, locally optimal.
- [ ] **Numerical underflow:** Real HMMs use log-space arithmetic.
- [ ] **Profile HMM:** Match/Insert/Delete states per column; used by HMMER/Pfam.
- [ ] **Bioinformatics Apps:** Gene finding (GENSCAN, GeneMark), protein family modeling (Pfam), transmembrane topology (TMHMM), signal peptide prediction (SignalP).
- [ ] **Rabiner, 1989** — classical reference for HMM tutorial (mention in viva if asked).
- [ ] **Durbin et al., "Biological Sequence Analysis"** — standard text for HMMs in computational biology (mention if needed for context).
- [ ] **Caveats:** EM converges to local maxima; HMMs cannot model long-range dependencies; pseudocounts are added to avoid zero probabilities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gene Discovery Using GeneMark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gene Discovery Using GeneMark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

**GeneMark** is a family of ab initio statistical gene-finding algorithms developed by **Mark Borodovsky** and colleagues (Georgia Institute of Technology, USA) that uses **probabilistic Markov chain models** to identify protein-coding genes (Open Reading Frames / ORFs) in genomic DNA sequences. It is one of the foundational tools in computational gene prediction and is officially listed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus under Module 3: Gene Discovery.

The GeneMark family includes several variants:
- **GeneMark** – for prokaryotic gene prediction
- **GeneMark.hmm** – for eukaryotic gene prediction using Hidden Markov Models
- **GeneMarkS** – a self-training version that automatically derives model parameters without a pre-annotated training set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "GeneMark uses Markov chain models of nucleotide sequences to predict the probability that any given region of DNA codes for a protein. It computes the likelihood ratio between coding and non-coding models to classify ORFs."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a long book written in a coded language. Some sentences are *meaningful stories* (genes → proteins), while others are *gibberish filler* (non-coding DNA). A linguist trained on this language knows that in meaningful sentences:
- Certain words (codons like ATG = Methionine) appear more often at the start.
- Word lengths follow patterns (3 letters per codon, stop codons like TAA, TAG, TGA appear periodically).

GeneMark is like that **statistical linguist**. It does not understand biology directly — instead, it has learned:
1. The statistical "word frequency" patterns of coding DNA.
2. The statistical "word frequency" patterns of non-coding DNA.

It then slides across any new DNA text and asks, *"Is this stretch more likely to be a story (gene) or filler (non-coding)?"* — answering this using **Markov chain probability mathematics**.

### Core Components of GeneMark

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of GeneMark's Approach:**
> 1. **Markov Chain Models** – Capture statistical dependencies between consecutive nucleotides (or between consecutive codons).
> 2. **Likelihood Ratio Test** – Compares the probability of a sequence being generated by the *coding model* vs. the *non-coding model*.
> 3. **State Transitions** – GeneMark.hmm adds explicit "states" (start codon, exon, intron, stop codon) governed by transition probabilities.

### Why GeneMark is Important in Bioinformatics

| Application Area | Utility |
|---|---|
| Genome Annotation | First-pass identification of all genes in newly sequenced genomes |
| Microbial Genomics | Rapid ORF calling for prokaryotes (bacteria, archaea) |
| Metagenomics | Gene prediction directly from environmental DNA samples |
| Eukaryotic Annotation | Predicts exons, introns, UTRs (GeneMark.hmm / GeneMark-ES) |
| Comparative Genomics | Provides reference gene sets for downstream comparative studies |

### Key Constant / Standard Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayes' Decision Rule (used by GeneMark):**  
> A sequence is classified as *coding* if and only if: **P(sequence | coding model) > P(sequence | non-coding model)**, equivalently when the **log-odds ratio (L)** exceeds **0**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Markov Chain Foundation

A **Markov chain** of order *m* assumes that the probability of seeing a particular nucleotide at position *i* depends only on the *m* nucleotides immediately preceding it.

For **order-0**: every nucleotide is independent.
For **order-1**: P(X_i | X_{i-1}) — depends on the previous base.
For **order-2**: P(X_i | X_{i-1}, X_{i-2}) — depends on two previous bases.
For **order-5**: codon-level model (5th-order), commonly used in GeneMark for prokaryotes.

The **likelihood** of a DNA sequence $S = s_1 s_2 s_3 \dots s_n$ under a Markov model of order *m* is:

$$
P(S \mid M) = P(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m) \cdot \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} P(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})
$$

### 2.2 The GeneMark Decision Rule

GeneMark computes a **log-likelihood ratio** between two competing models:

$$
L(S) = \log \frac{P(S \mid M_{coding})}{P(S \mid M_{noncoding})}
$$

Classification rule:
- If **L(S) > 0** → classify as **coding (gene)**
- If **L(S) ≤ 0** → classify as **non-coding**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This threshold of **0** corresponds to a Bayesian decision boundary assuming **equal priors** and **equal misclassification costs** — a standard assumption in ab initio gene prediction.

### 2.3 GeneMark.hmm — The Hidden Markov Model Layer

For eukaryotic gene prediction (where genes are split by introns), GeneMark extends to a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)**:

- **Hidden States**: Start, Initial-Exon, Internal-Exon, Terminal-Exon, Single-Exon, Intron, Intergenic
- **Observations**: The DNA nucleotides (A, C, G, T)
- **Transition Probabilities (a_ij)**: Probability of moving from state *i* to state *j* (e.g., Intron → Exon boundary obeys GT-AG rule).
- **Emission Probabilities (b_ik)**: Probability of emitting nucleotide *k* while in state *i*.

The Viterbi algorithm is used to find the globally optimal path of hidden states.

### 2.4 GeneMarkS — Self-Training Innovation

GeneMarkS solves a critical chicken-and-egg problem: *you need annotated genes to train a model, but you need a model to find genes for annotation.*

**Iterative Self-Training Procedure:**
1. Start with heuristics (e.g., all ORFs > 300 bp).
2. Train an initial Markov model on these putative coding regions.
3. Re-scan the genome; keep only ORFs whose likelihood ratio exceeds a high threshold.
4. Re-train the model on the refined set.
5. Iterate until convergence.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Formula / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $M_{cod}$ | Coding region Markov model | Order-5 (pentamer) typical for prokaryotes |
| $M_{non}$ | Non-coding region Markov model | Order-5 typical |
| $a_{ij}$ | HMM transition probability | $0 \le a_{ij} \le 1$, $\sum_j a_{ij} = 1$ |
| $b_{ik}$ | HMM emission probability | $P(\text{emit } k \mid \text{state } i)$ |
| $L(S)$ | Log-likelihood ratio | $\log \frac{P(S \vert M_{cod})}{P(S \vert M_{non})}$ |
| $\pi_i$ | Initial state probability | $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ |
| Viterbi DP | $\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1 \dots q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \dots q_t = i, o_1 \dots o_t)$ | $\delta_t(j) = \max_i [\delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ |
| GC content | Bias indicator | Strongly correlated with codon usage in prokaryotes |
| Stop codons | TAA, TAG, TGA | Used as gene terminators |
| Start codon | ATG (most), GTG, TTG (prokaryotic alternatives) | Translation initiation signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** GeneMark's HMM-based variants are now embedded in major annotation pipelines like **PGAP** (NCBI Prokaryotic Genome Annotation Pipeline) and **MAKER**, making it one of the most widely deployed gene finders in genomics labs worldwide.

### 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Significance

In **synthetic biology**, GeneMark-derived models help design codon-optimized genes. In **clinical genomics**, accurate gene prediction from pathogen genomes (e.g., SARS-CoV-2) directly impacts vaccine and diagnostic design. The Markov chain mathematics is identical to techniques used in **speech recognition** and **signal processing**, showing the cross-disciplinary nature of bioinformatics engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Log-Likelihood Ratio for a Sequence

We want to derive the formula GeneMark uses to score a candidate ORF.

**Step 1 — Probability under the Coding Model (order-m Markov chain)**

Let $S = s_1 s_2 \dots s_n$ be a DNA subsequence. Under a coding Markov model $M_{cod}$ of order $m$:

$$
P(S \mid M_{cod}) = P(s_1, \dots, s_m) \cdot \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})
$$

**Explanation:** The first *m* nucleotides establish the initial context. Each subsequent nucleotide's probability depends only on the preceding *m* bases — this is the Markov property.

**Step 2 — Probability under the Non-Coding Model**

Similarly, under $M_{non}$:

$$
P(S \mid M_{non}) = P(s_1, \dots, s_m) \cdot \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} P_{non}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Likelihood Ratio**

$$
\Lambda(S) = \frac{P(S \mid M_{cod})}{P(S \mid M_{non})}
$$

Assuming the initial $m$-nucleotide probabilities are approximately equal in both models (a standard simplification in GeneMark for long ORFs), they cancel out:

$$
\Lambda(S) \approx \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} \frac{P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}{P_{non}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Logarithm (Monotonic Transformation)**

Taking the natural log preserves ordering and converts products into sums (numerically stable):

$$
L(S) = \log \Lambda(S) = \sum_{i=m+1}^{n} \log \frac{P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}{P_{non}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification Threshold**

The Bayesian optimal decision boundary (equal priors, equal cost) is:

$$
L(S) > 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \Lambda(S) > 1 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \text{Sequence is coding}
$$

$$
L(S) \le 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \Lambda(S) \le 1 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \text{Sequence is non-coding}
$$

**Final Form (boxed KTU exam-ready equation):**

$$
\boxed{L(S) = \sum_{i=m+1}^{n} \log \frac{P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}{P_{non}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Markov Order 1, m=1)

Suppose we have the short sequence $S = \text{ATGAAATAA}$ (length 9). We use a first-order Markov chain (m = 1).

**Given training-derived probabilities (hypothetical):**

For the **coding model**:

$$
P_{cod}(T \mid A) = 0.30, \quad P_{cod}(G \mid T) = 0.40, \quad P_{cod}(A \mid G) = 0.35, \quad P_{cod}(A \mid A) = 0.45
$$

For the **non-coding model**:

$$
P_{non}(T \mid A) = 0.25, \quad P_{non}(G \mid T) = 0.25, \quad P_{non}(A \mid G) = 0.25, \quad P_{non}(A \mid A) = 0.25
$$

**Step-by-step log-likelihood ratio computation:**

| Position $i$ | Context $s_{i-1}$ | Nucleotide $s_i$ | $\log \frac{P_{cod}}{P_{non}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | A | T | $\log(0.30/0.25) = \log 1.20 = 0.182$ |
| 3 | T | G | $\log(0.40/0.25) = \log 1.60 = 0.470$ |
| 4 | G | A | $\log(0.35/0.25) = \log 1.40 = 0.336$ |
| 5 | A | A | $\log(0.45/0.25) = \log 1.80 = 0.588$ |
| 6 | A | A | $\log(0.45/0.25) = \log 1.80 = 0.588$ |
| 7 | A | A | $\log(0.45/0.25) = \log 1.80 = 0.588$ |
| 8 | A | A | $\log(0.45/0.25) = \log 1.80 = 0.588$ |
| 9 | A | T | $\log(0.30/0.25) = \log 1.20 = 0.182$ |

**Sum:**

$$
L(S) = 0.182 + 0.470 + 0.336 + 0.588 + 0.588 + 0.588 + 0.588 + 0.182
$$

$$
L(S) = 3.522
$$

Since $L(S) = 3.522 > 0$, **GeneMark classifies this ORF as coding**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Viterbi Recurrence for GeneMark.hmm

Given an HMM with states $Q = \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$, observations $O = o_1 o_2 \dots o_T$, we define:

**Initialization:**

$$
\delta_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1)
$$

**Recursion (for $t = 2, 3, \dots, T$):**

$$
\delta_t(j) = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

$$
\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right]
$$

**Termination:**

$$
P^* = \max_{1 \le i \le N} \delta_T(i)
$$

**Path Backtracking (for $t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 1$):**

$$
q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Viterbi instead of Forward-Backward?** GeneMark.hmm uses Viterbi to produce a *single best parse* of the sequence into gene structure (exons/introns), which is what genome annotators need — one definitive gene structure per locus.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GeneMark Algorithm Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Genomic DNA Sequence] --> B[Scan for ORFs and Long Regions]
    B --> C{Is Sequence Eukaryotic?}
    C -- No --> D[Apply GeneMark / GeneMarkS]
    C -- Yes --> E[Apply GeneMark.hmm]
    D --> F[Train Markov Model M_cod on Coding Regions]
    F --> G[Train Markov Model M_non on Non-Coding Regions]
    G --> H[Compute Log-Likelihood Ratio L S]
    H --> I{L S greater than 0}
    I -- Yes --> J[Classify as Gene ORF]
    I -- No --> K[Classify as Non-Coding]
    E --> L[Build HMM with States: Start, Exon, Intron, Stop]
    L --> M[Run Viterbi Algorithm]
    M --> N[Output Optimal Gene Structure with Exons]
    J --> O[Annotated Genome]
    K --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 GeneMarkS Self-Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Unannotated Genome] --> B[Identify Heuristic ORFs length greater than 300 bp]
    B --> C[Train Initial Markov Model M0]
    C --> D[Rescan Genome and Score All ORFs]
    D --> E{High Likelihood Ratio ORFs above Threshold}
    E -- Yes --> F[Use as New Training Set]
    F --> G[Retrain Model M1]
    G --> H{Convergence Check}
    H -- Not Converged --> D
    H -- Converged --> I[Final GeneMarkS Model]
    E -- No --> I
    I --> J[Predict All Genes in Genome]
```

### 4.3 Hidden Markov Model State Diagram for Eukaryotic Genes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0((Intergenic)) -->|start codon| S1((Initial Exon))
    S1 -->|splice donor GT| S2((Internal Exon))
    S2 -->|splice acceptor AG| S3((Internal Exon 2))
    S3 -->|stop codon| S4((Terminal Exon))
    S4 -->|after stop| S0
    S1 -->|stop codon| S0
    S0 -->|ATG only, no intron| S5((Single Exon Gene))
    S5 -->|stop codon| S0
```

### 4.4 Likelihood Ratio Decision Boundary (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNA Window of Length W] --> B[Compute P coding Model]
    A --> C[Compute P noncoding Model]
    B --> D[Numerator]
    C --> E[Denominator]
    D --> F[Log Likelihood Ratio L]
    E --> F
    F --> G{L greater than 0}
    G -- Yes --> H[GENE Coding]
    G -- No --> I[NON-CODING Intergenic]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define GeneMark and state the type of statistical model it uses for gene prediction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

GeneMark is an ab initio gene-finding algorithm developed by Mark Borodovsky (Georgia Tech, USA) that predicts protein-coding genes in genomic DNA sequences. It uses **probabilistic Markov chain models** (and Hidden Markov Models in GeneMark.hmm) to compute the likelihood that a given DNA region codes for a protein. The classifier compares a *coding model* against a *non-coding model* using a **log-likelihood ratio**, classifying the region as coding when the ratio exceeds zero. Variants include GeneMark (prokaryotes), GeneMark.hmm (eukaryotes), and GeneMarkS (self-training).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between GeneMark and GeneMarkS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | GeneMark | GeneMarkS |
|---|---|---|
| Training requirement | Requires pre-annotated training set | Self-training — no annotated set needed |
| Suitable for | Organisms with known genes | Completely novel genomes |
| Approach | Supervised Markov model fitting | Iterative unsupervised ORF training |
| Mathematical basis | Order-5 Markov chain | Iterative order-5 Markov chain refinement |
| Input | Sequence + annotated training genes | Sequence only |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the mathematical framework of GeneMark, including the role of Markov chains of order *m* in modeling coding and non-coding regions. Derive the log-likelihood ratio formula used to classify an ORF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

GeneMark represents any DNA sequence as the output of a probabilistic process. For modeling, it employs a **Markov chain of order *m***, where the probability of observing a nucleotide at any position depends only on the *m* preceding nucleotides.

**Step 1 — Coding Model Construction**

A reference set of confirmed protein-coding sequences is used to estimate transition probabilities:

$$
P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1}) = \frac{\text{Count of context } (s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1}) \text{ followed by } s_i}{\text{Count of context } (s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}
$$

For prokaryotes, **m = 5** is standard (capturing codon-periodic bias).

**Step 2 — Non-Coding Model Construction**

The same procedure is applied to a set of confirmed intergenic regions, yielding $P_{non}$.

**Step 3 — Sequence Likelihood Computation**

For a candidate ORF of length $n$:

$$
P(S \mid M_{cod}) = P(s_1, \dots, s_m) \cdot \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})
$$

$$
P(S \mid M_{non}) = P(s_1, \dots, s_m) \cdot \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} P_{non}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})
$$

**Step 4 — Log-Likelihood Ratio**

The initial $m$-mer probabilities cancel (assuming equal priors for long ORFs), and taking $\log$ yields:

$$
L(S) = \sum_{i=m+1}^{n} \log \frac{P_{cod}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}{P_{non}(s_i \mid s_{i-m}, \dots, s_{i-1})}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification**

If $L(S) > 0$, the ORF is classified as a coding gene; otherwise, as non-coding.

This framework allows GeneMark to capture the **periodic triplet structure** of codons and the **statistical asymmetry** between coding and non-coding DNA — features not visible to simple ORF-length heuristics.

**(b)** Describe the architecture of GeneMark.hmm for eukaryotic gene prediction. How does the Viterbi algorithm produce the final gene structure? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Eukaryotic genes are discontinuous, containing exons (coding) interrupted by introns (non-coding). Modeling this requires tracking which *state* the HMM is in, since the same nucleotide can belong to either an exon or an intron.

**Architecture of GeneMark.hmm:**

The HMM has the following states:
- **Intergenic (N)** — between genes
- **Initial Exon (E)** — from start codon to first splice donor
- **Internal Exon (I)** — between splice sites
- **Terminal Exon (T)** — from last splice acceptor to stop codon
- **Single-Exon (S)** — intronless gene
- **Intron (R)** — between splice sites

**Three Probability Components:**

1. **Initial state probability $\pi_i$** — fixed (e.g., $\pi_{N} = 1$ at the start of a sequence).
2. **Transition probabilities $a_{ij}$** — encode biological rules, e.g., high probability of transition from Exon → Intron only at a *GT* splice donor site; Intron → Exon only at an *AG* splice acceptor.
3. **Emission probabilities $b_{ik}$** — for each state *i*, the probability of emitting nucleotide *k*. Coding states use a coding Markov model; intronic and intergenic states use a non-coding model.

**Viterbi Algorithm:**

The Viterbi dynamic programming algorithm finds the **single most probable sequence of hidden states** consistent with the observed DNA:

**Initialization:**

$$
\delta_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1)
$$

**Recursion:**

$$
\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

**Path Backtracking:**

$$
q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)
$$

The recovered state path $q_1^* q_2^* \dots q_T^*$ is interpreted as the gene structure (which positions are exons, which are introns). This produces a complete gene annotation with precise exon boundaries — the definitive output required for downstream genome annotation pipelines.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the self-training algorithm used by GeneMarkS. Why is self-training essential for newly sequenced genomes? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Self-Training Problem:**

Most gene-finding tools (including the original GeneMark) are *supervised* — they require an already-annotated training set of verified genes. However, when a new genome is sequenced (e.g., a novel bacterial isolate), no such annotations exist. GeneMarkS solves this bootstrap problem.

**Self-Training Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Heuristic Initial Seed:** Identify all ORFs longer than a threshold (typically 300 bp, since bacterial genes average ~950 bp). These short ORFs are statistically very likely to be non-coding; longer ORFs are candidate genes.

2. **Initial Model Training:** Build an order-5 Markov chain model $M_0$ using the long ORFs as a tentative coding set, and the short ORFs (or flanking intergenic regions) as a tentative non-coding set.

3. **Re-Scanning:** Use $M_0$ to re-score every ORF in the genome using the log-likelihood ratio.

4. **Threshold Filtering:** Keep only ORFs whose $L(S)$ exceeds a high threshold (e.g., > 95th percentile of all ORFs). These constitute a refined, high-confidence coding set.

5. **Iterative Retraining:** Re-estimate the Markov model parameters using the refined coding and non-coding sets to obtain $M_1$.

6. **Convergence Check:** Repeat steps 3–5 until the model parameters (or the ORF predictions) stabilize. This typically converges in 5–10 iterations.

**Why Self-Training is Essential:**

- **Eliminates manual annotation bottleneck** for newly sequenced genomes.
- **Adapts to organism-specific codon usage** without external training data.
- **Robust against GC-content variation** (each genome has its own model).
- **Enables metagenomic gene prediction** where reference genomes are unavailable.

**(b)** Given a short DNA window of length 8 bp with the sequence `ATGCCGTA` and the following first-order Markov probabilities, compute the log-likelihood ratio and classify the sequence. **(7 marks)**

**Given Probabilities:**

| Context | Nucleotide | $P_{cod}$ | $P_{non}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | T | 0.30 | 0.25 |
| T | G | 0.40 | 0.25 |
| G | C | 0.20 | 0.25 |
| C | C | 0.25 | 0.25 |
| C | G | 0.35 | 0.25 |
| G | T | 0.30 | 0.25 |
| T | A | 0.45 | 0.25 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Contexts (m = 1)**

Sequence: A-T-G-C-C-G-T-A
Contexts (with the next nucleotide): (A→T), (T→G), (G→C), (C→C), (C→G), (G→T), (T→A)

**Step 2 — Compute Each Log-Likelihood Term**

| Position $i$ | Context | $s_i$ | $\log \frac{P_{cod}}{P_{non}}$ | Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | A | T | $\log(0.30/0.25)$ | $\log 1.20 = 0.1823$ |
| 3 | T | G | $\log(0.40/0.25)$ | $\log 1.60 = 0.4700$ |
| 4 | G | C | $\log(0.20/0.25)$ | $\log 0.80 = -0.2231$ |
| 5 | C | C | $\log(0.25/0.25)$ | $\log 1.00 = 0.0000$ |
| 6 | C | G | $\log(0.35/0.25)$ | $\log 1.40 = 0.3365$ |
| 7 | G | T | $\log(0.30/0.25)$ | $\log 1.20 = 0.1823$ |
| 8 | T | A | $\log(0.45/0.25)$ | $\log 1.80 = 0.5878$ |

**Step 3 — Sum the Log-Likelihood Ratios**

$$
L(S) = 0.1823 + 0.4700 + (-0.2231) + 0.0000 + 0.3365 + 0.1823 + 0.5878
$$

$$
L(S) = 1.5358
$$

**Step 4 — Classification**

Since $L(S) = 1.5358 > 0$, **the sequence is classified as a coding region (gene)**.

The positive contribution is dominated by the (T→A) and (T→G) transitions, which are strongly enriched in coding frames — consistent with the high frequency of leucine and valine codons in prokaryotic genes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Markov order assumption** — many students write generic formulas without specifying the order *m*. Always state that order-5 is typical for prokaryotic GeneMark.
> 2. **Confusing log-likelihood ratio with probability** — they are not the same. $L(S)$ is a *log-ratio*, not a probability. Do not claim "75% probability of being a gene" from $L(S) > 0$.
> 3. **Missing biological context** — Viterbi in GeneMark.hmm is not abstract; it produces *exon-intron boundaries*. Mentioning GT-AG splice rules earns extra marks.
> 4. **Self-training circularity confusion** — GeneMarkS does NOT train on annotations; it trains on its own predictions iteratively. State this explicitly.
> 5. **Sign errors in numerical calculations** — verify each $\log$ value against a calculator before summation. Even a single sign flip changes the classification outcome.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GeneMark is an ab initio statistical gene finder** that uses Markov chain models — it does *not* rely on sequence homology.
- **Three canonical variants** exist: GeneMark (prokaryotic), GeneMark.hmm (eukaryotic, HMM-based), GeneMarkS (self-training, unsupervised).
- **Markov chain order *m*** controls context length; **order-5** (pentamer) is the standard for prokaryotic gene prediction because it captures codon-periodic bias.
- **Log-likelihood ratio** $L(S) = \log \frac{P(S \vert M_{cod})}{P(S \vert M_{non})}$ is the core scoring function; **positive L(S) implies coding**.
- **GeneMark.hmm** extends to a Hidden Markov Model with explicit gene-structure states (initial exon, internal exon, intron, terminal exon) obeying **GT-AG splice rules**.
- **Viterbi algorithm** is used to find the optimal state path through the HMM, producing precise exon-intron annotations.
- **GeneMarkS iterative procedure**: heuristic seeding → train → rescan → threshold → retrain → converge.
- **Bayesian decision boundary of $L = 0$** assumes equal priors — standard ab initio assumption.
- **Real-world deployment**: GeneMark is embedded in NCBI's PGAP, MAKER, and many metagenomic annotation pipelines.
- **Engineering parallel**: The same Markov mathematics is used in speech recognition, NLP, and signal processing — making bioinformatics gene-finding a classic application of stochastic processes.
- **Compare with GLIMMER, GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS**: All are ab initio gene finders, but GeneMark is historically the first to use rigorous Markov chain theory (Borodovsky & McIninch, 1993).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Protein Structures - Primary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT861 - BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Protein Structures - Primary # 🧬 Introduction to Protein Structures — Primary Structure ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Structure (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > The **primary structure of a protein** is the **linear, covalent sequence of amino acid residues** linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds**, conventionally written from the **N-terminal (amino) end** to the **C-terminal (carboxyl) end**. It is the most fundamental level of protein organization, encoded directly by the nucleotide sequence of the corresponding gene via the genetic code. Every protein, regardless of its complexity, is built upon this single blueprint. The primary structure specifies not only the identity of each amino acid but also its precise position in the chain, which in turn dictates every higher level of folding (secondary, tertiary, quaternary). ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Beaded Necklace" Analogy Imagine a **necklace made of 20 different coloured beads**, where each colour represents one of the 20 standard amino acids.
